  1. VACUUM removes old files past a retention window; setting it too low breaks time travel and can break active readers/streams.
  2. Keep retention >= max(recovery need, streaming need); never RETAIN 0 in prod.
  3. It reclaims storage but destroys recoverability if mis-set.
  4. Guard VACUUM changes.
  5. Understand the tradeoff.

Starter pattern

VACUUM claims RETAIN 168 HOURS  -- >= recovery/streaming need
# never RETAIN 0 HOURS in production
Common mistake: Running VACUUM with low/zero retention to save storage, destroying time-travel recovery and breaking streams.
Principal follow-up: What retention must VACUUM respect to avoid breaking time travel and streaming reads?
044
Change data feed: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
AdvancedChange Data Feed

Model answer

Delta Change Data Feed (CDF) records row-level changes (insert/update/delete with _change_type) so downstream consumers can read just the deltas instead of recomputing. Enable it per table and read changes by version/timestamp for incremental propagation.

  • CDF emits row-level change records with _change_type and commit version.
  • Enable delta.enableChangeDataFeed=true on the table.
  • Downstream reads only changed rows → cheap incremental updates.
  • Great for feeding gold marts or external systems incrementally.

Healthcare angle: Propagating only changed claims to a downstream actuarial mart via CDF avoids full reloads each cycle.

spark.read.format("delta").option("readChangeFeed","true") \
     .option("startingVersion", v).table("claims")

What strong answers cover

  1. Delta Change Data Feed (CDF) exposes row-level changes (insert/update/delete) so downstream can consume only what changed.
  2. Enable it; read change types to drive incremental downstream MERGEs.
  3. Avoids full-table diffs.
  4. Powers efficient CDC pipelines.
  5. Mind retention of change data.

Starter pattern

spark.read.format('delta').option('readChangeFeed','true')
  .option('startingVersion', v).table('claims')
Common mistake: Computing downstream deltas by diffing full table snapshots when CDF would stream the exact changes.
Principal follow-up: How does Delta CDF make a downstream incremental pipeline cheaper than snapshot diffing?
045
Cdc upsert pattern: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
AdvancedCDC upsert pattern

Model answer

Apply source CDC (insert/update/delete events) to a Delta target via MERGE keyed on the natural key, ordered by the change sequence so the latest wins, mapping deletes to soft-deletes/end-dating, and made idempotent against replayed events.

  • MERGE on natural key; pick latest by LSN/commit time within the batch.
  • Map delete events to soft-delete/end-date in dimensions.
  • Dedup multiple events per key per batch before merging.
  • Idempotent so replays don't corrupt state.

Healthcare angle: CDC from a membership source system upserts the member dimension while preserving SCD2 history and honoring deletes.

latest = changes.withColumn("rn", row_number().over(
    Window.partitionBy("nk").orderBy(col("lsn").desc()))).filter("rn=1")
# then MERGE latest into target

What strong answers cover

  1. Apply CDC by MERGEing change rows on the natural key, ordered by change sequence, mapping inserts/updates/deletes (deletes to soft-deletes).
  2. Dedup to the latest change per key before the MERGE.
  3. Idempotent and ordered.
  4. Maintain SCD2 where history matters.
  5. Reconcile to source.

Starter pattern

latest = changes.withColumn('rn', row_number().over(
  Window.partitionBy('key').orderBy(col('seq').desc()))).filter('rn=1')
deltaT.merge(latest,'t.key=s.key').whenMatched...().whenNotMatched...().execute()
Common mistake: Applying CDC changes out of order or without deduping to latest-per-key, so an older change overwrites a newer one.
Principal follow-up: Why must you dedup to latest-per-key and order changes before the upsert MERGE?
046
Scd2 in pyspark: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
AdvancedSCD2 in PySpark

Model answer

Implement SCD2 by detecting changed rows via a tracked-column hash, then a two-step Delta MERGE: close the current version (set valid_to/is_current=false) for changed keys, and insert the new version. Make it idempotent and handle late-arriving effective dates.

  • Hash tracked columns to detect real changes.
  • Step 1: MERGE to expire current rows for changed keys.
  • Step 2: insert new current versions (changed + brand-new keys).
  • Idempotent + bitemporal handling for retro corrections.

Healthcare angle: Provider/member dimensions need SCD2 so claims join to the version effective at service date; the hash avoids churn on cosmetic changes.

# expire then insert (Delta MERGE), keyed on natural key + is_current
delta.alias("t").merge(changed.alias("s"),"t.nk=s.nk AND t.is_current=true") \
     .whenMatchedUpdate(set={"valid_to":"s.eff-1","is_current":"false"}).execute()

What strong answers cover

  1. SCD2 in PySpark: detect changes via a hash, close current rows (set valid_to/is_current=false) and insert new versions, idempotently.
  2. Use a two-part MERGE (or MERGE + insert) keyed on the natural key.
  3. Guarantee one current row per key.
  4. Handle late/out-of-order updates.
  5. Validate current-row invariant.

Starter pattern

deltaT.alias('t').merge(updates.alias('s'), 't.key=s.key AND t.is_current')
  .whenMatched('t.hash<>s.hash').updateExpr({'valid_to':'s.eff','is_current':'false'})
  .execute()  # then insert new current rows
Common mistake: A non-idempotent close+insert that leaves two current rows (or zero) on a rerun, fanning out joins.
Principal follow-up: How do you guarantee exactly one current row per key in a PySpark SCD2 merge, even on reruns?
047
Unit testing spark jobs: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Advancedunit testing Spark jobs

Model answer

Test transformation functions that take and return DataFrames using a local SparkSession and small crafted inputs, asserting exact output (schema + rows). Keep transforms pure and decoupled from I/O so tests are fast and deterministic; reserve integration tests for read/write.

  • Structure code as pure df→df transforms, separate from I/O.
  • Use a local SparkSession fixture with tiny inputs and known outputs.
  • Assert schema and row content, including edge cases (nulls/dupes/ties).
  • Mock or stub source/sink in integration tests.

Healthcare angle: Dedup/SCD2/skew-handling logic should be unit-tested with crafted duplicate/version rows to lock correctness before scale.

def test_dedup(spark):
    out = dedup(spark.createDataFrame([...]))
    assert out.collect() == expected

What strong answers cover

  1. Test transformation logic on small DataFrames with known input/expected output, including edge cases (nulls, dupes, late data).
  2. Factor transforms into pure functions (DataFrame in -> DataFrame out) testable with a local SparkSession.
  3. Use chispa/assertDataFrameEqual for comparison.
  4. Keep tests fast; avoid real clusters/IO.
  5. Run in CI.

Starter pattern

def test_dedup():
    out = dedup(spark.createDataFrame(rows))
    assertDataFrameEqual(out, expected)
Common mistake: Only testing on the cluster against real data, so logic bugs and edge cases aren't caught before deploy.
Principal follow-up: How do you structure Spark code so transformations are unit-testable without a cluster?
048
Logging in spark: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Advancedlogging in Spark

Model answer

Log at the driver for orchestration/metrics and avoid heavy per-row executor logging (it's distributed and noisy). Emit structured run-level metrics (counts, durations, control totals), use the Spark UI/event logs for stage detail, and never log PHI.

  • Driver logs for run lifecycle and metrics; avoid per-row executor logs.
  • Emit structured control totals/counts for observability.
  • Use Spark UI/event logs + Ganglia/metrics sinks for stage detail.
  • Never log member/claim PHI — log ids/counts.

Healthcare angle: Logging full claim records from executors both floods logs and risks PHI exposure; log aggregate counts and surrogate ids.

What strong answers cover

  1. Log structured, PHI-safe context (job, stage, batch_id, counts) for triage; remember executor logs are distributed.
  2. Don't log per-row inside executors (volume) or any PHI.
  3. Use metrics/accumulators for counts.
  4. Centralize logs for observability.
  5. Log decisions, not data.

Starter pattern

logger.info('stage_done', extra={'job':job_id,'rows':df.count(),'batch':batch_id})
# no PHI; aggregate counts, not per-row logs
Common mistake: Logging per-row inside executors (huge volume) or logging PHI-bearing records — noise plus a compliance breach.
Principal follow-up: What should and shouldn't you log in a distributed Spark job, and why?
049
Reprocessing six months: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Advancedreprocessing six months

Model answer

Reprocess a large window safely by partitioning the work (per service_month), using idempotent partition overwrites or keyed MERGE, running in bounded batches to control cost, and reconciling each partition before publishing. Avoid one giant job; checkpoint progress so failures resume.

  • Chunk by service_month; idempotent overwrite/MERGE per chunk.
  • Bound concurrency/cost; don't reprocess everything at once.
  • Reconcile counts/dollars per partition before flipping consumers.
  • Resumable: track completed partitions.

Healthcare angle: A six-month claims restatement after a logic fix runs month-by-month with per-month reconciliation, not as a single risky rerun.

What strong answers cover

  1. Reprocess per service_month, idempotently (replaceWhere/MERGE), in bounded batches, reconciling each before publishing, resumably.
  2. Isolate from live processing; communicate restatements.
  3. Never one giant rerun.
  4. Prioritize needed periods.
  5. Validate per month.

Starter pattern

for m in months:
  out = recompute(m)
  out.write.option('replaceWhere',f"service_month='{m}'").save(path)  # idempotent
  reconcile(m)
Common mistake: One massive six-month rerun that fails midway with no resumability and no per-month validation.
Principal follow-up: Why reprocess month-by-month with per-unit reconciliation instead of one large rerun?
050
Cost tuning clusters: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Advancedcost tuning clusters

Model answer

Cut Spark/Databricks cost by right-sizing clusters to the workload, using job clusters (auto-terminating) over always-on, autoscaling within sensible bounds, spot/preemptible for fault-tolerant batch, Photon for SQL-heavy work, and eliminating waste (small files, skew, over-caching, unnecessary recomputation).

  • Job clusters that auto-terminate; avoid idle all-purpose clusters.
  • Autoscale with min/max bounds; spot for retry-safe batch (not for drivers).
  • Photon for SQL/Delta workloads; fix small files and skew that waste compute.
  • Attribute cost by job/tag to find the expensive offenders.

Healthcare angle: A nightly claims pipeline on an auto-terminating job cluster with spot workers and Photon is far cheaper than an always-on all-purpose cluster.

What strong answers cover

  1. Cut cost via right-sized/auto-terminating clusters, instance pools, spot for fault-tolerant batch, incremental over full reloads, and fixing skew/small files/over-caching.
  2. Separate workloads; attribute cost.
  3. Biggest wins are usually algorithmic (incremental, skew), not just smaller clusters.
  4. Use autoscaling within limits.
  5. Measure before/after.

Starter pattern

# auto-terminate + pools + spot for batch; incremental loads
# fix skew/small files; right-size; attribute cost by job
Common mistake: Only shrinking cluster size while leaving full reloads, skew, and small-file scans that drive the real cost.
Principal follow-up: Where do the biggest Spark cost savings usually come from beyond cluster sizing?
051
Advanced payer spark scenario: narrow vs wide transformations in claims pipeline: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Advancedadvanced payer Spark scenario: narrow vs wide transformations in claims pipeline

Model answer

Narrow transformations (map, filter, withColumn) compute each output partition from one input partition with no data movement; wide transformations (groupBy, join, distinct, repartition) require a shuffle that redistributes data across the cluster. Shuffles are where cost, skew, and spill live, so design to minimize and tame them.

  • Narrow = no shuffle (filter/select/withColumn); wide = shuffle (join/groupBy/distinct/window).
  • Push filters/projections before wide ops to shrink the shuffle.
  • Each wide op is a stage boundary in the DAG; count them to estimate shuffles.
  • Most performance work is about the wide-transformation shuffles.

Healthcare angle: Filtering claims to the target service_month before joining the provider dimension cuts shuffle volume dramatically.

claims.filter("service_month=202406").select("claim_id","npi","paid") \
      .join(broadcast(provider), "npi")   # filter+project (narrow) before join (wide)

What strong answers cover

  1. Narrow transforms (map/filter/select) need no shuffle; wide transforms (groupBy/join/distinct) shuffle data across the cluster.
  2. Shuffles are the expensive part — minimize and order them.
  3. Filter early (narrow) to shrink data before wide ops.
  4. Each wide op is a stage boundary in the DAG.
  5. Explain the cost model, not just definitions.

Starter pattern

claims.filter(col('status')=='paid')          # narrow, no shuffle
      .groupBy('member_id').sum('paid')        # wide, shuffles
Common mistake: Doing wide ops (join/groupBy) before filtering, shuffling far more data than necessary.
Principal follow-up: How would you reorder a claims pipeline to push filters before shuffles, and why does it help?
052
Advanced payer spark scenario: repartition vs coalesce after filtering: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Advancedadvanced payer Spark scenario: repartition vs coalesce after filtering

Model answer

After a heavy filter you often have many tiny or skewed partitions. repartition(n) does a full shuffle to n balanced partitions (use when increasing parallelism or rebalancing skew); coalesce(n) merges partitions without a full shuffle (cheaper, for reducing partition count). Coalesce can create uneven partitions.

  • repartition: full shuffle, balanced output, can increase or decrease count.
  • coalesce: no full shuffle, only decreases, may leave imbalance.
  • Use coalesce to avoid tiny output files; repartition to fix skew/raise parallelism.
  • repartition(col) co-locates by key for a subsequent join/write.

Healthcare angle: After filtering a year of claims to one month, coalesce reduces output file count; repartition by member_id rebalances before a skewed aggregation.

filtered.coalesce(8).write...        # fewer output files
filtered.repartition(200,"member_id")  # rebalance for keyed work

What strong answers cover

  1. repartition does a full shuffle to any partition count (can increase); coalesce merges partitions without a full shuffle (decrease only).
  2. After a heavy filter you often have many tiny/empty partitions — coalesce to reduce them cheaply.
  3. Use repartition when you need even distribution or more partitions.
  4. coalesce(1) collapses parallelism (anti-pattern at scale).
  5. Match partition count to data size.

Starter pattern

filtered = claims.filter(...)
filtered.coalesce(50).write...   # cheap reduce, no full shuffle
# repartition(200, 'member_id') when you need even/more partitions
Common mistake: Using repartition (full shuffle) just to reduce partition count after a filter, when coalesce avoids the shuffle.
Principal follow-up: After filtering 2TB down to 50GB, do you repartition or coalesce, and why?
053
Advanced payer spark scenario: broadcast join provider dimension to claims: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Advancedadvanced payer Spark scenario: broadcast join provider dimension to claims

Model answer

When one side is small (a provider dimension), broadcast it to every executor so the large claims fact joins locally with no shuffle of the big table. Use broadcast() hint or rely on AQE/autoBroadcastJoinThreshold; ensure the small side truly fits in executor memory.

  • broadcast(small_df) ships it to all executors → no shuffle of the large fact.
  • Right when one side is well under the broadcast threshold (tune autoBroadcastJoinThreshold).
  • Too-large broadcast causes driver/executor OOM or broadcast timeout.
  • AQE can auto-convert to broadcast at runtime based on actual sizes.

Healthcare angle: Joining the provider dimension to billions of claim lines is the textbook broadcast join in payer pipelines.

from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast
claims.join(broadcast(provider_dim), "provider_npi", "left")

What strong answers cover

  1. Broadcast the small side (provider dim) to every executor so the large claims side isn't shuffled.
  2. Huge win when one side fits in memory (well under the broadcast threshold).
  3. AQE can auto-broadcast; you can hint it.
  4. Don't broadcast something too large (driver/executor OOM).
  5. Verify the broadcast in the plan.

Starter pattern

from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast
claims.join(broadcast(provider_dim), 'npi')
Common mistake: Broadcasting a dimension that's too large, OOMing executors — or NOT broadcasting a small dim and shuffling the huge claims fact.
Principal follow-up: How do you decide what's small enough to broadcast, and what happens if you broadcast too much?
054
Advanced payer spark scenario: aqe changing join strategy: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Advancedadvanced payer Spark scenario: AQE changing join strategy

Model answer

Adaptive Query Execution re-optimizes at runtime using actual shuffle statistics: it can switch a sort-merge join to a broadcast join when a side turns out small, coalesce shuffle partitions, and split skewed partitions. It fixes plans that static estimates got wrong.

  • AQE uses runtime stats, not just compile-time estimates.
  • Dynamic join switching (SMJ→broadcast), partition coalescing, and skew-join handling.
  • Enabled by default on modern Spark/Databricks; verify in the SQL plan ('AQE' nodes).
  • Reduces the need for manual hints, but you still set thresholds.

Healthcare angle: A claims join whose filtered dimension is smaller than estimated gets auto-broadcast by AQE, avoiding a costly shuffle.

spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled","true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled","true")

What strong answers cover

  1. Adaptive Query Execution re-optimizes at runtime using actual stats: switching to broadcast, coalescing shuffle partitions, handling skew.
  2. It fixes plans that static optimization (stale stats) gets wrong.
  3. Enable it; understand what it changed in the plan.
  4. AQE skewJoin splits hot partitions automatically.
  5. Know its three main features.

Starter pattern

spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true
spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled=true
spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled=true
Common mistake: Leaving AQE off and fighting bad static plans (wrong join strategy, fixed partition counts) manually.
Principal follow-up: What three things does AQE change at runtime, and how would you confirm it switched a join to broadcast?
055
Advanced payer spark scenario: skewed provider_npi join: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Advancedadvanced payer Spark scenario: skewed provider_npi join

Model answer

A few providers (or a default NPI) carry a hugely disproportionate row share, so one reduce task runs long while others idle. Tame it with AQE skew-join handling, salting the hot keys, isolating defaults, or broadcasting the small side to avoid the shuffle entirely.

  • Diagnose: long-tail task in the join stage; one partition far bigger.
  • Enable AQE skewJoin to auto-split large partitions.
  • Salt hot keys: add a random suffix to spread them, replicate the small side.
  • Broadcast the dimension if it fits — no shuffle, no skew.

Healthcare angle: Provider_npi=0 / UNKNOWN sinks often dominate; isolating or salting them removes month-end skew.

# salt hot keys
claims2 = claims.withColumn("salt",(rand()*10).cast("int"))
dim2 = dim.crossJoin(spark.range(10).toDF("salt"))
claims2.join(dim2, ["provider_npi","salt"])

What strong answers cover

  1. Skew = one join key (e.g. a default/high-volume NPI) has far more rows, creating a straggler task.
  2. Mitigate with AQE skewJoin, salting the hot key, or isolating it.
  3. Diagnose via per-key counts and uneven task durations in the Spark UI.
  4. More executors don't fix a single hot key.
  5. Validate even tasks after.

Starter pattern

# AQE handles it, or salt the hot key:
claims.withColumn('k', concat('npi', lit('_'), (rand()*10).cast('int')))
      .join(dim_salted, 'k')
Common mistake: Adding cores to fix a skewed NPI join — the one hot partition still bottlenecks the whole stage.
Principal follow-up: How do salting and AQE skewJoin each resolve a hot-NPI join, and how do you pick?
056
Advanced payer spark scenario: salting high-volume unknown member_id: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: salting high-volume UNKNOWN member_id

Model answer

When a sentinel key (member_id='UNKNOWN') concentrates rows on one partition, add a random salt to the join/group key to spread those rows across tasks, and explode the small side to match each salt value. Strip the salt after the operation.

  • Add salt = rand()%N to the skewed key on the big side.
  • Replicate the small side across all N salt values so the join still matches.
  • Aggregate per (key,salt) then re-aggregate by key to remove the salt.
  • Only salt the genuinely hot keys to avoid over-shuffling the rest.

Healthcare angle: A large share of claims default to UNKNOWN member when matching fails; salting prevents that one bucket from dominating runtime.

big = df.withColumn("salt",(rand()*16).cast("int"))
small_x = small.crossJoin(spark.range(16).toDF("salt"))
big.join(small_x, ["key","salt"]).drop("salt")

What strong answers cover

  1. Salting spreads a hot key across N synthetic sub-keys so its rows distribute across tasks, then aggregates in two stages.
  2. Common for default/UNKNOWN member_id concentrating rows.
  3. Salt both sides for a join; un-salt after.
  4. Choose N by the skew severity.
  5. Validate the result equals the unsalted aggregate.

Starter pattern

n=20
fact_s = fact.withColumn('salt', (rand()*n).cast('int'))
dim_s  = dim.crossJoin(spark.range(n).withColumnRenamed('id','salt'))
fact_s.join(dim_s, ['key','salt'])
Common mistake: Salting only one side of a join, so rows no longer match and the join silently drops or mismatches.
Principal follow-up: When you salt one side of a join, what must you do to the other side to keep results correct?
057
Advanced payer spark scenario: window function over 2tb claims: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: window function over 2TB claims

Model answer

Window functions shuffle and sort within each partition key, which is expensive at multi-TB scale. Reduce the data first (filter, pre-aggregate), partition the window by a high-cardinality key to spread work, and avoid unbounded frames where a running aggregate over the whole partition forces large sorts/spills.

  • PARTITION BY a selective, high-cardinality key (member_id) to parallelize.
  • Pre-filter/pre-aggregate to shrink the windowed input.
  • Bounded frames (ROWS BETWEEN ...) cost less than unbounded running sorts.
  • Watch spill on the sort; size shuffle partitions and enable AQE.

Healthcare angle: Latest-version-per-claim or running-paid-per-member over a 2TB claims history needs key partitioning and pre-filtering to avoid massive spill.

from pyspark.sql.window import Window
w=Window.partitionBy("claim_id").orderBy(col("ingest_ts").desc())
latest=claims.withColumn("rn",row_number().over(w)).filter("rn=1")

What strong answers cover

  1. Window functions shuffle/sort by the partition key; at 2TB, partition selectivity and spill control matter.
  2. Pre-filter/pre-aggregate; partition the window by a high-cardinality key to spread work.
  3. Watch for skew on the partition key and heavy spill.
  4. Avoid an unpartitioned global window (one task).
  5. Size for the sort.

Starter pattern

from pyspark.sql.window import Window
w = Window.partitionBy('member_id').orderBy('service_dt')
claims.withColumn('rn', row_number().over(w))
Common mistake: A window with no partitionBy (global ordering), forcing all 2TB through a single task.
Principal follow-up: Why does an unpartitioned window collapse to one task, and how do you parallelize it at 2TB?
058
Advanced payer spark scenario: cache vs persist on reused dataframe: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: cache vs persist on reused dataframe

Model answer

cache() persists with the default MEMORY_AND_DISK storage; persist(level) lets you choose the storage level. Cache only DataFrames reused multiple times in the same job, after narrowing them, and unpersist when done. Caching everything wastes memory and can cause eviction churn.

  • Cache reused, expensive-to-recompute, already-narrowed DataFrames only.
  • persist() to pick a level (e.g. DISK_ONLY for big data, MEMORY_ONLY for small hot sets).
  • cache is lazy — materialize with an action; unpersist() to release.
  • Don't cache single-use frames; it adds overhead, not speed.

Healthcare angle: A cleaned claims base reused by several gold marts is a good cache target; the raw full year usually isn't.

base = claims.filter(...).select(...).cache()
base.count()        # materialize
... # multiple downstream uses
base.unpersist()

What strong answers cover

  1. cache() = persist(MEMORY_AND_DISK); persist() lets you choose the storage level.
  2. Only cache a DataFrame reused multiple times (and after expensive computation).
  3. Caching is lazy — materialized on first action.
  4. Unpersist when done to free memory.
  5. Don't cache single-use or huge data.

Starter pattern

df = expensive.cache()
df.count()        # materialize
... reuse df several times ...
df.unpersist()
Common mistake: Caching a DataFrame used once (wasting memory) or never unpersisting, causing memory pressure and spill.
Principal follow-up: When does caching help versus hurt, and why must you unpersist?
059
Advanced payer spark scenario: udf vs built-in functions: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: UDF vs built-in functions

Model answer

Built-in Spark SQL functions run in the JVM/Catalyst and are vectorized and optimizable; Python UDFs serialize rows to Python and back, blocking optimizations and running far slower. Prefer built-ins/expr; if you must use Python logic, use pandas (vectorized) UDFs.

  • Built-ins are Catalyst-optimizable and avoid Python serialization.
  • Plain Python UDFs are a black box and slow at scale.
  • Pandas/Arrow UDFs vectorize and cut serialization cost when custom logic is unavoidable.
  • Many 'need a UDF' cases are solvable with when/regexp/higher-order functions.

Healthcare angle: Mapping CARC codes or parsing fields is usually expressible with built-ins; reserve UDFs for genuinely custom logic and prefer pandas UDFs.

# prefer built-ins
df.withColumn("bucket", when(col("paid")>1000,"high").otherwise("low"))

What strong answers cover

  1. Built-in Spark SQL functions run in the JVM and are optimized; Python UDFs serialize rows to Python (slow, no Catalyst optimization).
  2. Prefer built-ins/higher-order functions; use pandas UDFs (vectorized) if you must.
  3. UDFs are a black box to the optimizer (no pushdown).
  4. Reserve UDFs for genuinely custom logic.
  5. Measure the cost.

Starter pattern

# slow: udf(lambda x: x.strip())
# fast: F.trim(col('x')); pandas_udf for vectorized custom logic
Common mistake: Using a Python UDF where a built-in exists, paying serialization overhead and blocking Catalyst optimization.
Principal follow-up: Why are Python UDFs slow, and when is a pandas (vectorized) UDF the right compromise?
060
Advanced payer spark scenario: explode nested claim diagnosis array: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: explode nested claim diagnosis array

Model answer

explode turns each element of an array column into its own row; use it to normalize a claim's diagnosis/procedure array into a diagnosis fact. Use explode_outer to keep claims with empty/null arrays, and posexplode when you need the element position (primary vs secondary dx).

  • explode = one row per array element; explode_outer keeps null/empty arrays.
  • posexplode preserves ordinal position (Dx1 primary, Dx2 secondary...).
  • It multiplies rows — re-establish the new grain and keys.
  • Filter/limit before exploding huge arrays to control fan-out.

Healthcare angle: An 837 claim carries multiple diagnosis pointers; exploding them builds the diagnosis bridge with primary/secondary order preserved.

from pyspark.sql.functions import posexplode
dx = claims.select("claim_id", posexplode("diagnoses").alias("dx_seq","dx_code"))

What strong answers cover

  1. explode turns an array column (e.g. diagnoses) into one row per element — the standard way to normalize nested claim data.
  2. Use posexplode to keep ordinal position (primary vs secondary dx).
  3. explode_outer keeps rows with empty/null arrays.
  4. Filter before exploding large arrays.
  5. Watch row multiplication.

Starter pattern

from pyspark.sql.functions import posexplode
claims.select('claim_id', posexplode('diagnoses').alias('seq','dx'))
Common mistake: Using explode (not explode_outer) and silently dropping claims with no diagnoses, or losing dx order.
Principal follow-up: When do you need posexplode and explode_outer for claim diagnosis arrays?
061
Advanced payer spark scenario: higher-order functions on arrays: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: higher-order functions on arrays

Model answer

Spark SQL higher-order functions (transform, filter, exists, aggregate) operate on array columns without exploding or UDFs, keeping work in Catalyst. Use them to filter/transform nested arrays efficiently.

  • transform/filter/exists/aggregate run natively on arrays.
  • Avoid explode+regroup when you only need to reshape within the array.
  • No Python serialization cost — fully optimizable.
  • Combine with element_at/array_contains for lookups.

Healthcare angle: Checking whether any diagnosis in a claim's array is a chronic-condition code is a one-liner with exists, no explode needed.

df.withColumn("has_chronic", expr("exists(diagnoses, d -> d in ('E11','I10'))"))

What strong answers cover

  1. Higher-order functions (transform, filter, aggregate, exists) operate on array columns without exploding — faster, no row blow-up.
  2. Use them to process nested claim line/dx arrays in place.
  3. Avoid explode when you only need a derived value per array.
  4. JVM-optimized, no UDF.
  5. Cleaner than explode+regroup.

Starter pattern

from pyspark.sql.functions import filter as arr_filter, exists
claims.withColumn('has_chronic', exists('dx_codes', lambda c: c.isin(CHRONIC)))
Common mistake: Exploding an array just to compute one aggregate, multiplying rows then regrouping, when a higher-order function does it in place.
Principal follow-up: When is a higher-order function better than explode for nested claim arrays?
062
Advanced payer spark scenario: mappartitions for parser optimization: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: mapPartitions for parser optimization

Model answer

mapPartitions runs your function once per partition (an iterator of rows) instead of per row, so you amortize expensive setup — opening a parser, a connection, or loading a reference map — across the whole partition. Yield results lazily to keep memory flat.

  • One setup per partition, not per row — big win for heavy init.
  • Operates on an iterator; yield rows to stream output.
  • Reuse connections/parsers/reference objects within the partition.
  • Beware holding the whole partition in memory — stay iterator-based.

Healthcare angle: Initializing an X12 parser or reference lookup once per partition, then streaming claims through it, beats per-row construction.

def parse_part(rows):
    p = X12Parser()           # once per partition
    for r in rows: yield p.parse(r)
rdd.mapPartitions(parse_part)

What strong answers cover

  1. mapPartitions runs a function once per partition (not per row), so you can amortize setup (a parser/connection) across many rows.
  2. Great for expensive per-row init (e.g. building an 837 parser once).
  3. Returns an iterator; keep it lazy/streaming.
  4. Avoid loading the whole partition into memory.
  5. Use over map for heavy setup.

Starter pattern

def parse_part(rows):
    parser = build_parser()      # once per partition
    for r in rows: yield parser.parse(r)
rdd.mapPartitions(parse_part)
Common mistake: Building the parser per row (in a UDF/map) instead of once per partition, repeating expensive setup millions of times.
Principal follow-up: How does mapPartitions amortize parser setup, and how do you keep it memory-safe?
063
Advanced payer spark scenario: spark ui stage diagnosis: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: Spark UI stage diagnosis

Model answer

Use the Spark UI to find the bottleneck: the Stages tab shows task duration distribution (a long max vs median = skew), shuffle read/write sizes, and spill. The SQL tab maps the physical plan to stages. Diagnose by locating the slow stage, then its cause (skew, spill, too few/many partitions, big shuffle).

  • Compare max vs median task time per stage — divergence = skew.
  • Check shuffle read/write and spill metrics for the heavy stage.
  • SQL tab links operators to stages to find the offending join/aggregate.
  • Task count vs core count reveals under/over-parallelism.

Healthcare angle: A claims job slow only at month-end usually shows one straggler task in the join stage — skew, visible directly in the UI.

What strong answers cover

  1. The Spark UI shows stages/tasks: look for a straggler task (skew), heavy shuffle read/write, spill, and the dominant stage.
  2. Compare task durations (even = healthy, one long = skew).
  3. Trace a slow stage to its operator in the SQL plan.
  4. Check input/shuffle sizes per task.
  5. Target the one bottleneck.

Starter pattern

# Spark UI -> Stages: max vs median task time (skew),
# shuffle read/write, spill; SQL tab -> operator times
Common mistake: Guessing at performance instead of reading the Spark UI to find the actual straggler/shuffle/spill bottleneck.
Principal follow-up: Walk through diagnosing a slow claims job from the Spark UI stages and SQL tabs.
064
Advanced payer spark scenario: shuffle read/write interpretation: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: shuffle read/write interpretation

Model answer

Shuffle write is data a stage emits for the next stage; shuffle read is what the next stage pulls. Large shuffle volumes signal expensive wide ops; lopsided read across tasks signals skew; high spill means the shuffle exceeded memory. Reduce by filtering early, broadcasting, or choosing a better partitioning.

  • Big shuffle write/read = costly wide transformation — shrink inputs first.
  • Uneven shuffle read per task = skew.
  • Spill during shuffle = memory pressure; fix data size/skew or size up.
  • Broadcasting the small side removes its shuffle entirely.

Healthcare angle: A multi-TB claims↔dimension join showing huge shuffle read is the cue to broadcast the dimension.

What strong answers cover

  1. Shuffle write = data a stage produces for the next; shuffle read = data the next stage pulls. Large shuffles are the main cost.
  2. High shuffle signals a wide op moving too much data — filter/pre-aggregate first.
  3. Skewed shuffle read across tasks = key skew.
  4. Reduce by pruning columns/rows before the shuffle.
  5. Watch spill during shuffle.

Starter pattern

# Spark UI: large Shuffle Write -> reduce data before the wide op
# uneven Shuffle Read per task -> skew
Common mistake: Ignoring a huge shuffle and adding executors, when the fix is to shrink the data crossing the shuffle.
Principal follow-up: What does a large or uneven shuffle tell you, and how do you reduce it?
065
Advanced payer spark scenario: spill to disk: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: spill to disk

Model answer

Spill happens when shuffle/sort/aggregate data exceeds executor memory and is written to disk, slowing the stage. Fix by reducing per-task data (filter/pre-aggregate, more partitions), resolving skew, or, last, increasing memory. Distinguish memory spill from disk spill in metrics.

  • Spill = operator exceeded memory; disk I/O tanks performance.
  • Reduce input per task (filter early, raise shuffle partitions, fix skew).
  • Skew causes localized spill on the hot task.
  • Scale memory only after structural fixes.

Healthcare angle: A full-year claims aggregation spills; pre-aggregating to member-month before the final rollup eliminates it.

spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", 800)  # more, smaller tasks

What strong answers cover

  1. Spill = an operator (sort/join/aggregate) exceeds memory and writes to disk, slowing the stage.
  2. Reduce input (filter/pre-aggregate), raise parallelism, fix skew.
  3. Find the spilling operator in the UI.
  4. Spill can precede OOM.
  5. Validate spill dropped after the fix.

Starter pattern

# Spark UI shows spill (memory/disk) on a task
# fix: pre-aggregate, more shuffle partitions, fix skew
Common mistake: Treating heavy spill as merely slow until the same operator OOMs at higher volume.
Principal follow-up: What's your ordered checklist to reduce spill before adding memory?
066
Advanced payer spark scenario: small files problem: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: small files problem

Model answer

Many tiny files (from streaming or over-partitioned writes) overwhelm the driver/metastore and make reads slow due to per-file overhead. Fix with coalesce/repartition before write, Delta OPTIMIZE/auto-compaction, optimizeWrite, and avoiding excessive partition columns.

  • Tiny files inflate task count and metadata overhead.
  • coalesce/repartition before writing to target file sizes (~128MB–1GB).
  • Delta: OPTIMIZE, optimizeWrite, autoCompact handle compaction.
  • Don't partition by high-cardinality columns that fragment output.

Healthcare angle: Auto Loader micro-batches of 837 files create many small files; scheduled OPTIMIZE keeps the claims table read-efficient.

df.repartition(16).write.format("delta").mode("append").save(path)
# periodic: OPTIMIZE claims ZORDER BY (member_id)

What strong answers cover

  1. Many tiny files (from streaming or over-partitioning) crush read/metadata performance.
  2. Compact with OPTIMIZE (Delta) or repartition before write; enable optimizeWrite/autoCompact.
  3. Reduce partition granularity (avoid high-cardinality partition columns).
  4. Right-size output files (~100MB-1GB).
  5. Schedule periodic compaction.

Starter pattern

spark.databricks.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled=true
spark.databricks.delta.autoCompact.enabled=true
# OPTIMIZE claims  (compaction)
Common mistake: Partitioning by a high-cardinality column (member_id), producing millions of tiny files.
Principal follow-up: What causes small-file explosions in a streaming claims pipeline, and how do you prevent and fix them?
067
Advanced payer spark scenario: partitioning by service_month: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: partitioning by service_month

Model answer

Partition the claims fact by service_month so date-bounded queries prune to only relevant partitions and reprocessing a month is a clean partition overwrite. Pick a partition column with moderate cardinality and strong filter correlation; avoid over-partitioning into tiny files.

  • service_month gives pruning + easy month-level reprocessing.
  • Moderate cardinality avoids the small-files problem.
  • Filters must reference the partition column directly to prune.
  • Combine with ZORDER/clustering on member/provider for secondary access.

Healthcare angle: Most claims queries are month-bounded; partitioning by service_month is the highest-leverage layout choice.

df.write.partitionBy("service_month").format("delta").save(path)

What strong answers cover

  1. Partition a large claims fact by a low-cardinality, commonly-filtered column (service_month) so queries prune to a few partitions.
  2. Avoid high-cardinality partition columns (small files).
  3. Most claims queries are month-bounded — high payoff.
  4. Combine with ZORDER on member/provider for secondary selectivity.
  5. Validate pruning.

Starter pattern

claims.write.partitionBy('service_month').format('delta').save(...)
# queries filter service_month -> prune partitions
Common mistake: Partitioning by a high-cardinality column (member_id/claim_id), creating tiny files and worse performance.
Principal follow-up: Why partition by service_month and not member_id, and what do you ZORDER instead?
068
Advanced payer spark scenario: zorder member_id/provider_npi: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: ZORDER member_id/provider_npi

Model answer

ZORDER co-locates rows with similar values of the chosen columns within files, improving data skipping for filters on those columns. Use it for high-cardinality access keys (member_id, provider_npi) on top of partitioning; it's a maintenance OPTIMIZE operation, not a write-time layout.

  • ZORDER multi-dimensionally clusters data for better file skipping.
  • Choose the 1–2 columns most often filtered/joined after partition pruning.
  • Run via OPTIMIZE ... ZORDER BY; it rewrites files.
  • Liquid clustering is the newer, maintenance-friendly alternative.

Healthcare angle: After partitioning by service_month, ZORDER by member_id speeds member-level claim lookups within each month.

OPTIMIZE claims WHERE service_month=202406 ZORDER BY (member_id, provider_npi);

What strong answers cover

  1. ZORDER co-locates data by high-cardinality columns within files, improving data-skipping for selective filters (member/provider lookups).
  2. Complements partitioning (which handles the low-cardinality split).
  3. Apply via OPTIMIZE ... ZORDER BY.
  4. Choose columns by actual filter patterns.
  5. Costs compute to maintain.

Starter pattern

OPTIMIZE claims ZORDER BY (member_id, provider_npi)
# data-skipping for needle-in-haystack lookups
Common mistake: ZORDERing columns nobody filters on (wasted compute) or expecting it to replace partitioning.
Principal follow-up: How do partitioning and ZORDER work together for a claims fact, and how do you choose ZORDER columns?
069
Advanced payer spark scenario: delta merge performance: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: Delta MERGE performance

Model answer

Delta MERGE rewrites files containing matched rows, so cost scales with how many files the matched keys touch. Speed it up by narrowing the target (partition/predicate on the merge condition), pre-filtering the source, clustering the merge key, and enabling low-shuffle merge / deletion vectors where available.

  • Add partition/predicate filters to the merge condition so fewer files are rewritten.
  • Cluster/ZORDER the target on the merge key.
  • Pre-dedup and shrink the source before merging.
  • Use deletion vectors / low-shuffle merge to reduce rewrite amplification.

Healthcare angle: Upserting late-arriving claims into a billion-row Delta table is fast only if the MERGE is constrained to the affected service_months.

MERGE INTO claims t USING upd s
ON t.claim_id=s.claim_id AND t.service_month=s.service_month  -- prune target
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET ... WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ...

What strong answers cover

  1. A Delta MERGE rewrites touched files; constrain the target (partition/ZORDER on the merge key) so it touches few files.
  2. Shrink and pre-dedup the source; size the cluster to avoid spill.
  3. Keep the match condition selective.
  4. Watch for whole-table rewrites.
  5. Verify files rewritten in the metrics.

Starter pattern

deltaTable.alias('t').merge(src.alias('s'), 't.key=s.key AND t.service_month IN (...)')
  .whenMatchedUpdate(...).whenNotMatchedInsert(...).execute()
Common mistake: An unconstrained MERGE that rewrites the whole fact every run because the match condition doesn't prune partitions.
Principal follow-up: How do you constrain a Delta MERGE so it only rewrites affected partitions?
070
Advanced payer spark scenario: schema evolution: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: schema evolution

Model answer

Sources add/rename/retype columns over time. Handle it with explicit, governed evolution: mergeSchema for additive changes, schema enforcement to reject silent breaks, and a contract/registry so renames and type changes are deliberate, versioned decisions — not auto-applied surprises.

  • Additive columns: allow via mergeSchema/schema evolution.
  • Type changes/renames: treat as contract changes, version and review.
  • Keep enforcement on to catch breaking drift early.
  • Quarantine records that violate the expected schema.

Healthcare angle: Payer feeds add fields (new code columns) regularly; controlled evolution avoids both pipeline breaks and silent data loss.

df.write.option("mergeSchema","true").format("delta").mode("append").save(path)

What strong answers cover

  1. Delta supports additive schema evolution (new columns) with mergeSchema; breaking changes (type/rename) need explicit handling.
  2. Capture surprises (rescued data) and quarantine breaking changes.
  3. Enforce a contract; don't blindly auto-merge.
  4. Version schemas.
  5. Alert on drift.

Starter pattern

df.write.option('mergeSchema','true').format('delta').mode('append').save(...)
# additive only; type/rename changes -> review/quarantine
Common mistake: Auto-merging any schema change, so a silent type change or column reorder corrupts values without error.
Principal follow-up: Which schema changes can you safely auto-evolve, and which must hard-stop?
071
Advanced payer spark scenario: late-arriving claims: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: late-arriving claims

Model answer

Claims for past service dates arrive continuously. Route them to the correct service_month partition (by service date, not load date), reprocess affected aggregates, and use watermarks in streaming to bound state while still admitting reasonable lateness.

  • Attribute by service/event date so late facts land in the right period.
  • Trigger targeted reprocessing of impacted rollups.
  • Streaming: set a watermark that tolerates the real lateness distribution.
  • Track lag to size backfill windows.

Healthcare angle: IBNR and runout analysis exist because claims lag service by weeks; pipelines must accept and re-aggregate late claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. Attribute late claims by service date (not load date) and reopen/restate the affected service_month partitions.
  2. Track lateness to bound reprocessing; mark recent periods provisional (IBNR).
  3. Idempotent overwrite/MERGE of affected partitions.
  4. Keep aggregates restatement-capable.
  5. Validate restated totals.

Starter pattern

# write to partition of service_month (event date)
# reopen + recompute aggregates for affected months (idempotent)
Common mistake: Attributing late claims to load date, so historical months never restate and recent months look complete when they aren't.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep monthly claim aggregates correct when claims arrive months late?
072
Advanced payer spark scenario: incremental load with watermarks: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: incremental load with watermarks

Model answer

Process only new data per cycle using a watermark — a high-water mark on a monotonic column (received_ts) for batch, or structured-streaming event-time watermarks to bound state and define lateness. Persist the watermark transactionally so reruns are correct.

  • Batch: store max(received_ts) and pull > watermark next run.
  • Streaming: withWatermark(eventTime, delay) bounds state and allows late events within delay.
  • Persist the watermark atomically with the committed output.
  • Handle clock/order issues — use event time, not processing time, for correctness.

Healthcare angle: An eligibility/claims stream watermarked at, say, 7 days tolerates normal lateness while keeping stateful aggregation bounded.

stream.withWatermark("event_ts","7 days").groupBy(window("event_ts","1 day"),"member_id")...

What strong answers cover

  1. Process only new/changed data using a watermark (event-time high-water mark), applied idempotently.
  2. Persist the watermark transactionally with the write.
  3. Reopen periods for late/retro data; reconcile incrementally + periodic full.
  4. Avoid full reloads.
  5. Validate against source.

Starter pattern

last = read_watermark()
new = src.filter(col('ingest_ts') > last)
# MERGE/overwrite affected partitions; persist new watermark atomically
Common mistake: Watermarking on processing time or advancing the watermark before the write commits, skipping or duplicating data.
Principal follow-up: Why must the watermark be event-time and persisted atomically with the write?
073
Advanced payer spark scenario: idempotent delta writes: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: idempotent Delta writes

Model answer

Make Delta writes safe to retry: use MERGE keyed on the natural key (not append), partition overwrite for full-partition rebuilds, and txnAppId/txnVersion (or a processed-batch registry) so a replayed micro-batch isn't written twice.

  • Keyed MERGE or partition overwrite instead of blind append.
  • Streaming: foreachBatch with txnAppId/txnVersion for exactly-once-ish writes.
  • Track processed batch/file ids to skip replays.
  • Test by re-running the same input and asserting unchanged state.

Healthcare angle: Re-running a failed claims micro-batch must not duplicate rows; idempotent Delta writes guarantee that.

def upsert(batch_df, batch_id):
    (delta.merge(batch_df, "t.k=s.k").whenMatchedUpdateAll()
          .whenNotMatchedInsertAll().execute())
stream.writeStream.foreachBatch(upsert).start()

What strong answers cover

  1. Make writes idempotent so reruns don't duplicate: MERGE on a business key, INSERT OVERWRITE a partition, or use txnVersion/txnAppId for streaming.
  2. Never blind-append in a rerunnable job.
  3. Test: run twice == run once.
  4. Track processed inputs.
  5. Reconcile after.

Starter pattern

# overwrite a partition idempotently
df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite')\
  .option('replaceWhere','service_month="2025-01"').save(path)
Common mistake: Appending in a job that can be retried, so an on-call rerun duplicates a month of claims.
Principal follow-up: What's your idempotency strategy for a Delta write, and how do you test it?
074
Advanced payer spark scenario: dedup in streaming: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: dedup in streaming

Model answer

Deduplicate streaming records with dropDuplicates on the business key plus a watermark, so Spark only retains recent keys for matching and bounds state. Without a watermark, the dedup state grows forever.

  • dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark/dropDuplicates(keys) + withWatermark to bound state.
  • Choose keys that define a true duplicate (not a legitimate version).
  • Late duplicates beyond the watermark may slip through — set it to real lateness.
  • For exactly-once, pair with idempotent sink writes.

Healthcare angle: A resent claims event within the watermark window is dropped as a duplicate; a corrected version (different payload) should be keyed to update, not drop.

stream.withWatermark("event_ts","1 day").dropDuplicates(["claim_id","line"])

What strong answers cover

  1. Streaming dedup uses dropDuplicates with a watermark to bound state (it can't remember forever).
  2. Dedup on the business key within the watermark window.
  3. Downstream sink should also be idempotent (at-least-once delivery).
  4. Choose the watermark to cover expected lateness.
  5. Validate no duplicates.

Starter pattern

stream.withWatermark('event_ts','24 hours')
      .dropDuplicates(['claim_id','line'])
Common mistake: dropDuplicates without a watermark, so dedup state grows unbounded and eventually OOMs the stream.
Principal follow-up: Why does streaming dedup need a watermark, and how do you set its window?
075
Advanced payer spark scenario: checkpoint corruption: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: checkpoint corruption

Model answer

A structured-streaming checkpoint stores offsets and state; corruption (or schema/code changes that invalidate it) breaks restart. Recover by understanding what's broken: minor code changes may be safe, but incompatible changes require a new checkpoint and a controlled reprocess from a known source offset.

  • Checkpoint = offsets + state; don't hand-edit it.
  • Incompatible query/schema changes invalidate checkpoints — plan a migration.
  • Recovery: start a new checkpoint and replay from a known-safe source position.
  • Idempotent sinks make reprocessing safe.

Healthcare angle: Restoring a claims stream after checkpoint loss requires replaying source files/offsets into an idempotent sink to avoid duplicates.

What strong answers cover

  1. A corrupted/incompatible streaming checkpoint blocks restart; recover by starting a fresh checkpoint and replaying from a safe source offset into an idempotent sink.
  2. Distinguish corruption from an incompatible code/schema change.
  3. Idempotent sink makes replay safe (no duplicates).
  4. Control schema/code changes to avoid breaking checkpoints.
  5. Reconcile after recovery.

Starter pattern

# new checkpointLocation; replay from a known-good source offset
# sink MERGE on key -> replay is idempotent
Common mistake: Pointing a stream at a fresh checkpoint that replays into an append sink, duplicating everything reprocessed.
Principal follow-up: Why is an idempotent sink the precondition for recovering from checkpoint loss?
076
Advanced payer spark scenario: stateful aggregation: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: stateful aggregation

Model answer

Stateful streaming aggregations (counts, running sums, sessionization) keep state keyed by group; bound it with watermarks/timeouts or it grows without limit. Use mapGroupsWithState/flatMapGroupsWithState for custom state with explicit timeouts.

  • Always bound state with a watermark or state timeout.
  • Choose update vs append output mode deliberately.
  • Custom state via (flat)mapGroupsWithState for sessionization/timeouts.
  • Monitor state store size; unbounded keys are the failure mode.

Healthcare angle: Running member-level utilization counts in a stream must expire stale member state, or the state store grows unbounded.

What strong answers cover

  1. Stateful streaming aggregations (running counts/sums, sessionization) keep state across micro-batches, bounded by a watermark.
  2. Without a watermark, state grows unbounded (OOM).
  3. Use mapGroupsWithState/flatMapGroupsWithState for custom state.
  4. Tune state store and watermark to expected lateness.
  5. Monitor state size.

Starter pattern

stream.withWatermark('event_ts','1 hour')
      .groupBy(window('event_ts','10 minutes'),'member_id').sum('paid')
Common mistake: Stateful aggregation without a watermark, so state never expires and the stream eventually OOMs.
Principal follow-up: How does a watermark bound streaming aggregation state, and what happens without one?
077
Advanced payer spark scenario: stream-stream join limits: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: stream-stream join limits

Model answer

Stream-stream joins require watermarks on both sides and a time-bound join condition so Spark can expire buffered state. Without bounded state, the join buffers indefinitely. Outer joins additionally need watermarks to know when an unmatched row can be emitted.

  • Both streams need watermarks plus a time-range join condition.
  • State is buffered until events fall outside the watermark+range — keep it tight.
  • Outer joins emit unmatched rows only after the watermark passes.
  • If joins get complex, consider a stream-static join instead.

Healthcare angle: Joining a claims stream to an eligibility stream needs bounded event-time windows so membership state doesn't accumulate forever.

What strong answers cover

  1. Stream-stream joins buffer both sides as state, bounded by watermarks and time constraints; without them, state is unbounded.
  2. Specify watermarks on both streams and a time-range join condition.
  3. Outer joins need watermarks to emit unmatched rows.
  4. Know the state cost.
  5. Bound the join window.

Starter pattern

claimsStream.withWatermark('ts','2 hours').join(
  authStream.withWatermark('ts','2 hours'),
  expr('claim_id=auth_id AND ts BETWEEN auth_ts AND auth_ts + interval 2 hours'))
Common mistake: A stream-stream join without watermarks/time bounds, buffering unbounded state until it OOMs.
Principal follow-up: What two things must a stream-stream join specify to keep state bounded?
078
Advanced payer spark scenario: broadcast timeout: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: broadcast timeout

Model answer

A broadcast join fails with a timeout when the 'small' side is too large to collect/broadcast in time or the driver is under-resourced. Fix by lowering the broadcast threshold (force shuffle join), genuinely shrinking the small side, or sizing the driver and timeout appropriately.

  • Cause: broadcast side too big or driver memory/time insufficient.
  • Lower autoBroadcastJoinThreshold or set -1 to disable for that query.
  • Filter/aggregate the small side so it really is small.
  • Increase broadcastTimeout/driver memory only if broadcast is genuinely right.

Healthcare angle: A 'provider dimension' that has grown with history may exceed broadcast size at month-end; filter to current rows or fall back to a shuffle join.

spark.conf.set("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold", 50*1024*1024)

What strong answers cover

  1. A broadcast times out when the broadcast side is too large/slow to collect to the driver and ship out.
  2. Fix by not broadcasting an oversized side, raising the timeout only if genuinely needed, or letting AQE decide.
  3. Check the side's actual size.
  4. Often the real fix is a shuffle join for that size.
  5. Verify the plan.

Starter pattern

spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold = 10m  # raise/lower deliberately
# or stop broadcasting an oversized dimension
Common mistake: Raising the broadcast timeout to force-broadcast a too-large dimension, masking that it should be a shuffle join.
Principal follow-up: When is a broadcast timeout telling you to use a shuffle join instead of raising the timeout?
079
Advanced payer spark scenario: driver oom: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: driver OOM

Model answer

Driver OOM usually comes from pulling data to the driver — collect(), toPandas(), huge broadcast, or too many tasks/partitions metadata. Fix by removing driver-side collection, bounding broadcast size, and avoiding actions that materialize big results locally.

  • Top cause: collect()/toPandas() on large results — don't.
  • Oversized broadcast or massive partition counts strain the driver.
  • Write results to storage instead of returning to the driver.
  • Right-size driver memory only after removing collection anti-patterns.

Healthcare angle: A 'quick' collect() of a month of claims to inspect locally will OOM the driver — sample or write out instead.

# bad: rows = claims.collect()
claims.write.format("delta").save(out)   # keep data distributed

What strong answers cover

  1. Driver OOM usually comes from collect()/toPandas() on big data, an oversized broadcast, or too many partitions' metadata.
  2. Remove driver-side collection (write to storage instead), bound broadcast size.
  3. Don't pull large results to the driver.
  4. Distinguish from executor OOM.
  5. Right-size driver only if truly needed.

Starter pattern

# bad: big_df.collect() / .toPandas()
# good: big_df.write...   (let executors do the work)
Common mistake: Calling collect()/toPandas() on a month of claims to 'peek', pulling everything to the driver and OOMing.
Principal follow-up: What are the usual driver-OOM causes, and why is more executor memory the wrong fix?
080
Advanced payer spark scenario: executor oom: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Expertadvanced payer Spark scenario: executor OOM

Model answer

Executor OOM comes from too much data per task — skew, huge partitions, wide rows, large in-memory state, or excessive caching. Fix by increasing parallelism (more, smaller partitions), resolving skew, reducing cached/broadcast data, and tuning memory fractions.

  • Common causes: skew, oversized partitions, over-caching, large UDF objects.
  • Increase shuffle partitions / repartition to shrink per-task data.
  • Fix skew (salting/AQE) so one task isn't overloaded.
  • Reduce cache footprint; pick DISK_ONLY for big reused sets.

Healthcare angle: Month-end skew piling rows onto one executor is a frequent claims-pipeline OOM; salting or AQE skew-join resolves it.

spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", 1000)

What strong answers cover

  1. Executor OOM comes from skew, oversized partitions, over-caching, or large per-task objects.
  2. Increase parallelism (more partitions), fix skew (salt/AQE), reduce cache footprint.
  3. Check spill before raising memory.
  4. Find the offending stage in the UI.
  5. Validate even tasks.

Starter pattern

# more shuffle partitions; AQE/salt for skew; unpersist unused caches
spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 400
Common mistake: Throwing memory at an executor OOM that's actually skew — the hot task still exceeds memory at any size.
Principal follow-up: How do you tell skew-driven executor OOM from genuinely undersized executors?
081
Advanced payer spark scenario: collect anti-pattern: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: collect anti-pattern

Model answer

collect() brings the entire distributed dataset to the driver, defeating Spark and risking OOM. Use it only for tiny, known-small results. For inspection use limit().show()/take(); for output, write to storage; for driver-side logic, restructure to stay distributed.

  • collect() = whole dataset on the driver — avoid on big data.
  • Inspect with take(n)/show(); persist with write.
  • Aggregate in Spark, then collect only small summaries.
  • Driver-side loops over collected big data are a red flag.

Healthcare angle: Collecting all claims to drive a Python loop is the wrong layer; express the logic as Spark transformations.

summary = claims.groupBy("service_month").count()
rows = summary.collect()   # small, safe

What strong answers cover

  1. collect() pulls the entire dataset to the driver — fine for tiny results, catastrophic for large data.
  2. Use it only for small, known-bounded results; otherwise write to storage or aggregate first.
  3. take(n)/limit for sampling.
  4. Driver memory is finite.
  5. Prefer distributed actions.

Starter pattern

# bad: results = big_df.collect()
# good: big_df.write.parquet(...)  or aggregate then collect a small result
Common mistake: collect()-ing a large claims DataFrame, OOMing the driver, when the work should stay distributed.
Principal follow-up: When is collect() acceptable, and what do you use instead for large results?
082
Advanced payer spark scenario: topandas trap: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: toPandas trap

Model answer

toPandas() collects everything to the driver and into pandas memory — fine for small aggregates, fatal for large frames. Enable Arrow for efficiency on the sizes that are appropriate, and never toPandas() a raw fact table.

  • toPandas materializes the full frame on the driver — size-bound it.
  • Enable spark.sql.execution.arrow.pyspark.enabled for faster small conversions.
  • Use it for plotting/serving small summaries, not raw facts.
  • For ML on big data, use Spark ML or sample first.

Healthcare angle: toPandas() on a year of claims OOMs; aggregate to a small mart first, then convert for charting.

What strong answers cover

  1. toPandas() materializes the whole DataFrame on the driver as pandas — same OOM risk as collect, plus conversion overhead.
  2. Only for small results; use Arrow to speed small conversions.
  3. For big-data pandas-style work, use pandas API on Spark or pandas UDFs.
  4. Never toPandas() a large fact.
  5. Bound the size first.

Starter pattern

# bad: claims.toPandas()  (whole fact on driver)
# small only; or use pandas-on-Spark / pandas_udf for scale
Common mistake: toPandas() on a large claims DataFrame for 'convenience', OOMing the driver.
Principal follow-up: How would you do pandas-style logic at scale without toPandas() on the whole fact?
083
Advanced payer spark scenario: coalesce(1) anti-pattern: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: coalesce(1) anti-pattern

Model answer

coalesce(1) forces all data through a single task/partition to produce one output file, destroying parallelism and risking OOM on large data. Only use it for genuinely tiny outputs; otherwise write multiple files and compact, or use a single-file export tool downstream.

  • coalesce(1) serializes the final stage onto one core.
  • Acceptable only for small result files.
  • For large data, write partitioned/multi-file then OPTIMIZE/compact.
  • If a single file is a hard requirement, do it as a separate small post-step.

Healthcare angle: Writing a full claims extract as one file via coalesce(1) will choke; write many files and let the consumer or a compaction step handle it.

What strong answers cover

  1. coalesce(1) forces all data through a single task to write one file — destroying parallelism and risking OOM at scale.
  2. Acceptable only for tiny outputs; otherwise write multiple files and compact (OPTIMIZE) if needed.
  3. Don't collapse a large write to one partition.
  4. Use repartition for even distribution, not 1.
  5. Compact post-write instead.

Starter pattern

# bad: big_df.coalesce(1).write...  (single task, no parallelism)
# good: write partitioned; OPTIMIZE/compact afterward
Common mistake: coalesce(1) on a large write to get a single file, serializing the whole job through one task and OOMing.
Principal follow-up: Why is coalesce(1) dangerous at scale, and how do you produce fewer files safely?
084
Advanced payer spark scenario: partition pruning failure: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: partition pruning failure

Model answer

Pruning fails when the query doesn't filter the partition column directly (functions/casts on it, or the filter is only derivable at runtime). Restore static pruning with sargable predicates on the partition column; for join-derived filters, ensure dynamic partition pruning is enabled.

  • Filter the raw partition column with literal ranges — no functions/casts.
  • Join-derived filters need dynamic partition pruning (DPP) enabled.
  • Verify partitions read dropped (Spark UI / EXPLAIN).
  • Mismatched types silently disable pruning.

Healthcare angle: Filtering claims on a derived period string instead of the service_month partition reads the whole table.

spark.conf.set("spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled","true")

What strong answers cover

  1. Pruning skips partitions via metadata; it fails with a function/cast on the partition column, type mismatch, or a non-pushable filter.
  2. Symptom: all partitions read despite a selective filter.
  3. Use literal sargable ranges on the raw partition column; enable dynamic partition pruning.
  4. Confirm partitions read dropped.
  5. Standardize sargable filters.

Starter pattern

# good: filter(col('service_month').between('2025-01-01','2025-01-31'))
spark.sql.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled=true
Common mistake: Filtering on a derived expression of the partition column, scanning the whole fact instead of one month.
Principal follow-up: How does dynamic partition pruning help when the filter comes from a joined dimension?
085
Advanced payer spark scenario: predicate pushdown on parquet: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: predicate pushdown on Parquet

Model answer

Parquet stores row-group min/max stats and supports column projection, so Spark can skip row groups and read only needed columns when predicates are pushed down. Pushdown breaks with functions on columns, type mismatches, or complex expressions; keep filters simple and typed.

  • Parquet/Delta skip row groups via min/max stats when predicates push down.
  • Project only needed columns (columnar read benefit).
  • Functions/casts on filtered columns disable pushdown.
  • Sorted/clustered data makes skipping far more effective.

Healthcare angle: Selecting only claim_id, paid, service_month and filtering service_month directly lets Parquet skip most of the file.

df.select("claim_id","paid","service_month").filter("service_month=202406")

What strong answers cover

  1. Parquet pushdown uses column min/max and row-group stats to skip data; it needs sargable predicates and matching types.
  2. Column pruning (select only needed columns) also cuts I/O massively.
  3. Functions/casts on the filter column block pushdown.
  4. Verify scan size dropped.
  5. Order columns for better stats where possible.

Starter pattern

claims.select('claim_id','paid','service_dt')   # column pruning
      .filter(col('service_dt') >= '2025-01-01') # pushed down
Common mistake: Selecting all columns and wrapping filters in functions, so Parquet reads far more than necessary.
Principal follow-up: What enables (and blocks) predicate and projection pushdown on Parquet?
086
Advanced payer spark scenario: bucketing vs partitioning: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: bucketing vs partitioning

Model answer

Partitioning splits data into directories by a column (good for pruning, risks small files at high cardinality); bucketing hashes a column into a fixed number of buckets within files (good for avoiding shuffles on repeated joins of the same key). Use partitioning for date-range pruning, bucketing for stable high-cardinality join keys.

  • Partition by low/moderate-cardinality filter columns (service_month).
  • Bucket by high-cardinality join keys to enable shuffle-free joins.
  • Bucketing requires matching bucket specs on both join sides.
  • On Databricks, liquid clustering often supersedes manual bucketing.

Healthcare angle: Partition claims by service_month for pruning; bucketing by member_id can help repeated member-keyed joins, though clustering is usually preferred now.

What strong answers cover

  1. Partitioning splits files by a low-cardinality column for pruning; bucketing hashes rows into a fixed number of buckets for shuffle-free joins/aggregations on the bucket key.
  2. Bucketing both tables on the join key avoids the shuffle.
  3. Partition for pruning, bucket for repeated joins.
  4. Bucketing has maintenance constraints.
  5. Choose by access pattern.

Starter pattern

df.write.bucketBy(256,'member_id').sortBy('member_id').saveAsTable('claims_bucketed')
# bucketed-on-member joins avoid shuffle
Common mistake: Bucketing on a column you never join on, or expecting bucketing to give pruning (it doesn't).
Principal follow-up: When does bucketing on member_id pay off versus partitioning, and what are its constraints?
087
Advanced payer spark scenario: join hints tradeoff: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: join hints tradeoff

Model answer

Hints (BROADCAST, MERGE, SHUFFLE_HASH) override the optimizer. They help when you know something stats don't (e.g. a side is small after a runtime filter), but they ossify plans and can hurt when data changes. Prefer fixing stats/AQE; hint as a documented, revisited last resort.

  • BROADCAST forces broadcasting a side; useful when AQE/stats underestimate.
  • Hints freeze decisions that should adapt to data — review them.
  • AQE often makes manual hints unnecessary.
  • Document why a hint exists so it's not cargo-culted.

Healthcare angle: A broadcast hint on a known-small provider dimension is reasonable; hard-coding it as the dimension grows is a future regression.

claims.join(provider.hint("broadcast"), "provider_npi")

What strong answers cover

  1. Join hints (BROADCAST/MERGE/SHUFFLE_HASH) override the optimizer; useful when stats are wrong, risky when they go stale.
  2. Prefer fixing stats / letting AQE decide; hint only when you know better and the data is stable.
  3. A wrong hint can OOM (forced broadcast) or be slow.
  4. Document why a hint exists.
  5. Revisit hints as data grows.

Starter pattern

claims.join(broadcast(dim),'npi')        # hint
# prefer AQE/stats; hint only with justification
Common mistake: Hard-coding a broadcast hint that later OOMs when the 'small' dimension grows past memory.
Principal follow-up: When is a join hint justified versus fixing stats or trusting AQE?
088
Advanced payer spark scenario: skew only at month-end: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: skew only at month-end

Model answer

Volume spikes at month-end exaggerate an existing skew (a hot key now has far more rows), flipping a fine plan into a straggler. Diagnose the hot key, then apply AQE skew handling, salting, or broadcast — and load-test at month-end volumes so it doesn't surprise you.

  • Month-end volume amplifies a pre-existing hot-key skew.
  • Enable AQE skewJoin; salt or isolate the hot key.
  • Right-size partitions for peak, not average, volume.
  • Add a month-end load test and an alert on task-time skew.

Healthcare angle: Claims surge at month-end concentrate on default/high-volume keys; the fix is the same skew toolkit, tested at peak.

What strong answers cover

  1. Month-end volume amplifies hot-key skew (default/UNKNOWN keys) into stragglers/spill.
  2. Fix with AQE skewJoin, salting, or isolating the hot key; size for peak.
  3. Add a month-end load test and a task-skew alert.
  4. More cores don't fix one hot partition.
  5. Validate even tasks at peak.

Starter pattern

spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled=true   # or salt the hot key
# size for month-end peak; load-test at peak
Common mistake: Tuning for average volume so the job breaks every month-end, then adding cores that don't fix the straggler.
Principal follow-up: Why is skew a peak-only problem, and what's the durable fix beyond this month?
089
Advanced payer spark scenario: data quality expectations: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: data quality expectations

Model answer

Encode DQ as explicit expectations (not-null keys, valid code sets, ranges, uniqueness on grain, referential integrity) that run inline and either fail the job, quarantine bad rows, or warn — with metrics tracked over time. DLT expectations or a rules engine make this declarative.

  • Assert grain uniqueness, key not-null, valid code sets, value ranges, RI.
  • Choose action per rule: fail (critical), quarantine (recoverable), warn (monitor).
  • Track pass rates as metrics to catch drift, not just hard failures.
  • Quarantine with reason + source line for stewardship.

Healthcare angle: Claims DQ: valid CARC/CPT codes, member_id present, paid within sane bounds, one row per claim-line grain.

# DLT example
@dlt.expect_or_drop("valid_paid","paid_amt >= 0")
@dlt.expect("known_carc","carc IS NOT NULL")

What strong answers cover

  1. Encode DQ checks (not-null keys, valid code sets, ranges, uniqueness on grain) inline with per-rule actions (fail/quarantine/warn).
  2. Track pass rates as metrics to catch drift.
  3. Quarantine bad rows rather than failing whole loads.
  4. Use DLT expectations or explicit assertions.
  5. Steward quarantined data.

Starter pattern

valid = claims.filter(col('member_id').isNotNull() & col('carc').isin(VALID))
bad   = claims.subtract(valid)   # quarantine + alert
