Common mistake: No DQ gates, so an upstream change silently corrupts claims with no error raised.
Principal follow-up: What DQ checks would you assert on a claims feed, and what action per rule?
090
Advanced payer spark scenario: adaptive coalescing partitions: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: adaptive coalescing partitions

Model answer

AQE coalesces many small post-shuffle partitions into fewer, appropriately-sized ones at runtime based on actual data size, fixing the over-partitioning that static spark.sql.shuffle.partitions causes. It improves task efficiency without manual tuning per job.

  • AQE merges tiny shuffle partitions into right-sized ones at runtime.
  • Removes the need to hand-tune shuffle.partitions per dataset.
  • Controlled by adaptive.coalescePartitions settings.
  • Works alongside skew-join handling and dynamic join switching.

Healthcare angle: Claims jobs whose filtered volume varies by month benefit from AQE coalescing rather than a fixed shuffle partition count.

spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled","true")

What strong answers cover

  1. AQE coalesces many small shuffle partitions into fewer right-sized ones at runtime, avoiding tiny tasks and small files.
  2. Replaces manually tuning spark.sql.shuffle.partitions.
  3. Enabled with AQE.
  4. Improves post-shuffle efficiency.
  5. Verify partition counts in the UI.

Starter pattern

spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true
spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled=true
Common mistake: Manually fixing shuffle.partitions to a large number and getting many tiny tasks, instead of letting AQE coalesce.
Principal follow-up: How does AQE partition coalescing replace manual shuffle-partition tuning?
091
Advanced payer spark scenario: dynamic partition pruning: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: dynamic partition pruning

Model answer

DPP prunes partitions of a large fact at runtime using the values produced by a join to a filtered dimension — so a filter on the dimension translates into reading only matching fact partitions. It requires the partition column to participate in the join and DPP to be enabled.

  • Filter on a dimension prunes the fact's partitions via the join at runtime.
  • Needs the fact partitioned on the join/date key and DPP enabled.
  • Big win for star-schema queries (dim filter → fact pruning).
  • Verify in the plan that the dynamic pruning subquery appears.

Healthcare angle: Filtering a date dimension to one month can prune the claims fact to that month's partitions automatically via DPP.

spark.conf.set("spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled","true")

What strong answers cover

  1. DPP prunes fact partitions at runtime using the filtered dimension's keys in a join — pruning even when the filter is on the dimension, not the fact.
  2. Huge win for star-schema claims queries.
  3. Enabled with AQE/DPP settings.
  4. Verify partitions pruned in the plan.
  5. Needs the join on the partition key.

Starter pattern

spark.sql.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled=true
# WHERE date_dim.month='2025-01' prunes claims partitions via the join
Common mistake: Expecting pruning when the filter is on the dimension but DPP is disabled, so the whole fact is scanned.
Principal follow-up: How does DPP prune the fact when the filter is only on a joined dimension?
092
Advanced payer spark scenario: delta optimize: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: Delta OPTIMIZE

Model answer

OPTIMIZE compacts small files into larger ones and (with ZORDER) clusters data for better skipping, improving read and MERGE performance. Schedule it on hot tables/partitions; it's a maintenance op separate from writes.

  • Compacts small files → fewer, larger, faster to read.
  • ZORDER clause clusters by frequently-filtered columns.
  • Run on recently-written partitions, not the whole table each time.
  • Pair with VACUUM (carefully) to remove obsolete files.

Healthcare angle: Auto Loader-fed claims tables accumulate small files; nightly OPTIMIZE on recent service_months keeps queries fast.

OPTIMIZE claims WHERE service_month >= 202401 ZORDER BY (member_id);

What strong answers cover

  1. OPTIMIZE compacts small files into right-sized ones; with ZORDER it also co-locates by high-cardinality columns for data-skipping.
  2. Run it on tables with small-file buildup (streaming sinks).
  3. Costs compute; schedule it.
  4. Improves read/query performance.
  5. Pair with VACUUM (mind retention).

Starter pattern

OPTIMIZE claims WHERE service_month='2025-01' ZORDER BY (member_id)
Common mistake: Never compacting a streaming Delta sink, so small files accumulate and queries slow over time.
Principal follow-up: When and how would you schedule OPTIMIZE/ZORDER on a streaming claims table?
093
Advanced payer spark scenario: vacuum retention risk: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: VACUUM retention risk

Model answer

VACUUM permanently deletes data files no longer referenced by the Delta log older than the retention threshold — which also removes the ability to time-travel before that point and can break readers/streams relying on old files. Never lower retention below your time-travel/recovery needs.

  • VACUUM deletes old files → loses time travel beyond retention.
  • Default retention (e.g. 7 days) protects against this; don't force shorter without reason.
  • Active streams/long-running readers can break if files vanish.
  • Balance storage cost vs recovery/compliance windows.

Healthcare angle: VACUUMing a claims table too aggressively can destroy the ability to recover from a bad load via time travel — a real production incident.

VACUUM claims RETAIN 168 HOURS;   -- keep recovery window

What strong answers cover

  1. VACUUM removes old files past a retention window; setting it too low breaks time travel and can break active readers/streams.
  2. Keep retention >= max(recovery need, streaming need); never RETAIN 0 in prod.
  3. It reclaims storage but destroys recoverability if mis-set.
  4. Guard VACUUM changes.
  5. Understand the tradeoff.

Starter pattern

VACUUM claims RETAIN 168 HOURS  -- >= recovery/streaming need
# never RETAIN 0 HOURS in production
Common mistake: Running VACUUM with low/zero retention to save storage, destroying time-travel recovery and breaking streams.
Principal follow-up: What retention must VACUUM respect to avoid breaking time travel and streaming reads?
094
Advanced payer spark scenario: change data feed: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: Change Data Feed

Model answer

Delta Change Data Feed (CDF) records row-level changes (insert/update/delete with _change_type) so downstream consumers can read just the deltas instead of recomputing. Enable it per table and read changes by version/timestamp for incremental propagation.

  • CDF emits row-level change records with _change_type and commit version.
  • Enable delta.enableChangeDataFeed=true on the table.
  • Downstream reads only changed rows → cheap incremental updates.
  • Great for feeding gold marts or external systems incrementally.

Healthcare angle: Propagating only changed claims to a downstream actuarial mart via CDF avoids full reloads each cycle.

spark.read.format("delta").option("readChangeFeed","true") \
     .option("startingVersion", v).table("claims")

What strong answers cover

  1. Delta Change Data Feed (CDF) exposes row-level changes (insert/update/delete) so downstream can consume only what changed.
  2. Enable it; read change types to drive incremental downstream MERGEs.
  3. Avoids full-table diffs.
  4. Powers efficient CDC pipelines.
  5. Mind retention of change data.

Starter pattern

spark.read.format('delta').option('readChangeFeed','true')
  .option('startingVersion', v).table('claims')
Common mistake: Computing downstream deltas by diffing full table snapshots when CDF would stream the exact changes.
Principal follow-up: How does Delta CDF make a downstream incremental pipeline cheaper than snapshot diffing?
095
Advanced payer spark scenario: cdc upsert pattern: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: CDC upsert pattern

Model answer

Apply source CDC (insert/update/delete events) to a Delta target via MERGE keyed on the natural key, ordered by the change sequence so the latest wins, mapping deletes to soft-deletes/end-dating, and made idempotent against replayed events.

  • MERGE on natural key; pick latest by LSN/commit time within the batch.
  • Map delete events to soft-delete/end-date in dimensions.
  • Dedup multiple events per key per batch before merging.
  • Idempotent so replays don't corrupt state.

Healthcare angle: CDC from a membership source system upserts the member dimension while preserving SCD2 history and honoring deletes.

latest = changes.withColumn("rn", row_number().over(
    Window.partitionBy("nk").orderBy(col("lsn").desc()))).filter("rn=1")
# then MERGE latest into target

What strong answers cover

  1. Apply CDC by MERGEing change rows on the natural key, ordered by change sequence, mapping inserts/updates/deletes (deletes to soft-deletes).
  2. Dedup to the latest change per key before the MERGE.
  3. Idempotent and ordered.
  4. Maintain SCD2 where history matters.
  5. Reconcile to source.

Starter pattern

latest = changes.withColumn('rn', row_number().over(
  Window.partitionBy('key').orderBy(col('seq').desc()))).filter('rn=1')
deltaT.merge(latest,'t.key=s.key').whenMatched...().whenNotMatched...().execute()
Common mistake: Applying CDC changes out of order or without deduping to latest-per-key, so an older change overwrites a newer one.
Principal follow-up: Why must you dedup to latest-per-key and order changes before the upsert MERGE?
096
Advanced payer spark scenario: scd2 in pyspark: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: SCD2 in PySpark

Model answer

Implement SCD2 by detecting changed rows via a tracked-column hash, then a two-step Delta MERGE: close the current version (set valid_to/is_current=false) for changed keys, and insert the new version. Make it idempotent and handle late-arriving effective dates.

  • Hash tracked columns to detect real changes.
  • Step 1: MERGE to expire current rows for changed keys.
  • Step 2: insert new current versions (changed + brand-new keys).
  • Idempotent + bitemporal handling for retro corrections.

Healthcare angle: Provider/member dimensions need SCD2 so claims join to the version effective at service date; the hash avoids churn on cosmetic changes.

# expire then insert (Delta MERGE), keyed on natural key + is_current
delta.alias("t").merge(changed.alias("s"),"t.nk=s.nk AND t.is_current=true") \
     .whenMatchedUpdate(set={"valid_to":"s.eff-1","is_current":"false"}).execute()

What strong answers cover

  1. SCD2 in PySpark: detect changes via a hash, close current rows (set valid_to/is_current=false) and insert new versions, idempotently.
  2. Use a two-part MERGE (or MERGE + insert) keyed on the natural key.
  3. Guarantee one current row per key.
  4. Handle late/out-of-order updates.
  5. Validate current-row invariant.

Starter pattern

deltaT.alias('t').merge(updates.alias('s'), 't.key=s.key AND t.is_current')
  .whenMatched('t.hash<>s.hash').updateExpr({'valid_to':'s.eff','is_current':'false'})
  .execute()  # then insert new current rows
Common mistake: A non-idempotent close+insert that leaves two current rows (or zero) on a rerun, fanning out joins.
Principal follow-up: How do you guarantee exactly one current row per key in a PySpark SCD2 merge, even on reruns?
097
Advanced payer spark scenario: unit testing spark jobs: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: unit testing Spark jobs

Model answer

Test transformation functions that take and return DataFrames using a local SparkSession and small crafted inputs, asserting exact output (schema + rows). Keep transforms pure and decoupled from I/O so tests are fast and deterministic; reserve integration tests for read/write.

  • Structure code as pure df→df transforms, separate from I/O.
  • Use a local SparkSession fixture with tiny inputs and known outputs.
  • Assert schema and row content, including edge cases (nulls/dupes/ties).
  • Mock or stub source/sink in integration tests.

Healthcare angle: Dedup/SCD2/skew-handling logic should be unit-tested with crafted duplicate/version rows to lock correctness before scale.

def test_dedup(spark):
    out = dedup(spark.createDataFrame([...]))
    assert out.collect() == expected

What strong answers cover

  1. Test transformation logic on small DataFrames with known input/expected output, including edge cases (nulls, dupes, late data).
  2. Factor transforms into pure functions (DataFrame in -> DataFrame out) testable with a local SparkSession.
  3. Use chispa/assertDataFrameEqual for comparison.
  4. Keep tests fast; avoid real clusters/IO.
  5. Run in CI.

Starter pattern

def test_dedup():
    out = dedup(spark.createDataFrame(rows))
    assertDataFrameEqual(out, expected)
Common mistake: Only testing on the cluster against real data, so logic bugs and edge cases aren't caught before deploy.
Principal follow-up: How do you structure Spark code so transformations are unit-testable without a cluster?
098
Advanced payer spark scenario: logging in spark: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: logging in Spark

Model answer

Log at the driver for orchestration/metrics and avoid heavy per-row executor logging (it's distributed and noisy). Emit structured run-level metrics (counts, durations, control totals), use the Spark UI/event logs for stage detail, and never log PHI.

  • Driver logs for run lifecycle and metrics; avoid per-row executor logs.
  • Emit structured control totals/counts for observability.
  • Use Spark UI/event logs + Ganglia/metrics sinks for stage detail.
  • Never log member/claim PHI — log ids/counts.

Healthcare angle: Logging full claim records from executors both floods logs and risks PHI exposure; log aggregate counts and surrogate ids.

What strong answers cover

  1. Log structured, PHI-safe context (job, stage, batch_id, counts) for triage; remember executor logs are distributed.
  2. Don't log per-row inside executors (volume) or any PHI.
  3. Use metrics/accumulators for counts.
  4. Centralize logs for observability.
  5. Log decisions, not data.

Starter pattern

logger.info('stage_done', extra={'job':job_id,'rows':df.count(),'batch':batch_id})
# no PHI; aggregate counts, not per-row logs
Common mistake: Logging per-row inside executors (huge volume) or logging PHI-bearing records — noise plus a compliance breach.
Principal follow-up: What should and shouldn't you log in a distributed Spark job, and why?
099
Advanced payer spark scenario: reprocessing six months: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: reprocessing six months

Model answer

Reprocess a large window safely by partitioning the work (per service_month), using idempotent partition overwrites or keyed MERGE, running in bounded batches to control cost, and reconciling each partition before publishing. Avoid one giant job; checkpoint progress so failures resume.

  • Chunk by service_month; idempotent overwrite/MERGE per chunk.
  • Bound concurrency/cost; don't reprocess everything at once.
  • Reconcile counts/dollars per partition before flipping consumers.
  • Resumable: track completed partitions.

Healthcare angle: A six-month claims restatement after a logic fix runs month-by-month with per-month reconciliation, not as a single risky rerun.

What strong answers cover

  1. Reprocess per service_month, idempotently (replaceWhere/MERGE), in bounded batches, reconciling each before publishing, resumably.
  2. Isolate from live processing; communicate restatements.
  3. Never one giant rerun.
  4. Prioritize needed periods.
  5. Validate per month.

Starter pattern

for m in months:
  out = recompute(m)
  out.write.option('replaceWhere',f"service_month='{m}'").save(path)  # idempotent
  reconcile(m)
Common mistake: One massive six-month rerun that fails midway with no resumability and no per-month validation.
Principal follow-up: Why reprocess month-by-month with per-unit reconciliation instead of one large rerun?
100
Advanced payer spark scenario: cost tuning clusters: diagnose the Spark behavior, write the likely fix, and explain how you would verify it in Spark UI.
Principaladvanced payer Spark scenario: cost tuning clusters

Model answer

Cut Spark/Databricks cost by right-sizing clusters to the workload, using job clusters (auto-terminating) over always-on, autoscaling within sensible bounds, spot/preemptible for fault-tolerant batch, Photon for SQL-heavy work, and eliminating waste (small files, skew, over-caching, unnecessary recomputation).

  • Job clusters that auto-terminate; avoid idle all-purpose clusters.
  • Autoscale with min/max bounds; spot for retry-safe batch (not for drivers).
  • Photon for SQL/Delta workloads; fix small files and skew that waste compute.
  • Attribute cost by job/tag to find the expensive offenders.

Healthcare angle: A nightly claims pipeline on an auto-terminating job cluster with spot workers and Photon is far cheaper than an always-on all-purpose cluster.

What strong answers cover

  1. Cut cost via right-sized/auto-terminating clusters, instance pools, spot for fault-tolerant batch, incremental over full reloads, and fixing skew/small files/over-caching.
  2. Separate workloads; attribute cost.
  3. Biggest wins are usually algorithmic (incremental, skew), not just smaller clusters.
  4. Use autoscaling within limits.
  5. Measure before/after.

Starter pattern

# auto-terminate + pools + spot for batch; incremental loads
# fix skew/small files; right-size; attribute cost by job
Common mistake: Only shrinking cluster size while leaving full reloads, skew, and small-file scans that drive the real cost.
Principal follow-up: Where do the biggest Spark cost savings usually come from beyond cluster sizing?

Databricks Mastery

Lakehouse engineering, DLT, Auto Loader, Unity Catalog, workflows, governance and cost control.

75 questions
001
How would you implement Bronze/Silver/Gold claims architecture in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumBronze/Silver/Gold claims architecture

Model answer

Medallion layers progressively refine data: Bronze is raw, append-only ingested claims (audit/replay source); Silver is cleaned, deduplicated, conformed, validated claims at a defined grain; Gold is business-ready aggregates/marts (PMPM, denial, AR). Each layer has clear contracts and lets you reprocess downstream without re-pulling source.

  • Bronze: immutable raw landing, full fidelity, replayable.
  • Silver: dedup, type/standardize, validate, conform keys to dimensions.
  • Gold: curated marts/aggregates for actuaries, BI, and analytics.
  • Reprocess any layer from the one above without re-ingesting source.

Healthcare angle: Raw 837/835 land in Bronze; Silver produces clean claim-line facts; Gold serves PMPM and denial marts to actuarial/finance.

What strong answers cover

  1. Bronze = raw, replayable landing; Silver = cleaned/conformed claim-line facts; Gold = business marts (PMPM/denials).
  2. Each layer is a contract with quality gates; reprocess downstream without re-ingesting source.
  3. Bronze keeps full fidelity for audit/replay.
  4. Validate at each boundary (DQ, reconciliation).
  5. Map 837/835 to the layers explicitly.

Starter pattern

raw 837/835 -> bronze (Delta, append, replayable)
  -> silver (parsed, deduped, conformed claim-line)
  -> gold (PMPM, denial, AR marts)
Common mistake: Skipping the raw bronze layer, so a downstream bug means re-pulling from source instead of replaying bronze.
Principal follow-up: Why keep an immutable bronze layer, and what quality gates sit at each medallion boundary?
002
How would you implement Auto Loader for inbound 837 files in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumAuto Loader for inbound 837 files

Model answer

Auto Loader (cloudFiles) incrementally ingests new files from cloud storage with schema inference/evolution and exactly-once tracking via a checkpoint, scaling to high file counts. Use directory listing or file-notification mode; it remembers processed files so reruns don't reload.

  • Incremental file ingestion with built-in processed-file tracking.
  • Schema inference + evolution with a rescued-data column for surprises.
  • File-notification mode scales better than listing for huge volumes.
  • Checkpoint gives exactly-once file processing.

Healthcare angle: Inbound 837 EDI files arriving on SFTP→ADLS are picked up incrementally by Auto Loader without manual file bookkeeping.

spark.readStream.format("cloudFiles").option("cloudFiles.format","text")
  .option("cloudFiles.schemaLocation",chk).load(src)

What strong answers cover

  1. Auto Loader incrementally ingests new files from cloud storage with schema inference/evolution and exactly-once file tracking.
  2. Use it for continuously-arriving 837/835 files instead of scheduled directory scans.
  3. It's at-least-once into the sink — keep downstream idempotent.
  4. Stable checkpoint + schema location.
  5. Handle rescued data.

Starter pattern

spark.readStream.format('cloudFiles')\
 .option('cloudFiles.format','csv')\
 .option('cloudFiles.schemaLocation', chk).load(landing)
Common mistake: Resetting the schema/checkpoint location, so Auto Loader reprocesses all files and duplicates them downstream.
Principal follow-up: Why is Auto Loader at-least-once, and how do you keep duplicates harmless downstream?
003
How would you implement DLT expectations for data quality in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumDLT expectations for data quality

Model answer

Delta Live Tables expectations are declarative DQ rules attached to a table: expect (warn/track), expect_or_drop (quarantine bad rows), expect_or_fail (stop the pipeline). They produce DQ metrics automatically and keep quality logic next to the table definition.

  • Three actions: warn/track, drop, or fail per rule.
  • Metrics on pass/fail are captured automatically for observability.
  • Keep critical-key rules as fail; recoverable issues as drop+quarantine.
  • Rules live with the table, not scattered across notebooks.

Healthcare angle: Claims expectations: paid_amt>=0 (fail), valid CARC (drop to quarantine), member_id present (fail).

@dlt.expect_or_drop("nonneg_paid","paid_amt >= 0")
@dlt.expect_or_fail("has_key","claim_id IS NOT NULL")

What strong answers cover

  1. DLT expectations declare DQ rules with actions: warn (track), drop (quarantine row), or fail (stop pipeline).
  2. Calibrate so they catch real issues without dropping valid claims.
  3. Track violation metrics over time.
  4. Quarantine for review rather than silently dropping.
  5. Tie to upstream contracts.

Starter pattern

@dlt.expect_or_drop('valid_carc', "carc IN (SELECT code FROM carc_ref)")
@dlt.expect('member_present', 'member_id IS NOT NULL')
Common mistake: Expectations so strict they drop legitimate rows, or so loose they catch nothing — both corrupt or fail to protect.
Principal follow-up: How do you calibrate DLT expectations, and when do you choose drop vs fail vs warn?
004
How would you implement Unity Catalog governance in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumUnity Catalog governance

Model answer

Unity Catalog centralizes governance across workspaces: a three-level namespace (catalog.schema.table), fine-grained grants, column/row masking, lineage, and audit. It replaces per-workspace ad-hoc access with consistent, auditable RBAC and data discovery.

  • catalog.schema.table namespace with centralized grants.
  • Row filters + column masks enforce PHI minimization.
  • Automatic lineage and access audit for compliance.
  • Managed/external locations and volumes governed uniformly.

Healthcare angle: PHI columns (member_id, DOB) get masking policies and row-level filters in Unity Catalog so analysts see only what their role permits.

What strong answers cover

  1. Unity Catalog centralizes governance: a three-level namespace (catalog.schema.table), fine-grained access, lineage, and audit across workspaces.
  2. Govern PHI with grants, masking, row filters at the catalog level.
  3. One metastore, consistent policy.
  4. Lineage and audit come built in.
  5. Design catalogs by environment/domain.

Starter pattern

GRANT SELECT ON claims_prod.silver.claim_line TO `claims_readers`;
ALTER TABLE ... SET MASK ...  -- column masking
Common mistake: Managing access per-workspace/table ad hoc instead of centrally in Unity Catalog, fragmenting PHI governance.
Principal follow-up: How does Unity Catalog give consistent PHI governance, lineage, and audit across workspaces?
005
How would you implement Photon performance tradeoffs in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumPhoton performance tradeoffs

Model answer

Photon is a vectorized C++ execution engine that accelerates SQL and Delta operations (scans, joins, aggregations) at higher DBU cost. It helps SQL-heavy/Delta workloads most; it doesn't accelerate arbitrary Python/UDF code. Benchmark cost-per-job, not just runtime.

  • Vectorized engine → big speedups on SQL/Delta scan-join-aggregate.
  • Higher DBU/hour but often lower total cost via shorter runtime.
  • Limited benefit for Python UDF-heavy or non-SQL workloads.
  • Evaluate on your actual workload mix.

Healthcare angle: Gold-layer claims aggregations (SQL/Delta) benefit from Photon; a Python-UDF-heavy parser stage may not.

What strong answers cover

  1. Photon is a vectorized C++ engine that speeds SQL/DataFrame scans, joins, and aggregations — big wins on large claims queries.
  2. Costs more per DBU but often cheaper overall via speed; not every workload benefits (heavy Python UDFs).
  3. Benchmark your workload.
  4. Best for SQL-heavy analytics.
  5. Measure cost-per-query, not per-hour.

Starter pattern

# enable Photon on the cluster/warehouse; benchmark a representative
# claims aggregation: runtime + total cost vs non-Photon
Common mistake: Assuming Photon always wins (or always costs more) without benchmarking the actual workload's total cost.
Principal follow-up: Which claims workloads benefit most from Photon, and how do you judge cost-per-query vs per-hour?
006
How would you implement workflow job orchestration in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumworkflow job orchestration

Model answer

Databricks Workflows orchestrate multi-task jobs with dependencies, retries, parameters, and scheduling, running on job clusters that auto-terminate. Model the claims pipeline as a DAG (ingest→silver→gold→reconcile), with per-task retries and alerting.

  • Define task DAG with explicit dependencies and per-task retries.
  • Job clusters auto-terminate → cost control.
  • Parameterize for backfills/reprocessing; pass task values downstream.
  • Built-in alerting on failure/SLA.

Healthcare angle: Nightly claims flow: Auto Loader→silver dedup→gold marts→reconciliation, each a task with retry and failure alerts.

What strong answers cover

  1. Databricks Workflows orchestrate multi-task DAGs with dependencies, retries, parameters, and scheduling.
  2. Model raw->silver->gold->reconcile as a task DAG with explicit dependencies.
  3. Per-task retries and conditional execution.
  4. Gate publication on reconciliation success.
  5. Monitor and alert on failures.

Starter pattern

# Workflow DAG: ingest -> silver -> gold -> reconcile (gates publish)
# per-task retries; alert on failure
Common mistake: Hidden ordering via separate schedules instead of explicit task dependencies, so gold can run before silver finishes.
Principal follow-up: How would you structure a claims pipeline as a Workflow DAG so gold only builds after reconciliation passes?
007
How would you implement cluster policy design in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumcluster policy design

Model answer

Cluster policies constrain what clusters users can create — node types, autoscaling bounds, auto-termination, tags, Photon, spot usage — to control cost and enforce standards. Define policies per team/workload so self-service stays within guardrails.

  • Enforce auto-termination, max workers, allowed node types.
  • Mandate cost-attribution tags.
  • Separate policies for jobs vs interactive/dev.
  • Prevent runaway/oversized clusters by default.

Healthcare angle: A policy forcing tags + auto-termination + spot workers keeps ad-hoc analyst clusters from silently inflating the claims platform bill.

What strong answers cover

  1. Cluster policies enforce guardrails: instance types, autoscaling limits, auto-termination, tags, Photon, and cost controls.
  2. Prevent runaway clusters and enforce tagging for cost attribution.
  3. Different policies per team/workload.
  4. Mandate auto-termination.
  5. Standardize safe defaults.

Starter pattern

# policy: enforce auto_termination, max_workers, allowed node types,
# mandatory cost-center tag, Photon for SQL
Common mistake: No cluster policies, so teams spin up oversized, untagged, never-terminating clusters that blow the budget.
Principal follow-up: What guardrails would you enforce via cluster policies to control cost and enable attribution?
008
How would you implement job cluster vs all-purpose cluster in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumjob cluster vs all-purpose cluster

Model answer

Job clusters are created for a job run and terminate after — cheaper, isolated, reproducible. All-purpose (interactive) clusters stay up for notebooks/collaboration and cost more if left idle. Run production pipelines on job clusters; use all-purpose only for development.

  • Job clusters: ephemeral, cheaper, isolated per run.
  • All-purpose: shared, interactive, costly if idle.
  • Production = job clusters; dev/EDA = all-purpose with auto-terminate.
  • Lower DBU rate for jobs compute reinforces the split.

Healthcare angle: Nightly claims jobs belong on job clusters; leaving an all-purpose cluster on for them wastes money.

What strong answers cover

  1. Job clusters spin up for a job and terminate after (cheaper, isolated); all-purpose clusters are shared/interactive (for development).
  2. Use job clusters for scheduled production pipelines; all-purpose for notebooks/exploration.
  3. Job clusters avoid idle cost and contention.
  4. Pools reduce job-cluster startup latency.
  5. Don't run prod on shared all-purpose clusters.

Starter pattern

# production scheduled job -> job cluster (ephemeral)
# interactive dev -> all-purpose cluster
Common mistake: Running production jobs on a shared all-purpose cluster, causing contention, idle cost, and noisy-neighbor issues.
Principal follow-up: Why run scheduled claims pipelines on job clusters rather than a shared all-purpose cluster?
009
How would you implement Delta Live Tables vs hand-coded jobs in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumDelta Live Tables vs hand-coded jobs

Model answer

DLT gives declarative pipelines with managed dependencies, expectations, auto-scaling, and lineage; hand-coded jobs give full control and flexibility for complex/bespoke logic. Use DLT for standardized medallion flows with DQ; hand-code when logic exceeds what DLT expresses cleanly.

  • DLT: declarative, built-in DQ/lineage/orchestration, less boilerplate.
  • Hand-coded: maximum control for unusual patterns.
  • DLT shines for standard ingest→clean→serve with expectations.
  • Don't force highly custom logic into DLT just to use it.

Healthcare angle: A standard claims medallion pipeline with DQ fits DLT; a bespoke bitemporal reconstruction may be cleaner hand-coded.

What strong answers cover

  1. DLT is declarative (define tables + expectations; it manages dependencies, incremental refresh, quality); hand-coded jobs give full control.
  2. DLT reduces plumbing for standard medallion pipelines; hand-coded for complex/bespoke logic.
  3. DLT has built-in DQ and lineage.
  4. Choose by complexity and team skills.
  5. Both can coexist.

Starter pattern

@dlt.table
def silver_claims():
    return dlt.read_stream('bronze').transform(clean)  # managed incremental + expectations
Common mistake: Forcing genuinely complex bespoke logic into DLT (or hand-coding a standard medallion pipeline DLT would simplify).
Principal follow-up: When does DLT beat hand-coded jobs for a claims pipeline, and when is hand-coding the right call?
010
How would you implement Change Data Feed in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumChange Data Feed

Model answer

Delta Change Data Feed exposes row-level changes (_change_type, version) so consumers read only deltas. Enable per table, then read changes by version/timestamp to propagate incremental updates downstream cheaply.

  • Row-level change records with type + commit version.
  • Enable delta.enableChangeDataFeed=true.
  • Downstream incremental updates without full reloads.
  • Pairs with MERGE for efficient propagation.

Healthcare angle: Feeding only changed claims into a downstream actuarial mart via CDF avoids recomputing the whole mart nightly.

spark.read.format("delta").option("readChangeFeed","true").option("startingVersion",v).table("claims")

What strong answers cover

  1. Delta Change Data Feed exposes row-level changes (insert/update/delete) so downstream consumes only what changed.
  2. Enable per table; read change types to drive incremental MERGEs.
  3. Avoids full snapshot diffs.
  4. Mind change-data retention.
  5. Powers efficient downstream CDC.

Starter pattern

spark.read.format('delta').option('readChangeFeed','true')\
 .option('startingVersion', v).table('silver_claims')
Common mistake: Diffing full table snapshots downstream when CDF would stream the exact row changes far more cheaply.
Principal follow-up: How does CDF make a downstream incremental mart cheaper than snapshot diffing?
011
How would you implement structured streaming from ADLS in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumstructured streaming from ADLS

Model answer

Stream files/events from ADLS with structured streaming + Auto Loader, using a checkpoint for exactly-once file tracking and watermarks for stateful work. Tune trigger interval (or Trigger.AvailableNow for batch-like catch-up) and handle schema evolution.

  • Auto Loader + checkpoint = exactly-once incremental file streaming.
  • Trigger.AvailableNow processes the backlog then stops (good for batch).
  • Watermarks bound stateful operations.
  • Plan schema-evolution and rescued-data handling.

Healthcare angle: Claims files landing continuously on ADLS are streamed into Bronze; Trigger.AvailableNow handles backfills cleanly.

What strong answers cover

  1. Stream files/events from ADLS with checkpointing for exactly-once-effectively processing and bounded state.
  2. Use Auto Loader for files; manage checkpoints and watermarks.
  3. Idempotent sinks handle at-least-once.
  4. Tune trigger interval and backpressure.
  5. Monitor stream lag.

Starter pattern

spark.readStream.format('cloudFiles')...load(adls_path)\
 .writeStream.option('checkpointLocation', chk).trigger(processingTime='1 minute')...
Common mistake: No checkpoint (or a shared/unstable one), so the stream can't recover and reprocesses or loses data on restart.
Principal follow-up: What role does the checkpoint play, and how do you make an ADLS stream recover cleanly?
012
How would you implement schema drift handling in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumschema drift handling

Model answer

Detect and govern source schema changes: Auto Loader schema evolution adds new columns and captures unexpected data in a rescued-data column, while enforcement rejects breaking changes. Treat renames/type changes as contract changes requiring review, not silent auto-apply.

  • Additive columns: evolve automatically; capture surprises in _rescued_data.
  • Breaking changes (type/rename): contract change, reviewed and versioned.
  • Quarantine records that violate the expected schema.
  • Alert on drift so it's a decision, not a surprise.

Healthcare angle: A payer adding a new claim field shouldn't break ingestion; a changed data type for paid_amt must be reviewed.

What strong answers cover

  1. Handle drift by capturing rescued data, evolving additively (mergeSchema), and quarantining breaking changes.
  2. Enforce a contract; don't blindly auto-merge type/rename changes.
  3. Alert on drift.
  4. Auto Loader rescues unexpected columns.
  5. Version schemas.

Starter pattern

.option('cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode','addNewColumns')
# rescuedDataColumn captures surprises; breaking changes -> quarantine
Common mistake: Auto-merging every schema change, so a silent type change corrupts claim values without error.
Principal follow-up: Which drift can you auto-evolve, and how does the rescued-data column protect you?
013
How would you implement file notification mode in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumfile notification mode

Model answer

Auto Loader file-notification mode uses cloud event notifications (queues) to learn of new files instead of listing the directory, which scales to very high file counts and large backlogs far better than directory listing.

  • Notification mode avoids costly repeated directory listings.
  • Scales to millions of files; lower latency to detect new files.
  • Requires cloud resources (event grid/queue) and permissions.
  • Listing mode is simpler for low file volumes.

Healthcare angle: High-volume EDI drops (many small files per minute) need notification mode to keep ingestion latency and cost reasonable.

What strong answers cover

  1. Auto Loader's file notification mode uses cloud notifications (queues) to discover new files at scale, instead of listing directories.
  2. Far more efficient for high file volumes than directory listing.
  3. Requires cloud setup (notification + queue).
  4. Use for large-scale ingestion.
  5. Directory listing is fine for low volumes.

Starter pattern

.option('cloudFiles.useNotifications','true')  # scalable file discovery
# vs directory listing for low file counts
Common mistake: Using directory-listing mode at high file volumes, where listing becomes the bottleneck instead of notifications.
Principal follow-up: When do you switch Auto Loader to file-notification mode, and what setup does it need?
014
How would you implement checkpoint management in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumcheckpoint management

Model answer

Streaming checkpoints store source offsets and state and must be stable, backed up conceptually, and not hand-edited. Each query gets its own checkpoint; incompatible code/schema changes require migrating to a new checkpoint with a controlled reprocess from a known source position.

  • One checkpoint per streaming query; treat as critical state.
  • Don't share or hand-edit checkpoints.
  • Incompatible changes → new checkpoint + planned replay.
  • Idempotent sinks make replays safe.

Healthcare angle: Losing/altering a claims-stream checkpoint forces a careful replay; idempotent Delta writes prevent duplicate claims on recovery.

What strong answers cover

  1. Checkpoints store stream offsets/state for recovery; keep them stable, per-stream, and durable.
  2. Don't share or move checkpoints; changing code/schema can invalidate them.
  3. Recover via fresh checkpoint + replay into an idempotent sink.
  4. Back up conceptually; control changes.
  5. Monitor checkpoint health.

Starter pattern

writeStream.option('checkpointLocation', f'{base}/_checkpoints/silver_claims')
# one stable location per stream
Common mistake: Reusing one checkpoint location across streams or deleting it casually, breaking recovery and causing reprocessing.
Principal follow-up: What invalidates a streaming checkpoint, and how do you recover without duplicating data?
015
How would you implement DLT SCD Type 2 in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumDLT SCD Type 2

Model answer

DLT's APPLY CHANGES INTO with STORED AS SCD TYPE 2 handles SCD2 declaratively: you specify keys, sequence-by column, and tracked columns, and DLT manages versioning, current flags, and ordering for late/out-of-order changes.

  • APPLY CHANGES ... STORED AS SCD TYPE 2 manages versioning for you.
  • SEQUENCE BY a reliable ordering column for correct latest/version.
  • Handles out-of-order and late-arriving changes.
  • Less error-prone than hand-rolled two-step MERGE.

Healthcare angle: Member/provider dimensions get clean SCD2 via DLT APPLY CHANGES, sequenced by source change timestamp.

APPLY CHANGES INTO live.member_dim FROM stream(live.member_cdc)
KEYS (member_id) SEQUENCE BY change_ts STORED AS SCD TYPE 2;

What strong answers cover

  1. DLT's APPLY CHANGES INTO handles SCD Type 2 declaratively — versioning changes with effective dates from a CDC stream.
  2. Specify keys, sequence column, and SCD type.
  3. Far less plumbing than hand-coded SCD2 MERGE.
  4. Handles ordering/dedup.
  5. Validate one current row per key.

Starter pattern

dlt.apply_changes(target='dim_member', source='changes',
  keys=['member_id'], sequence_by='seq', stored_as_scd_type=2)
Common mistake: Hand-coding a fragile SCD2 MERGE when DLT APPLY CHANGES would handle ordering, dedup, and versioning declaratively.
Principal follow-up: How does DLT APPLY CHANGES simplify SCD2, and what do you still validate?
016
How would you implement Databricks SQL warehouse tuning in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumDatabricks SQL warehouse tuning

Model answer

Tune SQL Warehouses by sizing to concurrency/latency needs, enabling Photon, using serverless for fast startup and elasticity, setting auto-stop, and scaling out (more clusters) for concurrency vs up (bigger) for single-query speed. Optimize the underlying Delta tables (file sizes, ZORDER) too.

  • Size up for heavy single queries; scale out clusters for concurrency.
  • Serverless for fast start + elasticity; auto-stop to save cost.
  • Photon on; keep tables OPTIMIZEd and clustered.
  • Result caching helps repeated BI queries.

Healthcare angle: Power BI hitting gold claims marts needs scale-out concurrency plus well-OPTIMIZEd tables, not just a bigger warehouse.

What strong answers cover

  1. Tune SQL warehouses by size (per-query speed), multi-cluster scaling (concurrency), Photon, auto-stop, and result caching.
  2. Scale out for many BI users; up for heavy single queries.
  3. Separate BI from ETL.
  4. Auto-stop to cut idle cost.
  5. Serve gold marts, not raw facts.

Starter pattern

# size for query weight; enable multi-cluster for concurrency;
# Photon on; auto-stop short; serve pre-aggregated gold
Common mistake: Pointing BI at raw facts on an undersized single-cluster warehouse, causing slow queries and queuing.
Principal follow-up: When do you scale a SQL warehouse up vs out, and why serve pre-aggregated gold marts?
017
How would you implement Delta OPTIMIZE strategy in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumDelta OPTIMIZE strategy

Model answer

Run OPTIMIZE to compact small files and ZORDER to cluster hot filter columns, targeting recently-written partitions rather than the whole table each time. Schedule it after heavy writes; enable optimizeWrite/autoCompact to reduce small files at write time.

  • Compact recent partitions; don't OPTIMIZE everything nightly.
  • ZORDER by the 1–2 most-filtered high-cardinality columns.
  • optimizeWrite + autoCompact reduce small files proactively.
  • Measure read/MERGE improvement to justify the maintenance cost.

Healthcare angle: Claims tables fed by streaming need scheduled OPTIMIZE on current service_months, ZORDERed by member_id.

OPTIMIZE claims WHERE service_month>=202401 ZORDER BY (member_id);

What strong answers cover

  1. OPTIMIZE compacts small files; with ZORDER it co-locates by high-cardinality columns for data-skipping.
  2. Schedule it on tables with small-file buildup (streaming sinks); scope by partition.
  3. Costs compute — run off-peak.
  4. Pair with VACUUM (mind retention).
  5. Measure read improvement.

Starter pattern

OPTIMIZE silver_claims WHERE service_month='2025-01' ZORDER BY (member_id)
Common mistake: Never compacting a streaming Delta sink, so small files accumulate and every query slows over time.
Principal follow-up: How would you schedule OPTIMIZE/ZORDER on a high-write claims table without disrupting jobs?
018
How would you implement ZORDER strategy in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumZORDER strategy

Model answer

ZORDER multi-dimensionally clusters data so files contain narrow value ranges for the chosen columns, maximizing data skipping for filters/joins on them. Choose columns frequently used in predicates after partition pruning; too many ZORDER columns dilutes the benefit.

  • Pick 1–2 high-cardinality, frequently-filtered columns.
  • Improves file skipping on those columns within partitions.
  • Re-run OPTIMIZE ZORDER as data grows.
  • Liquid clustering is the lower-maintenance successor.

Healthcare angle: ZORDER claims by member_id (and maybe provider_npi) so member-level lookups skip most files.

What strong answers cover

  1. ZORDER on the high-cardinality columns users filter on (member_id, provider_npi) improves data-skipping within files.
  2. Complements partitioning (low-cardinality split).
  3. Choose columns from real query patterns; too many dilutes benefit.
  4. Re-run as data grows.
  5. Costs maintenance compute.

Starter pattern

OPTIMIZE claims ZORDER BY (member_id, provider_npi)
# pick 1-3 columns from actual filter patterns
Common mistake: ZORDERing on many columns or ones nobody filters on, diluting the skipping benefit and wasting compute.
Principal follow-up: How do you choose ZORDER columns, and why not ZORDER on everything?
019
How would you implement liquid clustering in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedliquid clustering

Model answer

Liquid clustering replaces rigid partitioning/ZORDER with adaptive, incremental clustering by chosen keys — no partition-column commitment, automatic maintenance, and you can change cluster keys over time. It avoids small-file and high-cardinality-partition pitfalls.

  • Adaptive clustering without fixed partition columns.
  • Change cluster keys without rewriting the whole table layout.
  • Avoids over-partitioning small-files problems.
  • Good default for evolving access patterns.

Healthcare angle: A claims table whose query patterns shift (member vs provider vs date) benefits from liquid clustering over static partitioning.

CLUSTER BY (member_id, service_month)

What strong answers cover

  1. Liquid Clustering replaces rigid partitioning/ZORDER with flexible, self-tuning clustering keys you can change without rewrites.
  2. Avoids small-file and over-partitioning problems; adapts as patterns change.
  3. Good default for evolving claims access patterns.
  4. Set clustering keys on the table.
  5. Newer approach over partition+ZORDER.

Starter pattern

ALTER TABLE claims CLUSTER BY (service_month, member_id)
# liquid clustering: change keys without full rewrite
Common mistake: Rigidly partitioning by a column that later proves wrong, requiring an expensive rewrite that liquid clustering avoids.
Principal follow-up: How does liquid clustering improve on fixed partitioning + ZORDER for evolving access patterns?
020
How would you implement medallion anti-patterns in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedmedallion anti-patterns

Model answer

Common medallion mistakes: doing transformations in Bronze (it should be raw), skipping Silver validation, building Gold straight off raw, mutating Bronze (breaks replay), and unclear grain/contracts between layers. Keep each layer's responsibility crisp and immutable where it should be.

  • Don't transform in Bronze — keep it raw and replayable.
  • Don't skip Silver dedup/validation/conforming.
  • Don't build Gold off raw, bypassing quality.
  • Define grain and contracts at each boundary.

Healthcare angle: Aggregating claims for actuaries directly off raw 837 (no Silver) bakes in dupes and bad codes — a classic medallion anti-pattern.

What strong answers cover

  1. Anti-patterns: no real bronze (can't replay), business logic in bronze, skipping silver conformance, gold reading raw, no DQ gates between layers, and mixing grains.
  2. Keep each layer's responsibility clean.
  3. Quality gates at boundaries.
  4. Bronze immutable; gold consumer-ready.
  5. Don't let layers leak responsibilities.

Starter pattern

# bronze: raw only | silver: clean/conform | gold: business marts
# DQ + reconciliation at each boundary
Common mistake: Putting transformation logic in bronze or letting gold read raw bronze, collapsing the layering and losing replayability.
Principal follow-up: What are the common medallion anti-patterns, and how do they bite you in production?
021
How would you implement secrets management in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedsecrets management

Model answer

Store credentials in Databricks secret scopes (or a cloud key vault backing them), reference them at runtime, and never hard-code or print secrets in notebooks/logs. Scope access by role and rotate regularly.

  • Secret scopes / key-vault-backed scopes, never inline creds.
  • Reference via dbutils.secrets.get; redacted in output.
  • Role-scoped access; rotate on schedule.
  • Never log secret values.

Healthcare angle: SFTP/warehouse credentials for claims feeds live in a key-vault-backed scope to meet PHI security controls.

dbutils.secrets.get(scope="claims", key="sftp_pwd")

What strong answers cover

  1. Store credentials in Databricks secret scopes (or a backing key vault), referenced at runtime — never hard-coded in notebooks.
  2. Grant scope access by principal; rotate secrets.
  3. Secrets are redacted in output.
  4. Use service principals over personal tokens.
  5. Audit access.

Starter pattern

dbutils.secrets.get(scope='claims', key='sftp_password')
# never hard-code; rotate; redacted in logs
Common mistake: Hard-coding credentials in a notebook (visible in history/exports) instead of using secret scopes.
Principal follow-up: How do secret scopes plus service principals keep credentials safe and rotatable?
022
How would you implement service principals in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedservice principals

Model answer

Run production jobs as service principals, not personal accounts, so pipelines don't break when people leave and access is auditable and least-privilege. Grant the principal only the catalogs/schemas it needs in Unity Catalog.

  • Production runs under a service principal, not a user.
  • Survives staff changes; cleanly auditable.
  • Least-privilege grants in Unity Catalog.
  • Credentials managed via secret scopes/OAuth.

Healthcare angle: The claims pipeline runs as a service principal with access only to the claims catalog, satisfying audit and continuity needs.

What strong answers cover

  1. Service principals are non-human identities for automated jobs, with least-privilege grants — not personal tokens.
  2. Production jobs run as a service principal so they survive employee changes and are auditable.
  3. Grant minimal access; rotate credentials.
  4. Separate per pipeline/domain.
  5. Audit principal activity.

Starter pattern

# run production workflows as a service principal (least privilege)
# not a personal access token
Common mistake: Running production claims jobs under a personal token, which breaks when the person leaves and is hard to audit.
Principal follow-up: Why run production pipelines as a service principal rather than a personal token?
023
How would you implement CI/CD notebooks vs wheels in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
AdvancedCI/CD notebooks vs wheels

Model answer

Package reusable logic as Python wheels with tests for CI/CD and import them into thin notebooks/jobs, rather than embedding all logic in notebooks. Notebooks are great for orchestration/EDA; libraries are versioned, testable, and reviewable.

  • Core logic in tested wheels; notebooks stay thin orchestration.
  • Wheels are versioned and unit-testable in CI.
  • Deploy via Databricks Asset Bundles/Repos.
  • Reduces copy-paste drift across notebooks.

Healthcare angle: Shared claims transforms (dedup/SCD2) belong in a tested wheel, imported by job notebooks across environments.

What strong answers cover

  1. Package reusable logic as tested Python wheels (versioned, unit-tested); keep notebooks thin orchestration.
  2. Wheels enable CI tests and reuse; notebooks alone are hard to test/version.
  3. Promote via bundles dev->test->prod.
  4. Pin wheel versions.
  5. No business logic buried in notebooks.

Starter pattern

# library code -> tested wheel (CI); notebook imports it (thin)
# deploy via Databricks Asset Bundles
Common mistake: Burying business logic in untested notebooks, so there's no CI coverage and reuse means copy-paste.
Principal follow-up: Why package logic as tested wheels and keep notebooks thin for CI/CD?
024
How would you implement Repos and bundle deployment in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
AdvancedRepos and bundle deployment

Model answer

Databricks Repos sync Git; Asset Bundles (DABs) define jobs, clusters, and configs as code for repeatable, environment-promoted deployments. Together they give version control plus declarative, CI/CD-driven promotion dev→test→prod.

  • Repos = Git-backed source in the workspace.
  • Asset Bundles = jobs/clusters/config as code, env-parameterized.
  • Promote the same bundle across environments via CI/CD.
  • Eliminates manual click-ops deployment drift.

Healthcare angle: Promoting the claims pipeline from dev to prod is a bundle deploy with env-specific configs, not manual job recreation.

What strong answers cover

  1. Databricks Repos (Git) + Asset Bundles give reproducible, parameterized deployment of jobs/pipelines across environments.
  2. Bundles define resources as code, promoted dev->test->prod with env parameters.
  3. No manual click-ops in prod.
  4. Version-controlled, reviewable, rollbackable.
  5. Post-deploy verification.

Starter pattern

# databricks.yml bundle: jobs + clusters + params per target
# databricks bundle deploy -t prod
Common mistake: Manually creating/editing jobs in the prod UI, so deployments aren't reproducible, reviewable, or rollbackable.
Principal follow-up: How do Repos + Asset Bundles make claims-pipeline deployment reproducible and reviewable?
025
How would you implement data lineage in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advanceddata lineage

Model answer

Unity Catalog captures table and column-level lineage automatically, showing upstream sources and downstream consumers. Use it for impact analysis (what breaks if I change this), audit, and trust — invaluable when a claims number is disputed.

  • Automatic table + column lineage in Unity Catalog.
  • Impact analysis before schema/logic changes.
  • Audit/compliance evidence of data flow.
  • Helps trace a disputed metric to its sources.

Healthcare angle: When an actuary disputes a PMPM figure, lineage traces it from the gold mart back through Silver to the source claims feed.

What strong answers cover

  1. Unity Catalog captures table and column lineage automatically, source-to-consumer.
  2. Use it for impact analysis, dispute resolution (trace a number to source), and audit.
  3. Lineage spans notebooks, jobs, DLT, and queries.
  4. Supplement with business documentation.
  5. Foundational for trust at scale.

Starter pattern

# Unity Catalog -> table/column lineage graph
# trace a disputed PMPM from gold mart back to source 837
Common mistake: Relying on tribal knowledge instead of lineage, so a disputed number can't be traced and change impact is unknown.
Principal follow-up: How does column lineage let you assess the blast radius of a claims-schema change before you make it?
026
How would you implement audit logging in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedaudit logging

Model answer

Enable Unity Catalog/workspace audit logs to record who accessed/changed what data and when. Centralize them, retain per policy, and monitor for anomalous PHI access. This is both a security control and compliance evidence.

  • Capture access/changes to data and objects.
  • Centralize + retain per regulatory policy.
  • Alert on anomalous/over-broad PHI access.
  • Provides audit evidence on demand.

Healthcare angle: HIPAA audit needs proof of who queried member-level claims; UC audit logs supply it.

What strong answers cover

  1. Unity Catalog + workspace audit logs record who accessed/changed what, essential for HIPAA evidence.
  2. Persist and query them; combine with lineage and reconciliation for full auditability.
  3. Cover access to PHI tables.
  4. Audit-ready by design, not after the fact.
  5. Retain logs per policy.

Starter pattern

# system.access.audit + table access events
# answer: who read member-level claim columns, when
Common mistake: No persisted audit logs, so a HIPAA request about who accessed member claims becomes a scramble with no answer.
Principal follow-up: What audit evidence would you need ready to answer a HIPAA access question instantly?
027
How would you implement PII/PHI masking in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
AdvancedPII/PHI masking

Model answer

Apply Unity Catalog column-masking functions so unprivileged roles see redacted/tokenized PHI (member_id, SSN, DOB) while authorized roles see clear values. Combine with row filters and minimum-necessary role design.

  • Column masks redact PHI by role at query time.
  • Pair with row-level filters for full minimum-necessary enforcement.
  • Centralized policy, not per-view duplication.
  • Audit who can unmask.

Healthcare angle: Analysts get masked member identifiers; only a small role sees raw PHI for the few legitimate use cases.

CREATE FUNCTION mask_id(v STRING) RETURNS STRING
RETURN CASE WHEN is_account_group_member('phi_full') THEN v ELSE 'XXXX' END;

What strong answers cover

  1. Unity Catalog column masks redact PHI based on the querying principal's role at query time.
  2. One masking function reused across columns; combine with row filters.
  3. Most consumers see masked data; a narrow role sees clear.
  4. Audit who can unmask.
  5. Minimum-necessary by design.

Starter pattern

CREATE FUNCTION mask_ssn(s STRING) RETURN CASE WHEN is_member('phi_full') THEN s ELSE 'XXX-XX-XXXX' END;
ALTER TABLE member ALTER COLUMN ssn SET MASK mask_ssn;
Common mistake: Granting broad access to clear PHI for convenience instead of role-based masking, violating minimum-necessary.
Principal follow-up: How do column masks enforce minimum-necessary PHI access without copying data into restricted tables?
028
How would you implement row-level security in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedrow-level security

Model answer

Row filters restrict which rows a role can see (e.g. a region/plan analyst sees only their population). Define a filter function bound to the table; combine with column masks. Keep policies centralized and tested so a filter bug doesn't over- or under-expose data.

  • Row-filter functions limit visible rows by role/attribute.
  • Combine with column masks for full control.
  • Centralize and test — filter bugs leak or hide data.
  • Audit policy changes.

Healthcare angle: A regional analyst sees only their region's members; a buggy row filter is itself a reportable incident.

What strong answers cover

  1. Unity Catalog row filters restrict which rows a principal sees based on attributes (region/plan), enforcing minimum-necessary at the row level.
  2. Define a filter function; attach to the table.
  3. Combine with column masking.
  4. Centralized and auditable.
  5. Test per role.

Starter pattern

CREATE FUNCTION region_filter(region STRING) RETURN is_member('region_'||region);
ALTER TABLE claims SET ROW FILTER region_filter ON (region);
Common mistake: A row-filter mapping bug that leaks another region's members — an exposure incident, not a minor filter issue.
Principal follow-up: How would you test a row filter so a mapping bug can't leak another region's members?
029
How would you implement cost alerts in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedcost alerts

Model answer

Set budget/cost alerts and resource monitors (tags by job/team, DBU dashboards) so a runaway cluster or query is caught fast. Attribute cost by tag, alert on anomalies, and review trends — don't wait for the monthly bill.

  • Tag-based cost attribution by job/team/product.
  • Alerts on DBU/budget thresholds and anomalies.
  • Dashboards for trend, not surprise.
  • Tie spikes back to specific jobs quickly.

Healthcare angle: An overnight claims cost spike should page on a DBU anomaly alert, with tags pinpointing the offending job.

What strong answers cover

  1. Use budget/cost monitoring with tags and alerts to catch runaway spend; attribute cost via cluster tags and system tables.
  2. Alert on spikes; cap with cluster policies and auto-termination.
  3. Attribute cost per job/team.
  4. Investigate spikes via tags.
  5. Make cost owned.

Starter pattern

# tag clusters by cost-center; query system.billing.usage
# alert when daily DBU > threshold
Common mistake: No tagging or cost alerts, so an overnight runaway job isn't noticed until the monthly bill and can't be traced.
Principal follow-up: How would you attribute and alert on Databricks cost so a runaway job is caught and traceable?
030
How would you implement cluster autoscaling in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedcluster autoscaling

Model answer

Autoscaling adds/removes workers within bounds based on load, balancing cost and performance. Set sensible min/max, avoid thrashing on bursty workloads, and note that not all stages parallelize — over-large max can waste money without speedup.

  • Min/max bounds tuned to workload shape.
  • Avoid thrash on spiky loads; some stages won't use extra workers.
  • Pair with auto-termination for idle savings.
  • Validate that scaling actually improves the job.

Healthcare angle: Month-end claims volume justifies a higher autoscale max; the rest of the month runs lean.

What strong answers cover

  1. Autoscaling adds/removes workers within min/max bounds to match load, balancing performance and cost.
  2. Set sensible bounds; pair with auto-termination.
  3. Good for variable workloads; less so for steady or very short jobs.
  4. Watch scale-up latency.
  5. Use pools to speed scaling.

Starter pattern

# autoscale min_workers=2 max_workers=20 + auto_termination
# pools to reduce scale-up latency
Common mistake: Setting a huge max for autoscaling with no policy, so a skewed job scales out uselessly and burns cost without fixing the bottleneck.
Principal follow-up: When does autoscaling help, and why doesn't it fix a skew-bound job?
031
How would you implement spot instances risk in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedspot instances risk

Model answer

Spot/preemptible workers cut cost but can be reclaimed mid-job, causing task reruns. Use spot for fault-tolerant batch workers, keep the driver and critical workers on-demand, and ensure idempotent writes so preemption doesn't corrupt output.

  • Spot for retry-safe batch workers; on-demand driver.
  • Expect reclamation → tasks rerun; jobs must tolerate it.
  • Idempotent writes so preemption can't double-load.
  • Avoid spot for latency-critical or stateful-fragile work.

Healthcare angle: A nightly claims batch tolerates spot worker loss; idempotency guarantees a reclaimed task's rerun won't duplicate claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. Spot/preemptible instances cut cost but can be reclaimed; safe for fault-tolerant batch, risky for latency-critical or stateful work.
  2. Use spot workers with on-demand driver; ensure the job tolerates loss (idempotent, checkpointed).
  3. Mix spot/on-demand.
  4. Avoid spot for the driver.
  5. Validate recovery on preemption.

Starter pattern

# workers on spot (fault-tolerant batch), driver on-demand
# job must be idempotent/checkpointed to survive reclaim
Common mistake: Running latency-critical or non-idempotent jobs entirely on spot, so a reclaim causes failures or duplicates.
Principal follow-up: Which claims workloads are safe on spot, and what makes a job resilient to reclaim?
032
How would you implement job retry strategy in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedjob retry strategy

Model answer

Configure bounded retries with backoff for transient failures, but only when the job is idempotent — otherwise retries duplicate data. Distinguish retryable (transient infra) from non-retryable (bad data/logic) and alert when retries exhaust.

  • Retry transient infra failures with backoff; cap attempts.
  • Idempotency is a precondition for safe retries.
  • Don't auto-retry deterministic data/logic errors.
  • Alert + page on exhausted retries.

Healthcare angle: A claims job retrying after a transient ADLS blip is fine only because its writes are idempotent.

What strong answers cover

  1. Configure bounded retries with backoff for transient failures, only for idempotent tasks; alert on exhaustion.
  2. Distinguish retryable (infra) from non-retryable (bad data/logic).
  3. Don't auto-retry deterministic data errors.
  4. Idempotency is the precondition.
  5. Surface repeated failures.

Starter pattern

# task retry: max_retries=3, min_retry_interval, backoff
# only safe because the task is idempotent
Common mistake: Auto-retrying a non-idempotent task, so a transient failure mid-write leads to duplicated claims on retry.
Principal follow-up: Which failures should retry vs fail fast, and why must the task be idempotent first?
033
How would you implement data quality quarantine in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advanceddata quality quarantine

Model answer

Route records failing DQ rules to a quarantine table with the failure reason and source location, instead of dropping or failing the whole load. Stewards review/repair quarantined rows; track quarantine volume as a quality signal.

  • Bad rows → quarantine table with reason + source line.
  • Don't silently drop or fail the entire file for a few bad rows.
  • Steward workflow to review/repair/reprocess.
  • Monitor quarantine volume for upstream drift.

Healthcare angle: Claim lines with invalid CARC codes go to quarantine for analyst review, while valid claims flow through.

What strong answers cover

  1. Route failing rows to a quarantine table with reason/source instead of failing the whole load; stewards fix and replay.
  2. Quarantine volume is a leading quality signal — alert on spikes.
  3. Replay corrected rows idempotently.
  4. Don't silently drop.
  5. Tie to upstream contracts.

Starter pattern

valid = df.filter(rules); bad = df.subtract(valid).withColumn('reason', ...)
bad.write.saveAsTable('quarantine_claims')  # steward + replay
Common mistake: Failing the entire claim load on a few bad rows, or silently dropping them with no quarantine/replay path.
Principal follow-up: Why quarantine bad rows rather than fail the load or drop them, and how do you replay fixes?
034
How would you implement late-arriving membership in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedlate-arriving membership

Model answer

Late/retro membership changes must reopen affected periods: route by effective date, re-version SCD2 spans, and reprocess dependent claims attribution and member-month/PMPM aggregates. Bitemporal modeling keeps restatements auditable.

  • Apply by effective date; re-version SCD2 spans.
  • Reprocess member-month/PMPM and claim attribution for affected window.
  • Bitemporal columns make restatements reproducible.
  • Quantify and communicate the blast radius.

Healthcare angle: A retro eligibility add shifts member-months and capitation; the pipeline must restate affected periods, not just append.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model eligibility effective-dated/bitemporal; when membership arrives late or retroactively, recompute member-months and restate dependents.
  2. Gate dependent steps if membership is missing.
  3. Restate affected periods idempotently.
  4. Communicate restatements.
  5. Track lateness.

Starter pattern

# on late/retro membership: recompute member_months for [from,to]
# restate PMPM/capitation; gate marts if membership missing
Common mistake: Publishing claims marts on stale/missing membership, so member-months and every per-member metric are wrong.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep member-months correct when membership arrives late or retroactively?
035
How would you implement FHIR API ingestion in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
AdvancedFHIR API ingestion

Model answer

Ingest FHIR resources via paginated REST (follow Bundle 'next' links) with resilient clients (timeouts, jittered retries, rate-limit handling), land raw JSON in Bronze, then flatten VARIANT/JSON into typed Silver tables. Scale with parallel partitioned pulls.

  • Paginate Bundles; resilient client (timeouts/retries/rate limits).
  • Land raw JSON in Bronze for replay; flatten to typed Silver.
  • Parallelize by resource type / date / patient shard for scale.
  • Handle versioning and incremental (since) pulls.

Healthcare angle: Pulling FHIR Claim/EOB/Coverage nationally needs partitioned parallel pulls plus robust rate-limit/backoff handling.

What strong answers cover

  1. Ingest FHIR via resilient paginated pulls (backoff/jitter, Retry-After), land raw VARIANT in bronze, normalize to silver (US Core).
  2. Partition pulls for scale; incremental (_since) with checkpoints; reconcile completeness.
  3. Map resources to dimensions.
  4. Resilient clients + rate-limit handling.
  5. PHI-safe.

Starter pattern

# partitioned parallel pulls + backoff/jitter -> bronze VARIANT
# normalize to silver (Patient/Coverage/EOB -> dims); reconcile
Common mistake: A naive sequential pull that can't scale and silently drops resources when the API throttles (no reconciliation).
Principal follow-up: How do you guarantee completeness of a large FHIR pull against rate limits and outages?
036
How would you implement MLflow for risk model features in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
AdvancedMLflow for risk model features

Model answer

MLflow tracks experiments, parameters, metrics, and model versions, and the registry manages stage promotion. For risk-adjustment features, use it to version feature/model lineage, reproduce training, and govern which model version is in production.

  • Track params/metrics/artifacts per run for reproducibility.
  • Model Registry manages staging→production promotion.
  • Version feature definitions alongside models.
  • Supports audit/governance of model changes.

Healthcare angle: HCC/RAF feature pipelines and models are versioned in MLflow so a risk-score drift incident can be traced to a model/feature change.

What strong answers cover

  1. MLflow tracks experiments, parameters, metrics, and models for reproducibility; register models with versions/stages.
  2. Track feature/model versions so risk-score drift is explainable.
  3. Reproducible training and lineage to data.
  4. Promote models through stages.
  5. Monitor in production.

Starter pattern

import mlflow
with mlflow.start_run():
    mlflow.log_params(params); mlflow.log_metric('auc', auc); mlflow.sklearn.log_model(m,'risk')
Common mistake: Training risk models with no experiment/version tracking, so a score change can't be reproduced or explained in audit.
Principal follow-up: How does MLflow make a RAF/risk-model score change explainable and reproducible?
037
How would you implement feature store vs tables in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedfeature store vs tables

Model answer

A feature store adds discoverability, point-in-time-correct retrieval (avoiding leakage), online/offline consistency, and lineage on top of plain tables. Use it when features are shared across models/teams and training/serving skew matters; plain tables suffice for one-off features.

  • Point-in-time joins prevent training/serving leakage.
  • Discoverability + reuse across teams/models.
  • Online/offline consistency for real-time scoring.
  • Plain tables are fine for isolated, non-shared features.

Healthcare angle: Shared member-risk features (utilization, chronic flags) belong in a feature store for reuse and point-in-time correctness.

What strong answers cover

  1. A feature store adds point-in-time-correct retrieval (no leakage), online/offline consistency, versioning, and discovery over plain tables.
  2. Use it for shared ML features (member risk) to prevent training-serving skew.
  3. Plain tables lack point-in-time guarantees.
  4. Govern PHI in features.
  5. Reuse across models.

Starter pattern

# feature store: point-in-time join for training (as-of label time)
# vs a plain table that leaks future info
Common mistake: Building training features with a plain table join that leaks post-label-time data, inflating offline metrics.
Principal follow-up: How does a feature store's point-in-time retrieval prevent training leakage that a plain table join allows?
038
How would you implement observability dashboard in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedobservability dashboard

Model answer

An observability dashboard surfaces pipeline health: freshness/latency vs SLA, row/dollar control totals, DQ pass rates, quarantine volume, job durations, and cost. It turns silent degradation into visible signals and supports on-call.

  • Freshness/latency vs SLA, control totals, DQ pass rates.
  • Quarantine volume and job duration/cost trends.
  • Drive alerts off the same metrics.
  • One place to answer 'is the data right and on time?'

Healthcare angle: A claims platform dashboard shows last load time, daily volume vs baseline, paid control totals, and DQ — catching issues before stakeholders.

What strong answers cover

  1. Surface freshness, control totals, DQ pass rates, quarantine volume, job durations, and cost — and drive alerts off the same metrics.
  2. Build over audit/control tables and system tables.
  3. Map alerts to failure modes and owners.
  4. Goal: detect issues before consumers.
  5. Not just a wall display.

Starter pattern

# dashboard: last load time, volume vs baseline, paid totals,
# DQ pass %, quarantine count, cost; alerts off these
Common mistake: A pretty dashboard nobody watches that drives no alerts, so issues are still found by consumers first.
Principal follow-up: What signals belong on a claims observability dashboard, and how do they drive alerting?
039
How would you implement SLA monitoring in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
AdvancedSLA monitoring

Model answer

Track each data product's freshness and completeness against its SLA, alert on breach risk before the deadline (not after), and record SLA attainment over time. Tie SLAs to consumer needs and to upstream dependencies' latencies.

  • Monitor freshness/completeness vs documented SLA.
  • Predictive alerts before breach, not post-mortem.
  • Track attainment trend; renegotiate when unrealistic.
  • Account for upstream feed latency in the SLA.

Healthcare angle: If the actuarial mart must be ready by 7am, alert at 5am if the claims load is behind, leaving time to act.

What strong answers cover

  1. Define each pipeline's SLA to consumer need; monitor freshness/completeness and alert predictively before breach.
  2. Design backward from the consumer deadline accounting for upstream latency.
  3. Track attainment; renegotiate if chronically missed.
  4. Gate on upstream readiness.
  5. Surface SLA status.

Starter pattern

# alert if gold.max_load_ts < deadline - buffer (predictive)
# design backward from the 7am actuarial deadline
Common mistake: Only discovering an SLA miss after the deadline, instead of a predictive alert when the upstream load runs late.
Principal follow-up: How would you build a predictive SLA alert for a 7am actuarial-mart deadline?
040
How would you implement Databricks vs Snowflake split in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
AdvancedDatabricks vs Snowflake split

Model answer

A common split: Databricks/lakehouse for ingestion, heavy ETL, streaming, ML, and unstructured/large-scale processing; Snowflake for governed SQL serving, BI concurrency, and data sharing. The decision hinges on workload type, team skills, cost model, and existing investment — many shops run both with a clear boundary.

  • Lakehouse: ingestion, big ETL, streaming, ML, semi-structured at scale.
  • Snowflake: governed SQL marts, BI concurrency, easy sharing.
  • Define a clear handoff (e.g. gold marts published to both/one).
  • Decide by workload, skills, cost, and governance, not hype.

Healthcare angle: Process and clean claims in Databricks; serve actuarial/BI gold marts from Snowflake for concurrency and sharing — with one source of truth for the marts.

What strong answers cover

  1. Common split: Databricks for ingestion/ETL/streaming/ML/semi-structured; Snowflake for governed SQL serving/concurrency/sharing.
  2. Clean handoff at curated gold marts; one source of truth.
  3. Decide by workload, skills, governance, cost — not hype.
  4. Many payers run both deliberately.
  5. Avoid duplicating the authoritative mart.

Starter pattern

# clean/transform claims in Databricks (lakehouse) ->
# serve actuarial/BI gold via Snowflake (one source of truth)
Common mistake: Duplicating the gold mart in both platforms with no declared source of truth, so the two quietly diverge.
Principal follow-up: Where do you draw the Databricks/Snowflake boundary for a payer platform, and how do you avoid two diverging copies?
041
How would you implement Delta sharing in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
AdvancedDelta sharing

Model answer

Delta Sharing is an open protocol to share live Delta data across organizations/clouds without copying, with governed, revocable access. Use it to share curated claims/quality data with partners or internal domains while keeping one source.

  • Share live data without copies; recipients read directly.
  • Open protocol → cross-platform/cross-cloud recipients.
  • Governed and revocable via Unity Catalog.
  • Avoids stale duplicated extracts.
