Healthcare angle: Sharing a curated provider-quality dataset with a partner via Delta Sharing avoids emailing stale extracts and keeps access auditable.

What strong answers cover

  1. Delta Sharing is an open protocol to share live Delta data across orgs/platforms without copying.
  2. Share curated/secure views; govern and revoke access.
  3. Recipients can be non-Databricks.
  4. No stale extracts.
  5. Audit shares.

Starter pattern

CREATE SHARE provider_quality;
ALTER SHARE provider_quality ADD TABLE gold.provider_quality;
-- grant to recipient
Common mistake: Emailing CSV extracts of provider/quality data instead of governed, revocable live Delta Sharing.
Principal follow-up: When would you use Delta Sharing over a data copy, and how do you limit what's exposed?
042
How would you implement cross-cloud data sharing in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertcross-cloud data sharing

Model answer

Share across clouds via Delta Sharing or replicated governed copies, weighing latency, egress cost, residency/compliance, and consistency. Prefer share-in-place over copy when possible; when copying, make it governed and reconciled.

  • Share-in-place (Delta Sharing) avoids egress + staleness.
  • If copying, govern, reconcile, and track lineage.
  • Mind data residency/PHI compliance across regions.
  • Account for cross-cloud latency and egress cost.

Healthcare angle: A multi-cloud payer must keep PHI residency and reconciliation intact when sharing claims data across cloud boundaries.

What strong answers cover

  1. Delta Sharing (open protocol) enables sharing across clouds without copying; mind egress cost and PHI residency.
  2. Share secure views; govern access.
  3. Open format avoids lock-in.
  4. Minimize cross-cloud movement.
  5. Respect residency rules.

Starter pattern

# Delta Sharing across clouds (open protocol)
# share secure views; watch egress + PHI residency
Common mistake: Copying PHI data across clouds for sharing, incurring egress cost and residency risk when live sharing would suffice.
Principal follow-up: What are the cost and PHI-residency considerations when sharing claims data across clouds?
043
How would you implement external locations in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertexternal locations

Model answer

Unity Catalog external locations bind a storage path to a storage credential with governed grants, so access to raw/landing data in ADLS/S3 is controlled and auditable rather than via scattered keys. Use them for Bronze landing zones and external tables.

  • Path + credential governed by UC grants.
  • Replaces ad-hoc storage keys with auditable access.
  • Underpins external tables and volumes.
  • Least-privilege per location.

Healthcare angle: The 837 landing zone is an external location with grants limited to the ingestion service principal.

What strong answers cover

  1. Unity Catalog external locations govern access to cloud storage paths via storage credentials, centralizing and securing storage access.
  2. Grant access by principal; no inline keys.
  3. Organize by source/zone.
  4. Audit access to PHI storage.
  5. Used by Auto Loader/tables.

Starter pattern

CREATE EXTERNAL LOCATION claims_landing
  URL 'abfss://...' WITH (STORAGE CREDENTIAL cred);
GRANT READ FILES ON ... TO `ingest_sp`;
Common mistake: Mounting storage with inline keys/DBFS mounts instead of governed external locations, bypassing Unity Catalog access control.
Principal follow-up: How do external locations + storage credentials secure and govern cloud storage access?
044
How would you implement volumes in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertvolumes

Model answer

Unity Catalog Volumes provide governed, path-based access to non-tabular files (raw EDI, attachments, model artifacts) within the catalog namespace, with the same RBAC/audit as tables. Use them for files you must store and govern but not model as tables.

  • Governed file storage in the UC namespace.
  • Same RBAC/audit as tables, for non-tabular data.
  • Good for raw EDI, documents, artifacts.
  • Reference by volume path in jobs.

Healthcare angle: Raw 837 files and remittance PDFs live in governed Volumes so file access is audited like table access.

What strong answers cover

  1. Unity Catalog Volumes govern access to non-tabular files (e.g. raw 837/PDF) under the catalog namespace.
  2. Use them for landing files, model artifacts, and unstructured data with the same governance as tables.
  3. Managed or external volumes.
  4. Auditable, access-controlled.
  5. Replace ad-hoc DBFS paths.

Starter pattern

# /Volumes/claims_prod/landing/837/...  (governed file storage)
# grant READ VOLUME to ingest principal
Common mistake: Storing raw claim files in ungoverned DBFS paths instead of Volumes, losing access control and audit.
Principal follow-up: When would you use a Volume versus a table, and how does it bring files under governance?
045
How would you implement catalog/schema design in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertcatalog/schema design

Model answer

Design the UC namespace to mirror domains and environments: catalogs per environment or business domain, schemas per data product/layer, consistent naming. Good structure makes grants, discovery, and least-privilege natural; bad structure forces sprawling exceptions.

  • Catalogs by environment or domain; schemas by product/layer.
  • Consistent naming for discovery and grants.
  • Structure enables clean least-privilege RBAC.
  • Separate raw/curated/serving clearly.

Healthcare angle: claims_prod.bronze / .silver / .gold with grants per layer keeps PHI access minimal and discovery sane.

What strong answers cover

  1. Design the Unity Catalog namespace by environment and domain (e.g. claims_prod.silver.claim_line), with least-privilege grants per layer.
  2. Consistent naming; separate environments.
  3. Structure enables governance and discovery.
  4. Grants per schema/layer.
  5. Plan for multi-team.

Starter pattern

# catalog: claims_prod | schema: bronze/silver/gold
# grant per schema; PHI access narrow
Common mistake: A flat, inconsistent namespace with broad grants, making PHI governance and discovery a mess.
Principal follow-up: How would you structure catalogs/schemas for environments, domains, and least-privilege PHI access?
046
How would you implement multi-team governance in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertmulti-team governance

Model answer

Support many teams with domain-owned catalogs/schemas, centralized policies (masking, tags, lineage), federated ownership of data products, and clear contracts between domains. Governance should enable self-service within guardrails, not bottleneck delivery.

  • Domain ownership of data products with central policy guardrails.
  • Standard tagging, masking, lineage across teams.
  • Contracts between domains define grain/SLA/quality.
  • Enable self-service; avoid a central-team bottleneck.

Healthcare angle: Claims, membership, and provider domains each own their data products under shared PHI/governance policies — a data-mesh-style operating model.

What strong answers cover

  1. Federate governance: domain teams own their data products under central Unity Catalog standards (PHI policy, naming, masking, lineage).
  2. Central policy + self-serve within rails.
  3. Avoid a central bottleneck and an ungoverned free-for-all.
  4. Clear ownership boundaries.
  5. Audit across teams.

Starter pattern

# central UC: PHI policy + standards; domain teams own catalogs/products
# self-serve within guardrails
Common mistake: Either a central team bottlenecking everything or each team governing PHI its own way, fragmenting compliance.
Principal follow-up: How do you let multiple teams move fast while keeping PHI governance centrally consistent?
047
How would you implement developer workflow in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertdeveloper workflow

Model answer

A healthy workflow: Git Repos, feature branches, code in tested libraries, local/unit tests, PR review, CI running tests, and Asset Bundle promotion across environments. Notebooks for exploration; production logic is reviewed, versioned code.

  • Git branches + PR review + CI tests.
  • Production logic in tested libraries, not ad-hoc notebooks.
  • Bundles promote dev→test→prod reproducibly.
  • Environments isolated via catalogs/configs.

Healthcare angle: A claims transform change goes through PR + CI tests + bundle promotion, not a hand-edited prod notebook.

What strong answers cover

  1. Developers work in Repos (Git) with feature branches, test wheels in CI, and deploy via bundles — not editing prod directly.
  2. Local/dev cluster for iteration; PR review; CI tests.
  3. Promote dev->test->prod reproducibly.
  4. No prod click-ops.
  5. Fast, safe iteration.

Starter pattern

# Repos branch -> CI (unit tests on wheel) -> bundle deploy to dev/test/prod
# PR review; no manual prod edits
Common mistake: Developing and editing jobs directly in the prod workspace UI, with no version control, review, or tests.
Principal follow-up: What does a safe developer-to-production workflow look like on Databricks?
048
How would you implement notebook testing in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertnotebook testing

Model answer

Test by extracting logic into importable, unit-tested functions/wheels and keeping notebooks thin; run those tests in CI. For notebook-level checks, use parameterized runs with assertions on small inputs. Avoid logic that's only testable by running the whole notebook in prod.

  • Move logic to importable, unit-tested code.
  • Thin notebooks orchestrate tested functions.
  • Run tests in CI, not by eyeballing prod output.
  • Parameterized smoke tests on small data for integration.

Healthcare angle: Claims dedup logic is unit-tested as a library function; the notebook just wires inputs/outputs.

What strong answers cover

  1. Factor logic into importable, unit-tested functions (in wheels); keep notebooks thin so logic is testable off-cluster.
  2. Use pytest + a local SparkSession / chispa for DataFrame assertions.
  3. Cover edge cases (nulls, dupes, late data).
  4. Run tests in CI.
  5. Don't test only on the cluster.

Starter pattern

# logic in module (tested via pytest); notebook imports + orchestrates
def test_dedup(): assertDataFrameEqual(dedup(inp), expected)
Common mistake: Putting untestable logic inline in notebooks, so correctness is only ever checked manually on the cluster.
Principal follow-up: How do you make Databricks logic unit-testable in CI rather than only on a cluster?
049
How would you implement parameterized jobs in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertparameterized jobs

Model answer

Parameterize jobs (dates, source paths, modes) so the same code handles daily runs, backfills, and reprocessing without edits. Pass parameters via job/task settings and widgets; combine with task values to coordinate multi-task DAGs.

  • Parameters for date ranges/source/mode → reusable for backfill.
  • No code edits to reprocess a window.
  • Task values pass outputs between tasks.
  • Validate parameters early to fail fast.

Healthcare angle: Reprocessing six months of claims is the same job run with a date-range parameter, not a code change.

What strong answers cover

  1. Parameterize jobs (dates, source paths, environment) so one definition runs across runs/environments — no hard-coded values.
  2. Pass params via job widgets/bundle variables; use for backfills and env promotion.
  3. Enables reuse and reprocessing.
  4. Validate params.
  5. Drive reprocessing by date param.

Starter pattern

dbutils.widgets.text('service_month','')
month = dbutils.widgets.get('service_month')  # reprocess any month
Common mistake: Hard-coding dates/paths in the notebook, so reprocessing a different month means editing and redeploying code.
Principal follow-up: How would parameterization let you reprocess any month or promote across environments without code changes?
050
How would you implement task values in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Experttask values

Model answer

Task values let one task pass small outputs (counts, statuses, computed dates) to downstream tasks in a Workflow, enabling coordination and conditional logic across the DAG without external state.

  • Pass small values (counts, dates, flags) between tasks.
  • Enables conditional/branching DAG logic.
  • Avoids ad-hoc files/tables for coordination.
  • Keep values small; use tables for data.

Healthcare angle: An ingestion task emits the loaded service_month; the reconciliation task reads it to reconcile exactly that window.

What strong answers cover

  1. Task values pass small data between Workflow tasks (e.g. a computed watermark, a row count) for conditional downstream logic.
  2. Use them to coordinate a DAG (e.g. skip publish if reconciliation failed).
  3. Keep payloads small.
  4. Enable conditional execution.
  5. Don't abuse for large data.

Starter pattern

dbutils.jobs.taskValues.set('recon_ok', True)
# downstream task reads it to gate publish
Common mistake: Passing large data through task values (they're for small signals) or hard-coding coordination instead of using them.
Principal follow-up: How would you use task values to gate gold-mart publication on a reconciliation task's result?
051
How would you implement orchestration with ADF in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertorchestration with ADF

Model answer

Azure Data Factory can orchestrate cross-system flows and trigger Databricks jobs; use ADF for enterprise scheduling/integration across many sources and Databricks Workflows for compute-native orchestration. Define a clear boundary so you don't duplicate orchestration logic.

  • ADF for enterprise/cross-system orchestration and triggers.
  • Databricks Workflows for compute-native DAGs.
  • Avoid double-orchestration; pick the boundary.
  • Pass parameters cleanly between them.

Healthcare angle: ADF moves 837 files from partner SFTP to ADLS and triggers the Databricks claims pipeline; Workflows runs the internal DAG.

What strong answers cover

  1. When Azure Data Factory orchestrates, it triggers Databricks jobs/notebooks; keep the boundary clean (ADF for movement/scheduling, Databricks for transform).
  2. Pass parameters; handle retries/failure at the right layer.
  3. Avoid duplicating orchestration logic.
  4. Monitor across both.
  5. One source of truth for dependencies.

Starter pattern

# ADF pipeline -> Databricks job (notebook/jar) activity with params
# ADF: orchestration/movement; Databricks: transform
Common mistake: Splitting dependency logic messily across ADF and Databricks so failures/retries are inconsistent and hard to trace.
Principal follow-up: How do you cleanly divide responsibilities between ADF orchestration and Databricks transformation?
052
How would you implement incident rollback in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertincident rollback

Model answer

Roll back a bad data load using Delta time travel (RESTORE to a prior version) or by re-running an idempotent rebuild of affected partitions. Confirm the last-good version, restore, reconcile, and communicate. Atomic publish patterns prevent partial-state exposure.

  • Identify last-good Delta version; RESTORE or rebuild partition.
  • Idempotent reprocessing for clean recovery.
  • Reconcile post-restore before re-enabling consumers.
  • Atomic publish prevents readers seeing bad state.

Healthcare angle: A duplicate-835 load is rolled back via RESTORE to the pre-load version, then reconciled before AR reporting resumes.

RESTORE TABLE claims TO VERSION AS OF 145;

What strong answers cover

  1. Roll back a bad deploy/load by reverting code (bundle) and restoring data via Delta time travel (RESTORE) or rebuilding the affected partition.
  2. Reconcile after; communicate.
  3. Prefer blue-green/atomic publish to make rollback trivial.
  4. Rollback both code and data.
  5. Verify after.

Starter pattern

RESTORE TABLE gold.claims_mart TO VERSION AS OF 142;
# revert bundle to prior version; reconcile
Common mistake: Rolling back code but leaving the corrupted data it wrote, so the pipeline is fixed but the mart is still wrong.
Principal follow-up: When a bad deploy has already written data, how do you roll back both code and data?
053
How would you implement time travel version restore in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Experttime travel version restore

Model answer

Delta time travel lets you query or RESTORE a table to a prior version/timestamp within retention. Use it to recover from bad loads, audit prior states, and reproduce historical reports — but VACUUM retention limits how far back you can go.

  • Query/RESTORE by VERSION AS OF / TIMESTAMP AS OF.
  • Recovery from bad loads and reproducible historical reads.
  • Bounded by VACUUM retention — set it to your recovery window.
  • RESTORE is itself a new versioned commit.

Healthcare angle: Recovering corrupted claims after a bad MERGE is a RESTORE to the last-good version — provided VACUUM hasn't purged it.

SELECT * FROM claims VERSION AS OF 145;

What strong answers cover

  1. Delta time travel lets you query or RESTORE a table to a prior version/timestamp within retention.
  2. Recover from a bad load by restoring to the last-good version; reconcile after.
  3. Set retention to your recovery window.
  4. Prefer atomic publish to avoid needing it.
  5. Mind VACUUM not purging needed versions.

Starter pattern

SELECT * FROM claims VERSION AS OF 140;
RESTORE TABLE claims TO VERSION AS OF 140;  -- recover bad load
Common mistake: Discovering corruption after VACUUM/retention purged the good version, leaving nothing to restore.
Principal follow-up: How do you size Delta retention against recovery needs, and how does VACUUM interact with time travel?
054
How would you implement production promotion in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertproduction promotion

Model answer

Promote via CI/CD: tested code in bundles, environment-specific configs, automated deployment dev→test→prod, and a verification/smoke check post-deploy. No manual prod edits; changes are reviewed, versioned, and reproducible.

  • Bundle-based, config-parameterized promotion.
  • Automated, reviewed, reproducible — no click-ops in prod.
  • Post-deploy smoke tests/verification.
  • Roll-back plan ready.

Healthcare angle: A new claims gold mart ships to prod via bundle promotion with a post-deploy reconciliation smoke test.

What strong answers cover

  1. Promote via Asset Bundles dev->test->prod with environment parameters, CI tests, and post-deploy verification — reproducible and rollbackable.
  2. No manual prod edits; review changes.
  3. Run a smoke/reconciliation check post-deploy.
  4. Keep rollback ready.
  5. Version everything.

Starter pattern

databricks bundle deploy -t test   # validate
databricks bundle deploy -t prod   # then promote; post-deploy recon
Common mistake: Promoting by manually recreating jobs in prod, so environments drift and deployments aren't reproducible.
Principal follow-up: What does a reproducible dev-to-prod promotion look like, including post-deploy verification?
055
How would you implement DR strategy in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
ExpertDR strategy

Model answer

DR for a lakehouse: replicate critical Delta data and metadata (Unity Catalog) to a secondary region, define RPO/RTO, automate failover of jobs and access, and regularly test the runbook. Decide hot/warm/cold per cost vs recovery-time needs.

  • Replicate data + UC metadata cross-region; define RPO/RTO.
  • Automate job/access failover; document the runbook.
  • Test DR regularly — untested DR is hope, not a plan.
  • Tier criticality (claims vs reference) to control cost.

Healthcare angle: Claims platform DR replicates gold marts and catalog to a second region with a tested failover runbook meeting the business RTO.

What strong answers cover

  1. DR replicates critical data and metadata (Unity Catalog) cross-region with defined RPO/RTO, automated failover, and tested runbooks.
  2. Tier by criticality (gold marts vs reference).
  3. Replicate Delta tables + catalog.
  4. Test failover regularly.
  5. Untested DR isn't DR.

Starter pattern

# Delta deep clone / replication cross-region + UC metastore replication
# RPO/RTO tiered; scheduled failover test
Common mistake: Configuring data replication but not the Unity Catalog metastore, so failover has data but no governed access to it.
Principal follow-up: What must a Databricks DR plan replicate beyond the data, and why is the test cadence part of it?
056
How would you implement ingestion audit tables in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertingestion audit tables

Model answer

Maintain audit tables recording each file/batch ingested: source, filename, row counts, control totals, status, timestamps, and run id. They give traceability, detect duplicates/missing files, support reconciliation, and provide audit evidence.

  • Log per-file/batch: source, name, counts, totals, status, ts, run_id.
  • Detect duplicate/missing files and reconcile loaded vs expected.
  • Traceability from a row back to its source file.
  • Audit evidence for compliance.

Healthcare angle: An ingestion audit table proves which 837 files loaded, their counts, and flags a duplicate before it corrupts claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. Persist per-file/per-batch audit rows (file, row count, load time, status, errors) to reconcile loaded vs expected and detect duplicates/gaps.
  2. Ingestion audit trail for compliance and triage.
  3. Tie to source trailers.
  4. Drive observability/alerts.
  5. Detect missing/duplicate files.

Starter pattern

INSERT INTO audit.ingest(file, rows, load_ts, status)
SELECT ... ;  -- reconcile vs expected; detect dup/missing files
Common mistake: No ingestion audit, so a missing or duplicate claim file isn't detected and can't be reconciled.
Principal follow-up: What would you record in an ingestion audit table to detect a missing or duplicate claim file?
057
How would you implement metadata-driven pipelines in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertmetadata-driven pipelines

Model answer

Drive ingestion/transformation from a metadata catalog (source, schema, DQ rules, target, SLA) so onboarding a feed is configuration, not new code. A generic engine executes the metadata; reserve custom code for genuinely bespoke logic to avoid over-abstraction.

  • Config/metadata defines feeds; one engine runs them.
  • New feed = config row, not new codebase.
  • Centralizes DQ/lineage/control totals/logging.
  • Know the limits — don't force bespoke logic into config.

Healthcare angle: Onboarding many payer feeds is far faster with metadata-driven ingestion, until a feed's logic exceeds what config can express.

What strong answers cover

  1. Drive pipelines from metadata (source, schema, DQ rules, mappings, target) executed by a shared engine, so a new feed is configuration.
  2. Built-in idempotency, DQ/quarantine, reconciliation, lineage.
  3. Speeds onboarding; enforces standards.
  4. Escape hatch for bespoke logic.
  5. Keep config simple/tested/versioned.

Starter pattern

FEEDS = read_metadata()   # per-feed config
for f in FEEDS: engine.run(f)   # shared idempotent engine + DQ + recon
Common mistake: Hand-building a bespoke job per feed (slow/inconsistent) or forcing genuinely bespoke logic through rigid config.
Principal follow-up: Where does a metadata-driven approach speed onboarding, and where does it need an escape hatch?
058
How would you implement CDC from SQL Server in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
ExpertCDC from SQL Server

Model answer

Capture SQL Server changes (CDC/CT, or a tool like Debezium/log-based replication) into Bronze, then apply via MERGE keyed on the natural key, ordered by LSN, mapping deletes to soft-deletes. Handle schema changes and ensure idempotent, ordered application.

  • Log-based CDC → Bronze change records with LSN ordering.
  • MERGE upsert; map deletes to soft-delete/end-date.
  • Idempotent + handle out-of-order/duplicate events.
  • Plan for source schema drift.

Healthcare angle: Membership/provider source systems on SQL Server feed the lakehouse via CDC; LSN ordering keeps the dimension's current view correct.

What strong answers cover

  1. Stream SQL Server changes via log-based CDC into bronze, apply with ordered idempotent MERGE (by LSN), deletes to soft-deletes, SCD2 where needed.
  2. Reconcile to source; handle schema drift.
  3. Order and idempotency are essential.
  4. Maintain current state correctly.
  5. Monitor CDC lag.

Starter pattern

# CDC -> bronze (LSN order) -> APPLY CHANGES / MERGE (idempotent)
# deletes -> soft-delete; reconcile to source
Common mistake: Applying CDC changes out of order or non-idempotently, so an older update overwrites a newer one or reruns duplicate.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep current state correct when streaming SQL Server CDC into the lakehouse?
059
How would you implement dedup constraints workaround in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Expertdedup constraints workaround

Model answer

Delta lacks enforced unique/PK constraints, so enforce uniqueness in the pipeline: dedup with QUALIFY/window before write, MERGE on the key (not append), and add a post-load uniqueness assertion that fails the job. Treat the grain as a tested invariant.

  • No native unique constraint → enforce in code.
  • Dedup before write; MERGE on key, never blind append.
  • Post-load assertion: zero keys with COUNT>1, or fail.
  • Document the grain as an invariant.

Healthcare angle: Claims-line uniqueness is enforced by dedup + keyed MERGE + a post-load uniqueness check, since Delta won't enforce a PK.

SELECT claim_id,line,COUNT(*) FROM claims GROUP BY 1,2 HAVING COUNT(*)>1; -- must be empty

What strong answers cover

  1. Delta has no enforced unique constraint, so dedup with MERGE on the key or QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER, and assert uniqueness as a DQ check.
  2. Keep the latest version with a deterministic tie-breaker.
  3. Run a uniqueness assertion post-load.
  4. Idempotent loads prevent re-introduction.
  5. Quarantine unexpected dupes.

Starter pattern

df.dropDuplicates(['claim_id','line'])  # or MERGE on key
# DQ: assert no key has count>1 after load
Common mistake: Assuming Delta enforces uniqueness like an RDBMS PK, so duplicates slip in undetected.
Principal follow-up: Delta has no enforced PK — how do you guarantee and verify claim-line uniqueness?
060
How would you implement Delta constraints in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
ExpertDelta constraints

Model answer

Delta supports CHECK constraints and NOT NULL enforcement (and column-level invariants), which fail writes violating them. Use them for value/range/not-null rules; uniqueness/PK still must be handled in the pipeline.

  • CHECK + NOT NULL constraints reject bad writes.
  • Good for range/value/not-null invariants (paid_amt>=0).
  • Uniqueness/PK not enforced — handle in pipeline.
  • Constraints document and protect the contract.

Healthcare angle: A CHECK (paid_amt >= -1000000) and NOT NULL on claim_id catch gross corruption at write time.

ALTER TABLE claims ADD CONSTRAINT pos_paid CHECK (paid_amt >= 0);

What strong answers cover

  1. Delta supports CHECK constraints and NOT NULL to enforce invariants at write time (reject bad rows).
  2. Use them for non-negotiable rules (e.g. paid >= 0, member_id NOT NULL).
  3. They fail the write on violation — pair with quarantine upstream.
  4. Complement DQ checks.
  5. Document constraints.

Starter pattern

ALTER TABLE claims ADD CONSTRAINT paid_nonneg CHECK (paid >= 0);
ALTER TABLE claims ALTER COLUMN member_id SET NOT NULL;
Common mistake: Relying only on app-side checks with no table constraints, so a bad write isn't rejected at the storage layer.
Principal follow-up: Which invariants would you enforce as Delta CHECK/NOT NULL constraints versus upstream DQ?
061
How would you implement expectations vs tests in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principalexpectations vs tests

Model answer

Expectations (DLT) run inline on production data each load, continuously gating/measuring quality; tests (unit/integration) validate code logic pre-deploy on known inputs. You need both: tests prove the code is right, expectations prove the data is right.

  • Tests: pre-deploy, code correctness on crafted inputs.
  • Expectations: in-production, data quality each run.
  • Tests catch logic bugs; expectations catch bad/drifting data.
  • Use them together, not interchangeably.

Healthcare angle: Unit tests lock claims dedup logic; DLT expectations catch a day's feed with invalid codes in production.

What strong answers cover

  1. DLT expectations validate data at runtime (is this row good?); unit tests validate code/logic at build time (does the transform work?).
  2. You need both: tests catch logic bugs pre-deploy; expectations catch data issues in production.
  3. They're complementary, not interchangeable.
  4. Tests in CI; expectations in the pipeline.
  5. Cover edge cases in both.

Starter pattern

# unit test: assert dedup(input)==expected (build time)
# DLT expectation: @dlt.expect_or_drop('valid_carc', ...) (runtime)
Common mistake: Treating DLT expectations as a substitute for unit tests (or vice versa), leaving either logic bugs or data issues uncaught.
Principal follow-up: Why do you need both unit tests and DLT expectations, and what does each catch?
062
How would you implement streaming backfill in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principalstreaming backfill

Model answer

Backfill a streaming pipeline by reprocessing historical files/offsets — often via Trigger.AvailableNow to drain the backlog then stop, or a separate batch job into the same idempotent sink. Keep the live stream and backfill from colliding (separate checkpoints) and ensure idempotency.

  • Trigger.AvailableNow drains backlog then stops.
  • Or batch-backfill into the same idempotent sink.
  • Isolate backfill checkpoint from live stream.
  • Idempotency prevents duplicates from overlap.

Healthcare angle: Backfilling six months of claim files into a streaming table uses AvailableNow with idempotent writes so it won't duplicate live data.

What strong answers cover

  1. Backfill historical data alongside a running stream by processing history in bounded batches into the same idempotent sink, then let the stream continue.
  2. Avoid reprocessing everything through the stream; isolate backfill compute.
  3. Idempotent sink so overlap is harmless.
  4. Reconcile before/after.
  5. Bound cost.

Starter pattern

# batch backfill history -> same Delta sink (idempotent MERGE/replaceWhere)
# stream continues for new data; overlap is safe
Common mistake: Backfilling by replaying everything through the live stream, overwhelming it, or using a non-idempotent sink that duplicates on overlap.
Principal follow-up: How do you backfill history into a streaming table without disrupting the live stream or duplicating?
063
How would you implement large MERGE tuning in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principallarge MERGE tuning

Model answer

Speed large MERGEs by constraining the target (partition/predicate matching the merge key), clustering on the key, pre-deduping/shrinking the source, raising parallelism, and using deletion vectors/low-shuffle merge to cut file-rewrite amplification.

  • Add target-pruning predicates to the merge condition.
  • Cluster/ZORDER target on the merge key.
  • Pre-dedup and minimize the source.
  • Deletion vectors / low-shuffle merge reduce rewrites.

Healthcare angle: Merging late claims into a billion-row Delta table is tractable only when scoped to affected service_months.

What strong answers cover

  1. Speed big MERGEs by constraining the target (partition/ZORDER/liquid cluster on the merge key), shrinking and pre-deduping the source, and sizing to avoid spill.
  2. Use replaceWhere/partition pruning in the match condition.
  3. Keep the join selective.
  4. Check files rewritten.
  5. Avoid whole-table rewrites.

Starter pattern

deltaT.merge(src, 't.key=s.key AND t.service_month IN (affected)')
  ...  # prune target; pre-dedup source; cluster on key
Common mistake: An unconstrained MERGE that rewrites the whole fact each run because the condition doesn't prune partitions.
Principal follow-up: How do you constrain a large Delta MERGE so it only rewrites affected partitions?
064
How would you implement vacuum compliance in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principalvacuum compliance

Model answer

Set VACUUM retention to satisfy both recovery (time travel) and data-retention/compliance requirements, then VACUUM to reclaim storage and (for right-to-delete) ensure purged data's files are removed. Never set retention below your recovery/legal needs.

  • Retention must cover time-travel recovery AND legal retention.
  • VACUUM reclaims storage of obsolete files.
  • For deletion mandates, ensure files are actually purged.
  • Document and govern the retention value.

Healthcare angle: Honoring a member data-deletion request means the underlying Delta files must eventually be VACUUMed, balanced against claims retention rules.

What strong answers cover

  1. VACUUM removes old files past retention; balance storage/cost against time-travel recovery, streaming reads, and regulatory/legal-hold needs.
  2. Keep retention >= recovery/streaming need; never RETAIN 0 in prod.
  3. Suspend purges for data under legal hold.
  4. Document retention.
  5. Guard VACUUM changes.

Starter pattern

VACUUM claims RETAIN 720 HOURS;  -- >= recovery/legal need
# suspend for legal-hold data; never RETAIN 0
Common mistake: Aggressive VACUUM that destroys time-travel recovery, breaks streams, or purges data under legal hold.
Principal follow-up: What must VACUUM retention respect for recovery, streaming, and legal holds?
065
How would you implement optimize write in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principaloptimize write

Model answer

optimizeWrite repartitions data before writing to produce fewer, appropriately-sized files, reducing the small-files problem at the source rather than fixing it later with OPTIMIZE. Combine with autoCompact for ongoing maintenance.

  • Repartitions pre-write for better file sizes.
  • Reduces small files proactively vs after-the-fact OPTIMIZE.
  • Pairs with autoCompact.
  • Especially useful for streaming/partitioned writes.

Healthcare angle: Enabling optimizeWrite on the streaming claims table curbs small files before they accumulate.

SET spark.databricks.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled = true;

What strong answers cover

  1. optimizeWrite rebalances data into right-sized files during the write, reducing small files without a separate compaction step.
  2. Enable on high-write tables (streaming sinks) to curb small files.
  3. Pairs with auto-compaction.
  4. Reduces the need for frequent OPTIMIZE.
  5. Mind the write-side overhead.

Starter pattern

spark.databricks.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled=true
spark.databricks.delta.autoCompact.enabled=true
Common mistake: Leaving optimizeWrite off on a streaming sink, accumulating small files that slow every downstream read.
Principal follow-up: How do optimizeWrite and auto-compaction reduce the small-file problem at write time?
066
How would you implement auto compaction in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principalauto compaction

Model answer

autoCompact automatically compacts small files after writes, keeping Delta tables read-efficient without scheduling manual OPTIMIZE for every write. Use it with optimizeWrite for streaming tables; still schedule ZORDER OPTIMIZE periodically for clustering.

  • Auto-compacts small files post-write.
  • Reduces manual OPTIMIZE burden for streaming.
  • Combine with optimizeWrite.
  • Periodic ZORDER OPTIMIZE still needed for clustering.

Healthcare angle: Streaming claims ingestion stays read-efficient with autoCompact, with periodic ZORDER for member-level access.

What strong answers cover

  1. Auto-compaction runs a small compaction after writes to coalesce small files automatically.
  2. Enable on streaming/high-write Delta tables; complements optimizeWrite.
  3. Reduces manual OPTIMIZE frequency.
  4. Small overhead per write.
  5. Monitor file sizes.

Starter pattern

spark.databricks.delta.autoCompact.enabled=true
# post-write compaction of small files
Common mistake: Relying solely on infrequent manual OPTIMIZE for a streaming sink, so small files pile up between runs.
Principal follow-up: When do you rely on auto-compaction versus scheduled OPTIMIZE for a streaming table?
067
How would you implement driver sizing in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principaldriver sizing

Model answer

Size the driver for orchestration, broadcast collection, and result handling — not for holding data. Bigger drivers help with large broadcasts and many tasks/partitions metadata, but the real fix for driver pressure is usually removing collect()/toPandas() and oversized broadcasts.

  • Driver handles scheduling, broadcasts, small results.
  • Large broadcasts/high partition counts strain it.
  • First remove collect()/toPandas() anti-patterns.
  • Size up only when genuinely needed.

Healthcare angle: If the claims job collects results to the driver, the fix is to stop collecting, not to buy a bigger driver.

What strong answers cover

  1. Size the driver for coordination and any (small) collected results — not for processing data, which belongs on executors.
  2. Driver OOM usually means collect/toPandas/oversized broadcast, not an undersized driver.
  3. Right-size only after removing driver-side data pulls.
  4. Mind partition-count metadata.
  5. Bigger driver isn't the fix for OOM.

Starter pattern

# driver coordinates; don't collect big data to it
# fix driver OOM by removing collect/toPandas, not just sizing up
Common mistake: Endlessly enlarging the driver to fix OOM that's really caused by collect()/toPandas() on large data.
Principal follow-up: When is a bigger driver justified versus removing driver-side data collection?
068
How would you implement shuffle tuning in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principalshuffle tuning

Model answer

Tune shuffles by minimizing wide ops, filtering/pre-aggregating before them, letting AQE coalesce/skew-handle partitions, and setting a sensible shuffle.partitions baseline. The biggest wins are reducing shuffled data and fixing skew, not just changing the partition count.

  • Reduce shuffled data: filter/pre-aggregate/broadcast first.
  • Let AQE coalesce small and split skewed partitions.
  • Baseline shuffle.partitions to cluster size/data volume.
  • Fix skew rather than only tuning counts.

Healthcare angle: A heavy claims aggregation shuffle improves most from pre-aggregating to member-month and enabling AQE, not from raw partition-count tweaks.

What strong answers cover

  1. Tune shuffles by enabling AQE (coalesces partitions), reducing data before wide ops, fixing skew, and right-sizing shuffle partitions.
  2. Large/uneven shuffle is the main cost — shrink it.
  3. Let AQE size post-shuffle partitions.
  4. Watch spill during shuffle.
  5. Diagnose in the Spark UI.

Starter pattern

spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true  # AQE coalesces shuffle partitions
# filter/pre-aggregate before wide ops; fix skew
Common mistake: Manually pinning a huge shuffle.partitions value and getting tiny tasks, instead of letting AQE coalesce.
Principal follow-up: How does AQE change shuffle tuning, and what reduces shuffle cost most?
069
How would you implement Spark config hygiene in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
PrincipalSpark config hygiene

Model answer

Keep Spark configs minimal, intentional, and version-controlled. Avoid cargo-culted settings; rely on AQE/Photon defaults, set only what your workload needs, document each override, and revisit them — stale configs cause mysterious regressions.

  • Minimal, documented, version-controlled overrides.
  • Trust modern AQE/Photon defaults; don't over-tune.
  • Each config has a reason and an owner.
  • Revisit configs as Spark/data evolve.

Healthcare angle: A claims job carrying dozens of copied configs is a regression risk; prune to the few that matter and document why.

What strong answers cover

  1. Keep Spark configs minimal, intentional, and documented; avoid cargo-culted settings that fight AQE or hide problems.
  2. Set configs for a reason; remove stale ones.
  3. Prefer modern defaults (AQE on).
  4. Don't over-tune; measure.
  5. Standardize via cluster policy.

Starter pattern

# enable AQE; set only justified configs; document why each exists
# avoid copy-pasted legacy tuning that fights the optimizer
Common mistake: Carrying a pile of copy-pasted legacy Spark configs that disable AQE or mask the real bottleneck.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep Spark config minimal and intentional rather than cargo-culted?
070
How would you implement healthcare cost analytics gold mart in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principalhealthcare cost analytics gold mart

Model answer

Build the cost mart at the grain analytics needs (member-month or claim-line aggregates), conform to member/provider/date dimensions, include allowed/paid/member-responsibility measures, and pre-compute common rollups (PMPM, trend). Make it restatable for late claims and reconciled to source.

  • Grain: member-month and/or claim-line aggregates.
  • Conformed dims; measures for allowed/paid/member-resp.
  • Pre-compute PMPM/trend; restatable for late claims.
  • Reconciled to source claims, with control totals.

Healthcare angle: The cost analytics gold mart powers PMPM and trend for actuaries; it must restate cleanly when late/retro claims arrive.

What strong answers cover

  1. A cost gold mart serves PMPM/trend at a clear grain (e.g. member-month) using allowed amounts, member-months, and IBNR-aware recent periods.
  2. Star schema with conformed dims; pre-aggregated for BI.
  3. Restatement-capable for late claims.
  4. Version-aware (no double-count).
  5. Documented definitions.

Starter pattern

# gold: member_month fact (allowed, paid, member_months)
# PMPM = SUM(allowed)/SUM(member_months); IBNR-adjust recent
Common mistake: Building a cost mart on paid-to-date with no IBNR or member-month rigor, so trend understates recent months.
Principal follow-up: What grain and adjustments make a claims cost gold mart trustworthy for actuarial use?
071
How would you implement actuarial consumption layer in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principalactuarial consumption layer

Model answer

Actuaries need stable grain, clear definitions, full history/restatement, and reproducibility (as-of snapshots) more than raw speed. Provide governed gold marts with documented metrics, incurred-vs-paid clarity, and the ability to reproduce a prior period exactly.

  • Stable, documented grain and metric definitions.
  • As-of/snapshot reproducibility for restatements.
  • Incurred vs paid vs IBNR clarity.
  • Governed, certified, reconciled.

Healthcare angle: An actuarial layer must let analysts reproduce last quarter's numbers exactly even after retro corrections — bitemporal/snapshotting matters.

What strong answers cover

  1. Serve actuaries a stable, governed, reproducible layer: defined metrics, bitemporal as-of history, incurred (paid+IBNR), trend decomposition, restatement.
  2. Prioritize correctness/auditability/reproducibility over speed.
  3. Reproduce any prior period exactly.
  4. Stable definitions.
  5. Document and govern.

Starter pattern

# bitemporal as-of history + incurred(=paid+IBNR) + decomposed trend
# reproducible prior periods after restatement
Common mistake: Serving actuaries fast but non-reproducible data, so they can't reproduce a prior period after restatements.
Principal follow-up: Why do actuaries need a reproducible, IBNR-aware consumption layer, and what design enables it?
072
How would you implement semantic layer integration in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principalsemantic layer integration

Model answer

A semantic layer centralizes metric definitions (PMPM, denial rate) so every tool computes them identically, preventing the same metric disagreeing across reports. Integrate it over governed gold marts and govern metric definitions as code.

  • Single source of metric definitions across BI tools.
  • Prevents 'two dashboards, two PMPMs' conflicts.
  • Sits over governed gold marts.
  • Govern metric definitions like code.

Healthcare angle: Defining PMPM once in a semantic layer stops finance and actuarial dashboards from quietly disagreeing.

What strong answers cover

  1. Expose gold marts through a semantic layer with one governed definition per metric (PMPM, denial rate) so every tool agrees.
  2. Central, versioned definitions over certified marts.
  3. Prevents metric drift across BI tools.
  4. Owned and change-controlled.
  5. Integrate with the BI tool's model.

Starter pattern

# semantic layer: PMPM defined once over gold; BI tools consume it
# governed, versioned definitions -> consistent numbers
Common mistake: Letting each BI report define PMPM itself, so finance and actuarial dashboards disagree with no authoritative definition.
Principal follow-up: How does a semantic layer over gold marts stop two dashboards from disagreeing on PMPM?
073
How would you implement Power BI serving in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
PrincipalPower BI serving

Model answer

Serve Power BI from well-modeled gold marts (star schema, right grain) via a tuned SQL Warehouse with scale-out concurrency, result caching, and aggregations/import where latency demands. Don't point Power BI at raw/huge tables; pre-aggregate and govern access.

  • Star-schema gold marts at BI-friendly grain.
  • Scale-out warehouse concurrency + result cache.
  • Pre-aggregate; consider import/aggregations for latency.
  • Govern PHI access at the warehouse, not the report.

Healthcare angle: Executive claims dashboards hit pre-aggregated gold marts via a concurrency-scaled warehouse, not the raw claim-line fact.

What strong answers cover

  1. Serve Power BI from a concurrency-scaled SQL warehouse over pre-aggregated star-schema gold marts, governing PHI at the data layer.
  2. Scale out for many users; result caching; import vs DirectQuery tradeoff.
  3. Don't point BI at raw facts.
  4. Pre-aggregate hot rollups.
  5. Mask PHI before serving.

Starter pattern

# Power BI -> SQL warehouse (multi-cluster) -> gold marts (pre-aggregated)
# DirectQuery vs import per freshness/volume; PHI masked
Common mistake: Pointing Power BI at the raw billion-row claim fact, causing slow visuals and queuing instead of serving gold marts.
Principal follow-up: How would you serve Power BI for many concurrent users without pointing it at raw facts?
074
How would you implement SLA-driven workflow design in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
PrincipalSLA-driven workflow design

Model answer

Design the workflow backward from the consumer SLA: required ready-time → load deadlines → upstream dependency latencies, with predictive alerting before breach, prioritized critical path, and parallelism where it buys time. Build slack and a degraded-mode plan for peak days.

  • Work backward from consumer ready-time to task deadlines.
  • Predictive breach alerts; prioritize the critical path.
  • Parallelize independent tasks; add slack for peaks.
  • Degraded-mode/partial-publish plan for bad days.

Healthcare angle: If actuarial marts must be ready by 7am, the claims DAG is timed and alerted so a slow upstream feed triggers action by 5am.

What strong answers cover

  1. Design the workflow backward from the consumer deadline, accounting for upstream latency, with predictive alerting and dependency gating.
  2. Gate publish on reconciliation; alert before breach.
  3. Size compute for the critical path.
  4. Track SLA attainment.
  5. Degraded-mode plan for late upstream.

Starter pattern

# deadline 7am -> work backward: source by 3am, silver 4am, gold 6am
# predictive alert at risk; gate publish on recon
Common mistake: Designing the workflow forward from when data happens to arrive, then missing the consumer deadline with no early warning.
Principal follow-up: How do you design a claims workflow backward from a 7am actuarial deadline with predictive alerting?
075
How would you implement platform migration roadmap in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Principalplatform migration roadmap

Model answer

Sequence a migration to reduce risk: inventory and prioritize workloads, run old/new in parallel with reconciliation, migrate by domain in waves, validate with control totals and consumer sign-off, then decommission. Lead with measurable value and keep rollback available at each wave.

  • Inventory/prioritize; migrate in domain waves.
  • Parallel-run + reconcile old vs new before cutover.
  • Consumer validation/sign-off per wave; keep rollback.
  • Decommission only after sustained parity.

Healthcare angle: Migrating claims from a legacy warehouse to the lakehouse runs both in parallel with daily reconciliation until numbers match for several cycles.

What strong answers cover

  1. Sequence migration to reduce risk: inventory/prioritize, migrate by domain in waves, parallel-run with reconciliation, consumer sign-off, keep rollback, decommission after parity.
  2. Lead with measurable value; communicate.
  3. Reconciliation-until-parity is the key discipline.
  4. Sequence by value/risk.
  5. Don't big-bang.

Starter pattern

# inventory -> migrate by domain wave -> parallel-run + reconcile to parity
# consumer sign-off + rollback -> decommission
Common mistake: A big-bang cutover with no parallel-run reconciliation, so the new platform's numbers aren't trusted and adoption stalls.
Principal follow-up: How would you sequence migrating a claims platform to Databricks to keep consumers' trust throughout?

Snowflake Mastery

Streams, Tasks, Dynamic Tables, Snowpipe, micro-partitions, Query Profile and warehouse operations.

75 questions
001
How would you implement Snowflake warehouse sizing in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumSnowflake warehouse sizing

Model answer

Warehouse size sets compute per cluster (credits/hour roughly doubles per size up). Size up to speed a single heavy query (more memory, less spill); scale out (multi-cluster) for concurrency. Start small, watch Query Profile for spill, and use auto-suspend/auto-resume to avoid paying for idle.

  • Size up for single heavy queries; multi-cluster out for concurrency.
  • Each size up ~doubles credits/hour — justify with spill/runtime data.
  • Auto-suspend (e.g. 60s) + auto-resume to cut idle cost.
  • Separate warehouses by workload to isolate cost/contention.

Healthcare angle: A month-end claims aggregation that spills wants a bigger warehouse; many concurrent BI users want multi-cluster.

What strong answers cover

  1. Size up for heavy single queries; scale out (multi-cluster) for concurrency/queuing.
  2. Separate ETL and BI onto different warehouses to avoid contention.
  3. Set auto-suspend/auto-resume to cut idle cost.
  4. Right-size from query profile, not guesswork.
  5. Bigger isn't always faster if the query is the problem.

Starter pattern

ALTER WAREHOUSE bi_wh SET WAREHOUSE_SIZE='LARGE'
  MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT=1 MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT=4 AUTO_SUSPEND=60;
Common mistake: Sizing UP a warehouse to fix a concurrency/queuing problem instead of scaling OUT with multi-cluster.
Principal follow-up: When do you scale up vs out a Snowflake warehouse, and how do you decide the ETL/BI split?
002
How would you implement Query Profile interpretation in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumQuery Profile interpretation

Model answer

Query Profile shows operator-level time, rows, partitions scanned vs total (pruning), and bytes spilled. Read it to find the dominant operator, confirm pruning worked, and spot spill or exploding joins. It is the primary Snowflake tuning tool.

  • Find the most expensive operator and why.
  • Partitions scanned vs total = pruning effectiveness.
  • Spill to local/remote storage = under-sized warehouse or too much data.
  • Row explosion between operators = a fan-out join.

Healthcare angle: A slow claims query showing partitions scanned = total means pruning failed, often a function on the filter column.

What strong answers cover

  1. Read the Query Profile to find the dominant operator, partitions scanned vs total (pruning), spilling, and exploding joins.
  2. Bytes spilled to local/remote storage signals memory pressure.
  3. Most time in a TableScan with full partitions = lost pruning.
  4. Trace cost to one operator.
  5. Target the bottleneck.

Starter pattern

-- Query Profile: partitions scanned vs total, bytes spilled,
-- most-expensive node, exploding join row counts
Common mistake: Guessing at slowness instead of reading the Query Profile to find the actual bottleneck (pruning/spill/join).
Principal follow-up: Walk through diagnosing a slow claims query from the Snowflake Query Profile.
003
How would you implement micro-partition pruning in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediummicro-partition pruning

Model answer

Snowflake stores data in immutable micro-partitions with per-column min/max metadata; queries skip partitions whose ranges cannot match the filter. Pruning is automatic but depends on clustering on the filter column and sargable predicates.

  • Min/max metadata per micro-partition enables skipping.
  • Effective when data is clustered on the filtered column.
  • Functions/casts on the column defeat pruning.
  • Check partitions scanned in Query Profile.

Healthcare angle: Claims roughly clustered by service date prune well when you filter service_dt with literal ranges.

What strong answers cover

  1. Snowflake prunes micro-partitions via per-partition min/max metadata; sargable filters on well-clustered columns prune the most.
  2. Functions/casts on the filter column or poor clustering defeat pruning.
  3. Check partitions scanned vs total in the profile.
  4. Cluster by common filter columns.
  5. Sargable predicates.

Starter pattern

-- good: WHERE service_dt BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-31'
-- bad: WHERE TO_CHAR(service_dt)='2025-01' (no pruning)
Common mistake: Wrapping the filter column in a function so min/max pruning is defeated and the whole table scans.
Principal follow-up: What enables and what defeats micro-partition pruning on a large claims table?
004
How would you implement clustering keys in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumclustering keys

Model answer

A clustering key keeps a large table organized by chosen columns so pruning stays effective as data grows; automatic clustering maintains it in the background at a credit cost. Use only on large tables with selective filters not already well-ordered.

  • Define on large tables where natural order degraded.
  • Pick columns most used in selective filters/joins.
  • Automatic clustering maintains it (costs credits).
  • Do not cluster small tables or low-selectivity columns.

Healthcare angle: A multi-billion-row claims fact filtered by service_month benefits from clustering once natural ordering degrades.

ALTER TABLE claims CLUSTER BY (service_month);

What strong answers cover

  1. Define a clustering key on high-cardinality columns frequently filtered, so micro-partitions co-locate for pruning.
  2. Only worth it on very large tables with selective queries.
  3. Monitor clustering depth; automatic clustering maintains it (at a cost).
  4. Choose keys from real query patterns.
  5. Don't over-cluster.

Starter pattern

ALTER TABLE claims CLUSTER BY (service_month, member_id);
SELECT SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION('claims');
Common mistake: Adding a clustering key on a column nobody filters on, paying reclustering cost for no pruning benefit.
Principal follow-up: How do you choose a clustering key, and how do you know it's actually helping?
005
How would you implement automatic clustering cost in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumautomatic clustering cost

Model answer

Automatic clustering consumes credits to continuously re-cluster as data changes, so it pays off only when read savings exceed maintenance cost. Monitor clustering credits and depth; pause it where churn makes it uneconomical.

  • Background re-clustering costs ongoing credits.
  • Justify with read savings vs maintenance cost.
  • Monitor SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION and credit usage.
  • High-churn tables may not be worth it.

Healthcare angle: Auto-clustering a frequently-rewritten claims staging table can cost more than it saves; reserve it for stable large facts.

What strong answers cover

  1. Automatic clustering reclusters in the background and consumes credits; balance pruning benefit against ongoing cost.
  2. High-churn tables cost more to keep clustered.
  3. Monitor reclustering credits; suspend if not worth it.
  4. Consider clustering only the hot partitions.
  5. Measure benefit vs cost.

Starter pattern

-- monitor AUTOMATIC_CLUSTERING_HISTORY credits
ALTER TABLE claims SUSPEND RECLUSTER;  -- if cost > benefit
Common mistake: Leaving automatic clustering on a high-churn table where reclustering credits exceed the query savings.
Principal follow-up: How do you decide whether automatic clustering is worth its credit cost?
006
How would you implement search optimization service in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumsearch optimization service

Model answer

Search Optimization builds a maintained access path for selective point and equality/IN lookups on high-cardinality columns, speeding needle-in-haystack queries pruning alone cannot help. It has maintenance cost, so apply per-column where it pays.

  • Speeds selective point/equality lookups on high-cardinality columns.
  • Complements clustering, not a replacement.
  • Maintenance cost — apply selectively.
  • Great for ad-hoc lookups by member_id/claim_id.

Healthcare angle: Finding one member or claim in a huge fact is a Search Optimization use case.

ALTER TABLE claims ADD SEARCH OPTIMIZATION ON EQUALITY(member_id, claim_id);

What strong answers cover

  1. Search Optimization Service speeds highly selective point lookups (e.g. find one member/claim) by maintaining a search index.
  2. Costs storage/credits to maintain; best for needle-in-haystack equality/IN lookups.
  3. Complements clustering (range) for point queries.
  4. Enable per table/column.
  5. Measure lookup speedup vs cost.

Starter pattern

ALTER TABLE claims ADD SEARCH OPTIMIZATION ON EQUALITY(member_id);
Common mistake: Enabling Search Optimization on a table only ever scanned in ranges (no point lookups), paying for unused index.
Principal follow-up: When does Search Optimization beat clustering, and what's the cost tradeoff?
007
How would you implement materialized views in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediummaterialized views

Model answer

Materialized views precompute and auto-maintain results for expensive, frequent aggregations, with automatic query rewrite to use them. They cost storage and maintenance credits and have restrictions (limited joins) — use for hot, narrow aggregates; dynamic tables for richer pipelines.

  • Precomputed + auto-maintained; automatic rewrite.
  • Maintenance credits + storage cost.
  • Join restrictions — check applicability.
  • Dynamic tables are often the more flexible choice.

Healthcare angle: A hot daily denial-rate aggregate fits an MV; complex multi-join marts fit dynamic tables.

What strong answers cover

  1. Materialized views precompute and auto-maintain a query result for fast reads; Snowflake keeps them fresh on the base table's changes.
  2. Good for expensive, frequently-queried aggregations with limited query shapes.
  3. Maintenance consumes credits; restrictions apply (no joins in some cases).
  4. Consider Dynamic Tables for complex pipelines.
  5. Weigh refresh cost.

Starter pattern

CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW mv_paid AS
  SELECT service_month, SUM(paid) FROM claims GROUP BY 1;
Common mistake: Using a materialized view for a complex multi-join pipeline it can't support, or paying maintenance for a rarely-read MV.
Principal follow-up: When is a materialized view the right choice versus a Dynamic Table?
008
How would you implement dynamic tables in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumdynamic tables

Model answer

Dynamic tables declaratively define a target as a query over sources with a TARGET_LAG; Snowflake incrementally refreshes them and chains them into pipelines, replacing much manual stream+task plumbing and supporting joins MVs cannot.

  • Declarative target query + TARGET_LAG; auto incremental refresh.
  • Chain into a DAG of dynamic tables.
  • More flexible than MVs (joins/transforms).
  • Tune lag vs cost; monitor refresh.

Healthcare angle: A claims silver to gold pipeline can be chained dynamic tables with a few-minute lag, replacing hand-built streams/tasks.

CREATE DYNAMIC TABLE claims_silver TARGET_LAG='10 minutes' WAREHOUSE=wh AS
SELECT ... FROM raw_claims QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER(...)=1;

What strong answers cover

  1. Dynamic Tables declaratively maintain a transformed result incrementally to a target lag — declarative pipelines in SQL.
  2. Set a realistic target lag the warehouse can sustain; chain them for silver->gold.
  3. Snowflake manages incremental refresh and dependencies.
  4. Monitor refresh history/lag.
  5. Alternative to streams+tasks.

Starter pattern

CREATE DYNAMIC TABLE dt_gold TARGET_LAG='1 hour' WAREHOUSE=etl AS
  SELECT ... FROM silver_claims;
Common mistake: Setting an aggressive target lag the warehouse can't sustain, so the dynamic table is always behind.
Principal follow-up: How do you balance a dynamic table's target lag against warehouse cost and query complexity?
009
How would you implement streams and tasks in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumstreams and tasks

Model answer

A Stream tracks table changes (a CDC offset); a Task runs scheduled SQL, often consuming a stream to MERGE changes downstream. Together they build incremental pipelines, and tasks chain into dependency DAGs.

  • Stream = change tracking; Task = scheduled SQL.
  • Task consumes stream via MERGE to apply deltas.
  • Tasks chain with AFTER into DAGs.
  • Dynamic tables now cover many stream+task cases declaratively.

Healthcare angle: A stream on raw claims feeds a task that MERGEs changes into silver on schedule.

CREATE TASK load_silver SCHEDULE='5 MINUTE' AS
MERGE INTO silver t USING claims_stream s ON t.k=s.k WHEN ...;

What strong answers cover

  1. Streams capture table changes (CDC); tasks run scheduled/triggered SQL — together they build incremental pipelines.
  2. A task consumes a stream (MERGE changes) on a schedule or after a predecessor.
  3. Streams go stale past retention if unconsumed.
  4. Idempotent MERGE on the stream.
  5. Dynamic Tables now cover many cases.

Starter pattern

CREATE STREAM s_claims ON TABLE claims;
CREATE TASK t AS MERGE INTO tgt USING s_claims ...;
Common mistake: Letting a stream go unconsumed past retention so it stales and silently misses changes.
Principal follow-up: How do streams and tasks build an incremental pipeline, and when would you use Dynamic Tables instead?
010
How would you implement Snowpipe in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumSnowpipe

Model answer

Snowpipe continuously and serverlessly loads files as they land in a stage (event-triggered or REST), giving low-latency ingestion without managing a warehouse. It tracks loaded files to avoid duplicates; use COPY for bulk batch loads.

  • Serverless, event-driven micro-batch file loading.
  • Load history prevents duplicate ingestion.
  • Low latency vs scheduled COPY.
  • Pairs with external stage + notifications.

Healthcare angle: Inbound claim/eligibility files auto-load via Snowpipe as they arrive, with load history preventing duplicates.

What strong answers cover

  1. Snowpipe continuously loads files as they land (event-driven) for low-latency ingestion, billed per compute used.
  2. Use for steadily-arriving files; COPY for bulk/batch.
  3. Idempotent file tracking prevents reloads.
  4. Pair with downstream streams/tasks.
  5. Monitor load latency/errors.

Starter pattern

CREATE PIPE p AS COPY INTO claims FROM @stage FILE_FORMAT=(TYPE=CSV);
-- auto-ingest on file arrival (event notification)
Common mistake: Using Snowpipe for huge bulk historical loads where a batch COPY would be cheaper and simpler.
Principal follow-up: When does Snowpipe beat a scheduled COPY, and how does it avoid reloading files?
011
How would you implement external stages in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumexternal stages

Model answer

An external stage references cloud storage via a storage integration for secure, keyless access, used by COPY/Snowpipe. Govern access through the integration and roles; organize paths by source for clean COPY patterns.

  • Points to cloud storage via storage integration (no inline keys).
  • Used by COPY INTO and Snowpipe.
  • Govern with roles; organize by source.
  • Define file formats for parsing.

Healthcare angle: The 837 landing bucket is an external stage that COPY/Snowpipe load into raw claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. External stages reference cloud storage (S3/ADLS/GCS) via a storage integration (no inline keys) for loading/unloading.
  2. Govern access centrally; organize by source/zone.
  3. Used by COPY and Snowpipe.
  4. Secure credentials via integration.
  5. Audit access to PHI storage.

Starter pattern

CREATE STAGE claims_stage URL='s3://...'
  STORAGE_INTEGRATION=claims_int FILE_FORMAT=ff_837;
Common mistake: Embedding cloud access keys directly in the stage instead of a governed storage integration.
Principal follow-up: How do storage integrations keep external-stage access secure and centrally governed?
012
How would you implement COPY INTO pattern in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumCOPY INTO pattern

Model answer

COPY INTO bulk-loads staged files with a file format, error handling (ON_ERROR), and load metadata that prevents reloading the same file. Use VALIDATION_MODE to test, pattern-match files, and capture rejects for quarantine.

  • Bulk file to table load with file format + error handling.
  • Load history prevents duplicate file loads.
  • ON_ERROR + rejected-row capture for quarantine.
  • VALIDATION_MODE to dry-run before loading.

Healthcare angle: Nightly claim batches load via COPY INTO with ON_ERROR='CONTINUE' and reject capture for malformed rows.

COPY INTO claims FROM @stage FILE_FORMAT=(TYPE=CSV) ON_ERROR='CONTINUE';

What strong answers cover

  1. COPY INTO bulk-loads files from a stage with a file format, validation, and error handling.
  2. Use ON_ERROR and VALIDATION_MODE; load metadata prevents reloading the same file.
  3. Idempotent by file tracking; reconcile loaded vs expected.
  4. Quarantine rejected rows.
  5. Tie to file control totals.

Starter pattern

COPY INTO claims FROM @stage FILE_FORMAT=ff_837
  ON_ERROR='CONTINUE';  -- then inspect rejected rows
Common mistake: Using ON_ERROR='ABORT' so one bad row fails the whole file, or ignoring rejected rows entirely.
Principal follow-up: How do you load good rows while capturing rejects, and avoid reloading the same file?
013
How would you implement file format errors in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumfile format errors

Model answer

Format errors (wrong delimiter, extra columns, bad dates, encoding) cause COPY failures or silent misparsing. Define an explicit file format, use ON_ERROR/VALIDATION_MODE, capture rejects, and assert column counts/trailer to catch malformed files early.

  • Explicit FILE FORMAT (delimiter, quoting, date/encoding).
  • ON_ERROR + reject capture instead of failing the whole load.
  • Validate column counts/trailer records.
  • Quarantine and alert on format breaks.

Healthcare angle: An 837/CSV with an extra delimiter or missing trailer should quarantine, not silently shift columns and corrupt claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. Diagnose load errors via VALIDATION_MODE and COPY history (bad delimiters, encoding, column count, date formats).
  2. Quarantine bad rows; fix the file format definition; reconcile.
  3. A shifted delimiter corrupts columns silently if not validated.
  4. Assert column counts.
  5. Push format fixes upstream.

Starter pattern

COPY INTO claims ... VALIDATION_MODE='RETURN_ERRORS';
-- inspect errors; fix FILE_FORMAT; quarantine bad rows
Common mistake: Loading rows where a delimiter shifted columns so member_id lands in paid_amt, with no validation.
Principal follow-up: How does VALIDATION_MODE catch a malformed file before it corrupts claims?
014
How would you implement Time Travel recovery in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
MediumTime Travel recovery

Model answer

Time Travel lets you query or restore prior state within retention (up to 90 days on Enterprise), enabling recovery from bad loads via AT/BEFORE or UNDROP. Beyond retention, Fail-safe is a Snowflake-managed last resort.

  • Query AT/BEFORE a timestamp/offset; CLONE or restore.
  • UNDROP recovers dropped objects within retention.
  • Set DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS to your recovery need.
  • Fail-safe is a 7-day support-only backstop.

Healthcare angle: Recover claims after a bad MERGE by cloning the table BEFORE the offending statement.

CREATE TABLE claims_fix CLONE claims BEFORE (STATEMENT => '<bad_query_id>');

What strong answers cover

  1. Time Travel lets you query or restore a table to a prior point within the retention window (1 day default, up to 90 on Enterprise).
  2. Recover from a bad load via AT/BEFORE or UNDROP; reconcile after.
  3. Set retention to your recovery window.
  4. Fail-safe is a separate 7-day Snowflake-only backstop.
  5. Prefer atomic publish to avoid needing it.

Starter pattern

SELECT * FROM claims AT(OFFSET => -3600);
CREATE TABLE claims_restored CLONE claims BEFORE(STATEMENT=>'<id>');
Common mistake: Setting retention to 1 day on a critical table, then discovering corruption after the window has passed.
Principal follow-up: How do you size Time Travel retention, and how does Fail-safe differ?
015
How would you implement zero-copy clone in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumzero-copy clone

Model answer

CLONE creates an instant, storage-free copy that shares micro-partitions until either side changes (copy-on-write). Use it for test/dev from prod, pre-change snapshots, and safe what-if work without duplicating storage.

  • Instant metadata clone; shares partitions until modified.
  • No upfront storage cost (copy-on-write).
  • Great for dev/test and pre-change snapshots.
  • Divergence accrues its own storage.

Healthcare angle: Clone the claims database to a lower environment instantly for testing, with no storage duplication until edits.

CREATE DATABASE claims_dev CLONE claims_prod;

What strong answers cover

  1. Zero-copy clone instantly creates a metadata-only copy (no storage duplication) for dev/test or blue-green deploys.
  2. Clones diverge as they're modified (copy-on-write).
  3. Use a validated clone for instant rollback via SWAP.
  4. Great for environment provisioning.
  5. Cheap and fast.

Starter pattern

CREATE TABLE claims_dev CLONE claims_prod;  -- instant, no copy
ALTER TABLE prod SWAP WITH prod_validated;   -- blue-green
Common mistake: Physically copying large tables for dev/test when a zero-copy clone is instant and storage-free.
Principal follow-up: How do clones plus SWAP give you instant dev provisioning and zero-downtime deploys?
016
How would you implement fail-safe limits in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumfail-safe limits

Model answer

Fail-safe is a 7-day, Snowflake-administered recovery period after Time Travel expires — not self-service, only via support, for disaster recovery. Do not rely on it operationally; set Time Travel retention to your real recovery needs.

  • 7-day, support-only recovery after Time Travel ends.
  • Not queryable/self-service; DR backstop only.
  • Set Time Travel retention for operational recovery.
  • Adds storage cost for the period.

Healthcare angle: Do not plan claims recovery around fail-safe; set Time Travel retention long enough to self-recover bad loads.

What strong answers cover

  1. Fail-safe is a 7-day, Snowflake-managed, non-queryable recovery backstop after Time Travel expires — recoverable only via support.
  2. It's not a substitute for backups or Time Travel; you can't self-serve it.
  3. Plan recovery around Time Travel retention, not Fail-safe.
  4. Incurs storage cost.
  5. Last resort only.

Starter pattern

-- Fail-safe: 7 days after Time Travel, support-only recovery
-- design recovery around Time Travel, not Fail-safe
Common mistake: Relying on Fail-safe as a recovery plan, when it's support-only and not queryable.
Principal follow-up: Why can't Fail-safe be your recovery plan, and what should you rely on instead?
017
How would you implement result cache in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediumresult cache

Model answer

The result cache returns prior results for 24 hours when the query text and underlying data are unchanged, at no compute cost. It speeds repeated identical BI queries; any data or query change invalidates it.

  • Reuses identical-query results for 24h, free of compute.
  • Invalidated by data or query-text changes.
  • Helps repeated dashboard queries.
  • Works even while the warehouse is suspended.

Healthcare angle: Repeated identical executive claims-dashboard queries hit the result cache until the mart refreshes.

What strong answers cover

  1. Snowflake's result cache returns identical query results for 24h without recompute (if data unchanged), at no warehouse cost.
  2. Benefits repeated identical BI queries.
  3. Invalidated by underlying data changes or non-deterministic functions.
  4. Free performance when applicable.
  5. Distinct from warehouse/metadata cache.

Starter pattern

-- identical query + unchanged data -> served from result cache (no compute)
-- broken by CURRENT_TIMESTAMP() etc.
Common mistake: Putting non-deterministic functions in dashboards, defeating the result cache and recomputing every time.
Principal follow-up: What invalidates the result cache, and how do you write queries to benefit from it?
018
How would you implement metadata cache in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Mediummetadata cache

Model answer

Snowflake answers some queries purely from metadata (COUNT(*), MIN/MAX, row counts) without scanning data, returning instantly. This explains why certain aggregates are near-free while others scan.

  • COUNT(*)/MIN/MAX-type queries served from metadata.
  • No warehouse compute needed.
  • Explains instant results on some aggregates.
  • Distinct from result and warehouse caches.

Healthcare angle: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM claims returns instantly from metadata, a cheap volume check.

What strong answers cover

  1. Snowflake serves some queries (e.g. COUNT, MIN/MAX) from micro-partition metadata without scanning data — instant and free.
  2. Leverage it for cheap counts/stats on large tables.
  3. Works because Snowflake tracks per-partition stats.
  4. Distinct from result cache.
  5. Useful for monitoring/reconciliation.

Starter pattern

SELECT COUNT(*), MAX(service_dt) FROM claims;
-- answered from metadata, no scan
Common mistake: Assuming every aggregate needs a full scan, when metadata answers counts/min/max instantly.
Principal follow-up: Which queries does Snowflake answer from metadata alone, and why is that useful for monitoring?
019
How would you implement warehouse cache in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedwarehouse cache

Model answer

A running warehouse caches recently-read micro-partition data locally, speeding re-reads of the same data; suspending clears it. This is why the first query after resume is slower and why keeping a warehouse warm helps latency-sensitive workloads.

  • Local data cache speeds re-reads on a running warehouse.
  • Cleared on suspend; cold after resume.
  • Balance keep-warm latency vs idle credits.
  • Distinct from result/metadata caches.

Healthcare angle: Frequent claims queries benefit from the warehouse cache; aggressive auto-suspend trades latency for cost.

What strong answers cover

  1. A running warehouse caches recently-read data locally, speeding repeated queries; suspending clears it (cold start next time).
  2. Balance aggressive auto-suspend (cost) against cache warmth (latency).
  3. Distinct from result/metadata cache.
  4. Tune suspend time deliberately.
  5. Warm cache helps iterative analysis.

Starter pattern

ALTER WAREHOUSE wh SET AUTO_SUSPEND=300;  -- keep cache warm longer
-- vs 60 to save idle cost (colder cache)
Common mistake: Setting auto-suspend to 0/very long 'to stay warm', paying for hours of idle to save seconds of cold start.
Principal follow-up: How do you balance auto-suspend against warehouse-cache warmth for a latency-sensitive workload?
020
How would you implement multi-cluster warehouse in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedmulti-cluster warehouse

Model answer

A multi-cluster warehouse auto-adds clusters to clear queued queries, scaling out for concurrency rather than up for single-query speed. Set min/max clusters and a scaling policy; it is for many simultaneous users, not faster individual queries.

  • Auto-adds clusters to clear queues (concurrency).
  • Min/max clusters + scaling policy.
  • Scale OUT for concurrency, UP for single-query speed.
  • Economy vs standard policy trades cost vs responsiveness.

Healthcare angle: Hundreds of concurrent analysts on claims marts need a multi-cluster warehouse to avoid queuing.

What strong answers cover

  1. A multi-cluster warehouse adds clusters automatically to handle concurrency, removing queuing for many simultaneous users.
  2. Scale OUT for concurrency; scale UP (size) for heavy single queries.
  3. Set min/max clusters and scaling policy.
  4. Ideal for BI with many users.
  5. Cost scales with active clusters.

Starter pattern

ALTER WAREHOUSE bi SET MIN_CLUSTER_COUNT=1 MAX_CLUSTER_COUNT=5
  SCALING_POLICY='STANDARD';
Common mistake: Sizing a single-cluster warehouse up to fix queuing, when multi-cluster scale-out is the right lever.
Principal follow-up: Why does multi-cluster solve concurrency where a bigger single warehouse doesn't?
021
How would you implement resource monitors in production for healthcare claims/member/provider data?
Advancedresource monitors

Model answer

Resource monitors cap credit consumption per warehouse/account over a period, with notify/suspend actions at thresholds, preventing runaway cost. Use tiered actions: warn early, suspend at the hard cap.
