  5. State what would change the design (volume, latency, batch vs stream).

Starter pattern

sources(837/835/SFTP/API) -> ingest(idempotent, validated)
 -> bronze(raw Delta) -> silver(conform+dedup) -> gold(PMPM/denial)
 + reconcile + DQ/quarantine + lineage + PHI governance
Common mistake: Jumping to tools/boxes without clarifying scale/SLA/PHI or addressing idempotency, reconciliation, and late data.
Principal follow-up: Walk me through the failure modes (duplicate file, late membership, skew) and how each is handled.
002
Design or evaluate a solution for membership eligibility platform. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediummembership eligibility platform

Model answer

Model eligibility as effective-dated coverage spans (SCD2/bitemporal), ingest enrollment feeds incrementally, support retro corrections with restatement, and derive member-months against a calendar spine. Serve as-of eligibility for claim adjudication and analytics, with reconciliation to source.

  • Effective-dated coverage spans (SCD2/bitemporal).
  • Retro corrections → restate affected periods.
  • Member-months via calendar spine.
  • As-of eligibility serving + reconciliation.

Healthcare angle: Retro eligibility shifts member-months and capitation; bitemporal modeling keeps restatements auditable.

What strong answers cover

  1. Eligibility is bitemporal: coverage validity dates plus knowledge time, to handle 45-day retro corrections.
  2. Model effective-dated spans; compute member-months; restate dependents on retro change.
  3. Support as-of eligibility lookups for adjudication/analytics.
  4. Reconcile to source; gate dependents if missing.
  5. The hard part is retro correctness and restatement.

Starter pattern

membership(valid_from/to, system_from/to) -> member_months
 as-of-eligibility view; on retro change -> recompute + restate PMPM
Common mistake: Single-time-axis eligibility, so a retro termination can't be reproduced and member-months silently go wrong.
Principal follow-up: How does a 45-day retro termination flow through member-months and PMPM, and how do you restate?
003
Design or evaluate a solution for provider master platform. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediumprovider master platform

Model answer

Build a governed provider MDM: ingest roster feeds, resolve identities (NPI + crosswalks), SCD2 attributes, model many-to-many group/network affiliations via bridges, handle late/soft-deleted providers, and serve a conformed provider dimension with as-of network status.

  • Identity resolution (NPI + crosswalks).
  • SCD2 attributes; bridges for group/network.
  • Late-arriving + soft-delete handling.
  • Conformed dimension with as-of network status.

Healthcare angle: Provider network/par status must be queried as-of service date for correct pricing and attribution.

What strong answers cover

  1. Build a conformed, identity-resolved provider dimension (NPI + crosswalks), effective-dated, with group/network bridges.
  2. Handle retroactive affiliation changes via as-of joins; manage many-to-many group/network.
  3. Integrate roster, taxonomy, geography.
  4. Late-arriving providers get placeholders, enriched later.
  5. Identity resolution and effective-dating are the hard parts.

Starter pattern

provider_dim(npi, effective-dated) + group/network bridge + crosswalk
 as-of join on service_dt; placeholder for late NPIs
Common mistake: Using current affiliation (not as-of) so historical attribution and network views are wrong after a change.
Principal follow-up: How do effective-dating and affiliation bridges keep historical attribution correct?
004
Design or evaluate a solution for pharmacy claims platform. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediumpharmacy claims platform

Model answer

Ingest NCPDP point-of-sale Rx claims (near-real-time), key by NDC with RxNorm normalization, model fills (days supply, quantity, fill date, DAW), and serve adherence (PDC/MPR) and drug-cost analytics — structurally separate from the medical claims model.

  • NCPDP near-real-time; NDC keyed, RxNorm normalized.
  • Model fills: days supply, quantity, DAW.
  • Serve adherence (PDC) and drug-cost analytics.
  • Separate from medical claims model.

Healthcare angle: Adherence and pharmacy spend come from NCPDP Rx claims grouped via RxNorm, not the medical claims model.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model NCPDP pharmacy claims separately from medical, with RxNorm drug grouping.
  2. Handle fills, reversals, rebills; compute days-supply/overlaps for adherence (PDC).
  3. Conform to a shared member/provider view.
  4. Support Stars/clinical consumption.
  5. Accurate fill processing and drug grouping are essential.

Starter pattern

NCPDP fills -> normalize(RxNorm) -> handle reversals/rebills
 -> adherence(PDC) + cost; conform member/provider
Common mistake: Treating pharmacy like medical claims or mishandling reversals, corrupting cost and adherence measures.
Principal follow-up: How do reversals, rebills, and overlapping fills complicate a pharmacy platform?
005
Design or evaluate a solution for 835 remittance platform. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Medium835 remittance platform

Model answer

Ingest 835s idempotently keyed on remittance identity (payer+check/EFT+remit id), parse CLP/SVC/CAS into claim/line/adjustment facts, reconcile SVC→CLP→trailer and to claims, and serve AR aging and denial analytics — with duplicate-835 detection front and center.

  • Idempotent load keyed on remittance identity.
  • Parse CLP/SVC/CAS into facts.
  • Reconcile SVC→CLP→trailer and to claims.
  • Duplicate-835 detection protects AR.

Healthcare angle: A duplicate 835 corrupts AR; identity-based idempotent loading is the core control.

What strong answers cover

  1. Process 835 remittances with strict remittance identity (payer + check/EFT + remit id) for idempotency.
  2. Apply payments/adjustments to AR; handle CARC/RARC denial mapping; reconcile paid totals.
  3. Detect duplicate files; back out and restate on error.
  4. Payment integrity is paramount.
  5. Version-aware, reconciled.

Starter pattern

835 -> idempotent load(MERGE on remit_key) -> apply to AR
 + CARC/RARC mapping + reconcile paid; dup detection
Common mistake: Non-idempotent payment loads, so a re-sent 835 double-applies payments and corrupts AR.
Principal follow-up: What uniquely identifies a remittance, and how do you recover AR after a duplicate posts?
006
Design or evaluate a solution for 837 ingestion and validation. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Medium837 ingestion and validation

Model answer

Parse 837P/I/D segments into claim header/line facts, validate structure and codes (quarantine bad rows), dedup versions by frequency code, conform keys, and reconcile counts/dollars to trailers and downstream. Make it idempotent against resent files.

  • Parse 837P/I/D into header/line facts.
  • Validate structure/codes; quarantine bad rows.
  • Dedup by frequency code; conform keys.
  • Reconcile to trailers; idempotent on replays.

Healthcare angle: Resent 837 files must be deduplicated while preserving legitimate replacements (frequency 7).

What strong answers cover

  1. Ingest 837 (P/I/D) with structural validation (segments, loops, trailers) and business DQ.
  2. Quarantine bad rows; load valid ones; map to a conformed claim-line model.
  3. Handle versions (frequency 7/8) and dedup on natural grain.
  4. Reconcile to file control totals.
  5. Keep raw for replay.

Starter pattern

837 -> structural validate(loops/trailer) -> parse -> DQ/quarantine
 -> conform claim-line(dedup, version-aware) -> reconcile
Common mistake: Failing the whole file on a few bad claims, or loading structurally-invalid claims that corrupt downstream.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep good claims loading while quarantining malformed ones, and reconcile to the trailer?
007
Design or evaluate a solution for 270/271 eligibility API lakehouse. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Medium270/271 eligibility API lakehouse

Model answer

Serve real-time eligibility (271) from a low-latency current-eligibility store fed by the membership platform, while logging 270/271 transactions to the lakehouse for verification analytics. Separate the serving path (fast point lookup, e.g. hybrid table) from the analytics path.

  • Real-time 271 from a low-latency current-eligibility store.
  • Log 270/271 to lakehouse for analytics.
  • Separate serving (point lookup) from analytics paths.
  • Hybrid/row-store table for fast serving.

Healthcare angle: An eligibility API needs sub-second point lookups (hybrid table) plus analytics logging — two distinct paths.

What strong answers cover

  1. Serve real-time 270/271 eligibility checks while landing transactions in the lakehouse for analytics.
  2. Low-latency serving path + async analytics path; idempotent capture.
  3. Reconcile API responses to the eligibility source of truth.
  4. Govern PHI on both paths.
  5. Separate hot serving from analytics.

Starter pattern

270 request -> eligibility service(low-latency, as-of) -> 271 response
 + async land transactions -> lakehouse analytics
Common mistake: Coupling the real-time eligibility response to the batch analytics pipeline, so analytics load slows live checks.
Principal follow-up: How do you separate the low-latency 270/271 serving path from the analytics path?
008
Design or evaluate a solution for FHIR ingestion platform. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
MediumFHIR ingestion platform

Model answer

Pull FHIR resources via paginated REST with resilient clients, land raw JSON (VARIANT) in Bronze for replay, flatten/normalize to typed Silver against US Core/USCDI, and link to member/provider. Scale with partitioned parallel pulls and incremental (_since) retrieval.

  • Paginated resilient pulls; raw VARIANT in Bronze.
  • Flatten/normalize to typed Silver (US Core/USCDI).
  • Partitioned parallel + incremental (_since) for scale.
  • Link to member/provider dimensions.

Healthcare angle: National-scale FHIR ingestion needs partitioned parallel pulls plus robust rate-limit/backoff handling.

What strong answers cover

  1. Resilient paginated pulls (backoff/jitter, Retry-After), raw VARIANT bronze for fidelity/replay, normalize to US Core silver.
  2. Partition pulls for national scale; incremental (_since) with checkpoints; reconcile completeness.
  3. Map resources to dimensions (Patient/Coverage/EOB).
  4. PHI-safe; rate-limit aware.
  5. Treat as a distributed-systems problem.

Starter pattern

partitioned parallel FHIR pulls(backoff) -> bronze VARIANT
 -> silver(US Core, mapped to dims) -> reconcile completeness
Common mistake: A naive sequential pull that can't scale nationally and silently drops resources when the API throttles.
Principal follow-up: How do you guarantee completeness and throughput of a national FHIR pull against rate limits?
009
Design or evaluate a solution for HL7 ADT pipeline. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
MediumHL7 ADT pipeline

Model answer

Ingest HL7 v2 ADT messages in near-real-time (stream), parse pipe-delimited segments, normalize event types (admit/discharge/transfer), and emit events that trigger care management and feed admission/readmission analytics. Handle out-of-order and duplicate messages.

  • Near-real-time ADT streaming + segment parsing.
  • Normalize event types; emit care-trigger events.
  • Feed admission/readmission analytics.
  • Handle out-of-order/duplicate messages.

Healthcare angle: Real-time ADT admit events trigger care-management outreach and feed readmission tracking.

What strong answers cover

  1. Ingest real-time HL7 v2 ADT messages (admit/discharge/transfer) via a streaming path, normalize to a common model.
  2. Drive event-based actions (care management, readmission tracking).
  3. Handle message ordering, duplicates, and malformed segments.
  4. Idempotent processing.
  5. Bridge HL7 with FHIR where both exist.

Starter pattern

HL7 ADT stream -> parse/normalize -> events(admit/discharge)
 -> care-mgmt actions + analytics; idempotent, ordered
Common mistake: Treating real-time ADT as a batch feed, losing the latency that makes admit/discharge events actionable.
Principal follow-up: Why does ADT warrant a streaming path, and how do you handle ordering and duplicates?
010
Design or evaluate a solution for CDC platform from SQL Server. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
MediumCDC platform from SQL Server

Model answer

Capture SQL Server changes (log-based CDC/Debezium) into Bronze, apply via ordered idempotent MERGE keyed on natural keys (LSN ordering), map deletes to soft-deletes, and handle schema drift. Maintain SCD2 history for dimensions and reconcile to source.

  • Log-based CDC into Bronze with LSN ordering.
  • Idempotent keyed MERGE; deletes → soft-delete.
  • SCD2 history; handle schema drift.
  • Reconcile to source.

Healthcare angle: Membership/provider source systems feed the lakehouse via CDC; LSN ordering keeps current views correct.

What strong answers cover

  1. Log-based CDC into bronze, applied with ordered idempotent MERGE (by LSN), deletes to soft-deletes, SCD2 where history matters.
  2. Reconcile to source; handle schema drift; monitor CDC lag.
  3. Re-snapshot if the stream stales past retention.
  4. Order and idempotency are essential.
  5. Maintain correct current state.

Starter pattern

SQL Server log -> CDC -> bronze(LSN order) -> idempotent MERGE
 deletes->soft-delete; SCD2; reconcile; monitor lag
Common mistake: Applying CDC out of order or non-idempotently, so an older change overwrites a newer one or reruns duplicate.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep current state correct, and what do you do after a multi-day CDC outage?
011
Design or evaluate a solution for metadata-driven ETL framework. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediummetadata-driven ETL framework

Model answer

Drive ingestion/transformation from a metadata catalog (source, schema, DQ rules, target, SLA) so onboarding a feed is configuration. A generic engine executes read→validate→transform→load with shared DQ/logging/lineage; keep an escape hatch for genuinely bespoke logic.

  • Metadata defines feeds; one engine executes them.
  • New feed = config, not new code.
  • Centralized DQ/logging/lineage/control totals.
  • Escape hatch for bespoke logic.

Healthcare angle: Onboarding many payer feeds is far faster with metadata-driven ingestion, until a feed exceeds what config can express.

What strong answers cover

  1. Drive ingestion/transform from metadata (source, schema, DQ, mappings, target) executed by a shared engine.
  2. Built-in idempotency, DQ/quarantine, reconciliation, lineage, observability.
  3. New feed = configuration; onboard in weeks.
  4. Escape hatch for genuinely bespoke logic.
  5. Keep config simple, tested, versioned.

Starter pattern

metadata(source/schema/DQ/mappings) -> shared engine
 (idempotent + DQ + reconcile + lineage) ; escape hatch for bespoke
Common mistake: Hand-building a bespoke pipeline per feed (slow/inconsistent), or forcing bespoke logic through rigid config.
Principal follow-up: Where does a metadata-driven framework speed onboarding, and where does it need an escape hatch?
012
Design or evaluate a solution for event-driven architecture. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediumevent-driven architecture

Model answer

Use events (file-landed, claim-received, eligibility-changed) to trigger processing asynchronously, decoupling producers/consumers and enabling near-real-time flows. Pair with idempotent consumers, ordering/dedup handling, and a durable log; reserve it for genuine event/low-latency needs vs simpler batch.

  • Events trigger async, decoupled processing.
  • Idempotent consumers; handle ordering/dedup.
  • Durable event log; near-real-time flows.
  • Use where events/latency justify vs batch.

Healthcare angle: File-landed events can trigger claim ingestion immediately rather than waiting for a nightly batch window.

What strong answers cover

  1. Use events where latency/decoupling help (file-landed, eligibility-changed, ADT actions) with idempotent consumers and a durable log.
  2. Keep batch for latency-tolerant analytics; design for at-least-once.
  3. Handle ordering/dedup.
  4. Don't event-ify everything.
  5. Match the pattern to the need.

Starter pattern

events(file-landed, eligibility-changed) -> idempotent consumers
 + durable log; batch for analytic trend
Common mistake: Making everything event-driven, adding ordering/dedup/operational complexity where batch was simpler and sufficient.
Principal follow-up: Where does event-driven genuinely beat batch in a claims platform, and where does it just add complexity?
013
Design or evaluate a solution for batch vs streaming decision. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedbatch vs streaming decision

Model answer

Choose by latency need, data arrival pattern, and complexity/cost. Batch suits periodic, large, tolerant-of-latency workloads (most claims/actuarial); streaming suits low-latency, continuously-arriving data (ADT, eligibility). Don't stream where batch suffices — streaming adds operational complexity.

  • Decide by latency need + arrival pattern + cost.
  • Batch: periodic, large, latency-tolerant.
  • Streaming: low-latency, continuous arrival.
  • Avoid streaming complexity when batch suffices.

Healthcare angle: Claims trend is fine as batch; real-time ADT care triggers justify streaming.

What strong answers cover

  1. Decide by latency need, not novelty: streaming for low-latency/event-driven (ADT, real-time eligibility), batch for periodic analytics.
  2. Streaming adds state/watermark/backpressure complexity.
  3. Many platforms are mostly batch with targeted streaming.
  4. De-risk streaming with parallel-run.
  5. Keep batch for backfills/reprocessing.

Starter pattern

latency-critical(ADT, 270/271) -> stream
 periodic analytics(PMPM, HEDIS) -> batch; keep batch for backfill
Common mistake: Adopting streaming everywhere for its own sake, taking on state/backpressure complexity where batch suffices.
Principal follow-up: Which claims workloads justify streaming, and which are better as batch?
014
Design or evaluate a solution for idempotency design. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedidempotency design

Model answer

Idempotency means re-running produces the same state. Achieve it with deterministic keys, keyed MERGE or partition overwrite (never blind append), processed-input tracking, and deterministic transforms. It is the foundation for safe retries, replays, and recovery.

  • Same end state no matter how many runs.
  • Keyed MERGE / partition overwrite, not append.
  • Track processed files/batches.
  • Deterministic transforms + keys.

Healthcare angle: On-call reruns are routine; idempotency means a rerun can't double-load claims or payments.

What strong answers cover

  1. Design so reruns produce the same state: keyed MERGE / partition overwrite, deterministic transforms/tie-breakers, processed-input tracking.
  2. Idempotency is the precondition for safe retries, replays, and recovery.
  3. Test: run twice == run once.
  4. Reconcile after.
  5. Foundation of reliable pipelines.

Starter pattern

MERGE on business key / INSERT OVERWRITE partition
 + deterministic tie-breakers + processed-file tracking
Common mistake: Blind appends and non-deterministic ordering, so any on-call rerun duplicates or reshuffles claims.
Principal follow-up: What's your concrete test that a pipeline is truly idempotent, and where does it usually break?
015
Design or evaluate a solution for reprocessing six months. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedreprocessing six months

Model answer

Reprocess by partition (per service_month), idempotent overwrite/MERGE, bounded batches, per-unit reconciliation before publish, and resumability. Run in parallel within cost limits, validate against source, and communicate restatements to consumers.

  • Chunk by service_month; idempotent overwrite/MERGE.
  • Bounded, parallel-within-budget, resumable.
  • Per-unit reconciliation before publish.
  • Communicate restatements.

Healthcare angle: A six-month claims restatement runs month-by-month with per-month reconciliation, not one risky rerun.

What strong answers cover

  1. Reprocess per service_month, idempotently, in bounded resumable batches, reconciling each before publishing, isolated from live load.
  2. Communicate restatements; prioritize needed periods.
  3. Never one giant rerun.
  4. Atomic publish per unit.
  5. Track completed months.

Starter pattern

for m in months: recompute(m) -> idempotent overwrite -> reconcile -> publish
 isolated compute; resumable
Common mistake: One massive six-month rerun that fails midway with no resumability and no per-month validation.
Principal follow-up: Why reprocess month-by-month with per-unit reconciliation rather than one big rerun?
016
Design or evaluate a solution for data quality framework. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advanceddata quality framework

Model answer

A DQ framework encodes rules (not-null keys, valid code sets, ranges, uniqueness-on-grain, referential integrity, reconciliation) that run inline with per-rule actions (fail/quarantine/warn), tracked as metrics over time, with stewardship for quarantined data.

  • Rules: keys, codes, ranges, uniqueness, RI, reconciliation.
  • Per-rule action: fail/quarantine/warn.
  • Track pass rates as metrics (catch drift).
  • Steward quarantined data.

Healthcare angle: Claims DQ: valid CARC/CPT, member present, paid in range, one row per line grain, reconciled to source.

What strong answers cover

  1. Layered DQ: schema/contract validation, business rules (valid codes, ranges, grain uniqueness), reconciliation, with per-rule actions.
  2. Quarantine bad rows + replay; track pass rates and quarantine volume as signals.
  3. Fail loudly on critical violations.
  4. Tie to upstream contracts.
  5. Make quality observable.

Starter pattern

validate schema/contract -> business rules -> reconcile
 bad rows -> quarantine(reason) + replay; track pass-rate/quarantine vol
Common mistake: DQ as scattered ad-hoc checks (or none), so an upstream change silently corrupts claims with no signal.
Principal follow-up: What DQ layers would you build, and what action per rule (fail/quarantine/warn)?
017
Design or evaluate a solution for observability framework. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedobservability framework

Model answer

Observability covers freshness/latency vs SLA, control totals, DQ pass rates, quarantine volume, job durations, cost, and lineage — surfaced on dashboards and driving alerts so silent degradation becomes visible and on-call can act fast.

  • Freshness/latency, control totals, DQ, quarantine, cost.
  • Dashboards + alerts off the same metrics.
  • Lineage for triage.
  • Make silent degradation visible.

Healthcare angle: A claims platform dashboard (last load, volume vs baseline, paid totals, DQ) catches issues before stakeholders do.

What strong answers cover

  1. Standardize signals every pipeline emits: freshness/latency, control totals, DQ pass rates, quarantine volume, cost, lineage.
  2. Shared dashboards + alerts mapped to failure modes and owners.
  3. Goal: detect issues before consumers do.
  4. Drive alerts off the same metrics.
  5. One standard, not per-team.

Starter pattern

every pipeline emits: freshness + control totals + DQ + quarantine + cost
 -> shared dashboards + actionable alerts(owner, severity)
Common mistake: Per-team ad-hoc monitoring with uneven coverage, so issues are still found by consumers first.
Principal follow-up: What signals would you standardize so the platform self-reports unhealthy before consumers notice?
018
Design or evaluate a solution for control totals. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedcontrol totals

Model answer

Control totals are per-stage/per-day counts and key dollar sums asserted to match across stages and against source trailers, localizing exactly where data was gained or lost and providing audit evidence of integrity.

  • Counts + dollar sums per stage/day.
  • Assert equality across stages + to source trailer.
  • Localize where data changed.
  • Audit evidence of integrity.

Healthcare angle: Tying loaded totals to the EDI file trailer is a basic but powerful claims integrity check.

What strong answers cover

  1. Assert per-stage and source-to-target count/dollar control totals each run, tied to file trailers.
  2. First failing boundary localizes loss/duplication.
  3. Persist results for audit and trend.
  4. Fail the run loudly on mismatch beyond tolerance.
  5. Reusable across products.

Starter pattern

assert trailer == raw == silver == gold (count & SUM(paid))
 persist recon; first failing boundary = the defect
Common mistake: Treating a control-total mismatch as rounding noise and overriding it, hiding a real loss or duplication.
Principal follow-up: How do stage-to-stage control totals localize exactly where claims were lost or duplicated?
019
Design or evaluate a solution for lineage and auditability. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedlineage and auditability

Model answer

Capture table/column lineage and audit columns (source_file, batch_id, timestamps) plus access logs, enabling impact analysis, dispute resolution (trace a number to source), and compliance evidence. Automate capture; supplement with business documentation.

  • Table/column lineage + audit columns + access logs.
  • Impact analysis and dispute resolution.
  • Compliance evidence.
  • Automated + documented.

Healthcare angle: When a PMPM number is disputed, lineage traces it from the gold mart back to the source feed.

What strong answers cover

  1. Capture table and column lineage (source-to-consumer) plus access/audit logs and reconciliation artifacts.
  2. Enables impact analysis, dispute resolution, and audit-ready-by-design.
  3. Automated capture + business context.
  4. Persist evidence continuously.
  5. Answer audits from existing artifacts.

Starter pattern

automated column lineage + access audit + reconciliation persisted
 -> impact analysis + dispute resolution + audit on demand
Common mistake: No column lineage, so a disputed number can't be traced and a schema change's blast radius is unknown.
Principal follow-up: How does lineage let you trace a disputed PMPM to source and scope a schema change's impact?
020
Design or evaluate a solution for PHI security model. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
AdvancedPHI security model

Model answer

Protect PHI with least-privilege RBAC, column masking, row-level security, de-identification for broad use, encryption, access auditing, and minimum-necessary design so most consumers never touch raw identifiers. Compliance is a design constraint throughout.

  • Least-privilege RBAC + masking + row policies.
  • De-identification for broad analytics.
  • Encryption + access auditing.
  • Minimum-necessary by design.

Healthcare angle: Analysts get masked/de-identified claims; raw member identifiers are restricted to legitimate uses.

What strong answers cover

  1. Least-privilege by default: masked/de-identified views, row-level security, narrow audited access to clear PHI.
  2. Govern centrally (e.g. Unity Catalog), audit access, run periodic recertification.
  3. Most consumers never touch raw PHI.
  4. Minimum-necessary by design.
  5. Contain and assess any exposure.

Starter pattern

default masked/de-id views + row policies + least privilege
 narrow audited unmask role; periodic access recertification
Common mistake: Granting broad raw-PHI access for convenience, violating minimum-necessary and creating breach risk.
Principal follow-up: How do you give analysts self-service speed without exposing raw PHI broadly?
021
Design or evaluate a solution for multi-tenant payer data. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedmulti-tenant payer data

Model answer

Isolate tenants (lines of business/clients) via separate catalogs/schemas or row-level tenant filtering, with per-tenant access control, cost attribution, and SLA. Balance isolation (security/blast radius) against shared infrastructure efficiency; never let one tenant's data leak to another.

  • Isolate via catalogs/schemas or row-level tenant filter.
  • Per-tenant access, cost, SLA.
  • Balance isolation vs shared efficiency.
  • Prevent cross-tenant leakage.

Healthcare angle: Serving multiple plans/clients requires strict tenant isolation so no client sees another's member data.

What strong answers cover

  1. Isolate tenants (LOBs/clients) via separate catalogs/schemas or enforced row-level tenant filtering, with per-tenant access/cost/SLA.
  2. Balance isolation (security/blast radius) vs shared-infra efficiency.
  3. Preventing cross-tenant PHI leakage is paramount — strong isolation by default, tested.
  4. Attribute cost per tenant.
  5. Test for leakage.

Starter pattern

isolate via catalogs/schemas or enforced row-level tenant filter
 per-tenant access/cost/SLA; test cross-tenant leakage
Common mistake: Weak isolation (a shared table with optional filters) risking one client seeing another's member PHI.
Principal follow-up: How do you isolate multiple clients so no tenant can ever see another's PHI?
022
Design or evaluate a solution for actuarial trend analytics layer. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedactuarial trend analytics layer

Model answer

Serve actuaries a stable, documented, reproducible layer: defined grain/metrics, as-of snapshots for restatement, incurred-vs-paid with IBNR support, and trend decomposition. Prioritize correctness, history, and auditability over raw query speed.

  • Stable grain/metric definitions; reproducible as-of.
  • Incurred vs paid + IBNR.
  • Trend decomposition.
  • Correctness/auditability over speed.

Healthcare angle: Actuaries must reproduce a prior period exactly even after retro corrections — bitemporal/snapshotting matters.

What strong answers cover

  1. Serve actuaries a stable, governed, reproducible layer: defined grain/metrics, bitemporal as-of history, incurred (paid+IBNR), trend decomposition, restatement.
  2. Prioritize correctness/auditability/reproducibility over speed.
  3. Reproduce any prior period exactly.
  4. Stable definitions.
  5. Mark recent periods provisional.

Starter pattern

bitemporal history + incurred(=paid+IBNR) + decomposed trend(util/unit/mix)
 reproducible prior periods; restate late data
Common mistake: Serving fast but non-reproducible data, so actuaries can't reproduce a prior period after restatements.
Principal follow-up: Why do actuaries need a reproducible, IBNR-aware layer, and what design enables it?
023
Design or evaluate a solution for HEDIS measure pipeline. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
AdvancedHEDIS measure pipeline

Model answer

Implement each HEDIS measure's exact eligibility, exclusion, and numerator spec, using member-months for continuous enrollment (45-day gap allowance) and claim/encounter/lab evidence for numerators. Make it reproducible and auditable to the spec, restating for late data.

  • Encode exact eligibility/exclusion/numerator per measure.
  • Continuous enrollment via member-months.
  • Numerator from claims/encounters/labs.
  • Reproducible/auditable; restate for late data.

Healthcare angle: A HEDIS pipeline must defend each measure's rate against the spec in audit.

What strong answers cover

  1. Implement HEDIS measures to spec: eligible population (continuous enrollment, gaps), numerator evidence, exclusions, as-of the measurement period.
  2. Reproducible and auditable; member-month rigor.
  3. Reconcile denominators to spec.
  4. Handle late data restatement.
  5. Spec-validated logic.

Starter pattern

denominator(eligible + continuous-enroll w/ 45d gap + exclusions)
 numerator(evidence); as-of period; reproducible + auditable
Common mistake: Loose continuous-enrollment/gap handling that silently shifts the denominator and moves the quality rate.
Principal follow-up: How do you make a HEDIS denominator defensible to spec and reproducible after late data?
024
Design or evaluate a solution for CMS Stars analytics. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
AdvancedCMS Stars analytics

Model answer

Aggregate measure-level performance (HEDIS, Part D, experience) into Star ratings, track gaps and improvement opportunities, and prioritize interventions on measures that move the overall rating (weighted). Reproducibility and timeliness affect bonus revenue.

  • Aggregate measures into Star ratings.
  • Track gaps; prioritize high-weight measures.
  • Drive intervention targeting.
  • Reproducible + timely (affects bonus revenue).

Healthcare angle: Star analytics target gap-closure on the measures that most move the rating and bonus payments.

What strong answers cover

  1. Aggregate measures (HEDIS, CAHPS, pharmacy adherence) into Star ratings with correct weighting and cut points.
  2. Prioritize improvement by measure weight x gap; timely, accurate, governed.
  3. Reproducible and auditable (revenue impact).
  4. Track measure-level performance.
  5. Tie analytics to interventions.

Starter pattern

measures(HEDIS/CAHPS/adherence) -> weighted Star rating(cut points)
 prioritize by weight x gap; reproducible + auditable
Common mistake: Computing Stars on inconsistent measure definitions or stale data, misdirecting improvement spend.
Principal follow-up: How would you prioritize Stars improvement, and why must the computation be reproducible?
025
Design or evaluate a solution for risk adjustment pipeline. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedrisk adjustment pipeline

Model answer

Capture diagnoses completely/accurately, map to HCCs by model version, compute RAF per period, handle hierarchies, and restate as late diagnoses arrive. Track inputs/version for reproducibility and audit; completeness directly drives risk-adjusted revenue.

  • Complete/accurate diagnosis capture.
  • HCC mapping (model version) + hierarchies → RAF.
  • Restate on late diagnoses.
  • Reproducible/auditable inputs.

Healthcare angle: Missed HCCs understate RAF and revenue; completeness and version tracking are critical.

What strong answers cover

  1. Compute RAF from diagnoses (claims/encounters/charts) mapped to HCCs per the model version, with reproducible, versioned inputs.
  2. Handle diagnosis capture, suspecting, and submission; drift must be explainable.
  3. Version model/features; monitor drift.
  4. Auditable and defensible.
  5. Restate on data changes.

Starter pattern

diagnoses -> HCC mapping(model version) -> RAF score
 versioned inputs + drift monitoring; reproducible + auditable
Common mistake: No model/feature versioning, so a RAF change is an unexplainable mystery in an incident and indefensible in audit.
Principal follow-up: How do you separate a model-version change from a real population shift when RAF drifts?
026
Design or evaluate a solution for value-based care attribution. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedvalue-based care attribution

Model answer

Attribute members/episodes to providers by documented rules (plurality/most-recent within a window), effective-dated with tie-breakers, restating on retro data changes. It underpins VBC settlements, so it must be transparent, reproducible, and reconcilable.

  • Rule-based attribution with tie-breakers.
  • Effective-dated; restate on retro changes.
  • Underpins VBC settlements.
  • Transparent + reproducible.

Healthcare angle: VBC settlements depend on correct attribution and restated cost when late claims arrive.

What strong answers cover

  1. Attribute members to providers/ACOs per the contract's rules (plurality of visits, PCP assignment), as-of the period.
  2. Use the effective-dated provider dimension and as-of joins; handle attribution changes/restatement.
  3. Feed quality/cost analytics and settlement.
  4. Rules vary by contract.
  5. Reproducible and auditable.

Starter pattern

attribution = member -> provider per contract rule(as-of period)
 on roster/affiliation change -> restate attribution
Common mistake: Using current provider affiliation instead of as-of, so a retro change silently re-attributes historical members.
Principal follow-up: How do contract-specific rules and as-of joins drive correct VBC attribution?
027
Design or evaluate a solution for cost optimization design. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedcost optimization design

Model answer

Optimize cost via right-sized/auto-terminating compute, workload separation, incremental processing (avoid full reloads), materialization of hot aggregates, eliminating small files/skew/over-caching, spot for fault-tolerant batch, and cost attribution to find offenders.

  • Right-size + auto-terminate compute; separate workloads.
  • Incremental over full reloads; materialize hot aggregates.
  • Fix small files/skew/over-caching; spot for batch.
  • Attribute cost to find offenders.

Healthcare angle: A claims platform's biggest savings often come from incremental loads and fixing skew/small files, not just smaller clusters.

What strong answers cover

  1. Optimize via incremental over full loads, fixing skew/small files, right-sized auto-terminating compute, workload separation, and per-product cost attribution.
  2. Biggest wins are usually algorithmic, not just smaller clusters.
  3. Set resource monitors/alerts.
  4. Make cost owned per product.
  5. Measure before/after.

Starter pattern

incremental loads + fix skew/small files + right-size/auto-terminate
 + workload separation + per-product cost attribution + alerts
Common mistake: Only shrinking clusters while leaving full reloads, skew, and small-file scans that drive the real cost.
Principal follow-up: Where do the biggest platform cost savings usually come from beyond cluster sizing?
028
Design or evaluate a solution for SLA management. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
ExpertSLA management

Model answer

Define each data product's SLA to consumer need, monitor freshness/completeness, alert predictively before breach, track attainment, and renegotiate when unrealistic. Design the workflow backward from the consumer deadline accounting for upstream latency.

  • SLA per consumer need; monitor freshness/completeness.
  • Predictive breach alerts.
  • Track attainment; renegotiate if unrealistic.
  • Design backward from consumer deadline.

Healthcare angle: If actuarial marts must be ready by 7am, alert at 5am when the claims load is behind.

What strong answers cover

  1. Define each pipeline's SLA to consumer need (freshness/completeness/availability), design backward from the deadline accounting for upstream latency.
  2. Predictive breach alerting; attainment tracking; dependency gating.
  3. Renegotiate if chronically infeasible.
  4. Degraded-mode plan for late upstream.
  5. Surface SLA status.

Starter pattern

design backward from deadline(account upstream latency)
 predictive alert at risk + attainment tracking + dependency gating
Common mistake: Discovering an SLA miss only after the deadline instead of a predictive alert when upstream runs late.
Principal follow-up: How would you design a predictive alert for a 7am actuarial-mart SLA?
029
Design or evaluate a solution for fault tolerance. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertfault tolerance

Model answer

Build for failure: idempotent/resumable jobs, retries with backoff on transient errors, checkpointing, isolation of failures (quarantine, partial-batch handling), graceful degradation, and tested recovery/DR. Assume components fail and ensure no data corruption results.

  • Idempotent/resumable + retries with backoff.
  • Checkpointing + failure isolation (quarantine/partial-batch).
  • Graceful degradation; tested recovery/DR.
  • Failures must not corrupt data.

Healthcare angle: A transient ADLS/SFTP blip should retry and resume cleanly, never duplicate or drop claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. Tolerate failures with idempotent retries+backoff, checkpointing/resumability, partial-failure isolation (per-partition), and graceful degradation.
  2. Distinguish retryable (infra) from non-retryable (bad data).
  3. Reprocess only the failed portion.
  4. Idempotency underpins it all.
  5. Test recovery paths.

Starter pattern

idempotent + bounded retries(backoff) + checkpoint/resume
 + per-partition failure isolation + degraded mode
Common mistake: A pipeline that must fully restart on any failure (no checkpointing/idempotency), so partial failures are costly.
Principal follow-up: How do idempotency, checkpointing, and partial-failure isolation make a pipeline fault-tolerant?
030
Design or evaluate a solution for backpressure handling. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertbackpressure handling

Model answer

Backpressure manages when upstream produces faster than downstream consumes — via bounded queues, rate limiting, batching, watermarks bounding state, and autoscaling. Prevent unbounded memory/state growth and cascading failures under load spikes.

  • Bounded queues + rate limiting + batching.
  • Watermarks bound streaming state.
  • Autoscale within limits.
  • Prevent unbounded growth/cascading failure.

Healthcare angle: A month-end claims surge needs backpressure (bounded state, batching) so the stream doesn't blow memory.

What strong answers cover

  1. Handle surges by bounding intake (max files/records per trigger), buffering durably, and scaling consumers within limits.
  2. Prevent overwhelming downstream; rate-limit external calls.
  3. Streaming: tune trigger and max offsets per batch.
  4. Monitor lag.
  5. Degrade gracefully under load.

Starter pattern

stream: maxFilesPerTrigger / maxOffsetsPerTrigger to bound intake
 durable buffer + scale consumers; rate-limit external calls
Common mistake: No intake bounds, so a volume surge (month-end backlog) overwhelms the pipeline or a downstream API.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep a month-end surge from overwhelming the pipeline or downstream systems?
031
Design or evaluate a solution for schema registry. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertschema registry

Model answer

A schema registry centrally manages and versions data schemas/contracts so producers and consumers agree, evolution is governed (compatibility rules), and breaking changes are caught before they propagate. Enforce compatibility on producers; consumers code to versioned schemas.

  • Central versioned schemas/contracts.
  • Compatibility rules govern evolution.
  • Catch breaking changes pre-propagation.
  • Producers enforce; consumers code to versions.

Healthcare angle: A payer adding a claim field is a compatible change; a type change is breaking and must be caught by the registry.

What strong answers cover

  1. A registry holds versioned schemas with compatibility rules, enforced at the boundary so producers can't break consumers silently.
  2. Additive changes evolve; breaking changes are rejected/quarantined.
  3. Central source of schema truth.
  4. Alert on drift.
  5. Underpins data contracts.

Starter pattern

registry(versioned schemas + compatibility rules) enforced at ingest
 additive -> evolve; breaking -> reject/quarantine + alert producer
Common mistake: No registry, so a producer's silent schema change (type/reorder) corrupts values downstream without error.
Principal follow-up: How does a schema registry plus compatibility rules stop a producer from breaking consumers silently?
032
Design or evaluate a solution for quarantine and replay. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertquarantine and replay

Model answer

Route failing records to a quarantine store with reason/source, let stewards fix root causes, and replay corrected/held data idempotently once resolved. This isolates bad data without failing whole loads and enables recovery.

  • Quarantine bad records with reason/source.
  • Steward fixes root cause.
  • Replay corrected data idempotently.
  • Isolate without failing whole loads.

Healthcare angle: Claims with unmapped codes quarantine; after the mapping is fixed they replay without duplicating good claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. Route failing rows to a quarantine table with reason/source instead of failing loads; stewards fix and replay idempotently.
  2. Quarantine volume is a leading quality signal — alert on spikes.
  3. Replay must be idempotent (no dupes).
  4. Don't silently drop.
  5. Tie to upstream contracts.

Starter pattern

bad rows -> quarantine(reason, source) ; steward fixes
 -> idempotent replay; alert on quarantine-volume spike
Common mistake: Failing the whole load on a few bad rows, or silently dropping them with no quarantine/replay path.
Principal follow-up: Why quarantine rather than fail or drop, and how do you make replay idempotent?
033
Design or evaluate a solution for data contracts. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertdata contracts

Model answer

Data contracts formalize the agreement between producer and consumer: grain, schema, semantics, quality guarantees, and SLA, versioned and enforced. They decouple teams, prevent silent breaks, and make ownership explicit.

  • Grain + schema + semantics + quality + SLA, versioned.
  • Enforced (tests/registry) at the boundary.
  • Decouple producer/consumer evolution.
  • Make ownership/expectations explicit.

Healthcare angle: The claim-line product's contract lets analytics rely on its grain/freshness while claims evolves internals.

What strong answers cover

  1. Contracts define schema, grain, semantics, freshness, and ownership, enforced at the boundary with violations quarantined and routed to producers.
  2. Compatibility rules govern evolution.
  3. Turns expectations into guarantees.
  4. Don't silently absorb breaks.
  5. Producer accountability.

Starter pattern

contract(schema/grain/semantics/freshness/owner) validated at ingest
 violations -> quarantine + producer; compatibility rules
Common mistake: Treating contracts as documentation only, so producers break them silently and consumers absorb the fragility.
Principal follow-up: When a producer breaks the contract, when do you adapt versus quarantine and push back?
034
Design or evaluate a solution for feature engineering platform. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertfeature engineering platform

Model answer

Provide reusable, governed features for ML (risk/quality models): point-in-time-correct retrieval (no leakage), online/offline consistency, versioning, lineage, and drift monitoring — via a feature store over governed gold data, with PHI controls.

  • Reusable governed features; point-in-time correct.
  • Online/offline consistency; versioning + lineage.
  • Drift monitoring; PHI controls.
  • Feature store over governed gold data.

Healthcare angle: Shared member-risk features (utilization, chronic flags) need point-in-time correctness to avoid training leakage.

What strong answers cover

  1. A feature store with point-in-time-correct retrieval (no leakage), online/offline consistency, versioning, lineage, and PHI controls over certified data.
  2. Prevents training-serving skew; reusable across models.
  3. Govern PHI in features.
  4. Reproducible training.
  5. Monitor drift.

Starter pattern

feature store: point-in-time retrieval + online/offline consistency
 + versioning + lineage + PHI controls over certified gold
Common mistake: Ad-hoc features computed differently in training vs serving, causing leakage and skew that can't be traced.
Principal follow-up: How does a feature store prevent training leakage and training-serving skew?
035
Design or evaluate a solution for serving layer for Power BI. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertserving layer for Power BI

Model answer

Serve BI from star-schema gold marts at BI-friendly grain via a concurrency-scaled SQL engine with result caching and pre-aggregation, governing PHI at the data layer. Don't point BI at raw/huge facts; pre-aggregate and conform.

  • Star-schema gold marts at BI grain.
  • Concurrency scale-out + result cache + pre-aggregation.
  • Govern PHI at the data layer.
  • Never serve raw huge facts directly.

Healthcare angle: Executive claims dashboards hit pre-aggregated marts via a concurrency-scaled warehouse, not the line fact.

What strong answers cover

  1. Serve BI from a concurrency-scaled warehouse over pre-aggregated star-schema gold marts, with PHI governed at the data layer and a semantic layer for consistent metrics.
  2. Scale out for many users; caching; import vs DirectQuery tradeoff.
  3. Don't point BI at raw facts.
  4. One metric definition.
  5. Freshness indicators.

Starter pattern

BI -> warehouse(multi-cluster) -> gold(pre-aggregated star)
 + semantic layer(one PMPM definition) + PHI masking
Common mistake: Pointing Power BI at the raw billion-row fact, causing slow visuals and queuing instead of serving gold marts.
Principal follow-up: How do you serve many concurrent BI users without pointing them at raw facts?
036
Design or evaluate a solution for Lakehouse vs warehouse split. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
ExpertLakehouse vs warehouse split

Model answer

A common split: lakehouse for ingestion, heavy ETL, streaming, ML, and semi-structured data; warehouse for governed SQL serving, BI concurrency, and sharing. Define a clear handoff (curated gold marts) and one source of truth; decide by workload, skills, cost, and governance.

  • Lakehouse: ingestion/ETL/streaming/ML/semi-structured.
  • Warehouse: governed SQL serving, BI concurrency, sharing.
  • Clear gold-mart handoff; one source of truth.
  • Decide by workload/skills/cost/governance.

Healthcare angle: Clean claims in the lakehouse; serve actuarial/BI gold marts from the warehouse — with one authoritative mart.

What strong answers cover

  1. Lakehouse for ingestion/ETL/streaming/ML/semi-structured; warehouse for governed SQL serving/concurrency/sharing.
  2. Clean handoff at curated gold marts; one source of truth.
  3. Decide by workload, skills, governance, cost — not hype.
  4. Many payers run both deliberately.
  5. Avoid duplicating the authoritative mart.

Starter pattern

lakehouse: ingest/ETL/stream/ML | warehouse: govern SQL serving/BI
 handoff = curated gold marts(one source of truth)
Common mistake: Choosing by hype and duplicating the gold mart in both with no source of truth, so the two diverge.
Principal follow-up: Where do you draw the lakehouse/warehouse boundary, and what would change it?
037
Design or evaluate a solution for DR design. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
ExpertDR design

Model answer

DR replicates critical data and metadata cross-region, defines RPO/RTO, automates failover of jobs/access, and is regularly tested. Tier by criticality (claims vs reference) to control cost; untested DR is not a plan.

  • Cross-region replication of data + metadata.
  • Defined RPO/RTO; automated failover.
  • Regularly tested runbook.
  • Tier by criticality for cost.

Healthcare angle: Claims platform DR replicates gold marts/catalog to a second region with a tested failover meeting the business RTO.

What strong answers cover

  1. DR with RPO/RTO tiered by criticality: cross-region replication of data and metadata/catalog, automated failover, regularly tested runbooks.
  2. Reference vs gold marts may differ in tier.
  3. Replicate the catalog, not just data.
  4. Untested DR isn't DR.
  5. Test cadence is part of the design.

Starter pattern

RPO/RTO tiered; replicate data + catalog cross-region; auto-failover
 scheduled, tested DR runbook
Common mistake: Replicating data but not the governance catalog, so failover has data but no governed access — or never testing failover.
Principal follow-up: What must DR replicate beyond data, and why is the test cadence part of the architecture?
038
Design or evaluate a solution for blue-green deployment. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertblue-green deployment

Model answer

Build the new version (green) alongside the live one (blue), validate it, then cut over atomically (table swap / pointer flip), with instant rollback by reverting. It eliminates partial-state exposure and de-risks releases.

  • Build green beside live blue; validate.
  • Atomic cutover (swap/pointer); instant rollback.
  • No partial-state exposure.
  • De-risks releases.

Healthcare angle: A refreshed claims mart is built and validated, then swapped in atomically so consumers never see in-progress data.

What strong answers cover

  1. Build and validate the new version aside, then swap atomically (table SWAP/pointer) with instant rollback; consumers never see partial state.
  2. Validate before cutover; keep the prior version for revert.
  3. Default for consumer-facing marts.
  4. Reconcile post-swap.
  5. Rollback is instant.

Starter pattern

build+validate mart_new aside -> ALTER TABLE mart SWAP WITH mart_new
 keep prior for instant rollback; reconcile post-swap
Common mistake: Editing the live mart in place, so consumers see partial state and rollback means a slow rebuild.
Principal follow-up: How does blue-green give zero-downtime deploys and instant rollback for marts?
039
Design or evaluate a solution for canary release for ETL. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertcanary release for ETL

Model answer

Release a change to a small slice (one feed/partition/tenant) first, validate output and reconciliation, then roll out broadly. It limits blast radius and catches issues on real data before full deployment.

  • Roll out to a small slice first.
  • Validate output + reconciliation.
  • Then expand; limit blast radius.
  • Catch issues on real data early.

Healthcare angle: A new claims transform runs on one payer/partition first, reconciled, before applying to all feeds.

What strong answers cover

  1. Release a change to a slice first (one source/partition/tenant), validate outputs against expectations, then roll out fully.
  2. Limits blast radius; catches regressions early.
  3. Automated rollback on failure.
  4. Reconcile the canary before full rollout.
  5. Good for risky logic changes.

Starter pattern

run new logic on a canary slice -> validate/reconcile -> full rollout
 auto-rollback on regression
Common mistake: Rolling a risky transform change to all claims at once, so a regression corrupts everything before it's caught.
Principal follow-up: How would you canary a risky adjudication-logic change to limit blast radius?
040
Design or evaluate a solution for CI/CD for data pipelines. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
ExpertCI/CD for data pipelines

Model answer

Version code (tested libraries, thin notebooks), run CI tests, and promote via parameterized bundles dev→test→prod with automated, reviewed, reproducible deployment and post-deploy verification. No manual prod edits; rollback ready.

  • Tested libraries + CI tests.
  • Bundle-based, parameterized promotion.
  • Reviewed, reproducible, post-deploy verified.
  • No prod click-ops; rollback ready.

Healthcare angle: A claims pipeline change ships via CI tests + bundle promotion with a post-deploy reconciliation check.

What strong answers cover

  1. Version control + unit-tested packaged logic + CI tests + reproducible parameterized deployment (bundles) dev->test->prod, with post-deploy verification.
  2. No manual prod edits; PR review; rollback ready.
  3. Test transforms and DQ.
  4. Promote reproducibly.
  5. Smoke/reconciliation check post-deploy.

Starter pattern

Git -> CI(unit tests on packaged logic) -> bundle deploy dev/test/prod
 + post-deploy reconciliation; rollback ready
Common mistake: Editing jobs directly in prod with no tests, version control, or reproducible promotion.
Principal follow-up: What does a safe CI/CD flow for claims pipelines look like, including post-deploy checks?
041
Design or evaluate a solution for dependency orchestration. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principaldependency orchestration

Model answer

Model the pipeline as a DAG with explicit dependencies so tasks run in correct order on predecessor success, with per-task retries, conditional execution, and SLA-aware scheduling. Avoid hidden ordering assumptions; make dependencies declarative.

  • DAG with explicit dependencies + ordering.
  • Per-task retries + conditional execution.
  • SLA-aware scheduling.
  • Declarative, not hidden assumptions.

Healthcare angle: raw→silver→gold→reconcile as a DAG ensures gold builds only after silver succeeds.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model the pipeline as an explicit DAG with declared dependencies, gating publication on quality/reconciliation tasks.
  2. Avoid hidden ordering via separate schedules.
  3. Conditional execution; alert on skipped critical tasks.
  4. Re-validate published outputs.
  5. One source of truth for dependencies.

Starter pattern

DAG: ingest -> silver -> gold -> reconcile (gates publish)
 explicit deps; alert if a critical task is skipped
Common mistake: Coordinating stages via separate schedules/timing, so gold can run before silver finishes or recon is skipped.
Principal follow-up: How do you ensure a skipped reconciliation task blocks publication rather than passing silently?
042
Design or evaluate a solution for workflow retry semantics. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalworkflow retry semantics

Model answer

Define retries to handle transient failures with bounded attempts and backoff, but only for idempotent tasks; distinguish retryable (infra) from non-retryable (bad data/logic), avoid retry storms, and alert on exhaustion. Retries on non-idempotent writes duplicate data.

  • Bounded retries + backoff for transient failures.
  • Idempotency precondition for safe retries.
  • Distinguish retryable vs non-retryable.
  • Avoid storms; alert on exhaustion.

Healthcare angle: Retrying a non-idempotent payment load doubles payments; idempotency must come first.

What strong answers cover

  1. Bounded retries with backoff for transient failures, only on idempotent tasks; distinguish retryable (infra) from non-retryable (data/logic); alert on exhaustion.
  2. Idempotency is the precondition.
  3. Don't auto-retry deterministic errors.
  4. Surface repeated failures.
  5. Per-task configuration.

Starter pattern

task retry(max, backoff) for transient infra errors only
 idempotent task -> retry is safe; bad-data errors -> quarantine, not retry
Common mistake: Auto-retrying non-idempotent tasks (or retrying bad-data errors), duplicating claims or wasting cycles.
Principal follow-up: Which failures retry vs fail fast, and why must the task be idempotent first?
043
Design or evaluate a solution for state management. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalstate management

Model answer

Manage pipeline state (watermarks, offsets, checkpoints, processed-file registries) durably and transactionally with output so reruns/recovery are correct. Bound streaming state with watermarks/timeouts; treat state as critical and recoverable.

  • Durable watermarks/offsets/checkpoints/registries.
  • Persist state transactionally with output.
  • Bound streaming state (watermarks/timeouts).
  • Treat state as critical/recoverable.

Healthcare angle: Persisting the claims watermark atomically with the load makes reruns correct, not duplicative.

What strong answers cover

  1. Manage streaming state (aggregations, dedup, joins) bounded by watermarks, in a durable state store, sized to expected lateness.
  2. Unbounded state OOMs the stream.
  3. Checkpoint state for recovery.
  4. Tune watermark to lateness.
  5. Monitor state size.

Starter pattern

withWatermark to bound state; durable state store; checkpoint
 size watermark to expected lateness; monitor state size
Common mistake: Stateful streaming without watermarks, so state grows unbounded until the stream OOMs.
Principal follow-up: How do watermarks bound streaming state, and what happens without them?
044
Design or evaluate a solution for exactly-once illusion. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalexactly-once illusion

Model answer

True exactly-once across distributed systems is hard; practically you achieve effectively-once via at-least-once delivery plus idempotent, deduplicated sinks. Don't assume exactly-once — design idempotent consumers so duplicates are harmless.

  • End-to-end exactly-once is largely an illusion.
  • Achieve effectively-once: at-least-once + idempotent sink.
  • Dedup on business key + transactional writes.
  • Design for harmless duplicates.

Healthcare angle: Claims events may be delivered twice; idempotent, deduplicated loads make exactly-once effectively true.

What strong answers cover

  1. True exactly-once delivery is rarely guaranteed end-to-end; achieve exactly-once *effect* via at-least-once delivery + idempotent sinks.
  2. Dedup with keys/watermarks; transactional/idempotent writes.
  3. Design for at-least-once and make duplicates harmless.
  4. Don't assume the framework guarantees it everywhere.
  5. Reconcile to verify.

Starter pattern

at-least-once delivery + idempotent sink(MERGE on key) = exactly-once EFFECT
 dedup with key/watermark; reconcile
Common mistake: Assuming the streaming framework gives true exactly-once everywhere and skipping idempotent sinks, then duplicating on replay.
Principal follow-up: Why is 'exactly-once' really exactly-once-effect, and what makes it real in practice?
045
Design or evaluate a solution for eventual consistency. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principaleventual consistency

Model answer

In distributed/async pipelines, replicas/derived views may be temporarily inconsistent before converging. Decide where strong consistency is required (payments) vs where eventual is acceptable (analytics), set freshness SLAs, and design consumers to tolerate lag.

  • Async systems converge over time.
  • Strong consistency where required (payments).
  • Eventual acceptable for analytics with freshness SLA.
  • Consumers tolerate lag.

Healthcare angle: Analytics marts can be eventually consistent with a freshness SLA; payment application needs stronger guarantees.

What strong answers cover

  1. Accept that distributed/replicated views converge over time; design consumers to tolerate brief staleness and surface freshness.
  2. Use it where strong consistency isn't required (analytics); reconcile to converge.
  3. Mark recent data provisional (IBNR parallels).
  4. Communicate freshness.
  5. Know where strong consistency IS required (payments).

Starter pattern

analytics: tolerate eventual consistency + show freshness
 payments/AR: require stronger consistency + reconciliation
Common mistake: Treating an eventually-consistent analytics view as instantly authoritative, or using it where payment integrity needs strong consistency.
Principal follow-up: Where is eventual consistency fine in a payer platform, and where do you need stronger guarantees?
046
Design or evaluate a solution for domain-driven data products. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principaldomain-driven data products

Model answer

Organize data by business domain (claims, membership, provider), each a product with an owner, contract, quality, and SLA, exposed to consumers via contracts. This federates governance and scales delivery beyond a central bottleneck (data-mesh thinking).

  • Domain-aligned data products with owners.
  • Contracts/quality/SLA per product.
  • Federated governance; scales delivery.
  • Consumers via contracts, not internals.

Healthcare angle: Claims, membership, and provider domains each own contracted data products under shared governance.

What strong answers cover

  1. Organize by domain data products (claims/membership/provider/pharmacy) with owners, contracts, quality, SLAs, on a self-serve platform.
  2. Federated governance with central PHI policy and standards.
  3. Scales delivery beyond a central bottleneck.
  4. Central PHI policy is non-negotiable.
  5. Clear ownership boundaries.

Starter pattern

domain products(owner + contract + SLA) on self-serve platform
 federated governance: central PHI policy + standards + lineage
Common mistake: Decentralizing PHI governance along with the domains, fragmenting compliance across teams.
Principal follow-up: What parts of a data-mesh approach fit a large payer, and what must stay centralized?
047
Design or evaluate a solution for semantic layer. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalsemantic layer

Model answer

A semantic layer centralizes metric/entity definitions (PMPM, denial rate, member) so all tools compute them identically over governed marts, preventing metric drift and disputes. Govern definitions as versioned, owned code.

  • Central metric/entity definitions over marts.
  • Consistent calculations across tools.
  • Prevents metric drift/disputes.
  • Govern definitions as code.

Healthcare angle: Defining PMPM once in a semantic layer stops finance and actuarial dashboards from disagreeing.

What strong answers cover

  1. Central, versioned metric/entity definitions (PMPM, denial rate, member) over certified marts, so every tool computes metrics identically.
  2. Owned and change-controlled.
  3. Prevents metric drift across BI tools.
  4. Underpinned by a claims ontology/glossary.
  5. One definition everywhere.

Starter pattern

semantic layer: one governed definition per metric over certified gold
 versioned + owned -> consistent across all tools
Common mistake: Each BI tool defining metrics itself, so the same metric drifts and dashboards disagree with no authority.
Principal follow-up: How does a semantic layer stop two dashboards from disagreeing on PMPM?
048
Design or evaluate a solution for API + batch hybrid. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
PrincipalAPI + batch hybrid

Model answer

Combine a low-latency API serving path (point lookups from a current-state store) with a batch analytics path (lakehouse/warehouse), fed from shared source data but optimized separately. Keep them consistent via shared source of truth and reconciliation.

  • API path: fast point lookups (current-state store).
  • Batch path: lakehouse/warehouse analytics.
  • Shared source of truth; reconcile.
  • Optimize each path separately.

Healthcare angle: An eligibility API (fast lookups) and analytics (batch) share source data but use different serving stores.

What strong answers cover

  1. Combine a low-latency API serving path (real-time eligibility/EOB) with a batch analytics path, sharing conformed data but isolated for performance.
  2. Idempotent capture of API transactions into the lakehouse.
  3. Reconcile serving vs analytics to one source of truth.
  4. Govern PHI on both paths.
  5. Separate hot serving from heavy analytics.

Starter pattern

API serving(low-latency, as-of) || async land -> lakehouse analytics
 shared conformed data; reconcile to source of truth
Common mistake: Coupling the real-time API to the batch pipeline, so analytics load degrades live response times.
Principal follow-up: How do you share data between a real-time API and batch analytics without one degrading the other?
049
Design or evaluate a solution for platform migration roadmap. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalplatform migration roadmap

Model answer

Sequence migration to reduce risk: inventory/prioritize, migrate by domain in waves, parallel-run with reconciliation, validate with control totals and consumer sign-off, keep rollback, then decommission after sustained parity. Lead with measurable value.

  • Inventory/prioritize; migrate in domain waves.
  • Parallel-run + reconcile before cutover.
  • Consumer sign-off; keep rollback.
  • Decommission after sustained parity.

Healthcare angle: Migrating claims to a new platform runs both in parallel with daily reconciliation until numbers match for several cycles.

What strong answers cover

  1. Sequence to reduce risk: inventory/prioritize, migrate by domain in waves, parallel-run with reconciliation to parity, consumer sign-off, keep rollback, decommission after parity.
  2. Lead with measurable value; communicate.
  3. Reconciliation-until-parity is the key discipline.
  4. Sequence by value/risk.
  5. Don't big-bang.

Starter pattern

inventory -> migrate by domain wave -> parallel-run + reconcile to parity
 consumer sign-off + rollback -> decommission
Common mistake: A big-bang cutover with no parallel-run reconciliation, so the new platform's numbers aren't trusted and adoption stalls.
Principal follow-up: How would you sequence a platform migration to keep consumers' trust throughout?
050
Design or evaluate a solution for principal design review. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalprincipal design review

Model answer

A principal-level design review checks grain/keys, history/SCD, idempotency/replay, reconciliation/DQ, PHI governance, SLA/observability, cost, lineage, failure/DR, and contracts against a repeatable bar — catching predictable failure modes and aligning to enterprise standards before build.

  • Grain/keys, history, idempotency, reconciliation, DQ.
  • PHI governance, SLA/observability, cost, lineage, DR.
  • Contracts + enterprise-standard alignment.
  • Repeatable bar catching predictable failures.

Healthcare angle: A claims platform review confirms grain, idempotency, reconciliation, PHI controls, and SLA before go-live.

What strong answers cover

  1. Review significant designs against a checklist: grain/keys, history/SCD, idempotency, reconciliation, DQ/quarantine, PHI governance, SLA/observability, cost, lineage, DR, contracts.
  2. Constructive feedback + ADRs; teach through the review.
  3. Catches predictable failure modes early.
  4. A quality gate, not a bottleneck.
  5. Same bar for every design.

Starter pattern

checklist: grain/idempotency/reconciliation/history/DQ/PHI/SLA/observability/DR/contracts
 constructive feedback + ADR
Common mistake: Either no design review (predictable failures reach prod) or a heavy bottleneck board that slows everything.
Principal follow-up: What's on your design-review checklist, and how do you keep it a gate without becoming a bottleneck?

Data Architecture

Dimensional modeling, medallion, data vault, bitemporal history, facts, dimensions and healthcare marts.

50 questions
001
Design or evaluate a solution for claim fact grain. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediumclaim fact grain

Model answer

The claim fact grain is the single thing one row represents. For claims it is usually one row per claim line for the latest version (claim_id+line), with the header as a separate fact or a parent. Declare the grain in one sentence and enforce it with a uniqueness test; everything else (keys, measures) follows from it.

  • State grain as one sentence; enforce with a uniqueness assertion.
  • Line-grain for line measures; header-grain for header measures — do not mix.
  • Keep the fact additive; push context to dimensions.
  • Latest-version handling is part of the grain decision.

Healthcare angle: Mixing header and line grain makes SUM(paid) double-count or lose detail; pick line grain for line-level analytics.

What strong answers cover

  1. State the grain in one sentence and enforce it: one row per claim line per version is the usual choice.
  2. Keep the fact additive (paid/allowed/billed); push descriptive context to dimensions.
  3. Handle versions (frequency 7/8) so you never sum across them.
  4. Partition by service_month; assert grain uniqueness after load.
  5. Grain drives everything downstream.

Starter pattern

-- grain: one row per claim_id + line_number + version
fact_claim_line(claim_id, line_no, version, member_sk, provider_sk,
  service_month, billed, allowed, paid)
Common mistake: Leaving the grain ambiguous so header and line (or versions) mix, double-counting paid amounts.
Principal follow-up: Why is claim-line-per-version the right grain, and how do you stop summing across versions?
002
Design or evaluate a solution for member dimension. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediummember dimension

Model answer

The member dimension holds member attributes (demographics, plan, eligibility-derived flags) keyed by a surrogate, with SCD2 for attributes that must be queried as-of (plan, address). Conform it across all marts so member-level analytics agree.

  • Surrogate key + natural key (member_id); SCD2 for as-of attributes.
  • Conformed across marts for consistency.
  • Separate slowly-changing (plan) from static (DOB) handling.
  • Mask PHI; minimum-necessary access.

Healthcare angle: Member plan/region must be queried as of service date for correct attribution, so those attributes are SCD2.

What strong answers cover

  1. Conform one member dimension with a surrogate key, identity resolution to a master member, and SCD2 for changing attributes.
  2. Bitemporal where eligibility history matters.
  3. Minimum-necessary PHI; mask/secure sensitive attributes.
  4. Support as-of lookups.
  5. Identity resolution is the hard part.

Starter pattern

dim_member(member_sk PK, master_member_id, ...attrs,
  valid_from, valid_to, is_current)  -- SCD2, identity-resolved
Common mistake: Keying the member dimension on a raw source id, so duplicate identities fragment member history and member-months.
Principal follow-up: How do identity resolution and SCD2 keep a member's history correct across source ids and changes?
003
Design or evaluate a solution for provider dimension. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediumprovider dimension

Model answer

The provider dimension holds provider attributes (name, specialty, taxonomy, network status, TIN) keyed by surrogate with NPI as natural key, SCD2 for time-varying attributes, and handling for late-arriving providers and many-to-many group affiliations (bridges).

  • Surrogate + NPI natural key; SCD2 for specialty/network/address.
  • Late-arriving provider handling (placeholder then enrich).
  • Many-to-many group/TIN via bridge tables.
  • Conform across claims/quality/network marts.

Healthcare angle: Provider network/par status changes over time and drives pricing, so it must be SCD2 and queried as-of service date.

What strong answers cover

  1. Conform a provider dimension keyed on a surrogate, with NPI + crosswalks, effective-dating, taxonomy, and geography.
  2. Use bridges for group/network many-to-many; as-of joins for attribution.
  3. Late providers get placeholders, enriched later.
  4. Effective-dating is essential.
  5. Identity resolution across ids.

Starter pattern

dim_provider(provider_sk PK, npi, taxonomy, ...,
  valid_from, valid_to, is_current) + provider_group_bridge
Common mistake: Using current affiliation (not as-of/effective-dated), so historical attribution is wrong after a change.
Principal follow-up: How do effective-dating and a group bridge keep historical attribution correct?
004
Design or evaluate a solution for date dimension. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediumdate dimension

Model answer

The date dimension is a dense calendar with one row per day and rich attributes (month, quarter, plan year, fiscal period, holiday flags), enabling consistent time analysis, rolling windows, and zero-filled trends. Generate it once as conformed reference.

  • One row per day with month/quarter/plan-year/fiscal attributes.
  • Dense (a spine) so metrics zero-fill correctly.
  • Conformed across all facts for consistent time logic.
  • Include plan-year boundaries for accumulator resets.

Healthcare angle: Plan-year boundaries in the date dimension drive deductible/accumulator resets and HEDIS measurement windows.

What strong answers cover

  1. One dense, conformed date dimension with calendar/fiscal/plan-year/holiday attributes that all facts join to.
  2. Drives consistent rolling windows, member-months, HEDIS windows, and zero-fill.
  3. Pre-populate the full range; no gaps.
  4. Plan-year attributes drive accumulator resets.
  5. A single spine prevents time-logic drift.

Starter pattern

dim_date(date_sk PK, date, year, month, plan_year,
  fiscal_period, is_holiday)  -- dense, all facts join
Common mistake: Each mart computing its own date logic, so PMPM, HEDIS windows, and trend use subtly different time logic and disagree.
Principal follow-up: Why is one conformed date dimension worth standardizing across every mart?
005
Design or evaluate a solution for claim line fact vs header fact. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediumclaim line fact vs header fact

Model answer

Header fact = one row per claim (totals, status, dates); line fact = one row per service line (procedure, line paid, diagnosis pointers). Line measures must sum to header totals. Model both with a clear parent-child relationship; analyze at the grain the question needs.

  • Header: claim totals/status/dates; line: per-service detail.
  • Line measures should reconcile to header totals.
  • Keep grains separate to avoid double-counting.
  • Diagnosis/procedure detail lives at line grain.

Healthcare angle: Line-level paid must roll up to header paid; reconciling the two is a basic claims integrity check.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model at line grain for service-level detail (procedure, dx, line paid); a header fact/aggregate serves claim-level metrics.
  2. Don't mix grains in one fact.
  3. Header totals must reconcile to the sum of lines.
  4. Choose grain by the questions asked.
  5. Keep both consistent.

Starter pattern

fact_claim_line(claim_id, line_no, ...)  -- detail
