fact_claim_header(claim_id, total_paid)   -- header == SUM(lines)
Common mistake: Mixing header and line rows in one fact, double-counting paid when both are summed.
Principal follow-up: When do you need a header fact alongside the line fact, and how do they stay reconciled?
006
Design or evaluate a solution for accumulating snapshot. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediumaccumulating snapshot

Model answer

An accumulating snapshot fact tracks a process with milestones in one row that updates as the process advances (e.g. claim received to adjudicated to paid to closed), with multiple date columns and durations. Use it to measure cycle times across pipeline stages.

  • One row per process instance, updated through milestones.
  • Multiple milestone dates + lag/duration measures.
  • Good for claim lifecycle cycle-time analysis.
  • Updates in place as the claim progresses.

Healthcare angle: Tracking claim received to paid cycle time fits an accumulating snapshot with received/adjudicated/paid dates.

What strong answers cover

  1. An accumulating snapshot tracks a process with milestone dates updated in place (e.g. claim received -> adjudicated -> paid).
  2. One row per process instance; columns for each milestone and lag.
  3. Good for pipeline/lifecycle metrics (turnaround time).
  4. Updated as the claim progresses.
  5. Different from immutable transaction facts.

Starter pattern

fact_claim_lifecycle(claim_id, received_dt, adjudicated_dt,
  paid_dt, days_to_pay)  -- updated in place per milestone
Common mistake: Modeling a lifecycle process as immutable transaction rows, making turnaround-time metrics awkward to compute.
Principal follow-up: When is an accumulating snapshot the right pattern for claim lifecycle metrics?
007
Design or evaluate a solution for periodic snapshot. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediumperiodic snapshot

Model answer

A periodic snapshot captures state at regular intervals (e.g. monthly member status, AR aging) — one row per entity per period — for trend and point-in-time reporting, even for entities with no activity that period.

  • One row per entity per period (month/day).
  • Captures state for trend and as-of reporting.
  • Includes inactive/zero-activity entities (via spine).
  • Good for AR aging, member-month, balances.

Healthcare angle: Member-month and monthly AR aging are periodic snapshots, giving consistent trend even when activity is zero.

What strong answers cover

  1. A periodic snapshot captures state at regular intervals (e.g. monthly member-months, AR aging).
  2. One row per entity per period; must be restatement-capable for late data.
  3. Recent periods are provisional (IBNR).
  4. Use a calendar spine for completeness.
  5. Reproducible point-in-time.

Starter pattern

fact_member_month(member_sk, month_sk, eligible_days,
  premium)  -- one row per member per month
Common mistake: Treating a periodic snapshot as immutable, so late claims never restate it and recent periods are silently wrong.
Principal follow-up: How do you make member-month/AR snapshots restate correctly for late-arriving data?
008
Design or evaluate a solution for transaction fact. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediumtransaction fact

Model answer

A transaction fact records one row per business event (a claim line, a payment, an adjustment) at the moment it occurs — the most granular, additive fact type. Most claims analytics start from transaction facts and aggregate up.

  • One row per event; most granular and additive.
  • Foundation for aggregates and snapshots.
  • Immutable events; corrections are new rows/versions.
  • Partition by event/service date.

Healthcare angle: The claim-line fact is a transaction fact; member-month and AR snapshots are derived from it.

What strong answers cover

  1. A transaction fact records discrete events (a claim line, a payment) at event grain, immutable and additive.
  2. Most claims modeling starts here.
  3. Attribute to event date; handle versions/reversals as new rows.
  4. Partition by event date.
  5. Foundation for snapshots/aggregates.

Starter pattern

fact_payment(payment_id, claim_id, payment_dt, amount)
  -- immutable event rows; reversals as new rows
Common mistake: Mutating transaction rows in place for adjustments instead of adding reversal/version rows, losing the audit chain.
Principal follow-up: Why model adjustments as new transaction rows rather than updating the original?
009
Design or evaluate a solution for star vs snowflake schema. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Mediumstar vs snowflake schema

Model answer

A star schema keeps dimensions denormalized (one table per dimension) for simple joins and BI performance; a snowflake schema normalizes dimensions into sub-tables, saving space but adding joins. Favor star for serving marts; reserve normalization for genuinely large/shared sub-hierarchies.

  • Star: denormalized dims, fewer joins, faster BI.
  • Snowflake: normalized dims, less redundancy, more joins.
  • Prefer star for consumption/serving layers.
  • Normalize only large shared hierarchies if needed.

Healthcare angle: Claims serving marts use a star (member/provider/date dims) for BI simplicity and predictable performance.

What strong answers cover

  1. Star = denormalized dimensions (fewer joins, BI-friendly); snowflake = normalized dimensions (less redundancy, more joins).
  2. Prefer star for serving/BI; normalize only where maintenance/consistency demands it.
  3. Conform dimensions either way.
  4. Decide by query patterns and maintenance.
  5. Star usually wins for analytics.

Starter pattern

-- star: fact -> dim_member, dim_provider, dim_date (denormalized)
-- snowflake: dim_provider -> dim_taxonomy -> ... (normalized)
Common mistake: Over-normalizing serving dimensions into a snowflake, adding joins that slow BI for little benefit.
Principal follow-up: When does normalizing a dimension (snowflaking) pay off versus a flat star?
010
Design or evaluate a solution for Kimball vs Inmon. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
MediumKimball vs Inmon

Model answer

Kimball builds dimensional marts bottom-up around business processes with conformed dimensions; Inmon builds a normalized enterprise data warehouse top-down, with marts derived from it. Many modern lakehouses blend them: a governed integrated layer (Inmon-ish) feeding dimensional marts (Kimball).

  • Kimball: bottom-up conformed dimensional marts.
  • Inmon: top-down normalized enterprise warehouse.
  • Modern lakehouse often blends: integrated layer to marts.
  • Choose by governance needs vs speed to value.

Healthcare angle: A payer might keep an integrated claims layer and serve Kimball-style dimensional marts to actuarial/BI.

What strong answers cover

  1. Kimball = bottom-up conformed dimensional marts (fast to value, BI-friendly); Inmon = top-down normalized enterprise warehouse feeding marts.
  2. Many payers blend: a conformed/normalized core feeding Kimball marts (often the medallion).
  3. Decide by governance needs and delivery speed.
  4. Conformed dimensions matter either way.
  5. Pragmatic hybrid is common.

Starter pattern

-- Kimball: conformed dimensional marts (bus matrix)
-- Inmon: normalized EDW -> dependent marts; hybrid via medallion
Common mistake: Treating it as a religious either/or instead of a pragmatic hybrid suited to the org's governance and speed needs.
Principal follow-up: How would you blend Kimball and Inmon ideas in a medallion lakehouse for a payer?
011
Design or evaluate a solution for Data Vault for payer hub. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
MediumData Vault for payer hub

Model answer

Data Vault models hubs (business keys), links (relationships), and satellites (descriptive, historized attributes), giving auditable, flexible, parallel-loadable history well-suited to integrating many source systems. It is an integration layer; you still build dimensional marts on top for consumption.

  • Hubs (keys), links (relationships), satellites (history).
  • Auditable, source-agnostic, parallel-loadable.
  • Great for integrating many payer source systems.
  • Build dimensional marts on top for consumers.

Healthcare angle: Integrating FACETS, QNXT, and external feeds into one auditable hub-and-satellite model, then serving Kimball marts, is a common payer pattern.

What strong answers cover

  1. Data Vault models hubs (business keys), links (relationships), and satellites (descriptive history) for auditable, agile integration.
  2. Strong for highly-integrated, audit-heavy, multi-source payer data; then serve Kimball marts on top.
  3. More complex; not a serving model.
  4. Good raw-integration layer.
  5. Pair with marts for consumption.

Starter pattern

hub_member(member_bk) , hub_provider(provider_bk)
 link_claim(member, provider) , sat_member(attrs, load_ts)
Common mistake: Serving BI directly off raw Data Vault structures, forcing many joins instead of building marts on top.
Principal follow-up: When does Data Vault earn its complexity for payer integration, and what serves consumption?
012
Design or evaluate a solution for Medallion architecture. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
MediumMedallion architecture

Model answer

Medallion layers data Bronze (raw), Silver (cleaned/conformed), Gold (business marts), giving clear contracts, replayability, and progressive quality. It is the lakehouse analog of staging/integration/presentation layers.

  • Bronze raw/replayable, Silver clean/conformed, Gold marts.
  • Clear contracts and quality gates per layer.
  • Reprocess downstream without re-ingesting source.
  • Lakehouse analog of staging/integration/presentation.

Healthcare angle: Raw 837/835 to clean claim-line facts to PMPM/denial marts maps directly onto Bronze/Silver/Gold.

What strong answers cover

  1. Bronze (raw/replayable) -> Silver (clean/conform) -> Gold (business marts), each a contract with quality gates.
  2. Reprocess downstream without re-ingesting; bronze keeps full fidelity.
  3. DQ/reconciliation at each boundary.
  4. Map dimensional modeling into silver/gold.
  5. Keep layer responsibilities clean.

Starter pattern

raw -> bronze(Delta, immutable) -> silver(conformed dims+facts)
 -> gold(PMPM/denial marts); DQ at each boundary
Common mistake: Putting business logic in bronze or letting gold read raw, collapsing the layering and losing replayability.
Principal follow-up: How does dimensional modeling map onto the medallion layers?
013
Design or evaluate a solution for surrogate vs natural keys. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedsurrogate vs natural keys

Model answer

Surrogate keys are system-generated, stable, and decouple the warehouse from source-key changes and SCD2 versioning; natural keys (NPI, member_id) carry business meaning but can change or be reused. Use surrogates as join keys, keep natural keys as attributes.

  • Surrogates: stable, integer, support SCD2 versioning.
  • Natural keys: business meaning but volatile/reusable.
  • Join on surrogates; retain natural keys for lookup.
  • Surrogates isolate the model from source key churn.

Healthcare angle: A member's surrogate key stays stable across plan changes and SCD2 versions even if the source member_id format changes.

What strong answers cover

  1. Surrogate keys (system-generated) decouple facts from volatile/composite natural keys and enable SCD2 versioning.
  2. Keep natural/business keys as attributes for lookup and reconciliation.
  3. Facts join on surrogate keys.
  4. SCD2 requires surrogates (one per version).
  5. Map natural->surrogate at load.

Starter pattern

dim_member(member_sk PK surrogate, member_id natural_key, ...)
fact joins on member_sk; member_id kept for lookup
Common mistake: Using the natural key as the fact's foreign key, so SCD2 versioning and key changes break the joins.
Principal follow-up: Why do surrogate keys enable SCD2, and why keep the natural key around too?
014
Design or evaluate a solution for late-arriving dimensions. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedlate-arriving dimensions

Model answer

When a fact references a dimension member not yet loaded, create an inferred placeholder (keyed on the natural key) so the fact loads, then enrich the placeholder when the real dimension record arrives. Avoid dumping everything into a generic unknown key.

  • Insert inferred placeholder keyed on natural key.
  • Enrich when the real record arrives.
  • Track inferred vs confirmed status.
  • Alert if inferred volume is high (feed problem).

Healthcare angle: Claims often reference providers before the roster loads; placeholder-then-enrich keeps attribution fixable.

What strong answers cover

  1. When a fact references a dimension member not yet present, insert an inferred placeholder (surrogate keyed on the natural key), enrich later.
  2. Keeps facts loadable and attributable.
  3. Track inferred vs confirmed; alert if inferred volume is high.
  4. Avoid a single generic 'unknown'.
  5. Reconcile when the real record arrives.

Starter pattern

-- on fact load: if member_id not in dim -> INSERT placeholder(is_inferred=true)
-- later: enrich placeholder when real record arrives
Common mistake: Routing all late dimension members to one 'UNKNOWN' surrogate, losing the ability to fix attribution later.
Principal follow-up: How do placeholders for late dimensions keep facts attributable without a generic unknown bucket?
015
Design or evaluate a solution for late-arriving facts. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedlate-arriving facts

Model answer

Facts arriving after their event date must land in the correct historical partition (by event date) and reopen affected aggregates/snapshots. Track the lateness distribution to bound reprocessing and keep aggregates restatable.

  • Attribute by event/service date, not load date.
  • Reopen/restate affected aggregates and snapshots.
  • Track lateness to size reprocessing windows.
  • Communicate restatements to consumers.

Healthcare angle: Claims commonly arrive weeks late; IBNR/runout analysis exists because of this, and marts must restate.

What strong answers cover

  1. Attribute late facts by event/service date (not load date) and reopen/restate affected snapshots/aggregates.
  2. Join to the dimension version valid as-of the event date.
  3. Track lateness to bound reprocessing; mark recent periods provisional.
  4. Keep aggregates restatement-capable.
  5. Validate restated totals.

Starter pattern

-- write to partition of service_month (event date)
-- as-of join to dim version; reopen + restate aggregates
Common mistake: Attributing late claims to load date and joining to current dim rows, so history never restates and is mis-attributed.
Principal follow-up: How do you place a late claim in the right period and join it to the right dimension version?
016
Design or evaluate a solution for SCD Type 2 membership. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
AdvancedSCD Type 2 membership

Model answer

Membership SCD2 versions coverage/plan/demographic changes with effective dates and a current flag, enabling as-of eligibility, member-month, and attribution logic, plus retroactive corrections via bitemporal handling.

  • Version changes with valid_from/valid_to + is_current.
  • As-of eligibility and member-month logic.
  • Bitemporal handling for retro corrections.
  • Hash tracked columns to avoid churn.

Healthcare angle: Eligibility/plan changes must be queried as of service date; SCD2 membership makes member-months and attribution correct.

What strong answers cover

  1. SCD2 versions changing member attributes: close the old row (valid_to, is_current=false) and insert a new current row.
  2. Detect change via a hash; guarantee one current row per member.
  3. Bitemporal where retro corrections matter.
  4. Idempotent merge; handle out-of-order.
  5. Enables as-of member lookups.

Starter pattern

dim_member(member_sk, member_id, attrs, valid_from, valid_to, is_current)
-- MERGE: close current on hash change, insert new version
Common mistake: A non-idempotent close+insert leaving two current rows (or zero) on a rerun, fanning out joins.
Principal follow-up: How do you guarantee exactly one current row per member, even on reruns?
017
Design or evaluate a solution for bitemporal provider history. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedbitemporal provider history

Model answer

Bitemporal modeling keeps two time axes: business validity (valid_from/to) and system knowledge time (system_from/to), so you can answer both what was true and what we knew then. It is essential when provider data is corrected retroactively.

  • Two axes: business validity + system knowledge time.
  • Answer as-of-event and as-of-known questions.
  • Supports auditable retroactive corrections.
  • More complex but necessary for restatements.

Healthcare angle: When a provider's specialty is corrected retroactively, bitemporal history reproduces both the old and corrected views for audit.

What strong answers cover

  1. Track two time axes: business validity (when an affiliation was effective) and knowledge time (when we learned it).
  2. Answers both what was true and what we knew then; handles retro provider changes auditably.
  3. Enables exact historical reconstruction.
  4. More complex but necessary for restatement.
  5. Foundation for as-of attribution.

Starter pattern

dim_provider(provider_sk, ..., valid_from, valid_to,
  system_from, system_to)  -- bitemporal
Common mistake: Single-time-axis provider history, so after a retro change you can't reproduce what a prior report originally showed.
Principal follow-up: What questions does the second (knowledge) time axis answer that business validity alone can't?
018
Design or evaluate a solution for effective dating. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedeffective dating

Model answer

Effective dating attaches validity spans to dimension rows so facts join to the version effective at their date. Spans must be non-overlapping and contiguous (or intentionally gapped) for as-of joins to return exactly one row.

  • Validity spans (valid_from/valid_to) per version.
  • As-of joins use service_dt BETWEEN the span.
  • Spans must be non-overlapping for one-row matches.
  • Detect/repair overlaps as a DQ control.

Healthcare angle: Effective-dated provider/member spans let claims price against the correct historical version.

What strong answers cover

  1. Effective-dated rows carry valid_from/valid_to so you can join facts to the version valid at a point in time.
  2. Non-overlapping, gap-free spans per key are the invariant.
  3. Powers as-of joins and SCD2.
  4. Detect/repair overlaps as a DQ control.
  5. Closed vs open current row.

Starter pattern

WHERE :as_of BETWEEN valid_from AND valid_to
-- spans must be non-overlapping and contiguous per key
Common mistake: Allowing overlapping effective-dated spans, so an as-of join returns multiple rows and fans out the fact.
Principal follow-up: What invariants must effective-dated spans satisfy, and how do you enforce them?
019
Design or evaluate a solution for as-of joins. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedas-of joins

Model answer

An as-of join matches a fact to the dimension version valid at the fact's date (service_dt BETWEEN valid_from AND valid_to), not the current row, ensuring correct historical attribution and pricing. Requires clean non-overlapping spans.

  • Join to the version valid at the event date.
  • Requires unique, non-overlapping spans.
  • Prevents using current attributes for past events.
  • Core to correct claims pricing/attribution.

Healthcare angle: Pricing a claim by the provider's network status as of service date is an as-of join, not a current-row join.

What strong answers cover

  1. An as-of join attaches the dimension version valid at the fact's event date (not the current row).
  2. Join on key plus event_date BETWEEN valid_from AND valid_to.
  3. Essential for correct historical attribution and pricing.
  4. Requires clean effective-dated spans.
  5. Returns exactly one version per fact.

Starter pattern

fact f JOIN dim d ON f.key=d.key
 AND f.service_dt BETWEEN d.valid_from AND d.valid_to
Common mistake: Joining facts to the current dimension row instead of the as-of version, mis-stating historical attribution.
Principal follow-up: Why must historical claims join to the as-of dimension version rather than the current row?
020
Design or evaluate a solution for bridge tables. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedbridge tables

Model answer

Bridge tables resolve many-to-many relationships (a claim to many diagnoses, a provider to many groups) with optional weighting factors to allocate measures without double-counting. They sit between a fact and a dimension.

  • Resolve many-to-many (claim-diagnosis, provider-group).
  • Optional weighting to allocate measures correctly.
  • Prevent double-counting from naive joins.
  • Effective-date bridges when relationships change.

Healthcare angle: A claim's multiple diagnoses are modeled with a diagnosis bridge so paid amounts are not multiplied per diagnosis.

What strong answers cover

  1. Bridge tables resolve many-to-many relationships (provider-to-group, claim-to-diagnosis) between a fact and a dimension.
  2. Carry the relationship and sometimes a weighting/allocation factor.
  3. Avoid fan-out by using allocation weights where needed.
  4. Keep grain explicit.
  5. Common in payer modeling.

Starter pattern

claim_diagnosis_bridge(claim_sk, diagnosis_sk, dx_sequence)
-- resolves claim<->many diagnoses without fan-out in the fact
Common mistake: Denormalizing a many-to-many into the fact (or naive join), multiplying paid amounts via fan-out.
Principal follow-up: How does a bridge table prevent fan-out, and when do you need an allocation weight?
021
Design or evaluate a solution for many-to-many provider relationships. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedmany-to-many provider relationships

Model answer

Providers belong to multiple groups/TINs/networks over time, a many-to-many that needs a bridge with effective dates and possibly role/weight. Naive joins fan out and double-count; the bridge plus as-of logic keeps measures correct.

  • Bridge with effective dates + role/weight.
  • As-of logic for time-varying affiliations.
  • Avoids fan-out double-counting.
  • Supports network and attribution analytics.

Healthcare angle: A provider in two groups at once needs a weighted/effective-dated bridge so attributed spend is allocated, not duplicated.

What strong answers cover

  1. Providers belong to multiple groups/networks over time — model via an effective-dated bridge, not flattened into the dimension.
  2. As-of join through the bridge for attribution.
  3. Handle weighting where a claim spans relationships.
  4. Avoid fan-out.
  5. Effective-date the bridge.

Starter pattern

provider_group_bridge(provider_sk, group_sk, valid_from, valid_to)
-- as-of join; avoid fan-out with explicit grain/weights
Common mistake: Flattening one provider's many group affiliations into the provider row, losing relationships and breaking attribution.
Principal follow-up: How do you model a provider in multiple groups over time without fan-out or losing history?
022
Design or evaluate a solution for diagnosis bridge. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advanceddiagnosis bridge

Model answer

A diagnosis bridge links a claim/line to its multiple diagnosis codes with sequence (primary, secondary), enabling diagnosis-based analysis (chronic conditions, HCC) without multiplying claim measures. Preserve ordinal position.

  • Link claim/line to many diagnoses with sequence.
  • Primary vs secondary position preserved.
  • Enables HCC/chronic analysis without double-counting paid.
  • Source from exploded 837 diagnosis pointers.

Healthcare angle: HCC/risk and chronic-condition analytics use the diagnosis bridge; paid amounts stay on the claim, not per-diagnosis.

What strong answers cover

  1. A claim has multiple diagnoses — model a claim-to-diagnosis bridge with sequence (primary vs secondary).
  2. Avoids exploding the fact or losing dx detail.
  3. Supports condition/HCC analytics via the bridge.
  4. Keep dx ordinal position.
  5. Join through the bridge for dx-level analysis.

Starter pattern

claim_dx_bridge(claim_sk, dx_sk, dx_seq)  -- primary=1
-- HCC/condition analytics join through the bridge
Common mistake: Cramming a fixed number of dx columns into the fact (dx1..dx12), losing diagnoses beyond the limit and ordinal clarity.
Principal follow-up: Why is a diagnosis bridge better than fixed dx columns on the claim fact?
023
Design or evaluate a solution for procedure bridge. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedprocedure bridge

Model answer

A procedure bridge links claim lines to procedure codes/modifiers where a line maps to multiple procedures or you need procedure-level rollups distinct from line grain. Often the line already is the procedure grain; use a bridge only when the relationship is genuinely many-to-many.

  • Link lines to procedures/modifiers when many-to-many.
  • Often line grain already equals procedure grain.
  • Use a bridge only for true many-to-many.
  • Supports procedure-level analytics.

Healthcare angle: Procedure/CPT analytics usually sit at line grain; a bridge is needed only when a line carries multiple procedures.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model claim/line procedures (and modifiers) via a bridge when multiple procedures relate to a claim beyond the line grain.
  2. Preserve procedure codes/modifiers for analytics.
  3. Keep grain explicit to avoid fan-out.
  4. Supports utilization/coding analytics.
  5. Join through the bridge.

Starter pattern

claim_procedure_bridge(claim_sk, proc_code, modifier, seq)
-- preserves multiple procedures/modifiers without fan-out
Common mistake: Losing secondary procedures/modifiers by forcing them into fixed line columns, corrupting utilization analytics.
Principal follow-up: When do you need a procedure bridge rather than relying on the line grain alone?
024
Design or evaluate a solution for CARC/RARC bridge. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
AdvancedCARC/RARC bridge

Model answer

A claim/line can carry multiple CARC (Claim Adjustment Reason) and RARC (Remittance Advice Remark) codes, so a bridge links them to the line, enabling denial-reason analysis without multiplying paid/adjusted amounts.

  • Link line to multiple CARC/RARC codes.
  • Enables denial-reason frequency/root-cause analysis.
  • Keeps adjustment amounts on the line, not per-code.
  • Source from 835 CAS segments.

Healthcare angle: Denial analytics aggregate CARC/RARC via the bridge while paid/adjusted amounts remain at line grain.

What strong answers cover

  1. A denied claim/line can carry multiple CARC/RARC codes — model a bridge from claim-line to denial codes.
  2. Preserves all denial reasons for accurate root-cause analytics.
  3. Map codes to governed buckets via reference data.
  4. Avoid losing secondary reasons.
  5. Join through the bridge for denial analysis.

Starter pattern

line_denial_bridge(line_sk, carc_code, rarc_code, seq)
-- multiple denial reasons per line; map to buckets via ref
Common mistake: Keeping only the first denial code on the line, so denial root-cause analytics miss secondary reasons.
Principal follow-up: Why model denial codes as a bridge, and how does it improve denial root-cause analysis?
025
Design or evaluate a solution for member-month fact. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedmember-month fact

Model answer

A member-month fact has one row per member per eligible month — the denominator for PMPM, utilization rates, and HEDIS rates. Built from membership spans against a calendar spine, it must reflect partial-month and retro eligibility correctly.

  • One row per member per eligible month.
  • Denominator for PMPM/utilization/HEDIS rates.
  • Built from coverage spans + calendar spine.
  • Handle partial months and retro eligibility.

Healthcare angle: PMPM = paid / member-months; the member-month fact is the denominator and must restate on retro eligibility changes.

What strong answers cover

  1. A member-month periodic snapshot (one row per member per eligible month) is the denominator backbone for PMPM, HEDIS, and capitation.
  2. Derive from effective-dated eligibility with correct partial-month handling.
  3. Restatement-capable for retro membership.
  4. Use a calendar spine.
  5. Reconcile to eligibility.

Starter pattern

fact_member_month(member_sk, month_sk, eligible_days, member_months)
-- derived from effective-dated eligibility; restate on retro
Common mistake: Computing member-months loosely (partial months, retro changes) so every per-member metric (PMPM) is off.
Principal follow-up: Why is the member-month fact the backbone of payer analytics, and how do retro changes restate it?
026
Design or evaluate a solution for provider attribution. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedprovider attribution

Model answer

Provider attribution assigns members/episodes to a responsible provider (e.g. PCP by plurality of visits) for value-based care and quality measurement. It needs clear rules, effective dating, and tie-breakers, and must restate when provider/claims data changes retroactively.

  • Rule-based assignment (e.g. plurality of PCP visits).
  • Effective-dated with tie-breakers.
  • Drives VBC and quality measurement.
  • Restates on retro provider/claims changes.

Healthcare angle: Attributing members to a PCP for value-based contracts must follow documented rules and restate if claims arrive late.

What strong answers cover

  1. Attribute members/claims to providers per contract rules (PCP assignment, plurality of visits), as-of the period.
  2. Use the effective-dated provider dimension and as-of joins; restate on roster/affiliation change.
  3. Feeds VBC, quality, and cost analytics.
  4. Rules vary by contract.
  5. Reproducible and auditable.

Starter pattern

attribution(member_sk, provider_sk, period, rule)
-- as-of join; restate on affiliation/roster change
Common mistake: Using current affiliation rather than as-of, so a retro change silently re-attributes historical members.
Principal follow-up: How do contract rules and as-of joins drive correct, restatement-capable attribution?
027
Design or evaluate a solution for HEDIS measure marts. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
AdvancedHEDIS measure marts

Model answer

HEDIS measure marts compute quality measures with precise numerator/denominator/exclusion logic per measure spec, using member-months for continuous enrollment and claim/encounter evidence for the numerator. They must be reproducible and auditable against the spec.

  • Numerator/denominator/exclusions per measure spec.
  • Continuous-enrollment via member-months (allow 45-day gap).
  • Numerator from claims/encounters/labs.
  • Reproducible and auditable to the HEDIS spec.

Healthcare angle: A HEDIS mart encodes each measure's exact eligibility, exclusion, and numerator rules, defensible in audit.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model HEDIS marts to spec: eligible-population denominator (continuous enrollment, gaps, exclusions), numerator evidence, as-of the measurement period.
  2. Member-month rigor; reproducible and auditable.
  3. Reconcile denominators to spec.
  4. Handle late-data restatement.
  5. One governed measure definition.

Starter pattern

fact_hedis(measure, member_sk, period, in_denominator, in_numerator,
  excluded)  -- spec-validated, reproducible
Common mistake: Loose continuous-enrollment/gap handling that silently shifts the denominator and moves the quality rate.
Principal follow-up: How do you make a HEDIS denominator defensible to spec and reproducible after late data?
028
Design or evaluate a solution for risk score fact. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertrisk score fact

Model answer

A risk score fact stores member RAF/HCC scores per measurement period, derived from diagnoses mapped to HCCs, used for risk adjustment, revenue, and stratification. It must be reproducible (model version, diagnosis source) and restatable as diagnoses arrive late.

  • Member RAF/HCC score per period.
  • Derived from diagnoses to HCC mapping (model version tracked).
  • Drives risk-adjusted revenue and stratification.
  • Restates as late diagnoses arrive.

Healthcare angle: RAF scores feed risk-adjusted payment; tracking model version and diagnosis source makes score drift explainable.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model RAF/risk scores with the contributing HCCs and model version, from versioned, reproducible inputs.
  2. Score drift must be explainable; keep diagnosis lineage.
  3. Effective-dated to the scoring period.
  4. Auditable and defensible.
  5. Restate on data changes.

Starter pattern

fact_risk_score(member_sk, period, model_version, raf,
  hcc_set)  -- versioned inputs; reproducible
Common mistake: No model/feature versioning, so a RAF change is an unexplainable mystery in an incident and indefensible in audit.
Principal follow-up: How do you make a RAF change explainable, separating a model-version change from a population shift?
029
Design or evaluate a solution for denial fact. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertdenial fact

Model answer

A denial fact captures denied claim lines with CARC/RARC reasons, amounts, and dates, enabling denial-rate, root-cause, and recovery analytics. Keyed at line grain with a CARC/RARC bridge for multiple reasons.

  • Denied lines with reasons/amounts/dates at line grain.
  • CARC/RARC bridge for multiple reasons.
  • Supports denial-rate and root-cause analytics.
  • Feeds payment-integrity and provider education.

Healthcare angle: Denial analytics drive provider education and rework prioritization; the denial fact plus CARC bridge powers them.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model denials at claim-line grain with a CARC/RARC bridge for multiple reasons, mapped to governed buckets.
  2. Preserve all denial reasons for accurate root-cause analytics.
  3. Distinguish denials from adjustments/reversals.
  4. Feed denial-prevention analytics.
  5. Governed mapping.

Starter pattern

fact_denial(line_sk, period, denied_amt) + line_denial_bridge(carc,rarc)
-- map codes -> governed buckets via reference
Common mistake: Keeping only the first denial code and a single bucket, so denial root-cause analytics miss secondary reasons.
Principal follow-up: How does a denial fact plus CARC/RARC bridge improve denial root-cause analysis?
030
Design or evaluate a solution for AR aging fact. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
ExpertAR aging fact

Model answer

An AR aging fact is a periodic snapshot of outstanding receivables bucketed by age (0-30, 31-60, etc.), used for revenue-cycle monitoring. It is sensitive to duplicate/void remittances and must reconcile to payments.

  • Periodic snapshot of outstanding AR by age bucket.
  • Drives revenue-cycle monitoring.
  • Sensitive to duplicate/void 835s.
  • Must reconcile to payments/remittances.

Healthcare angle: A duplicate 835 corrupts AR aging; the AR fact must be built from idempotent, reconciled remittance data.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model AR aging as a periodic snapshot (balance by age bucket per period), restatement-capable for late payments/adjustments.
  2. Idempotent payment application (remittance identity) underpins correct AR.
  3. Reconcile to source paid totals.
  4. Recent periods provisional.
  5. Handle reversals/recoupments.

Starter pattern

fact_ar_aging(period, age_bucket, balance)
-- periodic snapshot; restate on late payment/adjustment
Common mistake: Building AR aging on non-idempotent payment application, so a duplicate 835 corrupts the aging buckets.
Principal follow-up: Why must AR aging be restatement-capable and built on idempotent payment application?
031
Design or evaluate a solution for semantic layer design. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertsemantic layer design

Model answer

A semantic layer centralizes metric and entity definitions (PMPM, denial rate, member) so all tools compute them identically, decoupling business meaning from physical tables and preventing metric drift across reports.

  • Central metric/entity definitions over physical marts.
  • Consistent calculations across BI tools.
  • Prevents the same metric disagreeing across reports.
  • Govern definitions as code.

Healthcare angle: Defining PMPM once in a semantic layer stops finance and actuarial dashboards from quietly disagreeing.

What strong answers cover

  1. Central, versioned metric/entity definitions (PMPM, denial rate, member) over certified marts so every tool computes them identically.
  2. Owned and change-controlled; underpinned by a claims ontology/glossary.
  3. Prevents metric drift across BI tools.
  4. One definition everywhere.
  5. Maps to conformed dimensions.

Starter pattern

semantic layer: PMPM = SUM(allowed)/SUM(member_months) defined once
 over certified gold; versioned + owned
Common mistake: Each BI tool defining metrics itself, so the same metric drifts and dashboards disagree with no authority.
Principal follow-up: How does a semantic layer stop two dashboards from disagreeing on PMPM?
032
Design or evaluate a solution for conformed dimensions. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertconformed dimensions

Model answer

Conformed dimensions are shared, consistent dimensions (member, provider, date) used identically across multiple facts/marts, enabling cross-process analysis and consistent filtering/labels. They are the backbone of an integrated warehouse.

  • Shared, identical dimensions across facts/marts.
  • Enable cross-process drill-across analysis.
  • Consistent keys, attributes, and labels.
  • Govern centrally to prevent divergence.

Healthcare angle: A conformed member dimension lets claims, pharmacy, and quality marts agree on who a member is.

What strong answers cover

  1. Conformed dimensions (member, provider, date) are shared identically across all facts/marts, enabling consistent cross-domain analysis (the bus matrix).
  2. One member/provider/date definition org-wide.
  3. Foundation for Payer 360 and trustworthy cross-mart joins.
  4. Govern centrally.
  5. Avoid per-mart copies.

Starter pattern

shared dim_member / dim_provider / dim_date across claims,
 pharmacy, eligibility marts  -- Kimball bus matrix
Common mistake: Each mart building its own member/provider dimension, so cross-domain analysis is impossible and numbers disagree.
Principal follow-up: Why are conformed dimensions the prerequisite for cross-domain (Payer 360) analytics?
033
Design or evaluate a solution for data product ownership. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertdata product ownership

Model answer

Data-product thinking assigns each dataset an owner accountable for its contract, quality, SLA, and documentation, treating consumers as customers. It scales governance by federating responsibility to domains rather than a central bottleneck.

  • Each data product has an accountable owner.
  • Owner guarantees contract/quality/SLA/docs.
  • Consumers treated as customers.
  • Federates governance to domains.

Healthcare angle: Claims, membership, and provider data products each have owners accountable for their grain, SLA, and quality.

What strong answers cover

  1. Each domain data product (claims/membership/provider) has an owner, contract, quality SLA, and documentation.
  2. Ownership drives accountability for quality, freshness, and definitions.
  3. Federated governance with central PHI policy.
  4. Clear boundaries between products.
  5. Consumers know who owns what.

Starter pattern

data product(owner, contract, SLA, docs) per domain
 federated governance + central PHI policy
Common mistake: No clear ownership, so quality and definitions drift and no one is accountable when a product breaks.
Principal follow-up: How does data-product ownership improve quality and accountability versus a shared free-for-all?
034
Design or evaluate a solution for domain boundaries. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertdomain boundaries

Model answer

Domain boundaries separate data by business capability (claims, membership, provider, pharmacy), each owning its data products with explicit contracts at the seams. Clear boundaries reduce coupling and clarify ownership; cross-domain needs go through contracts, not back doors.

  • Separate by business capability/domain.
  • Each domain owns its products with contracts.
  • Cross-domain access via contracts, not back doors.
  • Reduces coupling and clarifies ownership.

Healthcare angle: The claims domain exposes a contracted claim-line product the analytics domain consumes, rather than reaching into claims internals.

What strong answers cover

  1. Draw domain boundaries around cohesive business areas (claims, membership, provider, pharmacy) with clear interfaces/contracts.
  2. Minimize cross-domain coupling; share via conformed dimensions and contracts.
  3. Enables independent ownership and evolution.
  4. Avoid a tangled monolith.
  5. Central standards across domains.

Starter pattern

domains: claims | membership | provider | pharmacy
 shared via conformed dims + contracts; minimal coupling
Common mistake: Tangled cross-domain dependencies so a change in one domain unexpectedly breaks others.
Principal follow-up: How do you draw domain boundaries that minimize coupling while sharing conformed dimensions?
035
Design or evaluate a solution for data contract grain. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertdata contract grain

Model answer

A data contract specifies a dataset's grain, schema, semantics, quality guarantees, and SLA, so producers and consumers agree explicitly and changes are governed. Grain is the contract's foundation — ambiguity there breaks everything downstream.

  • Contract = grain + schema + semantics + quality + SLA.
  • Grain is the foundation; state it precisely.
  • Changes are versioned and negotiated.
  • Enables independent producer/consumer evolution.

Healthcare angle: A claim-line product's contract pins grain (one row per line/version), schema, and freshness so consumers can rely on it.

What strong answers cover

  1. A data contract must pin the grain explicitly (e.g. one row per claim line per version) alongside schema, semantics, and freshness.
  2. Grain ambiguity is the most common contract gap and cause of double-counting.
  3. Enforce grain uniqueness at the boundary.
  4. Producer accountability.
  5. Validate on ingest.

Starter pattern

contract: grain='one row per claim_id+line+version'
 + schema + semantics + freshness; assert grain uniqueness
Common mistake: A contract that omits grain, so the producer changes it (header vs line) and consumers silently double-count.
Principal follow-up: Why is grain the most important (and most often missing) part of a data contract?
036
Design or evaluate a solution for lineage model. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertlineage model

Model answer

A lineage model captures how data flows source to consumer at table and column level, enabling impact analysis, trust, audit, and faster incident triage. Capture it automatically (Unity Catalog/ACCESS_HISTORY) plus business-level documentation.

  • Table + column lineage source to consumer.
  • Impact analysis before changes.
  • Trust, audit, and faster incident triage.
  • Automated capture + business documentation.

Healthcare angle: When a PMPM number is disputed, lineage traces it from the gold mart back to the source claims feed.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model lineage at table and column level, source-to-consumer, captured automatically plus business context.
  2. Enables impact analysis and dispute resolution (trace a number to source).
  3. Foundational for audit and trust.
  4. Spans the medallion layers.
  5. Persisted and queryable.

Starter pattern

lineage: source -> bronze -> silver -> gold -> report (column-level)
-- impact analysis + dispute resolution + audit
Common mistake: No column-level lineage, so a disputed PMPM can't be traced and a schema change's blast radius is unknown.
Principal follow-up: How does column lineage let you trace a disputed number and scope a change's impact?
037
Design or evaluate a solution for audit columns. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertaudit columns

Model answer

Audit columns (created_ts, updated_ts, source_file, batch_id, row_hash, is_current) on every table provide traceability, support incremental loads and SCD2, and supply audit evidence. They are cheap and invaluable for debugging and compliance.

  • created/updated ts, source_file, batch_id, row_hash, flags.
  • Enable incremental loads, SCD2, and dedup.
  • Traceability from a row to its load/source.
  • Audit/compliance evidence.

Healthcare angle: source_file and batch_id let you trace a bad claim row back to the exact file and load that produced it.

What strong answers cover

  1. Standard audit columns on every table: load timestamp, source file/batch id, record hash, and (for SCD2) valid_from/to + is_current.
  2. Enable reconciliation, debugging, dedup, and audit evidence.
  3. Exclude audit columns from change-detection hashes.
  4. Consistent across tables.
  5. Cheap, high-value.

Starter pattern

...business cols, _load_ts, _source_file, _batch_id, _row_hash,
 valid_from, valid_to, is_current
Common mistake: Tables with no audit columns, so you can't trace a row to its source file/batch or reconcile a load.
Principal follow-up: What standard audit columns do you add to every table, and what does each enable?
038
Design or evaluate a solution for soft delete modeling. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertsoft delete modeling

Model answer

Soft deletes mark rows inactive (is_active/deleted_flag/end-date) instead of physically removing them, preserving history that facts reference. Honor source deletes by end-dating SCD2 spans; only hard-delete when retention/right-to-delete requires it.

  • Mark inactive/end-date instead of physical delete.
  • Preserve history facts depend on.
  • Honor source deletes via SCD2 end-dating.
  • Hard-delete only for retention/right-to-delete.

Healthcare angle: A provider leaving the network is soft-deleted so past claims still join to the provider as it existed at service time.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model a source delete as an end-date/flag (is_active=false / valid_to=now), never a physical delete (facts still reference it).
  2. Distinguish a true delete from a record absent in a partial feed.
  3. Preserve history for as-of joins.
  4. Reversible and auditable.
  5. Downstream filters on is_active.

Starter pattern

-- on delete: UPDATE SET is_active=false, valid_to=now
-- never hard-delete a referenced dimension row
Common mistake: Hard-deleting a dimension row that historical claims join to, breaking attribution for past periods.
Principal follow-up: How do you tell a real delete from a record simply missing in a partial feed?
039
Design or evaluate a solution for de-identification model. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertde-identification model

Model answer

De-identification removes/obscures PHI to enable broader analytics, via HIPAA Safe Harbor (strip 18 identifiers) or Expert Determination. Model it as a governed transformation producing a de-identified dataset with documented method, while keeping the identified source restricted.

  • Safe Harbor (remove 18 identifiers) or Expert Determination.
  • Governed transform to a de-identified dataset.
  • Document the method for compliance.
  • Keep identified source tightly restricted.

Healthcare angle: A research/analytics sandbox uses a Safe Harbor de-identified claims dataset so PHI exposure is minimized.

What strong answers cover

  1. Produce de-identified datasets per HIPAA Safe Harbor (remove 18 identifiers) or Expert Determination (statistical), for broad analytics.
  2. Restrict the identified source tightly; document the method.
  3. Manage re-identification risk from quasi-identifiers.
  4. Separate identified vs de-identified models.
  5. Governed transformation.

Starter pattern

de_id_member: drop 18 identifiers (Safe Harbor) or expert-determined
-- restrict identified source; manage quasi-identifier risk
Common mistake: Incomplete de-identification leaving quasi-identifiers (rare zip+DOB), allowing re-identification — a compliance failure.
Principal follow-up: When do you use Safe Harbor vs Expert Determination, and what re-identification risks remain?
040
Design or evaluate a solution for PHI minimization. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
ExpertPHI minimization

Model answer

PHI minimization (HIPAA minimum-necessary) means each consumer sees only the PHI required for their purpose, enforced via masking, row policies, de-identification, and least-privilege roles. Design data products so most consumers never touch raw identifiers.

  • Minimum-necessary: only required PHI per consumer.
  • Masking, row policies, de-identification, least privilege.
  • Most consumers use masked/de-identified views.
  • Audit and review access regularly.

Healthcare angle: Analysts get masked/de-identified claims; only a narrow role accesses raw member identifiers for legitimate needs.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model so most consumers never see raw PHI: masked/de-identified views, minimum-necessary columns, row-level security.
  2. Keep identified PHI in a narrow, audited layer.
  3. Design marts to expose only what's needed.
  4. Minimum-necessary by design.
  5. Audit access.

Starter pattern

gold marts expose minimum-necessary fields; PHI masked by role
 identified PHI in narrow audited layer
Common mistake: Carrying full PHI into every mart for convenience, expanding the exposure surface beyond minimum-necessary.
Principal follow-up: How do you design marts so they expose only minimum-necessary PHI?
041
Design or evaluate a solution for reference data management. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalreference data management

Model answer

Reference data (code sets: CARC/RARC, CPT, ICD, NDC, taxonomy, plan codes) must be centrally mastered, versioned, effective-dated, and governed so every pipeline maps codes consistently and stale/unmapped codes are caught.

  • Central, versioned, effective-dated code sets.
  • Consistent mapping across pipelines.
  • Detect/alert on stale or unmapped codes.
  • Govern ownership of reference data.

Healthcare angle: An unmapped new CARC code should fail/quarantine, not silently misclassify denials; central reference management prevents that.

What strong answers cover

  1. Master reference/code data (CARC/RARC, CPT, ICD, NDC, taxonomy, plan codes) centrally: versioned, effective-dated, owned.
  2. Consistent mapping across marts; quarantine/alert unmapped/stale codes.
  3. Prevents silent misclassification.
  4. One governed reference, reused.
  5. Effective-date for historical correctness.

Starter pattern

ref_code(code_set, code, description, valid_from, valid_to, version)
-- one governed source; quarantine unmapped codes
Common mistake: Each mart maintaining its own code mappings, so the same code is classified differently across analytics.
Principal follow-up: How does central, effective-dated reference data prevent inconsistent code mapping?
042
Design or evaluate a solution for NPI taxonomy. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
PrincipalNPI taxonomy

Model answer

NPI taxonomy codes classify provider type/specialty (a standardized code set). Model taxonomy as effective-dated reference linked to the provider dimension, since a provider can have multiple taxonomies and they change; use it for specialty analytics and network classification.

  • Standardized provider type/specialty codes.
  • Effective-dated; providers may have multiple.
  • Link to provider dimension as reference.
  • Drives specialty and network analytics.

Healthcare angle: Top-providers-per-specialty analytics rely on accurate, effective-dated NPI taxonomy mapping.

What strong answers cover

  1. Model NPI provider taxonomy (specialty classification) as governed reference linked to the provider dimension, effective-dated.
  2. Supports specialty analytics, network adequacy, attribution.
  3. Handle taxonomy changes over time (as-of).
  4. Validate against the standard taxonomy set.
  5. One governed source.

Starter pattern

dim_provider.taxonomy_code -> ref_taxonomy(code, specialty, grouping)
-- effective-dated; as-of for historical specialty
Common mistake: Hard-coding specialty on the provider row without governed/effective-dated taxonomy, so specialty analytics drift.
Principal follow-up: Why model NPI taxonomy as governed, effective-dated reference rather than a static provider attribute?
043
Design or evaluate a solution for LOINC/RxNorm mapping. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
PrincipalLOINC/RxNorm mapping

Model answer

LOINC standardizes lab/observation codes; RxNorm standardizes drug naming and links NDCs to ingredients. Maintain them as governed reference and map source codes to these standards for interoperable lab and pharmacy analytics (adherence, gaps in care).

  • LOINC for labs/observations; RxNorm for drugs.
  • RxNorm links NDCs to normalized drug concepts.
  • Governed reference mapping for interoperability.
  • Powers lab-result and pharmacy-adherence analytics.

Healthcare angle: Medication adherence (MPR/PDC) needs NDC-to-RxNorm mapping to group equivalent drugs correctly.

What strong answers cover

  1. Normalize lab (LOINC) and drug (RxNorm) codes via governed mappings so clinical/pharmacy analytics group consistently.
  2. Effective-dated reference; handle code updates.
  3. RxNorm groups NDCs to ingredients for adherence.
  4. Quarantine/alert unmapped codes.
  5. One governed source.

Starter pattern

ndc -> RxNorm ingredient (grouping) ; lab -> LOINC (normalize)
-- governed, effective-dated; quarantine unmapped
Common mistake: Analyzing on raw NDC/lab codes without normalization, so the same drug/lab fragments across representations.
Principal follow-up: Why normalize to RxNorm/LOINC, and how does it improve adherence/clinical analytics?
044
Design or evaluate a solution for calendar spine. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalcalendar spine

Model answer

A calendar spine is a dense date/period table joined so metrics have rows for every period even with no activity, enabling correct rolling windows, gap detection, member-months, and continuous trends. Standardize one enterprise spine.

  • Dense date/period table for zero-filled metrics.
  • Correct rolling windows and gap detection.
  • Foundation for member-months and trend.
  • One conformed enterprise spine.

Healthcare angle: Member-month and PMPM metrics need a calendar spine so zero-activity months appear, not vanish.

What strong answers cover

  1. A dense calendar spine (every date/month in range) lets facts zero-fill, compute rolling windows, and reveal gaps (missing dates).
  2. All time-series analytics join to it.
  3. Carries plan-year/fiscal/holiday attributes.
  4. Prevents invisible missing periods.
  5. One conformed spine.

Starter pattern

calendar(date) cross join entities -> LEFT JOIN facts
-- zero-fill metrics; absent dates become visible
Common mistake: Computing trends without a calendar spine, so months with no claims silently vanish instead of showing zero.
Principal follow-up: Why is a calendar spine essential for zero-fill and detecting missing periods?
045
Design or evaluate a solution for snapshot reconstruction. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalsnapshot reconstruction

Model answer

Reconstruct a historical snapshot by selecting bitemporal versions valid at the target date and known as of that time, against a calendar spine. This reproduces prior reports exactly for restatement and audit.

  • Select versions valid at the target business date.
  • Add system-time for what-we-knew-then.
  • Join to spine for completeness.
  • Reproducible restatements and audits.

Healthcare angle: Restating last quarter's PMPM after retro corrections requires reconstructing membership/provider as of the original snapshot.

What strong answers cover

  1. Reconstruct a prior state by selecting dimension versions valid at the target date (and known as-of then, if bitemporal) joined to facts.
  2. Use a calendar spine for completeness.
  3. Reproduce prior reports exactly.
  4. Requires clean effective-dating/bitemporal history.
  5. Validate against a known snapshot.

Starter pattern

SELECT ... FROM fact f JOIN dim d
 ON f.key=d.key AND :as_of BETWEEN d.valid_from AND d.valid_to
Common mistake: Reconstructing history with current dimension rows instead of the as-of versions, mis-stating the past.
Principal follow-up: How does bitemporal history let you reproduce both what was true and what you knew at the as-of date?
046
Design or evaluate a solution for incremental aggregates. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalincremental aggregates

Model answer

Incremental aggregates update only the keys/periods affected by new or changed facts rather than recomputing the whole aggregate, with late/retro facts reopening their periods. They cut cost while staying correct if restatement is handled.

  • Update only affected keys/periods from deltas.
  • Reopen periods for late/retro facts.
  • Cheaper than full recompute.
  • Reconcile incrementally + periodic full check.

Healthcare angle: PMPM/denial aggregates update from claim deltas; late claims trigger restatement of their months.

What strong answers cover

  1. Maintain aggregates incrementally (update only changed periods/keys) rather than full recompute, with periodic full-refresh backstop.
  2. Reopen periods for late/retro data; keep them restatement-capable.
  3. Idempotent updates.
  4. Reconcile incremental vs full.
  5. Cheaper and faster.

Starter pattern

-- update aggregates for [changed periods] only; full refresh weekly
-- reopen affected months for late data; idempotent
Common mistake: Full-recomputing every aggregate each run (costly) or incrementing without reopening periods for late data (wrong).
Principal follow-up: How do you maintain aggregates incrementally while still restating for late-arriving claims?
047
Design or evaluate a solution for summary table design. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalsummary table design

Model answer

Summary tables pre-aggregate common rollups (member-month, provider-month, denial-by-reason) so dashboards hit small, fast tables instead of scanning base facts. Define grain clearly, keep them restatable, and reconcile to the base.

  • Pre-aggregate common rollups for fast serving.
  • Clear grain; additive measures.
  • Restatable for late facts; reconcile to base.
  • Refresh incrementally where possible.

Healthcare angle: Executive claims dashboards hit a member-month summary, not the billion-row line fact.

What strong answers cover

  1. Pre-aggregate hot rollups (PMPM by plan/month, denial rates) at a defined grain to serve BI fast, derived from the detail fact.
  2. Keep summaries reconcilable to detail and restatement-capable.
  3. Refresh incrementally.
  4. Document grain and definitions.
  5. One source of truth (detail).

Starter pattern

summary_pmpm(plan, month, allowed, member_months, pmpm)
-- derived from detail; reconciles to fact; incremental refresh
Common mistake: Building summary tables that drift from the detail fact (no reconciliation), so rollups disagree with detail.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep a pre-aggregated summary reconcilable to the detail fact and correct after late data?
048
Design or evaluate a solution for materialized mart strategy. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalmaterialized mart strategy

Model answer

Decide what to materialize (precompute/store) vs compute on the fly by query frequency, cost, latency needs, and freshness tolerance. Materialize hot, expensive, stable aggregates (via MVs/dynamic tables/summary tables); leave rare/volatile queries on-demand.

  • Materialize hot, expensive, stable aggregates.
  • On-demand for rare/volatile queries.
  • Use MVs/dynamic tables/summary tables.
  • Balance freshness vs cost.

Healthcare angle: Daily PMPM/denial aggregates are materialized; ad-hoc deep-dive queries run on-demand against the base.

What strong answers cover

  1. Decide what to materialize (cost/latency vs freshness) — materialize hot, expensive, frequently-queried marts; compute cheap/rare ones on demand.
  2. Use incremental refresh and atomic publish.
  3. Keep one source of truth; reconcile.
  4. Balance storage/refresh cost vs query speed.
  5. Document refresh cadence/SLA.

Starter pattern

materialize hot gold marts (incremental + atomic swap)
 compute rare/cheap views on demand; reconcile to source
Common mistake: Materializing everything (storage/refresh cost) or nothing (slow BI), instead of deciding by query frequency and cost.
Principal follow-up: How do you decide which marts to materialize versus compute on demand?
049
Design or evaluate a solution for metric definition governance. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalmetric definition governance

Model answer

Govern metric definitions (PMPM, denial rate, readmission) centrally as versioned, documented, owned definitions so every report computes them identically and changes are deliberate. This prevents the recurring same-metric-different-number conflict.

  • Central, versioned, owned metric definitions.
  • One calculation reused everywhere (semantic layer).
  • Changes reviewed and communicated.
  • Prevents metric drift across reports.

Healthcare angle: When finance and actuarial disagree on PMPM, governed definitions plus a semantic layer resolve and prevent it.

What strong answers cover

  1. Govern metric definitions centrally (semantic layer + glossary): one versioned, owned definition per metric, change-controlled.
  2. Tie definitions to the implementing data/lineage.
  3. Prevents the same metric meaning different things across reports.
  4. Steward and maintain.
  5. Embed at the point of use.

Starter pattern

glossary + semantic layer: PMPM/denial-rate defined once, versioned, owned
 linked to implementing marts/lineage
Common mistake: Definitions living in scattered report logic, so PMPM/denial rate drift and finance vs actuarial disagree.
Principal follow-up: How do you govern metric definitions so the same metric means one thing org-wide?
050
Design or evaluate a solution for architecture review checklist. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalarchitecture review checklist

Model answer

An architecture review checks grain/keys, SCD/history handling, idempotency/replay, reconciliation/control totals, DQ gates, PHI governance, SLA/observability, cost, lineage, failure/DR handling, and contracts. It is a repeatable bar that catches the predictable failure modes before production.

  • Grain/keys, history/SCD, idempotency/replay.
  • Reconciliation, DQ gates, PHI governance.
  • SLA/observability, cost, lineage, DR.
  • Contracts and consumer impact.

Healthcare angle: A claims platform review confirms grain, idempotency, reconciliation, PHI controls, and SLA before go-live.

What strong answers cover

  1. Review designs against a checklist: grain/keys, history/SCD, idempotency, reconciliation, DQ/quarantine, PHI governance, conformed dimensions, lineage, SLA/observability, cost, DR, contracts.
  2. Constructive feedback + ADRs; teach through review.
  3. Catches predictable failure modes early.
  4. A quality gate, not a bottleneck.
  5. Same bar for every design.

Starter pattern

checklist: grain/keys/history/idempotency/reconciliation/DQ/PHI/
 conformed-dims/lineage/SLA/cost/DR/contracts + ADR
Common mistake: Reviewing for style/naming while missing grain, idempotency, or PHI issues that cause real incidents.
Principal follow-up: What's on your architecture-review checklist, and how do you keep it a gate not a bottleneck?

Healthcare Domain

Claims, membership, provider, 835/837, CARC/RARC, HEDIS, CMS Stars, risk adjustment and VBC.

100 questions
001
Explain medical claim lifecycle from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediummedical claim lifecycle

Model answer

A medical claim flows: provider renders service, submits an 837 claim, the payer adjudicates it (edits, eligibility, benefits, pricing), produces an 835 remittance with paid/adjusted/denied amounts, and the claim closes or is adjusted/replaced/voided. Each stage emits data engineers must capture, version, and reconcile.

  • Submit (837) → adjudicate → remit (835) → close/adjust.
  • Adjudication applies eligibility, benefits, pricing, edits.
  • Adjustments/replacements/voids create new versions.
  • Each stage produces reconcilable data.

Healthcare angle: Understanding the lifecycle tells you why the same claim appears multiple times (versions) and why paid amounts change.

What strong answers cover

  1. Walk the business flow: provider renders service, 837 submit, payer adjudicates (eligibility, benefits, pricing, edits), 835 remit, close or adjust/replace/void.
  2. Map each stage to data: raw 837/835 landing, header+line facts, versioned claims, conformed dims, gold marts.
  3. Call out the two hard realities: claims arrive late (recent periods incomplete) and each claim has many versions (only net latest is truth).
  4. Name the controls: grain, idempotency, as-of joins, reconciliation, restatement.
  5. Tie correctness to consumers: actuarial pricing, reserving, risk, quality all sit on this data.

Starter pattern

raw_837 -> parse(header,line) -> dedup latest version -> conform(member,provider,date) -> marts(PMPM,denials,AR)
   controls: source-trailer reconciliation at every boundary; idempotent keyed loads
Common mistake: Defining the lifecycle but skipping versioning and lateness — so paid is double-counted across versions and recent-month trend looks artificially low, misleading pricing and reserving.
Principal follow-up: How does the lifecycle differ for Medicare Advantage cost analytics (incurred/IBNR, risk) versus provider RCM/AR reporting (paid/denials)?
002
Explain 837 professional vs institutional from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Medium837 professional vs institutional

Model answer

837P (professional) is for individual providers/physician services (CMS-1500 equivalent); 837I (institutional) is for facilities/hospitals (UB-04 equivalent) and carries revenue codes, bill types, and facility-specific fields. They differ in segments and required data, so parsers and models handle them distinctly.

  • 837P = professional/physician (CMS-1500).
  • 837I = institutional/facility (UB-04), revenue codes + bill type.
  • Different required segments/fields → distinct parsing.
  • Also 837D for dental.

Healthcare angle: Institutional claims add revenue codes and bill-type logic professional claims lack, affecting grain and pricing.

What strong answers cover

  1. 837P = professional/physician (CMS-1500 equivalent); 837I = institutional/facility (UB-04) with revenue codes and bill type; 837D = dental.
  2. Explain the data consequences: different required segments/fields, so parsers and models branch by claim type.
  3. Institutional adds revenue codes, bill-type logic, and facility specifics that professional lacks.
  4. Decide grain per type but conform both to a shared claim/line model for unified analytics.
  5. Validate type-specific required fields on ingest.

Starter pattern

parse_837(file):
   if ISA/GS indicates 837I: extract revenue_code, bill_type, facility fields
   elif 837P: extract place_of_service, rendering_npi
   -> normalize to common claim_header/claim_line schema
Common mistake: Parsing 837I with the 837P field map (or vice versa) silently drops revenue codes/bill type, corrupting facility cost and inpatient analytics.
Principal follow-up: How would you reconcile professional and institutional claims for the same inpatient stay so cost isn't double-counted across claim types?
003
Explain claim header vs line from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumclaim header vs line

Model answer

The header carries claim-level data (member, provider, dates, totals, status); lines carry per-service detail (procedure, units, line charge/paid, diagnosis pointers). Line amounts roll up to the header. Analytics pick the grain the question needs and reconcile the two.

  • Header: member/provider/dates/totals/status.
  • Line: procedure, units, line amounts, dx pointers.
  • Lines sum to header totals.
  • Choose grain per question; reconcile.

Healthcare angle: SUM(line paid) must equal header paid; reconciling header vs line is a core claims integrity check.

What strong answers cover

  1. Header = claim-level (member, provider, dates, totals, status); line = per-service (procedure, units, line charge/paid, diagnosis pointers).
  2. State the rule: line amounts must roll up to header totals — assert it as a control.
  3. Pick the grain the question needs; never mix header and line grain in one measure.
  4. Diagnosis/procedure detail lives at line grain; status/dates at header.
  5. Model header and line as parent-child with a shared claim key.

Starter pattern

-- integrity check: line paid must reconcile to header paid
SELECT claim_id FROM line GROUP BY claim_id
HAVING SUM(line_paid) <> MAX(header_paid);
Common mistake: Joining a header measure to line rows multiplies the header amount by the line count, inflating paid — a classic fan-out that corrupts every cost rollup.
Principal follow-up: When would you build a header-grain fact, a line-grain fact, or both, and how do you keep them reconciled?
004
Explain claim status from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumclaim status

Model answer

Claim status reflects where a claim is: submitted, pending, adjudicated, paid, denied, adjusted, voided. Status drives reporting (AR, denials) and determines whether a row is current or superseded. Track status history, not just the latest value.

  • Statuses: submitted/pending/paid/denied/adjusted/voided.
  • Determines current vs superseded rows.
  • Drives AR and denial reporting.
  • Track status history for cycle-time analysis.

Healthcare angle: Denial and AR analytics depend on accurate, time-stamped claim status.

What strong answers cover

  1. Enumerate statuses: submitted, pending, paid, denied, adjusted, voided — and treat status as time-stamped history, not just a latest value.
  2. Status determines whether a row is current or superseded.
  3. It drives AR (open vs paid) and denial reporting.
  4. Track status transitions to compute cycle times (received->adjudicated->paid).
  5. Reconcile status counts to source.

Starter pattern

-- current status with full history retained
SELECT *, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY claim_id ORDER BY status_ts DESC) rn
FROM claim_status_history  -- rn=1 = current; keep all for cycle-time
Common mistake: Keeping only the latest status (no history) makes cycle-time and aging analytics impossible and hides how long claims sat pending.
Principal follow-up: How would you measure adjudication cycle time and detect claims stuck in pending beyond SLA?
005
Explain claim adjustment from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumclaim adjustment

Model answer

An adjustment modifies a previously processed claim — a correction, reprocessing, or recoupment — producing a new version with an adjustment sequence. The net effect (not the sum of all versions) is the current truth; keep the full chain for audit.

  • Adjustments create new versions with a sequence.
  • Use net latest version; never sum across versions.
  • Includes corrections, reprocessing, recoupments.
  • Keep the chain for audit.

Healthcare angle: Summing original + adjustment instead of the net latest inflates paid trend — a frequent reporting error.

What strong answers cover

  1. Adjustments create a new version with an adjustment sequence — corrections, reprocessing, recoupments.
  2. Use the net latest version as current truth; keep the full chain for audit.
  3. Never sum across versions — that double-counts.
  4. Map source mechanisms (837 replacement, 835 reversal) into the version chain.
  5. Restate affected aggregates when an adjustment lands.

Starter pattern

-- net latest version per claim
SELECT * FROM claim_versions
QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY claim_id ORDER BY adj_seq DESC, ingest_ts DESC)=1;
Common mistake: Summing original + adjustment instead of the net latest inflates paid and overstates cost trend — and breaks reserving.
Principal follow-up: How do you make aggregates restatement-capable so a late adjustment to an old claim correctly reopens its period?
006
Explain coordination of benefits from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumcoordination of benefits

Model answer

Coordination of Benefits (COB) determines payment order when a member has multiple coverages: the primary pays first, the secondary covers remaining eligible amounts. Data must capture primary/secondary roles and other-payer paid amounts to compute member responsibility correctly.

  • Primary pays first; secondary covers remaining eligible.
  • Capture other-payer paid amounts (from 837/835).
  • Determines correct payer liability and member responsibility.
  • Misorder distorts paid amounts.

Healthcare angle: COB affects how much your plan actually pays; ignoring other-payer amounts overstates your plan's liability.

What strong answers cover

  1. Primary pays first; secondary covers remaining eligible — capture primary/secondary role and other-payer paid amounts.
  2. Compute your plan's true liability net of other-payer payments.
  3. Source COB info from 837/835 (other-payer segments).
  4. Determine correct member responsibility after all payers.
  5. Flag COB-relevant claims for special handling.

Starter pattern

plan_paid = max(0, allowed - other_payer_paid - member_responsibility)
-- never treat allowed as your plan's cost when a primary already paid
Common mistake: Ignoring other-payer paid amounts overstates your plan's liability and inflates cost/PMPM for COB members.
Principal follow-up: How would COB errors distort PMPM and provider cost comparisons, and how do you detect them?
007
Explain capitation vs fee-for-service from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumcapitation vs fee-for-service

Model answer

Fee-for-service (FFS) pays per service rendered (claim-driven cost); capitation pays a fixed PMPM per member regardless of utilization (risk shifts to the provider). They require different cost models — FFS aggregates claim paid; capitation tracks per-member fixed payments plus encounter data for utilization.

  • FFS: pay per service; cost = claim paid.
  • Capitation: fixed PMPM; provider bears utilization risk.
  • Capitation needs encounter data for utilization (no paid signal).
  • Different cost/trend modeling.

Healthcare angle: Under capitation, claims may be encounters (no paid amount), so utilization, not paid, measures cost.

What strong answers cover

  1. FFS pays per service (cost = claim paid); capitation pays fixed PMPM and shifts utilization risk to the provider.
  2. Under capitation, claims may be encounters with no paid amount — utilization, not paid, measures cost.
  3. Model the two cost paradigms separately.
  4. Capitation needs complete encounter capture or utilization is understated.
  5. Blend correctly when a population mixes both.

Starter pattern

-- capitated cost: fixed payments + encounter utilization (paid often 0)
-- FFS cost: SUM(claim_paid)
-- do NOT SUM(paid) on capitated encounters as 'cost'
Common mistake: Treating capitated encounters' paid (often zero) as cost makes capitated populations look free, badly skewing cost and trend.
Principal follow-up: How do you measure and compare cost/utilization across a population that is partly capitated and partly FFS?
008
Explain allowed amount vs paid amount from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumallowed amount vs paid amount

Model answer

Billed is the provider's charge; allowed is the contracted amount the payer recognizes; paid is what the plan actually pays after member cost-share and adjustments. Member responsibility = allowed − paid (deductible/copay/coinsurance). Confusing these distorts cost analytics.

  • Billed = charge; allowed = contracted recognized amount.
  • Paid = plan payment after cost-share/adjustments.
  • Member responsibility = allowed − paid.
  • Use allowed for cost trend, paid for plan liability.

Healthcare angle: Cost trend uses allowed (true cost); plan-liability reporting uses paid — mixing them misstates both.

What strong answers cover

  1. Billed = charge; allowed = contracted recognized amount; paid = plan payment after cost-share/adjustments.
  2. Member responsibility = allowed - paid.
  3. Use allowed for true cost/trend; paid for plan liability.
  4. Assert billed >= allowed >= paid per line as a DQ check.
  5. Out-of-network changes the allowed logic.

Starter pattern

member_resp = allowed - paid
-- cost trend uses allowed; plan-liability reporting uses paid; don't mix
Common mistake: Mixing allowed and paid (e.g. trending on paid) misstates cost: paid omits member cost-share, so it understates the true cost of care.
