Principal follow-up: When does allowed-based trend and paid-based trend diverge most, and which does an actuary want for pricing?
009
Explain billed vs allowed vs member responsibility from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumbilled vs allowed vs member responsibility

Model answer

Billed (charge) ≥ allowed (contracted) ≥ paid (plan portion); member responsibility (deductible + copay + coinsurance) fills the gap between allowed and paid. These relationships must hold per line; violations signal data errors.

  • Billed ≥ allowed ≥ paid; member resp = allowed − paid.
  • Deductible + copay + coinsurance compose member responsibility.
  • Relationships should hold per line as a DQ check.
  • Out-of-network changes the allowed logic.

Healthcare angle: Asserting billed ≥ allowed ≥ paid per line catches pricing/data defects early.

What strong answers cover

  1. Relationship holds per line: billed >= allowed >= paid; member responsibility (deductible+copay+coinsurance) fills allowed-paid.
  2. Use this as a per-line data-quality assertion.
  3. Out-of-network and COB alter the allowed logic.
  4. Deductible/copay/coinsurance compose member responsibility.
  5. Violations signal pricing or parse errors.

Starter pattern

-- DQ: flag impossible economics
SELECT * FROM line
WHERE billed < allowed OR allowed < paid OR member_resp <> allowed - paid;
Common mistake: Not validating billed>=allowed>=paid lets corrupt pricing rows through, quietly distorting cost, member-responsibility, and trend.
Principal follow-up: How would you investigate a spike of lines where allowed < paid — data bug or legitimate scenario?
010
Explain deductible copay coinsurance from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumdeductible copay coinsurance

Model answer

Deductible is the amount a member pays before the plan pays; copay is a fixed per-service amount; coinsurance is a percentage of allowed. They accumulate toward out-of-pocket maximums and reset at plan-year boundaries — accumulator logic data engineers must model.

  • Deductible (pay-first), copay (fixed), coinsurance (%).
  • Accumulate toward OOP max; reset per plan year.
  • Compute member responsibility per line.
  • Plan-year boundary drives accumulator resets.

Healthcare angle: Running deductible/OOP accumulators must reset at plan-year start and net out reversals.

What strong answers cover

  1. Deductible (pay-first), copay (fixed per service), coinsurance (% of allowed) — they accumulate to OOP max and reset at plan-year.
  2. Compute member responsibility per line from these.
  3. Model running accumulators that reset at plan-year boundary.
  4. Net out reversals so accumulators stay correct.
  5. Plan-year-aware date logic drives resets.

Starter pattern

running_deductible = SUM(member_resp_deductible) OVER (
  PARTITION BY member_id, plan_year ORDER BY service_dt)
-- reset at plan_year boundary; cap at OOP max
Common mistake: Not resetting accumulators at the plan-year boundary carries balances forward, miscomputing member cost-share and benefit exhaustion.
Principal follow-up: How do retroactive claim adjustments mid-plan-year affect accumulator correctness, and how do you restate them?
011
Explain pharmacy claim basics from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumpharmacy claim basics

Model answer

Pharmacy (Rx) claims are adjudicated in near-real-time at point of sale via NCPDP standards (not 837), keyed by NDC, with fields like days supply, quantity, fill date, and DAW codes. They differ structurally from medical claims and feed adherence and drug-cost analytics.

  • NCPDP (point-of-sale), not 837; near-real-time.
  • Keyed by NDC; days supply, quantity, fill date, DAW.
  • Feeds adherence (MPR/PDC) and drug-cost analytics.
  • Structurally distinct from medical claims.

Healthcare angle: Medication adherence and pharmacy spend come from NCPDP Rx claims, modeled separately from medical claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. Rx claims adjudicate near-real-time at point of sale via NCPDP (not 837), keyed by NDC.
  2. Key fields: days supply, quantity, fill date, DAW codes.
  3. Feed adherence (PDC/MPR) and drug-cost analytics.
  4. Model structurally separate from medical claims.
  5. Reversals/rebills are common — handle like versions.

Starter pattern

-- Rx model: one row per fill
-- key: (member_id, ndc, fill_dt, rx_number); fields: days_supply, qty, daw
-- group equivalents via NDC->RxNorm for adherence
Common mistake: Forcing pharmacy into the medical-claims model loses NCPDP-specific fields (days supply, fill date), breaking adherence and drug-cost analytics.
Principal follow-up: How would you reconcile pharmacy reversals and rebills so drug spend isn't double-counted?
012
Explain NDC and RxNorm from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
MediumNDC and RxNorm

Model answer

NDC (National Drug Code) uniquely identifies a drug product/package; RxNorm normalizes drug naming and links NDCs to ingredients/strengths so equivalent products group together. Map NDC to RxNorm for adherence and therapeutic-class analytics.

  • NDC identifies a specific drug product/package.
  • RxNorm normalizes naming, links NDCs to ingredients.
  • Map NDC → RxNorm for grouping equivalents.
  • Watch NDC reuse/packaging variations.

Healthcare angle: Adherence (PDC) requires grouping equivalent drugs via NDC-to-RxNorm, not raw NDC.

What strong answers cover

  1. NDC identifies a specific drug product/package; RxNorm normalizes naming and links NDCs to ingredients/strengths.
  2. Map NDC->RxNorm to group therapeutic equivalents.
  3. Watch NDC reuse and packaging variations.
  4. Maintain mapping as governed, versioned reference.
  5. Adherence/therapeutic-class analytics need the grouping, not raw NDC.

Starter pattern

fill JOIN ndc_rxnorm USING(ndc)
-- adherence groups by rxnorm ingredient, not raw NDC (else same drug splits)
Common mistake: Computing adherence on raw NDC splits the same drug across package variants, understating PDC/MPR and misjudging adherence.
Principal follow-up: How do NDC reuse and new packaging affect a drug-spend time series, and how do you keep it stable?
013
Explain provider NPI from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumprovider NPI

Model answer

The NPI (National Provider Identifier) is a 10-digit standard identifier for providers (Type 1 individual, Type 2 organization). It is the natural key for the provider dimension, but can be reused/retired, so model with surrogates and effective dating.

  • 10-digit standard provider id (Type 1 individual, Type 2 org).
  • Natural key for provider dimension.
  • Can be reused/retired → use surrogate + effective dating.
  • Rendering vs billing NPI distinction matters.

Healthcare angle: Rendering vs billing NPI on a claim affects attribution; model both roles.

What strong answers cover

  1. NPI is a 10-digit standard id: Type 1 individual, Type 2 organization; it's the provider natural key.
  2. Distinguish rendering vs billing NPI on a claim — it affects attribution.
  3. NPIs can be reused/retired, so use a surrogate key + effective dating.
  4. Validate NPI format/checksum on ingest.
  5. Crosswalk to internal/legacy provider ids.

Starter pattern

-- attribution uses rendering NPI; payment uses billing NPI
SELECT rendering_npi, billing_npi FROM claim_line
-- surrogate key in provider_dim; NPI is natural key
Common mistake: Using billing NPI where rendering NPI is needed mis-attributes care to the group instead of the treating provider, distorting provider analytics.
Principal follow-up: When would rendering vs billing NPI give different provider cost/quality results, and which do you use for attribution?
014
Explain provider taxonomy from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumprovider taxonomy

Model answer

Provider taxonomy codes classify provider type and specialization (a standard code set). A provider may have multiple taxonomies; model them effective-dated as reference linked to the provider dimension for specialty and network analytics.

  • Standard codes for provider type/specialization.
  • Providers may carry multiple taxonomies.
  • Effective-dated reference linked to provider dim.
  • Drives specialty/network classification.

Healthcare angle: Specialty-level provider analytics depend on accurate taxonomy mapping.

What strong answers cover

  1. Taxonomy codes classify provider type/specialization; a provider may have multiple taxonomies.
  2. Model effective-dated as reference linked to the provider dimension.
  3. Drives specialty and network classification.
  4. Pick the primary taxonomy deliberately when several exist.
  5. Keep the code set current/governed.

Starter pattern

provider_taxonomy(npi, taxonomy_code, is_primary, valid_from, valid_to)
-- specialty analytics join on primary taxonomy as-of service date
Common mistake: Treating a multi-taxonomy provider as one specialty (or using a stale primary) misclassifies specialty-level cost and network adequacy.
Principal follow-up: How do you choose the right taxonomy when a provider has several, and how does it affect specialty cost benchmarks?
015
Explain member eligibility from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediummember eligibility

Model answer

Eligibility defines whether and when a member is covered, as effective-dated coverage spans by plan/product. It drives claim acceptance, member-months, and HEDIS continuous-enrollment; retro changes restate history, so model it SCD2/bitemporally.

  • Effective-dated coverage spans per plan/product.
  • Drives claim acceptance, member-months, HEDIS.
  • Retro changes restate history (bitemporal).
  • Gaps and overlaps need explicit handling.

Healthcare angle: Eligibility spans determine member-months and which claims are payable; retro eligibility shifts both.

What strong answers cover

  1. Eligibility = effective-dated coverage spans per plan/product; drives claim acceptance, member-months, HEDIS.
  2. Model SCD2/bitemporal so retro changes restate history.
  3. Handle gaps and overlaps explicitly (DQ).
  4. Serve as-of eligibility for adjudication and analytics.
  5. Reconcile member counts/spans to source.

Starter pattern

-- as-of eligibility for a service date
SELECT * FROM eligibility e
WHERE e.member_id=:m AND :service_dt BETWEEN e.valid_from AND e.valid_to;
Common mistake: Using current eligibility instead of as-of-service-date wrongly accepts/rejects claims and corrupts member-months and HEDIS denominators.
Principal follow-up: How does a 45-day retroactive eligibility change ripple into member-months, PMPM, and capitation, and how do you restate it?
016
Explain coverage spans from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumcoverage spans

Model answer

Coverage spans are the start/end periods of a member's enrollment in a plan. Clean spans (non-overlapping, with intended gaps) enable as-of eligibility, gap detection, and member-month counting. Overlaps/gaps are data-quality concerns to detect and resolve.

  • Start/end enrollment periods per member/plan.
  • Enable as-of eligibility and member-months.
  • Detect overlaps (defect) and gaps (HEDIS-relevant).
  • Clean spans are prerequisite for correct denominators.

Healthcare angle: Enrollment gaps (>45 days) affect HEDIS continuous-enrollment; overlaps inflate member-months.

What strong answers cover

  1. Coverage spans = start/end enrollment periods per member/plan; clean spans (non-overlapping, intended gaps) enable as-of eligibility.
  2. Detect overlaps (defect) and gaps (HEDIS-relevant).
  3. Foundation for member-months and continuous-enrollment logic.
  4. Merge contiguous spans; flag true gaps.
  5. Span quality is prerequisite for correct denominators.

Starter pattern

-- gaps & islands: collapse contiguous coverage
SELECT member_id, MIN(valid_from), MAX(valid_to)
FROM (... grp by consecutive spans ...) GROUP BY member_id, grp;
Common mistake: Overlapping spans inflate member-months; unhandled gaps break HEDIS continuous-enrollment, silently changing quality rates.
Principal follow-up: How do you detect and resolve overlapping coverage spans without dropping legitimate plan changes?
017
Explain member months from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediummember months

Model answer

A member-month is one month of coverage for one member — the denominator for PMPM and utilization/quality rates. Computed from coverage spans against a calendar, it must handle partial months and retro eligibility, and restate when eligibility changes.

  • One covered month per member = the rate denominator.
  • From coverage spans × calendar; handle partial months.
  • Restates on retro eligibility.
  • Foundation of PMPM and utilization rates.

Healthcare angle: PMPM = paid / member-months; errors in member-months distort every per-member metric.

What strong answers cover

  1. A member-month = one covered month per member — the denominator for PMPM and utilization/quality rates.
  2. Compute from coverage spans against a calendar spine; handle partial months.
  3. Restate on retro eligibility.
  4. Zero-activity months still count (use the spine).
  5. Errors here distort every per-member metric.

Starter pattern

SELECT member_id, month FROM coverage_spans s
JOIN calendar c ON c.month BETWEEN month(s.valid_from) AND month(s.valid_to)
-- one row per member per eligible month
Common mistake: Wrong member-months (overlaps, missed retro, partial-month errors) corrupt PMPM, utilization, and HEDIS simultaneously — they all divide by it.
Principal follow-up: How do you handle partial-month eligibility and mid-month terminations in the member-month count?
018
Explain plan benefit from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumplan benefit

Model answer

Plan benefit design (covered services, cost-share, limits, networks) determines adjudication outcomes — what is paid, denied, or member-responsible. Benefit data is reference that explains claim results; model it so analytics can attribute outcomes to benefit rules.

  • Covered services, cost-share, limits, networks.
  • Determines paid/denied/member-responsible outcomes.
  • Reference data explaining adjudication.
  • Varies by product/plan year.

Healthcare angle: Why a service was denied or member-responsible traces back to plan benefit rules.

What strong answers cover

  1. Benefit design (covered services, cost-share, limits, networks) explains adjudication outcomes — paid/denied/member-responsible.
  2. Model benefit as reference that explains claim results.
  3. Varies by product and plan year.
  4. Link claims to the governing benefit for attribution of outcomes.
  5. Effective-date benefit configurations.

Starter pattern

claim JOIN plan_benefit ON claim.plan_id=pb.plan_id
  AND claim.service_dt BETWEEN pb.valid_from AND pb.valid_to
-- explains why a service was covered/denied/member-resp
Common mistake: Not effective-dating benefits applies this year's benefit to last year's claims, mis-explaining denials and member cost-share.
Principal follow-up: How would you analyze whether a benefit design change caused a shift in denials or member cost-share?
019
Explain payer vs provider data from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Mediumpayer vs provider data

Model answer

Payer data is claims/eligibility/payment-centric (administrative, complete for covered members, lagged); provider/clinical data (EHR) is encounter/clinical-detail-centric (richer clinically, limited to that provider). They complement each other; interoperability (FHIR) bridges them.

  • Payer: administrative claims/eligibility/payments, lagged but broad.
  • Provider/EHR: clinical detail, deep but provider-scoped.
  • Complementary; FHIR bridges them.
  • Different completeness, latency, and grain.

Healthcare angle: Claims see cost across all providers but lack clinical depth; EHR has clinical depth but only one provider's view.

What strong answers cover

  1. Payer data: administrative claims/eligibility/payments — broad across covered members, standardized, lagged.
  2. Provider/EHR data: clinical detail — deep but provider-scoped, less standardized.
  3. They complement each other; FHIR bridges them.
  4. Different completeness, latency, and grain.
  5. Combine for the fullest member picture.

Starter pattern

claims: full cost across all providers, clinically shallow
EHR: clinical depth, one provider's view
-> link via member identity + interoperability (FHIR)
Common mistake: Assuming claims contain clinical depth (or EHR contains full cost) leads to wrong conclusions — e.g. measuring quality from claims alone misses lab results.
Principal follow-up: For a HEDIS measure needing a lab value, how do you combine claims and clinical data, and what are the pitfalls?
020
Explain 835 remittance from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Medium835 remittance

Model answer

The 835 (ERA) is the electronic remittance the payer sends explaining how a claim was paid/adjusted/denied, line by line, with payment and adjustment reasons. It is the source of paid amounts, denials (CARC/RARC), and reconciliation to the 837.

  • Electronic remittance explaining payment/adjustment/denial.
  • Line-level paid/adjusted amounts + reason codes.
  • Source of paid and denial data.
  • Reconciled against the 837.

Healthcare angle: Paid amounts and denial reasons come from the 835; duplicate 835s corrupt AR and paid totals.

What strong answers cover

  1. The 835/ERA explains how each claim/line was paid/adjusted/denied, with CARC/RARC reasons — the source of paid and denial data.
  2. Parse CLP (claim), SVC (line), CAS (adjustments).
  3. Reconcile SVC->CLP->file trailer and to the original 837.
  4. Load idempotently keyed on remittance identity.
  5. Duplicate 835s corrupt AR/paid totals.

Starter pattern

parse_835: CLP(claim paid/status/ICN) + SVC(line paid) + CAS(group,CARC,amount)
idempotency key: payer + check/EFT + remit_id
Common mistake: Loading an 835 with a blind append (no remittance-identity key) double-applies payments, corrupting AR aging and paid totals.
Principal follow-up: What exactly makes a remittance identity unique, and how do you prevent duplicate-835 double-payment?
021
Explain CLP segment from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
MediumCLP segment

Model answer

The CLP segment in an 835 is the claim-level payment record: claim status, charge, payment, patient responsibility, and the payer claim control number (ICN). It anchors claim-level remittance data and links to the original 837.

  • Claim-level payment record in the 835.
  • Status, charge, paid, patient responsibility, ICN.
  • Links remittance to the original claim.
  • Anchors claim-level reconciliation.

Healthcare angle: The CLP carries the ICN and claim-level paid amount used to reconcile remittance to claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. CLP is the claim-level payment record in the 835: status, charge, payment, patient responsibility, and the ICN.
  2. It anchors claim-level remittance and links to the original 837 via ICN.
  3. Roll line (SVC) amounts up to CLP for integrity.
  4. Carries claim status codes for paid/denied.
  5. Use ICN for claim-to-remittance matching.

Starter pattern

CLP*<claim_id>*<status>*<charge>*<paid>*<patient_resp>*...*<ICN>
-- reconcile: SUM(SVC paid) == CLP paid
Common mistake: Misreading CLP status codes mislabels paid vs denied claims, throwing off denial rate and AR.
Principal follow-up: How do you match an 835 CLP back to the originating 837 when the ICN format varies by payer?
022
Explain SVC segment from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
MediumSVC segment

Model answer

The SVC segment in an 835 is the service-line payment detail: procedure, charge, paid amount, and units at the line level. It provides line-level remittance that must roll up to the CLP claim total.

  • Service-line payment detail in the 835.
  • Procedure, charge, paid, units per line.
  • Rolls up to CLP claim total.
  • Source of line-level paid amounts.

Healthcare angle: Line-level paid (SVC) must reconcile to claim-level paid (CLP) — a remittance integrity check.

What strong answers cover

  1. SVC is the service-line payment detail: procedure, charge, paid, units — line-level remittance.
  2. SVC amounts must roll up to the CLP claim total.
  3. Source of line-level paid for cost analytics.
  4. Pairs with line-level CAS adjustments.
  5. Reconcile SVC to CLP as a control.

Starter pattern

-- line remittance reconciliation
SUM(SVC.line_paid) per claim == CLP.claim_paid  (else flag)
Common mistake: Aggregating SVC paid without reconciling to CLP lets line/claim mismatches through, corrupting line-level cost and procedure analytics.
Principal follow-up: How would you handle an 835 where SVC lines don't sum to the CLP total?
023
Explain CAS segment from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
MediumCAS segment

Model answer

The CAS segment carries claim/line adjustments: group code (CO, PR, OA, PI), CARC reason code, and amount — explaining why billed ≠ paid. It is the source for denial/adjustment analytics and member-responsibility breakdown.

  • Adjustment detail: group code + CARC + amount.
  • Explains the gap between billed/allowed and paid.
  • Source for denials and member-responsibility.
  • Multiple CAS per line possible (bridge).

Healthcare angle: Denial and adjustment analytics parse CAS group/reason codes from the 835.

What strong answers cover

  1. CAS carries adjustments: group code (CO/PR/OA/PI), CARC reason, and amount — explains why billed != paid.
  2. Source for denials/adjustments and member-responsibility breakdown.
  3. A line can have multiple CAS — model with a bridge.
  4. Group code distinguishes contractual vs patient responsibility.
  5. Drives denial analytics.

Starter pattern

CAS*<group>*<CARC>*<amount>...  (e.g. CAS*PR*1*250 = patient deductible 250)
-- explode multiple CAS per line into a bridge
Common mistake: Collapsing multiple CAS codes per line (or ignoring group code) misattributes adjustment dollars, distorting denial and member-responsibility analytics.
Principal follow-up: How do you separate contractual adjustments (CO) from patient responsibility (PR) when analyzing why claims weren't fully paid?
024
Explain CARC from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
MediumCARC

Model answer

CARC (Claim Adjustment Reason Code) is a standardized code explaining why an adjustment/denial occurred (e.g. non-covered, deductible, duplicate). It is central to denial root-cause analytics; a line can carry several CARCs, so model with a bridge.

  • Standard code for why an adjustment/denial happened.
  • Central to denial root-cause analytics.
  • Multiple per line → bridge table.
  • Paired with group codes (CO/PR/OA/PI).

Healthcare angle: Top denial CARCs drive payment-integrity and provider-education priorities.

What strong answers cover

  1. CARC = standardized adjustment/denial reason code (e.g. non-covered, deductible, duplicate); central to denial root-cause analytics.
  2. A line can carry several CARCs — use a bridge.
  3. Pair with group codes (CO/PR/OA/PI).
  4. Bucket CARCs into actionable themes.
  5. Maintain as governed reference; quarantine unmapped.

Starter pattern

denial_reason_bridge(claim_line_id, carc, group_code, amount)
-- top denial CARCs drive payment-integrity & provider education
Common mistake: Mapping CARCs wrong (or dropping multiples) misclassifies denials, sending root-cause and recovery efforts after the wrong problems.
Principal follow-up: How would you build a denial root-cause view that survives new CARC codes appearing in the feed?
025
Explain RARC from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
MediumRARC

Model answer

RARC (Remittance Advice Remark Code) supplements CARC with additional explanation/remarks on a remittance. Capture RARCs alongside CARCs (bridge) for fuller denial context and appeals support.

  • Supplemental remark codes on remittance.
  • Add context beyond CARC.
  • Capture with CARC via bridge.
  • Useful for appeals/denial analysis.

Healthcare angle: RARCs add the detail needed to understand and appeal specific denials.

What strong answers cover

  1. RARC = remittance advice remark code; supplements CARC with extra explanation/remarks.
  2. Capture with CARC via a bridge for fuller denial context.
  3. Useful for appeals and granular denial analysis.
  4. Multiple RARCs per line are possible.
  5. Govern the code set.

Starter pattern

remark_bridge(claim_line_id, rarc)  -- joined to CARC for full denial context
-- appeals teams need CARC+RARC together
Common mistake: Ignoring RARCs loses the detail needed to action or appeal denials, weakening recovery analytics.
Principal follow-up: How do CARC and RARC together change how you prioritize denials for rework versus appeal?
026
Explain denial buckets from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advanceddenial buckets

Model answer

Denial bucketing groups CARC/RARC codes into actionable categories (eligibility, authorization, coding, timely filing, duplicate, non-covered) so denials are analyzed and worked by root-cause theme rather than raw codes. Maintain the mapping as governed reference.

  • Group CARC/RARC into actionable themes.
  • Eligibility, auth, coding, timely filing, duplicate, non-covered.
  • Enables root-cause analysis and rework prioritization.
  • Governed, versioned mapping.

Healthcare angle: Bucketing denials into themes lets teams fix root causes (e.g. auth gaps) instead of chasing individual codes.

What strong answers cover

  1. Group CARC/RARC into actionable themes: eligibility, authorization, coding, timely filing, duplicate, non-covered.
  2. Analyze and work denials by root-cause theme, not raw codes.
  3. Maintain the mapping as governed, versioned reference.
  4. Quantify recoverable dollars per bucket.
  5. Quarantine/alert on unmapped codes.

Starter pattern

denial_bucket = map(carc, group_code)  -- governed lookup
-- report denied$ and count by bucket; trend over time
Common mistake: A stale or wrong bucket mapping misroutes denial-prevention effort (e.g. chasing coding when the real driver is auth), wasting recovery work.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep denial buckets stable and complete as payers introduce new CARC/RARC codes?
027
Explain AR aging from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedAR aging

Model answer

AR aging buckets outstanding receivables by age (0-30, 31-60, 61-90, 90+) to monitor revenue-cycle health. It is a periodic snapshot sensitive to duplicate/void remittances; build it from reconciled, idempotent payment data.

  • Outstanding AR bucketed by age.
  • Periodic snapshot for revenue-cycle health.
  • Sensitive to duplicate/void 835s.
  • Build from reconciled, idempotent data.

Healthcare angle: A duplicate 835 corrupts AR aging; idempotent remittance loading protects it.

What strong answers cover

  1. AR aging buckets outstanding receivables by age (0-30/31-60/61-90/90+) for revenue-cycle health.
  2. It's a periodic snapshot sensitive to duplicate/void 835s.
  3. Build from reconciled, idempotent payment data.
  4. Reconcile to payments/remittances.
  5. Drives collections prioritization.

Starter pattern

-- snapshot per period
bucket = CASE WHEN days_open<=30 THEN '0-30' ... END
-- source from idempotent remittance; reconcile to payments
Common mistake: A duplicate 835 inflates payments and understates AR; non-idempotent remittance loading corrupts the entire aging report.
Principal follow-up: Why is AR aging so sensitive to duplicate remittances, and how do you make it duplicate-proof?
028
Explain timely filing from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advancedtimely filing

Model answer

Timely filing is the deadline (e.g. 90-365 days from service) by which a claim must be submitted or it is denied. It drives a denial category and requires service-date vs received-date logic; late-arriving claims interact with these rules.

  • Submission deadline from service date.
  • Missing it → timely-filing denial (a CARC).
  • Needs service-date vs received-date logic.
  • Interacts with late-arriving claims.

Healthcare angle: Timely-filing denials are a distinct bucket requiring accurate service-to-received date tracking.

What strong answers cover

  1. Timely filing = submission deadline from service date (e.g. 90-365 days); missing it yields a denial.
  2. Needs service-date vs received-date logic.
  3. It's a distinct denial bucket.
  4. Interacts with late-arriving claims.
  5. Rules vary by payer/contract.

Starter pattern

is_timely = datediff(received_dt, service_dt) <= filing_limit_days
-- deny/flag when exceeded; track by payer contract
Common mistake: Computing timely filing on load date instead of received date wrongly denies (or passes) claims, distorting the timely-filing denial bucket.
Principal follow-up: How do you distinguish a true timely-filing denial from a pipeline-induced late receipt?
029
Explain 270/271 eligibility from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advanced270/271 eligibility

Model answer

270 is an eligibility inquiry; 271 is the response confirming coverage/benefits. These are typically real-time transactions; a lakehouse may ingest them for eligibility verification analytics or to support a real-time eligibility API.

  • 270 = eligibility inquiry; 271 = response.
  • Typically real-time transactions.
  • Confirms coverage/benefit details.
  • Ingested for verification analytics/API.

Healthcare angle: A real-time eligibility API serves 271 responses; logging 270/271 supports verification analytics.

What strong answers cover

  1. 270 = eligibility inquiry; 271 = response confirming coverage/benefits; typically real-time.
  2. Ingest for verification analytics or to back a real-time eligibility API.
  3. Separate the fast serving path from analytics logging.
  4. Confirms coverage/benefit detail at point of need.
  5. Log transactions for audit/analytics.

Starter pattern

serving: 271 from low-latency current-eligibility store (point lookup)
analytics: log 270/271 to lakehouse (separate path)
Common mistake: Serving eligibility from a slow analytics table (not a point-lookup store) makes the real-time API too slow and unreliable.
Principal follow-up: How would you architect sub-second eligibility lookups while still logging every 270/271 for analytics?
030
Explain 278 prior auth from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advanced278 prior auth

Model answer

278 is the prior authorization/referral transaction (request and response). Prior-auth data supports utilization management, denial analysis (missing auth), and turnaround analytics; model request/decision/status with timestamps.

  • 278 = prior authorization/referral request and response.
  • Supports utilization management and auth-denial analysis.
  • Model request/decision/status with timestamps.
  • Turnaround-time analytics.

Healthcare angle: Missing prior auth is a common denial reason; 278 data links auths to claims for analysis.

What strong answers cover

  1. 278 = prior authorization/referral request and response; supports utilization management.
  2. Model request/decision/status with timestamps.
  3. Link auths to resulting claims for denial and turnaround analysis.
  4. Missing auth is a common denial reason.
  5. Track turnaround time.

Starter pattern

auth(auth_id, member, provider, service, decision, status, decision_ts)
-- link auth_id -> claim to analyze missing-auth denials
Common mistake: Failing to link auths to claims hides whether denials are truly missing-auth versus linkage failures, misdirecting UM fixes.
Principal follow-up: How do you measure auth-to-claim match rates and detect auths that should have prevented a denial?
031
Explain HEDIS from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedHEDIS

Model answer

HEDIS is a widely-used set of standardized quality measures (e.g. screenings, control of chronic conditions) with precise eligibility, exclusion, and numerator specs. Pipelines must implement specs exactly, use member-months for continuous enrollment, and be auditable.

  • Standardized quality measures with strict specs.
  • Eligibility/exclusion/numerator per measure.
  • Continuous enrollment via member-months (45-day gap allowance).
  • Must be reproducible/auditable to spec.

Healthcare angle: HEDIS rates affect quality ratings and revenue; the mart must encode each measure's exact spec.

What strong answers cover

  1. HEDIS = standardized quality measures with strict eligibility/exclusion/numerator specs.
  2. Continuous enrollment via member-months (allow 45-day gap).
  3. Numerator from claims/encounters/labs.
  4. Must be reproducible/auditable to spec.
  5. Restate for late data.

Starter pattern

-- per measure: denominator(eligible+continuous-enroll) , minus exclusions , numerator(evidence)
rate = numerator / (denominator - exclusions)
Common mistake: Loosely implementing continuous-enrollment or exclusion logic silently changes HEDIS rates, which affect quality ratings and revenue.
Principal follow-up: How do you make a HEDIS measure defensible in audit and reproducible after late claims arrive?
032
Explain CMS Stars from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedCMS Stars

Model answer

CMS Star Ratings rate Medicare Advantage/Part D plans (1-5 stars) across measures (including many HEDIS), driving quality bonuses and member choice. Analytics track measure performance, gaps, and improvement opportunities feeding the overall rating.

  • 1-5 star rating for MA/Part D plans.
  • Built from many measures (incl. HEDIS).
  • Drives bonus payments and enrollment.
  • Track measure-level gaps and trends.

Healthcare angle: Star measures drive bonus revenue, so gap-closure analytics target the measures that move the rating.

What strong answers cover

  1. CMS Star Ratings (1-5) rate MA/Part D plans across measures (many HEDIS), driving bonus revenue and enrollment.
  2. Aggregate weighted measure performance into the overall rating.
  3. Track gaps and improvement opportunities.
  4. Prioritize high-weight measures.
  5. Reproducibility/timeliness affect bonus.

Starter pattern

star = weighted_rollup(measure_scores)
-- prioritize gap-closure on measures with highest weight x gap
Common mistake: Optimizing low-weight measures (ignoring measure weights) wastes intervention budget without moving the rating or bonus.
Principal follow-up: Given limited outreach budget, how do you target gap closure to maximize the Star rating?
033
Explain risk adjustment from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advancedrisk adjustment

Model answer

Risk adjustment compensates plans for member health risk using diagnoses mapped to HCCs to compute a RAF score; sicker populations yield higher payment. Pipelines must capture diagnoses completely/accurately, map to HCCs by model version, and handle late diagnoses (restatement).

  • Diagnoses → HCCs → RAF score → risk-adjusted payment.
  • Completeness/accuracy of diagnosis capture is critical.
  • Model-version-specific HCC mapping.
  • Late diagnoses restate scores.

Healthcare angle: Accurate, complete diagnosis capture drives risk-adjusted revenue; missed HCCs understate payment.

What strong answers cover

  1. Diagnoses map to HCCs to compute RAF; sicker populations yield higher risk-adjusted payment.
  2. Completeness/accuracy of diagnosis capture is critical to revenue.
  3. Model-version-specific HCC mapping.
  4. Late diagnoses restate scores.
  5. Track inputs/version for reproducibility.

Starter pattern

raf = demographic_factor + SUM(hcc_weights for captured HCCs)
-- diagnoses(complete) -> HCC(model version) -> RAF
Common mistake: Incomplete diagnosis capture understates RAF and revenue; using the wrong model version makes scores non-reproducible and audit-risky.
Principal follow-up: How would you detect under-capture of HCCs and quantify the revenue impact of late-arriving diagnoses?
034
Explain HCC from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedHCC

Model answer

HCC (Hierarchical Condition Category) groups related diagnoses into payment-relevant categories with hierarchies (a more severe condition supersedes a related lesser one). HCCs map diagnoses to the RAF score; the hierarchy logic matters for correct scoring.

  • Groups diagnoses into payment categories.
  • Hierarchies: severe condition supersedes lesser related.
  • Feeds RAF score computation.
  • Model-version-dependent.

Healthcare angle: HCC hierarchy logic ensures conditions are counted correctly for risk-adjusted payment.

What strong answers cover

  1. HCC groups related diagnoses into payment categories with hierarchies (severe supersedes lesser related).
  2. Feeds the RAF score.
  3. Model-version-dependent.
  4. Hierarchy logic matters for correct scoring.
  5. Map diagnoses to HCCs via governed crosswalk.

Starter pattern

-- apply hierarchy: keep most severe in a family
hcc_set = resolve_hierarchy(map(diagnoses -> hcc, model_version))
Common mistake: Skipping HCC hierarchy logic counts a lesser and a superseding condition together, mis-scoring RAF and overstating/understating revenue.
Principal follow-up: How does the HCC hierarchy change the score when a member has both a severe and a related lesser condition?
035
Explain RAF score from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedRAF score

Model answer

The RAF (Risk Adjustment Factor) score summarizes a member's expected cost from demographics + HCCs; payment scales with it. It is computed per measurement period, reproducibly (model version, diagnosis source), and restates as diagnoses arrive late.

  • Member risk score from demographics + HCCs.
  • Payment scales with RAF.
  • Reproducible per period (model version tracked).
  • Restates on late diagnoses.

Healthcare angle: RAF directly affects revenue; tracking inputs/version makes score drift explainable in an incident.

What strong answers cover

  1. RAF summarizes expected cost from demographics + HCCs; payment scales with it.
  2. Compute per measurement period, reproducibly (model version, diagnosis source).
  3. Restate as diagnoses arrive late.
  4. Track all inputs for audit.
  5. Drives risk-adjusted revenue.

Starter pattern

raf_period(member, period) = demo + SUM(hcc_weights)
-- store model_version + source so drift is explainable
Common mistake: Not versioning RAF inputs makes score drift unexplainable in an incident and indefensible in audit.
Principal follow-up: If RAF scores shift quarter over quarter, how do you tell a model change from a real population/diagnosis change?
036
Explain value-based care from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advancedvalue-based care

Model answer

Value-based care (VBC) ties provider payment to quality/cost outcomes (shared savings, capitation, bundles) rather than volume. It requires attribution, quality measurement, cost/utilization analytics, and timely, accurate data to settle contracts fairly.

  • Pay for outcomes/quality, not volume.
  • Needs attribution + quality + cost analytics.
  • Shared savings, capitation, bundled payments.
  • Data accuracy/timeliness affects settlements.

Healthcare angle: VBC settlements depend on correct attribution and restated cost when late claims arrive.

What strong answers cover

  1. VBC ties provider payment to quality/cost outcomes (shared savings, capitation, bundles) not volume.
  2. Needs attribution + quality + cost/utilization analytics.
  3. Data accuracy/timeliness affects settlements.
  4. Restate cost when late claims arrive.
  5. Transparent, reproducible measurement.

Starter pattern

settlement = f(attributed_members, quality_scores, actual_vs_target_cost)
-- cost must be IBNR-aware and restated for late claims
Common mistake: Settling VBC on incomplete (non-IBNR-adjusted) cost rewards/penalizes providers on immature data, creating disputes and clawbacks.
Principal follow-up: How do late claims and attribution changes affect a shared-savings settlement, and how do you handle restatement?
037
Explain attribution from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advancedattribution

Model answer

Attribution assigns members (or episodes) to a responsible provider/group by documented rules (e.g. plurality of PCP visits within a window), with effective dating and tie-breakers. It underpins VBC and quality measurement and must restate on retro data changes.

  • Rule-based member-to-provider assignment.
  • Plurality/most-recent logic with tie-breakers.
  • Effective-dated; restates on retro changes.
  • Foundation for VBC and quality.

Healthcare angle: Attributing members to a PCP for VBC must follow documented rules and restate when claims arrive late.

What strong answers cover

  1. Attribution assigns members/episodes to a provider by documented rules (e.g. plurality of PCP visits).
  2. Effective-dated with tie-breakers.
  3. Underpins VBC and quality measurement.
  4. Restate on retro provider/claims changes.
  5. Must be transparent and reproducible.

Starter pattern

attributed_pcp = argmax(visit_count) OVER window  -- with documented tie-breaker
-- effective-dated; recompute on retro changes
Common mistake: Ambiguous or non-reproducible attribution rules cause VBC disputes and shift quality/cost credit to the wrong provider.
Principal follow-up: How do you handle ties and retro claim changes in attribution so settlements stay defensible?
038
Explain gaps in care from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advancedgaps in care

Model answer

Gaps in care are recommended services a member is due but has not received (e.g. overdue screening), identified by comparing care guidelines/measure logic to claims/clinical evidence. They drive outreach and quality (HEDIS/Stars) improvement.

  • Recommended-but-not-received services.
  • Identified via measure/guideline logic vs evidence.
  • Drive outreach and quality improvement.
  • Tie to HEDIS/Stars numerators.

Healthcare angle: Gap-closure pipelines find members missing screenings and drive outreach to improve HEDIS/Stars.

What strong answers cover

  1. Gaps = recommended-but-not-received services, found by comparing measure/guideline logic to claims/clinical evidence.
  2. Drive outreach and quality (HEDIS/Stars) improvement.
  3. Tie to measure numerators.
  4. Prioritize by impact and closeability.
  5. Need timely, accurate evidence.

Starter pattern

gap = eligible_for_service AND NOT exists(evidence_of_service)
-- evidence from claims/encounters/labs; prioritize by Stars weight
Common mistake: Stale evidence flags false gaps (member already got the service), wasting outreach and annoying members/providers.
Principal follow-up: How do you minimize false-positive care gaps when clinical evidence arrives with a lag?
039
Explain utilization management from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advancedutilization management

Model answer

Utilization management (UM) reviews medical necessity and appropriateness (prior auth, concurrent/retrospective review) to manage cost and quality. UM data (278, auth decisions) supports denial analysis, appropriateness analytics, and care management.

  • Reviews necessity/appropriateness of services.
  • Prior auth, concurrent, retrospective review.
  • Data from 278/auth decisions.
  • Supports cost/quality and denial analysis.

Healthcare angle: UM/auth data links to claims to analyze appropriateness and auth-related denials.

What strong answers cover

  1. UM reviews medical necessity/appropriateness (prior auth, concurrent, retrospective).
  2. Data from 278/auth decisions.
  3. Supports cost/quality and denial analysis.
  4. Link UM decisions to claims.
  5. Track appropriateness and turnaround.

Starter pattern

join auth_decisions -> claims to analyze: missing-auth denials, appropriateness, turnaround SLA
Common mistake: Analyzing UM without linking auths to claims overstates or understates 'avoidable' utilization and misguides interventions.
Principal follow-up: How would you quantify the cost impact of UM decisions while avoiding selection bias?
040
Explain cost trend analytics from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advancedcost trend analytics

Model answer

Cost trend analytics measure how per-member cost changes over time (PMPM trend), decomposed into utilization vs unit-cost vs mix drivers. They rely on allowed amounts, member-months, and restatement for late claims (IBNR-aware).

  • PMPM trend over time.
  • Decompose into utilization, unit cost, mix.
  • Use allowed + member-months; IBNR-aware.
  • Restate for late claims.

Healthcare angle: Actuaries need trend decomposed and restated as late claims complete; raw paid-to-date understates recent months.

What strong answers cover

  1. Trend = how PMPM changes over time, decomposed into utilization, unit cost, and mix.
  2. Use allowed amounts and member-months; be IBNR-aware.
  3. Restate recent months for late claims.
  4. Compare like-for-like populations.
  5. Drives pricing and reserving.

Starter pattern

trend = pmpm(t)/pmpm(t-12); decompose into util x unit_cost x mix
-- adjust recent months with completion factors (IBNR)
Common mistake: Reporting raw paid-to-date trend understates recent months (claims still incoming), making cost look flat or falling and mispricing the product.
Principal follow-up: How do you decompose a PMPM increase into utilization vs unit-cost vs mix, and why does each imply a different action?
041
Explain PMPM from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedPMPM

Model answer

PMPM (Per Member Per Month) normalizes cost/utilization by member-months, enabling comparison across populations and time. PMPM = measure ÷ member-months; both numerator (allowed/paid, restated for lateness) and denominator (member-months) must be correct.

  • Cost/utilization ÷ member-months.
  • Comparable across populations/time.
  • Numerator restated for late claims (IBNR).
  • Denominator from clean member-months.

Healthcare angle: PMPM is the workhorse payer metric; both its parts must be right, and recent months need IBNR adjustment.

What strong answers cover

  1. PMPM = measure / member-months; normalizes cost/utilization for comparison.
  2. Numerator restated for late claims (IBNR); denominator from clean member-months.
  3. Use allowed for cost, paid for liability.
  4. The workhorse payer metric.
  5. Both parts must be correct.

Starter pattern

pmpm = SUM(allowed) / SUM(member_months)
-- IBNR-adjust numerator for recent periods; clean member-months denominator
Common mistake: Either a wrong numerator (un-IBNR'd, version-double-counted) or wrong denominator (bad member-months) silently breaks PMPM and everything built on it.
Principal follow-up: Walk through every input to PMPM that can be wrong and how each error shows up in the final number.
042
Explain admissions and readmissions from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advancedadmissions and readmissions

Model answer

Admissions are inpatient stays; readmissions are subsequent admissions within a window (e.g. 30 days), a key quality/cost measure. Computing them is a gaps-and-islands problem over admit/discharge dates, with rules for transfers, planned readmissions, and all-cause vs condition-specific.

  • Inpatient stays; readmissions within a window (30-day).
  • Gaps-and-islands over admit/discharge dates.
  • Handle transfers, planned readmits, all-cause vs specific.
  • CMS quality/penalty relevance.

Healthcare angle: 30-day all-cause readmission is a CMS measure computed as consecutive-admission islands with transfer/planned exclusions.

What strong answers cover

  1. Admissions = inpatient stays; readmissions = subsequent admission within a window (e.g. 30 days) — a quality/cost measure.
  2. Compute as gaps-and-islands over admit/discharge dates.
  3. Handle transfers, planned readmissions, all-cause vs condition-specific.
  4. CMS quality/penalty relevance.
  5. Exclude per measure spec.

Starter pattern

-- 30-day readmit: next admit within 30d of prior discharge
LEAD(admit_dt) OVER (PARTITION BY member ORDER BY admit_dt) - discharge_dt <= 30
-- merge transfers into one stay; exclude planned
Common mistake: Counting transfers as separate admissions (not merging stays) inflates admissions and readmission rates, triggering false quality penalties.
Principal follow-up: How do you correctly merge transfers and exclude planned readmissions in a 30-day all-cause measure?
043
Explain ED utilization from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedED utilization

Model answer

ED (emergency department) utilization tracks emergency visits, often to identify avoidable/non-emergent use for care-management intervention. Identified via place-of-service/revenue codes on claims; rate is per member-months.

  • Emergency department visit volume/rate.
  • Identify avoidable/non-emergent use.
  • From place-of-service/revenue codes.
  • Rate per member-months.

Healthcare angle: High avoidable-ED utilization flags members for care management; identification uses POS/revenue codes.

What strong answers cover

  1. ED utilization tracks emergency visits, often to flag avoidable/non-emergent use for care management.
  2. Identify via place-of-service/revenue codes on claims.
  3. Rate per member-months.
  4. Distinguish emergent vs non-emergent.
  5. Feeds care-management targeting.

Starter pattern

ed_visit = claim WHERE pos IN (23) OR revenue_code IN (045x)
-- rate = ed_visits / member_months
Common mistake: Identifying ED visits by inconsistent code logic over/under-counts ED use, misdirecting care-management outreach.
Principal follow-up: How would you classify avoidable vs non-avoidable ED visits, and what are the data limits of that classification?
044
Explain chronic condition flags from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advancedchronic condition flags

Model answer

Chronic condition flags mark members with conditions (diabetes, CHF, COPD) based on diagnosis patterns over time (often requiring multiple/persistent diagnoses). They drive risk stratification, care management, and measure denominators; the identification logic must be specified and auditable.

  • Flag members with chronic conditions from diagnosis patterns.
  • Often require persistent/multiple diagnoses.
  • Drive stratification, care management, measures.
  • Specified, auditable identification logic.

Healthcare angle: Chronic flags target care-management programs and feed condition-specific measures.

What strong answers cover

  1. Flag members with chronic conditions from diagnosis patterns (often persistent/multiple diagnoses).
  2. Drive stratification, care management, measure denominators.
  3. Specify, document, and make the logic auditable.
  4. Effective-date condition onset.
  5. Avoid one-off-diagnosis false positives.

Starter pattern

has_diabetes = COUNT(distinct claim WHERE dx in diabetes_set) >= 2 within 1yr
-- require persistence to avoid rule-out false positives
Common mistake: Flagging chronic conditions off a single rule-out diagnosis creates false positives, inflating disease prevalence and program denominators.
Principal follow-up: How do you balance sensitivity and specificity in chronic-condition identification, and how does it affect prevalence reporting?
045
Explain care management lists from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advancedcare management lists

Model answer

Care management lists identify members for outreach/programs based on risk, gaps, conditions, or utilization. They combine risk scores, gaps in care, and chronic flags with program criteria, and must be timely, accurate, and minimum-necessary in PHI exposure.

  • Member lists for outreach/programs.
  • Combine risk, gaps, conditions, utilization.
  • Timely + accurate for effective outreach.
  • Minimum-necessary PHI.

Healthcare angle: A care-management list blends risk, gaps, and utilization to prioritize outreach while limiting PHI exposure.

What strong answers cover

  1. Lists identify members for outreach/programs by risk, gaps, conditions, utilization.
  2. Combine risk scores, gaps, chronic flags with program criteria.
  3. Must be timely and accurate.
  4. Minimum-necessary PHI.
  5. Close the loop on outreach outcomes.

Starter pattern

outreach_list = members WHERE risk_score high AND open_gaps AND program_criteria
-- minimum-necessary PHI; refresh on schedule
Common mistake: A stale or over-broad list wastes scarce outreach capacity and exposes more PHI than necessary.
Principal follow-up: How would you prioritize a care-management list when outreach capacity is far smaller than the eligible population?
046
Explain interoperability from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Advancedinteroperability

Model answer

Interoperability is the standards-based exchange of health data across systems (FHIR, HL7, C-CDA, USCDI). For payers it underpins data exchange (e.g. CMS interoperability rules, Patient Access APIs), requiring normalization of clinical and claims data into shared standards.

  • Standards-based exchange (FHIR/HL7/C-CDA/USCDI).
  • Underpins Patient Access/payer-to-payer APIs.
  • Requires normalization to shared standards.
  • Regulatory drivers (CMS rules).

Healthcare angle: CMS interoperability mandates require payers to expose claims/clinical data via FHIR APIs.

What strong answers cover

  1. Interoperability = standards-based exchange (FHIR/HL7/C-CDA/USCDI); underpins Patient Access/payer-to-payer APIs.
  2. Requires normalizing clinical and claims data to shared standards.
  3. Regulatory drivers (CMS rules).
  4. Conform to US Core/USCDI.
  5. Bridge HL7 and FHIR during transition.

Starter pattern

ingest FHIR/HL7/C-CDA -> normalize to internal model conformed to USCDI
-> expose via FHIR APIs (Patient Access)
Common mistake: Treating interoperability as one-off integrations (not standardized normalization) creates brittle pipelines that fail audits and new mandates.
Principal follow-up: How would you sequence FHIR API enablement against CMS interoperability deadlines and existing HL7 feeds?
047
Explain FHIR Patient from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedFHIR Patient

Model answer

The FHIR Patient resource represents demographics and identifiers for a person. It maps to the member dimension; data engineers parse it (often from VARIANT/JSON), normalize identifiers, and link to internal member keys for a unified view.

  • FHIR resource for person demographics/identifiers.
  • Maps to the member dimension.
  • Parse JSON, normalize identifiers, link to member key.
  • Watch identifier systems/multiple identifiers.

Healthcare angle: FHIR Patient feeds the member dimension; identifier normalization links it to internal member keys.

What strong answers cover

  1. FHIR Patient = demographics/identifiers for a person; maps to the member dimension.
  2. Parse JSON (often VARIANT), normalize identifiers, link to internal member key.
  3. Watch multiple identifier systems.
  4. Resolve identity to a master member.
  5. Conform to US Core Patient profile.

Starter pattern

patient.identifier[] -> resolve to master_member_id (crosswalk)
flatten name/dob/gender; link to member_dim
Common mistake: Linking FHIR Patient on the wrong identifier system fragments a member's record, splitting their history across duplicate keys.
Principal follow-up: How do you resolve a FHIR Patient with several identifiers to one internal member without creating duplicates?
048
Explain FHIR Coverage from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedFHIR Coverage

Model answer

The FHIR Coverage resource represents a member's insurance coverage (payer, plan, period, subscriber). It maps to eligibility/coverage spans; parse and normalize it into effective-dated coverage for as-of eligibility.

  • FHIR resource for insurance coverage.
  • Payer, plan, period, subscriber/beneficiary.
  • Maps to eligibility/coverage spans.
  • Normalize into effective-dated spans.

Healthcare angle: FHIR Coverage feeds coverage spans used for member-months and as-of eligibility.

What strong answers cover

  1. FHIR Coverage = a member's insurance coverage (payer, plan, period, subscriber); maps to eligibility/coverage spans.
  2. Normalize into effective-dated spans for as-of eligibility.
  3. Link to member and plan.
  4. Handle subscriber vs beneficiary.
  5. Conform to US Core Coverage.

Starter pattern

coverage.period.start/end -> eligibility span(valid_from,valid_to)
link coverage.beneficiary -> member; coverage.payor -> plan
Common mistake: Mapping FHIR Coverage periods loosely creates overlapping/gapped spans, corrupting member-months and as-of eligibility.
Principal follow-up: How do you reconcile FHIR Coverage against your internal eligibility source when they disagree?
049
Explain FHIR Claim from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedFHIR Claim

Model answer

The FHIR Claim resource represents a claim submitted for adjudication (items, diagnoses, provider, patient). It maps to claim header/line facts; parse and flatten items/diagnoses into your claim model, linking to member/provider dimensions.

  • FHIR resource for a submitted claim.
  • Items, diagnoses, provider, patient.
  • Maps to claim header/line facts.
  • Flatten items/diagnoses; link to dims.

Healthcare angle: FHIR Claim is an alternative claim source to 837; flatten items into the claim-line fact.

What strong answers cover

  1. FHIR Claim = a submitted claim (items, diagnoses, provider, patient); an alternative claim source to 837.
  2. Flatten items/diagnoses into the claim-line model.
  3. Link to member/provider dimensions.
  4. Reconcile against 837 if both feeds exist.
  5. Conform to relevant profiles.

Starter pattern

claim.item[] -> claim_line rows; claim.diagnosis[] -> diagnosis bridge
link patient->member, provider->provider_dim
Common mistake: Treating FHIR Claim and 837 as independent sources double-counts the same claim if you don't dedup across feeds.
Principal follow-up: If you receive the same claim via both 837 and FHIR, how do you pick a source of truth and avoid duplication?
050
Explain FHIR ExplanationOfBenefit from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedFHIR ExplanationOfBenefit

Model answer

The FHIR ExplanationOfBenefit (EOB) resource combines claim and adjudication detail (what was billed, allowed, paid, member responsibility) — the FHIR analog of an 835-plus-claim. It is central to Patient Access APIs; flatten its adjudication arrays into paid/denial facts.

  • FHIR resource combining claim + adjudication.
  • Billed/allowed/paid/member-responsibility detail.
  • Central to Patient Access APIs.
  • Flatten adjudication arrays into facts.

Healthcare angle: EOB powers member-facing Patient Access APIs and carries the paid/adjudication detail analytics need.

What strong answers cover

  1. FHIR EOB combines claim + adjudication (billed/allowed/paid/member-responsibility) — the FHIR analog of 835-plus-claim.
  2. Central to Patient Access APIs.
  3. Flatten adjudication arrays into paid/denial facts.
  4. Member-responsibility accuracy is paramount (members see it).
  5. Conform to US Core / CARIN profiles.

Starter pattern

eob.item[].adjudication[] -> {billed, allowed, paid, member_resp}
-- powers member-facing Patient Access
Common mistake: Misflattening EOB adjudication categories misstates member responsibility on a member-facing API — a visible, trust-damaging error.
Principal follow-up: How do you guarantee the member-responsibility shown via the EOB API exactly matches adjudication?
051
Explain FHIR Observation from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedFHIR Observation

Model answer

The FHIR Observation resource carries clinical measurements/results (labs, vitals) coded with LOINC. It supplements claims with clinical evidence for quality measures and risk; parse, normalize codes, and link to member/encounter.

  • FHIR resource for clinical results/vitals (LOINC-coded).
  • Supplements claims with clinical evidence.
  • Supports quality measures and risk.
  • Normalize LOINC; link to member/encounter.

Healthcare angle: Lab Observations (e.g. HbA1c) supply numerator evidence for HEDIS measures claims alone cannot confirm.

What strong answers cover

  1. FHIR Observation = clinical results/vitals (LOINC-coded); supplements claims with clinical evidence.
  2. Supports quality measures and risk.
  3. Normalize LOINC; link to member/encounter.
  4. Provides numerator evidence claims lack.
  5. Handle value types/units carefully.

Starter pattern

observation.code(LOINC) + value -> evidence for HEDIS numerator
-- e.g. HbA1c value for diabetes control measure
Common mistake: Ignoring LOINC normalization or units mis-evaluates clinical thresholds (e.g. HbA1c control), corrupting quality measures.
Principal follow-up: How would you use lab Observations to satisfy a HEDIS numerator that claims alone can't confirm?
052
Explain HL7 ADT from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedHL7 ADT

Model answer

HL7 v2 ADT (Admit/Discharge/Transfer) messages signal patient movement events in near-real-time from facilities. They feed admission/discharge tracking, readmission analytics, and care-management triggers; parse the pipe-delimited segments and normalize event types.

  • HL7 v2 messages for admit/discharge/transfer events.
  • Near-real-time patient movement.
  • Feed admission/readmission and care triggers.
  • Parse segments; normalize event types.

Healthcare angle: Real-time ADT feeds trigger care-management outreach on admission and support readmission tracking.

What strong answers cover

  1. HL7 v2 ADT = admit/discharge/transfer events in near-real-time from facilities.
  2. Parse pipe-delimited segments; normalize event types.
  3. Feed admission/readmission analytics and care triggers.
  4. Handle out-of-order/duplicate messages.
  5. Stream for low latency.

Starter pattern

parse ADT (PID, PV1) -> event(admit/discharge/transfer, ts)
-- trigger care-management on admit; feed readmission tracking
Common mistake: Mis-ordering or duplicating ADT events double-counts admissions and fires false care-management triggers.
Principal follow-up: How do you handle out-of-order and duplicate ADT messages so admission counts and triggers stay correct?
053
Explain HL7 ORU from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedHL7 ORU

Model answer

HL7 v2 ORU messages carry observation/result data (lab results). They feed clinical-evidence pipelines for quality and risk, complementing claims; parse segments and map results to standard codes (LOINC).

  • HL7 v2 messages for observation/lab results.
  • Clinical evidence complementing claims.
  • Support quality/risk measures.
  • Map results to LOINC.

Healthcare angle: ORU lab results provide measure evidence (e.g. control values) not present in claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. HL7 v2 ORU = observation/lab result messages; clinical evidence complementing claims.
  2. Parse segments; map results to LOINC.
  3. Support quality/risk measures.
  4. Link to member/order.
  5. Handle result corrections.

Starter pattern

parse ORU (OBR/OBX) -> result(LOINC, value, unit)
-- feeds measure evidence; map to standard codes
Common mistake: Not handling ORU result corrections leaves stale lab values feeding quality measures, producing wrong rates.
Principal follow-up: How do you reconcile corrected lab results (ORU) so a measure uses the final value, not the preliminary one?
054
Explain CCDA from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedCCDA

Model answer

C-CDA (Consolidated Clinical Document Architecture) is an XML clinical document standard (e.g. care summaries) exchanged between systems. Payers parse C-CDA for clinical detail (problems, meds, results) to supplement claims for quality/risk; it is document-centric, not transactional.

  • XML clinical document standard (care summaries).
  • Problems, meds, results, allergies.
  • Supplements claims with clinical detail.
  • Document-centric parsing.

Healthcare angle: C-CDA documents enrich member records with clinical detail for risk/quality beyond claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. C-CDA = XML clinical document (care summaries) with problems, meds, results, allergies.
  2. Supplements claims with clinical detail for quality/risk.
  3. Document-centric parsing (not transactional).
  4. Extract structured sections.
  5. Link to member record.

Starter pattern

parse C-CDA XML sections (problems, meds, results) -> structured facts
-- enrich member record for risk/quality
Common mistake: Parsing only narrative (not structured sections) loses coded clinical data, weakening risk/quality enrichment.
Principal follow-up: What clinical data from C-CDA most improves risk/quality, and how do you handle inconsistent document quality?
055
Explain USCDI from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
AdvancedUSCDI

Model answer

USCDI (US Core Data for Interoperability) defines the standardized set of health data classes/elements required for interoperable exchange. It shapes what FHIR APIs must expose; align your normalized model to USCDI elements for compliance.

  • Standard set of data classes/elements for exchange.
  • Shapes required FHIR API content.
  • Align normalized model to USCDI.
  • Regulatory relevance for interoperability.

Healthcare angle: USCDI defines the minimum data your interoperability APIs must expose, guiding the normalization target.

What strong answers cover

  1. USCDI defines the standardized set of data classes/elements required for interoperable exchange.
  2. Shapes what FHIR APIs must expose.
  3. Align the normalized model to USCDI elements.
  4. Regulatory relevance.
  5. Track version updates.

Starter pattern

map internal model -> USCDI data classes (required elements present)
-- gap analysis: which USCDI elements are missing/unmapped
Common mistake: Not aligning to USCDI means your interoperability APIs miss required elements, failing compliance.
Principal follow-up: How would you run a gap analysis between your data model and USCDI requirements?
056
Explain US Core from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
ExpertUS Core

Model answer

US Core is the set of FHIR profiles constraining base resources to US realm requirements (aligned to USCDI). Conform ingestion/serving to US Core profiles so exchanged data meets expected structure and required elements.

  • US-realm FHIR profiles aligned to USCDI.
  • Constrain base resources to required elements.
  • Conform ingestion/serving to US Core.
  • Ensures interoperable structure.

Healthcare angle: Serving Patient Access data via US Core profiles ensures partners receive conformant FHIR.

What strong answers cover

  1. US Core = FHIR profiles constraining base resources to US-realm/USCDI requirements.
  2. Conform ingestion/serving to US Core profiles.
  3. Ensures interoperable structure and required elements.
  4. Validate against profiles.
  5. Versioned with USCDI.

Starter pattern

validate resources against US Core profiles (required elements, value sets)
-- serve Patient Access as conformant US Core FHIR
Common mistake: Serving non-conformant FHIR (missing US Core required elements) breaks partner integrations and fails certification.
Principal follow-up: How do you validate outbound FHIR against US Core profiles in your pipeline?
057
Explain PHI and HIPAA from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
ExpertPHI and HIPAA

Model answer

PHI (Protected Health Information) is individually identifiable health data; HIPAA governs its privacy and security (minimum necessary, access controls, audit, breach rules). Every design decision — masking, RBAC, de-identification, logging — must protect PHI by default.

  • PHI = identifiable health data; HIPAA governs it.
  • Minimum necessary, access control, audit, breach rules.
  • Mask/de-identify; never log PHI.
  • Compliance is a design constraint, not an add-on.

Healthcare angle: Every claims pipeline decision (masking, RBAC, logging) must protect member PHI to satisfy HIPAA.

What strong answers cover

  1. PHI = identifiable health data; HIPAA governs privacy/security (minimum necessary, access control, audit, breach).
  2. Mask/de-identify; never log PHI.
  3. Every design decision protects PHI by default.
  4. Compliance is a design constraint, not an add-on.
  5. Audit access.

Starter pattern

default: mask PHI columns; role-based unmasking; access logged
-- never write PHI to logs/error messages
Common mistake: Logging PHI in error messages or exposing raw identifiers broadly is a reportable breach with legal and financial consequences.
Principal follow-up: Where does PHI most often leak in a data pipeline, and how do you systematically prevent it?
058
Explain minimum necessary from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertminimum necessary

Model answer

HIPAA's minimum-necessary principle requires limiting PHI access/use to what is needed for the task. Implement with role-based masking, row-level security, de-identified datasets for broad use, and least-privilege roles so most consumers never see raw identifiers.

  • Limit PHI to what each task requires.
  • Masking + row policies + de-identified datasets.
  • Least-privilege roles.
  • Most consumers use masked/de-identified views.

Healthcare angle: Analysts get masked/de-identified claims; raw identifiers are restricted to the few legitimate uses.

What strong answers cover

  1. Minimum necessary = limit PHI access/use to what the task requires.
  2. Masking, row policies, de-identified datasets, least-privilege roles.
  3. Most consumers use masked/de-identified views.
  4. Audit and review access regularly.
  5. Design products so raw identifiers are rarely needed.

Starter pattern

analyst role -> de-identified/masked view
PHI role -> narrow, audited, justified access only
Common mistake: Granting broad PHI access 'for convenience' violates minimum-necessary and is a common audit finding and breach risk.
Principal follow-up: How would you design data products so the vast majority of analytics never touches raw PHI?
059
Explain de-identification from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertde-identification

Model answer

De-identification removes/obscures identifiers so data is no longer PHI, via HIPAA Safe Harbor (remove 18 identifier types) or Expert Determination (statistical). Produce governed de-identified datasets for broad analytics while restricting the identified source.

  • Safe Harbor (remove 18 identifiers) or Expert Determination.
  • Result is no longer PHI → broader use.
  • Govern and document the method.
  • Restrict the identified source.

Healthcare angle: A de-identified claims dataset enables research/analytics with minimized PHI risk.

What strong answers cover

  1. De-identification removes/obscures identifiers (Safe Harbor: 18 identifiers; or Expert Determination).
  2. Result is no longer PHI -> broader use.
  3. Govern and document the method.
  4. Restrict the identified source.
  5. Manage re-identification risk.

Starter pattern

safe_harbor(dataset): drop/obscure the 18 identifier types
-- document method; restrict identified source
Common mistake: Incomplete de-identification (leaving quasi-identifiers like rare zip+DOB) allows re-identification, a serious compliance failure.
Principal follow-up: What re-identification risks remain after Safe Harbor, and when would you use Expert Determination instead?
060
Explain claims reconciliation from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertclaims reconciliation

Model answer

Claims reconciliation proves claims moved correctly across systems/stages — counts and dollars tie source to target by period/key, with tolerances and an exceptions list. It is a core control catching loss, duplication, and amount drift.

  • Tie counts + dollars source to target by period/key.
  • Tolerances + exceptions list.
  • Catch loss, duplication, amount drift.
  • Persist results as a control.

Healthcare angle: Reconciling 837 source to the adjudication system proves no claims were lost or double-counted.

What strong answers cover

  1. Reconciliation ties counts and dollars source-to-target by period/key, with tolerances and an exceptions list.
  2. Catches loss, duplication, and amount drift.
  3. Persist results as a control.
  4. Tie to source file trailers.
  5. Run at every stage boundary.

Starter pattern

assert SUM(paid) and COUNT(*) per period match: source_trailer == raw == silver == gold
-- emit exceptions where they don't
Common mistake: Skipping reconciliation lets silent loss/duplication through, so cost and volume metrics are wrong with no error raised.
Principal follow-up: Where in the pipeline are claims most likely silently lost or duplicated, and how does reconciliation localize it?
061
Explain enrollment reconciliation from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertenrollment reconciliation

Model answer

Enrollment reconciliation verifies membership/eligibility loaded correctly — member counts, coverage spans, and member-months tie to source, with retro changes accounted for. Errors here distort every per-member metric.

  • Tie member counts/spans/member-months to source.
  • Account for retro eligibility changes.
  • Detect missing/duplicate members.
  • Protects PMPM denominators.

Healthcare angle: Bad enrollment reconciliation silently corrupts member-months and PMPM across all reports.

What strong answers cover

  1. Verify membership/eligibility loaded correctly: member counts, spans, member-months tie to source.
  2. Account for retro eligibility changes.
  3. Detect missing/duplicate members.
  4. Protects PMPM denominators.
  5. Reconcile per period.

Starter pattern

assert member_count and member_months per period == source
-- flag retro deltas explicitly
Common mistake: Bad enrollment reconciliation silently corrupts member-months, and therefore PMPM and every rate, across all reports.
Principal follow-up: How do you reconcile enrollment when retroactive changes constantly shift prior-period member-months?
062
Explain provider roster reconciliation from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertprovider roster reconciliation

Model answer

Provider roster reconciliation checks that the provider dimension matches the authoritative roster — counts, network status, and key attributes tie, with late/soft-deleted providers handled. It prevents attribution and pricing errors.

  • Tie provider counts/status/attributes to roster.
  • Handle late-arriving and soft-deleted providers.
  • Detect missing/duplicate providers.
  • Protects attribution and pricing.

Healthcare angle: A malformed/late roster causes claims to mis-attribute; reconciliation catches the drift.

What strong answers cover

  1. Check the provider dimension matches the authoritative roster: counts, network status, key attributes tie.
  2. Handle late-arriving and soft-deleted providers.
  3. Detect missing/duplicate providers.
  4. Protects attribution and pricing.
  5. Reconcile network status as-of.

Starter pattern

assert provider_count and active_network_count == roster
-- placeholder for late providers; reconcile network status
Common mistake: An unreconciled roster lets claims mis-attribute to wrong/missing providers, corrupting network and attribution analytics.
Principal follow-up: How do you reconcile a provider roster that arrives late or partially, without blocking claim loads?
063
Explain remittance reconciliation from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertremittance reconciliation

Model answer

Remittance reconciliation ties 835 payments to claims and to expected totals — line (SVC) to claim (CLP) to file trailer — and detects duplicate/missing remittances. It protects AR aging and paid trend.

  • Tie SVC → CLP → file trailer.
  • Match remittance to claims.
  • Detect duplicate/missing 835s.
  • Protects AR and paid trend.

Healthcare angle: Reconciling remittance to claims and trailers catches a duplicate 835 before it corrupts AR.

What strong answers cover

  1. Tie 835 payments to claims and totals: SVC->CLP->file trailer, and detect duplicate/missing remittances.
  2. Protects AR and paid trend.
  3. Match remittance to claims via ICN.
  4. Idempotent identity-keyed loads.
  5. Persist reconciliation results.

Starter pattern

assert SUM(SVC) == CLP == file_trailer; dedup on remittance identity
-- match CLP.ICN -> claim
Common mistake: Without remittance reconciliation, a duplicate or missing 835 corrupts AR and paid totals undetected.
Principal follow-up: How would your reconciliation catch a duplicate 835 before it corrupts AR aging?
064
