Explain duplicate 835 from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertduplicate 835

Model answer

A duplicate 835 is a re-loaded remittance file that double-applies payments, corrupting AR aging and paid totals. Prevent it by tracking remittance identity (payer + check/EFT + remit id) in a control table and using idempotent, identity-keyed loads.

  • Re-loaded remittance double-applies payments.
  • Corrupts AR aging and paid totals.
  • Track remittance identity in a control table.
  • Idempotent, identity-keyed loads prevent it.

Healthcare angle: Duplicate 835 is a classic incident; identity-based detection plus idempotent loads stop double-payment.

What strong answers cover

  1. A re-loaded remittance double-applies payments, corrupting AR and paid totals.
  2. Track remittance identity (payer + check/EFT + remit id) in a control table.
  3. Idempotent, identity-keyed loads prevent it.
  4. Reconcile to detect.
  5. Back out via Time Travel + reconcile to recover.

Starter pattern

remittance_key = hash(payer, check_eft_number, remit_id)
MERGE on remittance_key  -- second load is a no-op
Common mistake: Blind-append 835 loads double-pay, inflating payments and understating AR — a payment-integrity incident, not just reporting.
Principal follow-up: Exactly what fields make a remittance unique, and how do you recover AR after a duplicate already loaded?
065
Explain duplicate 837 from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertduplicate 837

Model answer

A duplicate 837 is a resubmitted claim file/claim that can inflate counts/exposure if not deduplicated. Distinguish true duplicates (identical) from replacements (frequency code 7) and adjustments; dedup on natural grain and keep the latest version.

  • Resubmitted claim file/claim.
  • Distinguish true dup vs replacement (freq 7) vs adjustment.
  • Dedup on natural grain; keep latest version.
  • Track files to detect replays.

Healthcare angle: A resent 837 must be deduplicated as a true duplicate but kept if it is a legitimate replacement.

What strong answers cover

  1. A resubmitted claim can inflate counts/exposure if not deduplicated.
  2. Distinguish true duplicate (identical) from replacement (freq 7) and adjustment.
  3. Dedup on natural grain; keep latest version.
  4. Track files to detect replays.
  5. Preserve legitimate replacements.

Starter pattern

if frequency_code in (7,8): treat as replacement/void (version)
else dedup identical (claim_id,line,payload) keep latest
Common mistake: Deduping away a legitimate replacement (freq 7) loses the correction; failing to dedup a true duplicate double-counts the claim.
Principal follow-up: How do you tell a true duplicate 837 from a legitimate replacement, and treat each correctly?
066
Explain late arriving claims from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertlate arriving claims

Model answer

Claims routinely arrive weeks-to-months after service. Attribute them by service date to the correct period, restate affected aggregates, and account for the lag in trend (IBNR). Recent months are always incomplete until runout.

  • Claims lag service by weeks/months.
  • Attribute by service date; restate aggregates.
  • IBNR accounts for not-yet-received claims.
  • Recent months incomplete until runout.

Healthcare angle: Trend on recent months understates cost until late claims complete; IBNR adjusts for it.

What strong answers cover

  1. Claims lag service by weeks/months; attribute by service date and restate affected aggregates.
  2. IBNR accounts for not-yet-received claims.
  3. Recent months are incomplete until runout.
  4. Track the lateness distribution.
  5. Communicate provisional periods.

Starter pattern

attribute claim to service_month (not load_month); reopen that month's aggregates
-- completion factors estimate IBNR for recent months
Common mistake: Reporting recent-month cost as final (ignoring lateness/IBNR) understates it, making trend look flat and mispricing the product.
Principal follow-up: How would you estimate completion factors and present recent months as provisional to consumers?
067
Explain retro eligibility from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertretro eligibility

Model answer

Retroactive eligibility changes add/remove coverage for past periods, shifting member-months, claim payability, capitation, and risk eligibility. Model bitemporally, restate affected periods, and quantify the blast radius.

  • Coverage changes applied to past periods.
  • Shifts member-months, payability, capitation, risk.
  • Bitemporal modeling; restate affected periods.
  • Quantify blast radius.

Healthcare angle: Retro eligibility 45 days back restates member-months and any PMPM/capitation built on them.

What strong answers cover

  1. Retroactive eligibility adds/removes past coverage, shifting member-months, payability, capitation, risk.
  2. Model bitemporally; restate affected periods.
  3. Quantify the blast radius.
  4. Reprocess affected aggregates.
  5. Communicate restatements.

Starter pattern

on retro change: recompute member_months for [from,to]; restate PMPM/capitation
-- bitemporal: know what was true vs what we knew
Common mistake: Not restating on retro eligibility leaves member-months (and PMPM, capitation) wrong for prior periods that everyone already reported.
Principal follow-up: Trace how a 45-day retro termination flows through member-months, PMPM, capitation, and risk eligibility.
068
Explain voids and replacements from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertvoids and replacements

Model answer

A void cancels a prior claim; a replacement supersedes it with corrected data (claim frequency codes 8 and 7). Apply them as versions: void zeroes/cancels, replacement substitutes the latest; never sum original and replacement.

  • Void cancels (freq 8); replacement supersedes (freq 7).
  • Apply as versions; keep latest/net.
  • Never sum original + replacement.
  • Preserve chain for audit.

Healthcare angle: An 837 replacement must replace, not add to, the original — otherwise paid is double-counted.

What strong answers cover

  1. Void cancels (freq 8); replacement supersedes (freq 7) with corrected data.
  2. Apply as versions; keep latest/net.
  3. Never sum original + replacement.
  4. Preserve the chain for audit.
  5. Restate affected periods.

Starter pattern

apply by frequency_code: 7 -> supersede prior; 8 -> cancel prior
keep net latest version for current state
Common mistake: Adding a replacement to the original (instead of replacing) double-counts paid; ignoring a void leaves a cancelled claim in totals.
Principal follow-up: How do you implement void/replacement logic idempotently so reruns don't corrupt the version chain?
069
Explain claim frequency code from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertclaim frequency code

Model answer

The claim frequency code (3rd digit of bill type / a claim field) indicates whether a claim is original (1), replacement (7), or void (8). It drives versioning logic — which prior claim to supersede or cancel — so dedup/MERGE must read it.

  • Indicates original (1), replacement (7), void (8).
  • Drives versioning/supersession logic.
  • MERGE/dedup must honor it.
  • Misreading it double-counts or loses corrections.

Healthcare angle: Frequency code 7/8 tells the pipeline to replace or void the prior claim version, not append.

What strong answers cover

  1. Indicates original (1), replacement (7), void (8) — drives versioning/supersession.
  2. MERGE/dedup must honor it.
  3. Misreading it double-counts or loses corrections.
  4. Source from the claim record / bill type.
  5. Test version transitions.

Starter pattern

CASE frequency_code WHEN 7 THEN replace_prior WHEN 8 THEN void_prior ELSE insert_original
Common mistake: Ignoring the frequency code makes the pipeline append replacements as new claims, double-counting paid amounts.
Principal follow-up: How would you validate that your version logic correctly handles a sequence of original -> replacement -> void?
070
Explain ICN from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
ExpertICN

Model answer

The ICN (Internal Control Number, payer claim control number) is the payer's unique identifier for a processed claim, linking the 837 to the 835 (carried in CLP). It is essential for claim-to-remittance matching and tracking adjustments across versions.

  • Payer's unique processed-claim id.
  • Links 837 to 835 (in CLP segment).
  • Key for claim-to-remittance matching.
  • Tracks adjustment chains.

Healthcare angle: Matching remittance to claims relies on the ICN carried from adjudication into the 835.

What strong answers cover

  1. ICN = payer's unique processed-claim id; links 837 to 835 (carried in CLP).
  2. Key for claim-to-remittance matching.
  3. Tracks adjustment chains across versions.
  4. Format varies by payer.
  5. Essential for reconciliation.

Starter pattern

match: 835.CLP.ICN -> claim.icn  (claim-to-remittance)
-- track ICN lineage across adjustments
Common mistake: Failing to match on ICN orphans remittances from claims, so paid amounts can't be tied back and AR breaks.
Principal follow-up: How do you match remittance to claims when the ICN format and reuse rules differ across payers?
071
Explain EOB from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
ExpertEOB

Model answer

The EOB (Explanation of Benefits) is the member-facing statement of how a claim was processed (billed, allowed, paid, member responsibility). It is built from adjudication/remittance data; the FHIR ExplanationOfBenefit resource is its API form for Patient Access.

  • Member-facing claim processing statement.
  • Billed/allowed/paid/member responsibility.
  • Built from adjudication/remittance.
  • FHIR EOB serves it via Patient Access APIs.

Healthcare angle: EOB/EOB-FHIR data must accurately reflect member responsibility for member-facing transparency.

What strong answers cover

  1. EOB = member-facing statement of how a claim was processed (billed/allowed/paid/member-responsibility).
  2. Built from adjudication/remittance.
  3. FHIR EOB serves it via Patient Access.
  4. Member-responsibility accuracy is critical.
  5. Members see it — errors are visible.

Starter pattern

eob = claim + adjudication(billed,allowed,paid,member_resp)
-- expose via FHIR EOB; member_resp must tie to remittance
Common mistake: An inaccurate member-responsibility on the EOB directly misinforms members and erodes trust (and risks complaints/regulatory issues).
Principal follow-up: How do you guarantee member-facing EOB numbers exactly reconcile to internal adjudication?
072
Explain ERA from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
ExpertERA

Model answer

The ERA (Electronic Remittance Advice) is the electronic remittance the payer sends to providers — the 835 transaction. It carries paid/adjusted/denied detail and reason codes; ERA processing feeds AR, denial, and reconciliation analytics.

  • Electronic remittance to providers = the 835.
  • Paid/adjusted/denied + reason codes.
  • Feeds AR, denials, reconciliation.
  • Duplicate ERAs corrupt AR.

Healthcare angle: ERA (835) is the source of provider-side paid/denial data and must be loaded idempotently.

What strong answers cover

  1. ERA = electronic remittance to providers = the 835.
  2. Carries paid/adjusted/denied + reason codes.
  3. Feeds AR, denials, reconciliation.
  4. Duplicate ERAs corrupt AR.
  5. Load idempotently.

Starter pattern

ERA(835) -> provider-side paid/denial facts
-- idempotent identity-keyed load (same as 835)
Common mistake: Double-loading an ERA double-applies provider payments, corrupting AR and provider-side cost analytics.
Principal follow-up: How do provider-side ERA analytics differ from payer-side, and how do you keep both reconciled?
073
Explain payer source systems from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertpayer source systems

Model answer

Payer data originates in core admin systems (FACETS, QNXT, Amisys, HealthRules), pharmacy systems, eligibility systems, and external feeds. Integrating them requires understanding each system's grain, keys, and quirks, and reconciling across them to a single source of truth.

  • Core admin (FACETS/QNXT/Amisys), pharmacy, eligibility, external.
  • Each has its own grain/keys/quirks.
  • Integrate + reconcile to a source of truth.
  • Understand system-specific behaviors.

Healthcare angle: Integrating FACETS claims with external 837 and pharmacy feeds requires reconciling differing grains and keys.

What strong answers cover

  1. Data originates in core admin (FACETS/QNXT/Amisys/HealthRules), pharmacy, eligibility, and external feeds.
  2. Each has its own grain/keys/quirks.
  3. Integrate and reconcile to a single source of truth.
  4. Understand system-specific behaviors.
  5. Map each system's claim model to the conformed model.

Starter pattern

for each source: profile grain/keys -> map to conformed claim/member/provider model
-- reconcile across systems to one source of truth
Common mistake: Assuming all source systems share a grain/key model leads to mis-joins and double-counting when you integrate them.
Principal follow-up: How would you reconcile claims that exist in both a core admin system and the raw 837 feed?
074
Explain FACETS from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
ExpertFACETS

Model answer

FACETS is a widely-used core payer administration platform (claims, membership, provider, benefits). As a source system, its data model and adjudication outputs feed the warehouse; engineers must understand its grain and reconcile it against raw EDI.

  • Core payer admin platform (claims/membership/provider/benefits).
  • A primary source system for the warehouse.
  • Understand its grain and adjudication outputs.
  • Reconcile against raw 837/835.

Healthcare angle: FACETS-adjudicated claims are reconciled against inbound 837 to prove integrity.

What strong answers cover

  1. FACETS = core payer admin platform (claims, membership, provider, benefits).
  2. A primary source system for the warehouse.
  3. Understand its grain and adjudication outputs.
  4. Reconcile against raw 837/835.
  5. Map its model to conformed dimensions.

Starter pattern

FACETS adjudicated claims -> reconcile vs inbound 837
-- map FACETS keys to surrogate keys
Common mistake: Taking FACETS-adjudicated output as truth without reconciling to source EDI hides adjudication or load gaps.
Principal follow-up: How do you validate that FACETS-adjudicated claims fully reflect the inbound 837s?
075
Explain QNXT from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
ExpertQNXT

Model answer

QNXT is another major core payer administration platform serving the same functions as FACETS. Treat it as a source system with its own schema/keys; integration and reconciliation patterns mirror FACETS.

  • Core payer admin platform (alternative to FACETS).
  • Source system with its own schema/keys.
  • Integrate + reconcile like FACETS.
  • Know its grain and outputs.

Healthcare angle: A payer on QNXT integrates and reconciles it the same way as FACETS for claims/membership/provider.

What strong answers cover

  1. QNXT = core payer admin platform (alternative to FACETS).
  2. Source system with its own schema/keys.
  3. Integrate and reconcile like FACETS.
  4. Know its grain and outputs.
  5. Conform to shared model.

Starter pattern

QNXT claims/membership/provider -> conformed model; reconcile to source
-- same discipline as FACETS
Common mistake: Hard-coding logic to one platform's quirks makes the pipeline brittle when a line of business runs on a different core system.
Principal follow-up: How would you build ingestion that handles both FACETS and QNXT without duplicating logic?
076
Explain Epic/Cerner boundary from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
ExpertEpic/Cerner boundary

Model answer

Epic/Cerner are provider EHRs, not payer systems — the boundary between clinical (provider) and administrative (payer) data. Payers receive clinical data via interoperability (FHIR/HL7/C-CDA), not direct EHR access; respect the boundary and normalize incoming clinical data.

  • Epic/Cerner are provider EHRs, not payer systems.
  • Boundary between clinical and administrative data.
  • Payers receive clinical data via FHIR/HL7/C-CDA.
  • Normalize incoming clinical data.

Healthcare angle: Payers don't query Epic directly; they ingest standardized clinical data to supplement claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. Epic/Cerner are provider EHRs, not payer systems — the clinical/administrative boundary.
  2. Payers receive clinical data via FHIR/HL7/C-CDA, not direct EHR access.
  3. Normalize incoming clinical data.
  4. Respect the boundary.
  5. Link clinical to claims via member identity.

Starter pattern

payer does NOT query Epic directly; ingests standardized clinical (FHIR/HL7/C-CDA)
-> normalize -> link to member
Common mistake: Assuming direct EHR access (or that EHR data is as complete/standardized as claims) leads to wrong architecture and analytics gaps.
Principal follow-up: What clinical data can a payer realistically get from provider EHRs, and how complete is it versus claims?
077
Explain actuarial analytics from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertactuarial analytics

Model answer

Actuarial analytics quantify cost, risk, reserves, and trend for pricing and financial reporting. They need stable definitions, full history, restatement (as-of reproducibility), incurred-vs-paid clarity, and IBNR — prioritizing correctness and auditability over speed.

  • Cost, risk, reserves, trend for pricing/financials.
  • Stable definitions + reproducible as-of history.
  • Incurred vs paid; IBNR.
  • Correctness/auditability over speed.

Healthcare angle: Actuaries must reproduce a prior period exactly even after retro corrections — bitemporal/snapshotting matters.

What strong answers cover

  1. Quantify cost, risk, reserves, trend for pricing/financials.
  2. Stable definitions + reproducible as-of history.
  3. Incurred vs paid; IBNR.
  4. Restate for late data.
  5. Correctness/auditability over speed.

Starter pattern

serve actuaries: bitemporal history, defined metrics, IBNR-aware incurred cost
-- reproduce any prior period exactly
Common mistake: Serving actuaries non-reproducible data (no as-of snapshots) means they can't reproduce a prior period after restatements, breaking pricing/financials.
Principal follow-up: Why do actuaries need to reproduce a prior period exactly, and what data design makes that possible?
078
Explain reserving vs paid claims from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertreserving vs paid claims

Model answer

Paid claims are what has been disbursed; reserves estimate liabilities for incurred-but-not-yet-paid claims (including IBNR). Financial reporting needs incurred (paid + reserves), not just paid, because recent periods are incomplete. Data must support both views.

  • Paid = disbursed; reserves = estimated unpaid liability.
  • Incurred = paid + reserves (incl. IBNR).
  • Recent periods incomplete → reserves matter.
  • Support both views in data.

Healthcare angle: Reporting only paid for recent months understates cost; incurred (with IBNR) is the actuarial truth.

What strong answers cover

  1. Paid = disbursed; reserves estimate incurred-but-not-yet-paid (incl. IBNR).
  2. Incurred = paid + reserves.
  3. Recent periods incomplete -> reserves matter.
  4. Support both views in data.
  5. Drives financial reporting.

Starter pattern

incurred = paid + reserves(IBNR)
-- report incurred for recent periods, not paid-to-date
Common mistake: Reporting only paid for recent months understates cost; the financials look better than reality until runout corrects them.
Principal follow-up: How does the paid vs incurred gap behave over a claim's runout, and why does finance need incurred?
079
Explain incurred but not reported from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertincurred but not reported

Model answer

IBNR estimates claims for services already rendered but not yet received/processed. It corrects the incompleteness of recent periods using historical lag patterns (completion factors). Pipelines support IBNR by tracking service-vs-received dates and claim lag.

  • Estimate of incurred-but-not-yet-received claims.
  • Corrects recent-period incompleteness.
  • Uses lag/completion-factor patterns.
  • Needs service-vs-received date tracking.

Healthcare angle: IBNR makes recent-month cost/trend meaningful by estimating claims still in the pipeline.

What strong answers cover

  1. IBNR estimates claims for services already rendered but not yet received/processed.
  2. Corrects recent-period incompleteness using lag/completion factors.
  3. Needs service-vs-received date tracking.
  4. Restate as claims complete.
  5. Core to trend and reserving.

Starter pattern

ibnr(service_month) = expected_ultimate - paid_to_date
expected_ultimate = paid_to_date / completion_factor(lag)
Common mistake: Omitting IBNR makes recent-month cost and trend look artificially low, leading to under-pricing and under-reserving.
Principal follow-up: How do you derive completion factors from a runout triangle and apply them to estimate IBNR?
080
Explain runout triangle from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Expertrunout triangle

Model answer

A runout (lag) triangle arrays claims by service period (rows) and payment/processing lag (columns), revealing completion patterns used to estimate IBNR/completion factors. Building it requires accurate service and paid/processed dates.

  • Service period × payment lag matrix.
  • Reveals completion patterns.
  • Basis for IBNR/completion factors.
  • Needs accurate service + paid dates.

Healthcare angle: The runout triangle is how actuaries derive completion factors to estimate IBNR for recent months.

What strong answers cover

  1. A runout/lag triangle arrays claims by service period (rows) x payment lag (columns), revealing completion patterns.
  2. Basis for IBNR/completion factors.
  3. Needs accurate service + paid dates.
  4. Diagonal = as-of view; rows mature over time.
  5. Drives reserving.

Starter pattern

triangle[service_month][lag_months] = cumulative_paid
completion_factor = mature_total / cumulative_at_lag
Common mistake: Building the triangle on load dates instead of service/paid dates distorts completion factors and therefore IBNR.
Principal follow-up: Walk through deriving a completion factor from a runout triangle and the assumptions it relies on.
081
Explain trend analytics from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principaltrend analytics

Model answer

Trend analytics measure rate of change in cost/utilization over time, decomposed into drivers (utilization, unit cost, mix, severity) and adjusted for incompleteness (IBNR) and population change (PMPM). Disputes often arise from definition/restatement differences.

  • Rate of change in cost/utilization.
  • Decompose: utilization, unit cost, mix, severity.
  • Adjust for IBNR and use PMPM.
  • Definition/restatement drives disputes.

Healthcare angle: Trend disputes with actuaries usually trace to incompleteness or definition differences — restatement and clear definitions resolve them.

What strong answers cover

  1. Trend = rate of change in cost/utilization, decomposed into utilization, unit cost, mix, severity.
  2. Adjust for IBNR; use PMPM.
  3. Definition/restatement drives disputes.
  4. Compare like populations.
  5. Tie to pricing/actions.

Starter pattern

trend = pmpm(t)/pmpm(t-12); decompose util x unit_cost x mix
-- IBNR-adjust recent; align definitions
Common mistake: Trend disputes usually come from incompleteness or definition differences; presenting un-adjusted, ill-defined trend invites wrong decisions.
Principal follow-up: When an actuary and your dashboard disagree on trend, what are the top causes and how do you reconcile them?
082
Explain network analytics from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalnetwork analytics

Model answer

Network analytics assess provider network composition, adequacy, utilization, and cost (in- vs out-of-network), supporting network management and regulatory adequacy. They rely on accurate provider dimension, taxonomy, and geographic attribution.

  • Network composition, adequacy, utilization, cost.
  • In- vs out-of-network analysis.
  • Regulatory adequacy support.
  • Needs accurate provider dim + geography.

Healthcare angle: Network adequacy (enough providers by specialty/geography) depends on accurate provider/taxonomy/location data.

What strong answers cover

  1. Assess network composition, adequacy, utilization, cost (in- vs out-of-network).
  2. Regulatory adequacy support.
  3. Needs accurate provider dimension, taxonomy, geography.
  4. As-of network status for the period.
  5. Drives network management.

Starter pattern

adequacy = providers_by_specialty within distance/ratio standard
-- use as-of network status + geography
Common mistake: Using current (not as-of-service-date) network status mislabels in/out-of-network, distorting cost steerage and adequacy compliance.
Principal follow-up: How would you measure network adequacy by specialty and geography, and what data quality does it demand?
083
Explain out-of-network analysis from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalout-of-network analysis

Model answer

Out-of-network (OON) analysis identifies care received outside the contracted network, which carries different pricing, higher cost, and member-responsibility implications. Identified via provider network status as-of service date; drives steerage and cost management.

  • Identify care outside the contracted network.
  • Different pricing/higher cost/member impact.
  • Use provider network status as-of service date.
  • Drives steerage and cost management.

Healthcare angle: OON identification needs the provider's network status as of service date (an as-of join), not current status.

What strong answers cover

  1. OON = care outside the contracted network; different pricing, higher cost, member impact.
  2. Identify via provider network status as-of service date.
  3. Drives steerage and cost management.
  4. Distinguish emergency OON.
  5. Quantify member balance-billing exposure.

Starter pattern

is_oon = provider_network_status(as_of service_dt) = 'out'
-- as-of join, not current status
Common mistake: Determining OON from current network status mislabels claims (providers join/leave networks), corrupting OON cost and steerage analytics.
Principal follow-up: Why must OON use as-of network status, and how do you handle providers who changed network status over time?
084
Explain provider quality metrics from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalprovider quality metrics

Model answer

Provider quality metrics score providers on outcomes, adherence to guidelines, utilization efficiency, and patient experience, supporting network and VBC decisions. They require attribution, risk adjustment for fair comparison, and adequate sample sizes.

  • Score providers on outcomes/guidelines/efficiency.
  • Support network/VBC decisions.
  • Risk-adjust for fair comparison.
  • Mind sample size/attribution.

Healthcare angle: Comparing providers fairly requires risk adjustment and correct attribution, or scores mislead.

What strong answers cover

  1. Score providers on outcomes, guideline adherence, efficiency, experience.
  2. Risk-adjust for fair comparison.
  3. Mind sample size and attribution.
  4. Support network/VBC decisions.
  5. Make methodology transparent.

Starter pattern

provider_score = risk_adjusted(outcomes, adherence, efficiency)
-- suppress low-sample providers; correct attribution
Common mistake: Comparing providers without risk adjustment penalizes those with sicker panels, producing unfair and misleading quality scores.
Principal follow-up: How do you make provider quality comparisons fair across very different patient panels and small samples?
085
Explain member risk stratification from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalmember risk stratification

Model answer

Risk stratification segments members by predicted risk/cost (using RAF, conditions, utilization, predictive models) to prioritize care management. Requires accurate diagnoses, complete data, and validated models; must avoid bias and respect minimum-necessary PHI.

  • Segment members by predicted risk/cost.
  • Use RAF, conditions, utilization, models.
  • Prioritize care management.
  • Validate models; avoid bias; minimum PHI.

Healthcare angle: Stratification targets high-risk members for intervention; model/data quality and fairness are critical.

What strong answers cover

  1. Segment members by predicted risk/cost (RAF, conditions, utilization, models).
  2. Prioritize care management.
  3. Validate models; avoid bias; minimum PHI.
  4. Accurate diagnoses and complete data.
  5. Monitor for drift.

Starter pattern

risk_tier = model(raf, conditions, utilization, gaps)
-- validate, check fairness, refresh features
Common mistake: Stratifying on incomplete/biased data targets the wrong members and can systematically disadvantage groups — a quality and ethics failure.
Principal follow-up: How would you validate a risk-stratification model for both accuracy and fairness before using it for outreach?
086
Explain pharmacy adherence from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalpharmacy adherence

Model answer

Pharmacy adherence measures whether members take medications as prescribed, via metrics like PDC/MPR from fill data. It is a quality measure (Stars) and clinical signal; requires accurate fill dates, days supply, and drug grouping (RxNorm).

  • Whether members take meds as prescribed.
  • PDC/MPR from fill data.
  • Stars quality + clinical signal.
  • Needs fill dates, days supply, RxNorm grouping.

Healthcare angle: Adherence (PDC) is a Stars measure computed from Rx fills grouped by RxNorm.

What strong answers cover

  1. Adherence = whether members take meds as prescribed (PDC/MPR from fills).
  2. Stars quality + clinical signal.
  3. Needs fill dates, days supply, RxNorm grouping.
  4. Handle overlapping fills/switches.
  5. Measure per drug class/measurement period.

Starter pattern

pdc = covered_days / period_days  (cap overlaps)
-- group equivalents via RxNorm; handle switches
Common mistake: Computing adherence on raw NDC or mishandling overlapping fills over/understates PDC, corrupting a Stars measure.
Principal follow-up: How do you handle overlapping fills, early refills, and drug switches in a PDC calculation?
087
Explain medication possession ratio from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalmedication possession ratio

Model answer

MPR (and the related PDC) estimates the proportion of days a member had medication on hand over a period, from fill dates and days supply. PDC (proportion of days covered) is generally preferred (caps overlap). Both require careful handling of overlapping fills and switches.

  • Proportion of days with medication on hand.
  • From fill dates + days supply.
  • PDC preferred (caps overlapping fills).
  • Handle overlaps/switches carefully.

Healthcare angle: PDC/MPR adherence feeds Stars; overlapping fills must be handled so adherence isn't overstated.

What strong answers cover

  1. MPR/PDC = proportion of days with medication on hand, from fill dates and days supply.
  2. PDC preferred (caps overlapping fills).
  3. Handle overlaps/switches carefully.
  4. Per drug class and period.
  5. Feeds Stars.

Starter pattern

mpr = SUM(days_supply) / period_days  (can exceed 1)
pdc = covered_days / period_days  (<=1, preferred)
Common mistake: Using MPR (uncapped) where PDC is expected overstates adherence by counting stockpiling, inflating a quality measure.
Principal follow-up: Why is PDC generally preferred over MPR for Stars, and how do they diverge with early refills?
088
Explain prior auth data from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalprior auth data

Model answer

Prior authorization data (from 278/UM systems) records auth requests, decisions, and status. It links to claims to analyze auth-related denials, turnaround, and appropriateness; model request/decision/status with timestamps and tie to the resulting claim.

  • Auth requests/decisions/status (278/UM).
  • Link to claims for denial/turnaround analysis.
  • Model with timestamps.
  • Tie auth to resulting claim.

Healthcare angle: Missing-auth denials and auth turnaround analytics require linking prior-auth data to claims.

What strong answers cover

  1. Auth requests/decisions/status from 278/UM systems.
  2. Link to claims for denial/turnaround analysis.
  3. Model with timestamps.
  4. Tie auth to resulting claim.
  5. Measure turnaround vs SLA.

Starter pattern

auth(auth_id, decision, status, ts) JOIN claim ON auth_id
-- analyze missing-auth denials, turnaround
Common mistake: Poor auth-to-claim linkage makes 'missing auth' denials ambiguous (real vs linkage failure), misdirecting UM fixes.
Principal follow-up: How would you measure prior-auth turnaround and its effect on downstream denials?
089
Explain denial root cause from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principaldenial root cause

Model answer

Denial root-cause analysis groups denials by CARC/RARC into themes (eligibility, auth, coding, timely filing) and traces them to upstream causes (provider behavior, data errors, benefit rules) to drive prevention and rework prioritization.

  • Group denials into themes via CARC/RARC.
  • Trace to upstream causes.
  • Drive prevention + rework prioritization.
  • Quantify recoverable dollars.

Healthcare angle: Denial root-cause turns raw codes into actionable fixes (e.g. close auth gaps) and recovery priorities.

What strong answers cover

  1. Group denials by CARC/RARC into themes, then trace to upstream causes (provider behavior, data errors, benefit rules).
  2. Drive prevention and rework prioritization.
  3. Quantify recoverable dollars.
  4. Tie denials to root systems/processes.
  5. Track resolution.

Starter pattern

denial -> bucket -> upstream_cause(provider/data/benefit)
-- prioritize by recoverable$ x volume
Common mistake: Stopping at the CARC code (not the upstream cause) treats symptoms; denials recur because the real driver (e.g. auth gaps) is never fixed.
Principal follow-up: How do you move from denial codes to actionable upstream root causes that actually reduce future denials?
090
Explain payment integrity from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalpayment integrity

Model answer

Payment integrity ensures claims are paid correctly — detecting overpayments, duplicates, coding errors, and improper payments before/after payment. It blends rules, analytics, and FWA detection; data must be accurate, deduplicated, and reconciled.

  • Ensure claims paid correctly.
  • Detect overpayments, duplicates, coding errors.
  • Pre- and post-pay review + recovery.
  • Needs accurate, deduplicated, reconciled data.

Healthcare angle: Duplicate-claim and improper-payment detection are core payment-integrity analytics protecting plan dollars.

What strong answers cover

  1. Ensure claims are paid correctly: detect overpayments, duplicates, coding errors, improper payments.
  2. Pre- and post-pay review + recovery.
  3. Needs accurate, deduplicated, reconciled, version-aware data.
  4. Explainable findings for recovery.
  5. Protects plan dollars.

Starter pattern

flag: duplicate claims, overpayment vs contract, upcoding patterns
-- on deduplicated, version-aware claims
Common mistake: Running payment-integrity on non-deduplicated/version-unaware data produces false positives (and misses), wasting recovery effort.
Principal follow-up: What data-quality foundations must be in place before payment-integrity analytics are trustworthy?
091
Explain fraud waste abuse from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalfraud waste abuse

Model answer

FWA detection identifies fraudulent (intentional), wasteful (unnecessary), and abusive (improper) billing via anomaly detection, peer comparison, and rules (e.g. impossible volumes, upcoding patterns). Requires clean, complete data and risk-adjusted peer baselines; outputs feed investigation.

  • Detect fraud, waste, abuse in billing.
  • Anomaly detection, peer comparison, rules.
  • Risk-adjusted baselines; investigate outliers.
  • Needs clean, complete data.

Healthcare angle: FWA analytics flag outlier providers (e.g. improbable service volumes) for investigation while controlling false positives.

What strong answers cover

  1. FWA detection via anomaly detection, peer comparison, rules (impossible volumes, upcoding).
  2. Risk-adjusted baselines; control false positives.
  3. Prioritized investigation queues.
  4. Explainability and data quality critical.
  5. Outputs drive investigations with real consequences.

Starter pattern

flag providers where metric >> risk_adjusted_peer_baseline
-- e.g. services/day exceeding feasible; explain the flag
Common mistake: FWA flags without risk-adjusted baselines or explainability generate false accusations and waste investigator time.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep FWA false positives low while still catching real outliers, and why does explainability matter?
092
Explain clinical vs administrative data from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalclinical vs administrative data

Model answer

Administrative (claims/eligibility/payment) data is complete for covered members and standardized but clinically shallow; clinical (EHR/labs/notes) data is clinically rich but provider-scoped and less standardized. Combining them (via interoperability) gives the fullest member picture.

  • Administrative: broad, standardized, clinically shallow.
  • Clinical: deep, provider-scoped, less standardized.
  • Combine via interoperability for full picture.
  • Different completeness/latency/use.

Healthcare angle: Claims show all cost; clinical data shows why — combining them improves risk/quality analytics.

What strong answers cover

  1. Administrative (claims/eligibility/payment): broad, standardized, clinically shallow.
  2. Clinical (EHR/labs/notes): deep, provider-scoped, less standardized.
  3. Combine via interoperability for the full picture.
  4. Different completeness/latency/use.
  5. Choose the right source per question.

Starter pattern

claims: what was billed (all providers); clinical: what happened (one provider)
-> combine via member identity for full view
Common mistake: Answering a clinical question from claims alone (or a cost question from EHR alone) gives an incomplete, misleading answer.
Principal follow-up: For a measure needing both a diagnosis and a lab value, how do you combine claims and clinical data soundly?
093
Explain data latency expectations from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principaldata latency expectations

Model answer

Different consumers tolerate different latency: real-time eligibility/ADT, daily claims/financials, monthly actuarial. Set SLAs per data product to its consumer need, and don't over-engineer real-time where daily suffices or under-deliver where it doesn't.

  • Match latency SLA to consumer need.
  • Real-time (eligibility/ADT) vs daily (claims) vs monthly (actuarial).
  • Don't over- or under-engineer freshness.
  • Document per data product.

Healthcare angle: Care-management triggers need near-real-time ADT; actuarial trend is fine monthly — SLAs differ by product.

What strong answers cover

  1. Match latency SLA to consumer need: real-time (eligibility/ADT), daily (claims/financials), monthly (actuarial).
  2. Don't over- or under-engineer freshness.
  3. Document per data product.
  4. Design backward from the consumer deadline.
  5. Different products, different SLAs.

Starter pattern

per product: set SLA = consumer need
ADT->real-time; claims->daily; actuarial->monthly
Common mistake: Over-engineering everything to real-time wastes cost; under-delivering on a real-time need (e.g. ADT triggers) misses care opportunities.
Principal follow-up: How do you decide the right latency for each payer data product, and where is real-time genuinely worth it?
094
Explain source-of-truth strategy from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalsource-of-truth strategy

Model answer

A source-of-truth strategy designates one authoritative system/dataset per data domain (claims, eligibility, provider) so conflicts resolve consistently and consumers know what to trust. It requires reconciliation, governance, and clear ownership; ambiguity here causes the most damaging disputes.

  • One authoritative source per domain.
  • Resolves conflicts consistently.
  • Requires reconciliation + governance + ownership.
  • Ambiguity causes damaging disputes.

Healthcare angle: When two systems disagree on a member's eligibility, a declared source of truth resolves it; without one, reports diverge.

What strong answers cover

  1. Designate one authoritative source per domain (claims, eligibility, provider) so conflicts resolve consistently.
  2. Requires reconciliation, governance, ownership.
  3. Ambiguity here causes the most damaging disputes.
  4. Migrate consumers to the certified source.
  5. Document precedence.

Starter pattern

per domain: declare source of truth; reconcile others to it
-- consumers read the certified source
Common mistake: Without a declared source of truth, two systems disagree on (say) eligibility and reports silently diverge, destroying trust.
Principal follow-up: When two systems disagree on a member's eligibility, how does a source-of-truth strategy resolve it?
095
Explain business glossary from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalbusiness glossary

Model answer

A business glossary defines key terms and metrics (member, paid amount, PMPM, denial) consistently across the organization, linked to the data that implements them. It prevents semantic drift and supports onboarding, governance, and metric-dispute resolution.

  • Consistent definitions of terms/metrics.
  • Linked to implementing data/lineage.
  • Prevents semantic drift.
  • Supports governance and dispute resolution.

Healthcare angle: A glossary defining 'paid amount' and 'PMPM' precisely stops teams from computing them differently.

What strong answers cover

  1. Define key terms/metrics (member, paid amount, PMPM, denial) consistently, linked to implementing data/lineage.
  2. Prevents semantic drift.
  3. Supports governance and dispute resolution.
  4. Assign stewards; keep current.
  5. Embed at point of need.

Starter pattern

glossary_term(name, definition, owner) -> linked to mart/column + lineage
-- single authoritative definition
Common mistake: Without a glossary, teams compute 'paid amount' or 'PMPM' differently and reports disagree with no way to adjudicate.
Principal follow-up: How would you drive glossary adoption so definitions are actually used, not shelfware?
096
Explain data steward workflow from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principaldata steward workflow

Model answer

Data stewards own definitions, quality, and issue resolution for their domain — reviewing quarantined records, approving definition changes, and adjudicating data disputes. A clear steward workflow (intake, triage, resolution, audit) operationalizes governance.

  • Stewards own domain definitions/quality/issues.
  • Review quarantine, approve definitions, adjudicate disputes.
  • Workflow: intake → triage → resolve → audit.
  • Operationalizes governance.

Healthcare angle: Quarantined claims with bad codes route to a steward who fixes the mapping or upstream feed.

What strong answers cover

  1. Stewards own definitions, quality, and issue resolution for their domain.
  2. Review quarantine, approve definition changes, adjudicate disputes.
  3. Workflow: intake -> triage -> resolve -> audit.
  4. Operationalizes governance.
  5. Clear ownership per domain.

Starter pattern

quarantined record -> steward queue -> fix root cause / approve mapping -> replay
-- audited decisions
Common mistake: No steward workflow means quarantined/disputed data piles up unresolved, and quality issues never get fixed at the source.
Principal follow-up: How would you design a steward workflow so quarantined claims get resolved and replayed quickly?
097
Explain healthcare KPI design from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalhealthcare KPI design

Model answer

Good healthcare KPIs are precisely defined (numerator/denominator, inclusions/exclusions), risk-adjusted where comparing populations/providers, restatement-aware (late claims), and tied to a decision. Avoid vanity metrics and ambiguous definitions that fuel disputes.

  • Precise numerator/denominator/exclusions.
  • Risk-adjust population/provider comparisons.
  • Restatement-aware (late claims/IBNR).
  • Tie to a decision; avoid vanity metrics.

Healthcare angle: A PMPM or readmission KPI needs exact definition, risk adjustment, and IBNR-awareness to be trustworthy.

What strong answers cover

  1. Good KPIs: precise numerator/denominator/exclusions; risk-adjusted comparisons; restatement-aware (late claims); tied to a decision.
  2. Avoid vanity metrics and ambiguous definitions.
  3. Document and govern definitions.
  4. Make completeness explicit.
  5. Validate reproducibility.

Starter pattern

kpi = precise(numerator/denominator, exclusions) + risk_adjusted + IBNR_aware
-- tie to an action
Common mistake: Ambiguous or non-risk-adjusted KPIs fuel disputes and drive wrong actions (e.g. penalizing providers with sicker panels).
Principal follow-up: What turns a healthcare metric from vanity into decision-grade, and how do you test it before publishing?
098
Explain executive dashboard pitfalls from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principalexecutive dashboard pitfalls

Model answer

Common pitfalls: showing incomplete recent periods as if final (no IBNR caveat), inconsistent metric definitions across tiles, no drill-to-source, over-aggregation hiding drivers, and stale data presented as live. Fix with clear definitions, completeness indicators, and lineage.

  • Incomplete recent periods shown as final.
  • Inconsistent definitions across tiles.
  • No drill-to-source; over-aggregation.
  • Stale data shown as live.

Healthcare angle: An exec dashboard showing recent-month cost without an IBNR/completeness caveat misleads leadership.

What strong answers cover

  1. Pitfalls: incomplete recent periods shown as final; inconsistent definitions across tiles; no drill-to-source; over-aggregation; stale data shown as live.
  2. Fix with clear definitions, completeness indicators, lineage.
  3. Add freshness/IBNR caveats.
  4. Govern metric definitions (semantic layer).
  5. Enable drill-down to source.

Starter pattern

show: freshness timestamp + completeness/IBNR caveat + consistent definitions
-- drill to source mart
Common mistake: An exec dashboard showing recent-month cost without an IBNR/completeness caveat misleads leadership into wrong conclusions.
Principal follow-up: What guardrails would you add to an exec cost dashboard so incomplete recent months aren't misread as a real trend?
099
Explain data ethics from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
Principaldata ethics

Model answer

Data ethics in healthcare covers fairness (avoiding biased risk/quality models), transparency, consent/appropriate use, minimum-necessary PHI, and avoiding harm from analytics decisions. Build fairness checks, documentation, and governance into model and data pipelines.

  • Fairness/bias, transparency, consent, minimum-necessary.
  • Avoid harm from analytics-driven decisions.
  • Fairness checks + documentation + governance.
  • Especially for risk/quality models.

Healthcare angle: A member-risk model must be checked for bias so care/resource decisions don't unfairly disadvantage groups.

What strong answers cover

  1. Ethics: fairness/bias, transparency, consent/appropriate use, minimum-necessary PHI, avoiding harm.
  2. Build fairness checks and documentation into pipelines.
  3. Especially for risk/quality models.
  4. Govern use of member data.
  5. Member wellbeing first.

Starter pattern

before deploying a member-impacting model: fairness check across groups + documentation + human review
Common mistake: Deploying a biased risk/quality model can systematically disadvantage groups in care/resource decisions — a serious ethical and reputational harm.
Principal follow-up: How would you check a member-risk model for bias, and what would you do if you found it?
100
Explain AI feature governance from both business and data-engineering angles. How would mistakes in this area affect payer analytics?
PrincipalAI feature governance

Model answer

AI feature governance manages how features for ML (e.g. risk models) are defined, sourced, validated, versioned, monitored for drift, and audited — ensuring reproducibility, fairness, PHI compliance, and traceability from prediction to source data.

  • Define/source/validate/version features.
  • Monitor drift; audit lineage.
  • Reproducibility + fairness + PHI compliance.
  • Trace prediction to source data.

Healthcare angle: Risk-model features need governance so a score-drift incident traces to a feature/data change and stays compliant.

What strong answers cover

  1. Govern ML features: define/source/validate/version, monitor drift, audit lineage.
  2. Reproducibility + fairness + PHI compliance.
  3. Trace prediction to source data.
  4. Point-in-time correctness (no leakage).
  5. Online/offline consistency.

Starter pattern

feature_store: versioned, point-in-time-correct, lineage-tracked, PHI-controlled
-- monitor drift; trace prediction->source
Common mistake: Ungoverned features cause training-serving skew and leakage, so a risk-score incident can't be traced and may violate PHI rules.
Principal follow-up: How would you trace a drifting risk score back to a specific feature/data change, and prevent training leakage?

Production Incidents

RCA, triage, recovery, prevention and SLA-driven production support scenarios.

100 questions
001
Nightly claims job sla miss: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumnightly claims job SLA miss

Model answer

Investigate first: is the job slow, stuck, or failed? Check the run, stage durations, and upstream feed arrival times. Diagnose the cause — late source file, volume spike, skew/spill, contention, or a plan regression. Resolve with the smallest safe action (rerun the failed stage, add resources, or fix the hot key). Prevent with predictive SLA alerts, capacity for peaks, and a critical-path review.

  • Investigate: stage durations + upstream arrival times.
  • Diagnose: late file vs volume spike vs skew vs contention vs plan change.
  • Resolve: smallest safe fix; communicate revised ETA.
  • Prevent: predictive alert before deadline, peak capacity, critical-path tuning.

Healthcare angle: If the actuarial mart depends on this, alert early and consider a partial/degraded publish to protect downstream consumers.

What strong answers cover

  1. Triage first: is the job slow, stuck, or failed, and is the upstream file even here yet?
  2. Diagnose the bottleneck on the critical path (late file, volume spike, skew/spill, contention, plan regression).
  3. Resolve with the smallest safe action; communicate a revised ETA to downstream consumers.
  4. Prevent: predictive SLA alerting, peak capacity, critical-path tuning.
  5. Protect the consumer (partial/degraded publish) when the actuarial mart depends on it.

Starter pattern

1. check run + stage durations + upstream arrival time
2. find slowest stage (Spark UI / query profile)
3. rerun failed stage OR scale OR fix hot key
4. notify consumers w/ ETA
Common mistake: Blindly rerunning the whole job instead of diagnosing the bottleneck — wastes the window and still misses the SLA.
Principal follow-up: How would you design a predictive alert that pages at 5am when the 7am actuarial-mart SLA is at risk?
002
Duplicate 835 file loaded: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumduplicate 835 file loaded

Model answer

Investigate: confirm the same remittance identity (payer + check/EFT + remit id) loaded twice via load/copy history. Diagnose the impact on AR aging and paid totals. Resolve by backing out the duplicate (Time Travel restore or targeted delete of the second application) and reconciling. Prevent with identity-based duplicate detection and idempotent, identity-keyed loads.

  • Investigate: load history for repeated remittance identity.
  • Diagnose: AR aging / paid total corruption.
  • Resolve: back out duplicate (restore/targeted delete) + reconcile.
  • Prevent: identity dup-detection + idempotent load.

Healthcare angle: Duplicate 835 double-applies payments and corrupts AR; the durable fix is remittance-identity idempotency.

What strong answers cover

  1. Confirm the same remittance identity (payer + check/EFT + remit id) loaded twice via load/copy history.
  2. Diagnose the impact on AR aging and paid totals.
  3. Resolve by backing out the second application (Time Travel restore or targeted reversal) and reconciling.
  4. Prevent with identity-based duplicate detection and idempotent, identity-keyed loads.
  5. Treat as payment integrity: document and communicate.

Starter pattern

-- back out + prevent
RESTORE TABLE ar BEFORE (statement => '<dup_load_id>');
MERGE ... ON remittance_key  -- second load becomes a no-op
Common mistake: Deleting 'the duplicate rows' by hand without an identity key — you miss some, corrupt AR further, and it recurs next week.
Principal follow-up: What exact fields define a unique remittance, and how do you recover AR after the duplicate already posted?
003
Late membership file: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumlate membership file

Model answer

Investigate whether the feed is late or missing, and what downstream depends on it. Diagnose impact: stale eligibility, member-months, claim adjudication. Resolve by holding dependent steps (don't publish stale), processing when it lands, and restating affected aggregates if it arrives after publish. Prevent with feed-arrival SLAs, alerts, and dependency gating.

  • Investigate: late vs missing; downstream dependencies.
  • Diagnose: stale eligibility/member-months impact.
  • Resolve: gate dependents; process on arrival; restate if needed.
  • Prevent: arrival SLA + alert + dependency gating.

Healthcare angle: Retro/late membership shifts member-months; gate dependent marts rather than publishing stale eligibility.

What strong answers cover

  1. Determine: is the feed late or missing, and what downstream depends on it?
  2. Diagnose impact: stale eligibility, member-months, claim adjudication.
  3. Resolve by gating dependent steps (don't publish stale), processing on arrival, restating if it lands after publish.
  4. Prevent with feed-arrival SLAs, alerts, and dependency gating.
  5. Escalate to the source owner.

Starter pattern

if not arrived(membership) by SLA:
   hold dependents; alert source owner
else process; if after publish -> restate member_months
Common mistake: Publishing the claims/eligibility marts on stale membership rather than waiting — every per-member metric is then wrong.
Principal follow-up: When is it better to publish a degraded/partial result versus hold everything until the late feed lands?
004
Provider roster malformed: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumprovider roster malformed

Model answer

Investigate the file (schema, delimiters, trailer, counts) to locate the malformation. Diagnose impact on the provider dimension and claim attribution. Resolve by quarantining bad rows, loading valid ones, and holding/inferring for affected providers. Prevent with schema/contract validation, control-total checks, and a steward replay path.

  • Investigate: file structure/counts to locate the defect.
  • Diagnose: provider-dim and attribution impact.
  • Resolve: quarantine bad rows; load valid; infer/hold affected.
  • Prevent: schema validation + control totals + replay.

Healthcare angle: A malformed roster mis-attributes claims; quarantine-and-validate plus placeholder providers keeps facts loadable.

What strong answers cover

  1. Inspect the file (schema, delimiters, trailer, counts) to locate the malformation.
  2. Diagnose impact on the provider dimension and claim attribution.
  3. Resolve by quarantining bad rows, loading valid ones, holding/inferring for affected providers.
  4. Prevent with schema/contract validation, control-total checks, and a steward replay path.
  5. Alert the source on structural breaks.

Starter pattern

validate(roster): schema + trailer count
quarantine bad rows -> steward; load valid
placeholder provider for late/affected NPIs
Common mistake: Failing the whole roster load over a few bad rows (blocking attribution) — or loading it blindly and corrupting the provider dim.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep claims loading and attributable when the provider roster is partially malformed?
005
Fhir api timeout: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
MediumFHIR API timeout

Model answer

Investigate: which endpoint, error rate, and whether it's throttling vs outage. Diagnose scope (full vs partial failure) and data gaps. Resolve with bounded retries + backoff, resume from the last successful page/cursor, and reconcile retrieved vs expected. Prevent with resilient clients (timeouts/jitter/circuit-breaker), rate-limit handling, and incremental checkpoints.

  • Investigate: endpoint, error rate, throttle vs outage.
  • Diagnose: data-gap scope.
  • Resolve: retry/backoff, resume from cursor, reconcile.
  • Prevent: resilient client + rate-limit handling + checkpoints.

Healthcare angle: National FHIR ingestion needs jittered retries and circuit-breaking so a throttled endpoint doesn't drop data.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify the endpoint, error rate, and whether it's throttling vs a real outage.
  2. Diagnose the data-gap scope.
  3. Resolve with bounded retries + backoff, resume from the last successful page/cursor, reconcile retrieved vs expected.
  4. Prevent with resilient clients (timeouts/jitter/circuit-breaker), rate-limit handling, incremental checkpoints.
  5. Don't silently accept a partial pull.

Starter pattern

retry w/ exp backoff + jitter; honor Retry-After
resume from last cursor/_since checkpoint
reconcile: count(retrieved) vs expected
Common mistake: Treating a partial pull as complete (no reconciliation) — members silently missing from downstream analytics.
Principal follow-up: How do you guarantee completeness of a paginated national FHIR pull when the API intermittently times out?
006
Snowflake warehouse contention: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
MediumSnowflake warehouse contention

Model answer

Investigate queue times and which workloads share the warehouse. Diagnose: heavy ETL queuing BI, or undersized concurrency. Resolve by separating workloads onto dedicated warehouses and/or enabling multi-cluster scaling. Prevent with workload isolation, resource monitors, and concurrency sizing.

  • Investigate: queue time + shared workloads.
  • Diagnose: ETL vs BI contention / undersized concurrency.
  • Resolve: separate warehouses + multi-cluster.
  • Prevent: isolation + monitors + concurrency sizing.

Healthcare angle: A heavy claims load queuing BI means ETL and BI should run on separate warehouses.

What strong answers cover

  1. Look at queue times and which workloads share the warehouse.
  2. Diagnose: heavy ETL queuing BI, or undersized concurrency.
  3. Resolve by separating workloads onto dedicated warehouses and/or enabling multi-cluster scaling.
  4. Prevent with workload isolation, resource monitors, and concurrency sizing.
  5. Watch for runaway queries hogging the warehouse.

Starter pattern

-- diagnose
SELECT query_type, queued_provisioning_time FROM query_history ...
-- fix: separate ETL vs BI warehouses; enable multi-cluster
Common mistake: Sizing the warehouse UP for a concurrency problem (queuing) instead of scaling OUT (multi-cluster) — spends more, still queues.
Principal follow-up: When do you scale up vs scale out a Snowflake warehouse, and how do you decide the workload split?
007
Databricks cluster startup delay: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
MediumDatabricks cluster startup delay

Model answer

Investigate startup times and whether it's pool availability, cloud capacity, or oversized clusters. Diagnose SLA impact. Resolve with instance pools (warm capacity), right-sized job clusters, and serverless where appropriate. Prevent by pooling, pre-warming for peak windows, and sizing to need.

  • Investigate: startup time, pool/cloud capacity.
  • Diagnose: SLA impact of cold starts.
  • Resolve: instance pools/serverless; right-size.
  • Prevent: pre-warm peaks; pool capacity.

Healthcare angle: Cold-start delays on the nightly claims job are mitigated with instance pools or serverless compute.

What strong answers cover

  1. Check startup times and whether it's pool availability, cloud capacity, or oversized clusters.
  2. Diagnose the SLA impact of cold starts.
  3. Resolve with instance pools (warm capacity), right-sized job clusters, serverless where appropriate.
  4. Prevent by pooling, pre-warming for peak windows, sizing to need.
  5. Separate startup time from run time in the analysis.

Starter pattern

use instance pool (warm nodes) for the nightly job
pre-warm before peak window; right-size cluster
Common mistake: Endlessly enlarging the cluster to fix slowness that's actually cold-start latency — the run was fine, the startup was the delay.
Principal follow-up: How would you cut nightly-job latency that comes from cluster cold starts rather than processing time?
008
Spark skew month-end: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
MediumSpark skew month-end

Model answer

Investigate the Spark UI: one straggler task in a join/aggregate stage. Diagnose the hot key (often a default/UNKNOWN key amplified by month-end volume). Resolve with AQE skew handling, salting, or isolating the hot key. Prevent by enabling AQE skewJoin, sizing for peak, and adding a task-skew alert plus a month-end load test.

  • Investigate: straggler task in the heavy stage.
  • Diagnose: hot key amplified by month-end volume.
  • Resolve: AQE skewJoin / salting / isolate hot key.
  • Prevent: enable AQE, peak sizing, skew alert + load test.

Healthcare angle: Claims surge at month-end concentrates on default keys; the fix is the skew toolkit, tested at peak.

What strong answers cover

  1. Open the Spark UI: one straggler task in a join/aggregate stage.
  2. Diagnose the hot key (often a default/UNKNOWN key amplified by month-end volume).
  3. Resolve with AQE skew handling, salting, or isolating the hot key.
  4. Prevent: enable AQE skewJoin, size for peak, add a task-skew alert + month-end load test.
  5. Confirm fix with even task durations.

Starter pattern

spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true; adaptive.skewJoin.enabled=true
-- or salt hot key: key || '_' || (rand()*N)::int ; aggregate in two stages
Common mistake: Adding executors for skew (one task is the bottleneck) — more cores don't help a single hot partition; it still straggles.
Principal follow-up: Why does adding executors not fix skew, and how do salting and AQE skewJoin actually resolve it?
009
Unexpected row-count drop: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumunexpected row-count drop

Model answer

Investigate by comparing volume to a trailing baseline and per-period source vs target counts. Diagnose: missing file, failed partition, over-aggressive dedup/filter, or a bad join dropping rows. Resolve by reprocessing the affected window from source. Prevent with daily volume-baseline alerts and reconciliation gates.

  • Investigate: volume vs baseline; per-period source/target counts.
  • Diagnose: missing file / failed partition / bad dedup-filter-join.
  • Resolve: reprocess affected window from source.
  • Prevent: volume-baseline alert + reconciliation gate.

Healthcare angle: A claims volume cliff usually means a skipped file or partition; baseline-by-date monitoring catches it first.

What strong answers cover

  1. Compare volume to a trailing baseline and per-period source vs target counts.
  2. Diagnose: missing file, failed partition, over-aggressive dedup/filter, or a bad join dropping rows.
  3. Resolve by reprocessing the affected window from source.
  4. Prevent with daily volume-baseline alerts and reconciliation gates.
  5. Localize which stage lost the rows.

Starter pattern

alert if today_count < 0.7 * trailing_avg(28d)
-- per-stage counts to localize where rows vanished
Common mistake: Assuming a low day is 'just slow volume' and publishing anyway — a skipped file or bad join silently dropped claims.
Principal follow-up: How would a volume-baseline-by-date monitor distinguish a real low-volume day from a missing file?
010
Paid amount doubled: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumpaid amount doubled

Model answer

Investigate by comparing paid control totals to source and prior runs. Diagnose: a duplicate file/load, a fan-out join (duplicate current dimension row/overlapping spans), or summing across claim versions. Resolve by removing the duplication and restating. Prevent with idempotent loads, dimension-uniqueness checks, and version-aware aggregation.

  • Investigate: paid control totals vs source/prior.
  • Diagnose: duplicate load / join fan-out / version double-count.
  • Resolve: remove duplication; restate.
  • Prevent: idempotency + dim uniqueness + version-aware sums.

Healthcare angle: Doubled paid often traces to a duplicate 835 or a two-current-row provider join exploding amounts.

What strong answers cover

  1. Compare paid control totals to source and prior runs.
  2. Diagnose: a duplicate file/load, a fan-out join (duplicate current dim row / overlapping spans), or summing across claim versions.
  3. Resolve by removing the duplication and restating.
  4. Prevent with idempotent loads, dimension-uniqueness checks, and version-aware aggregation.
  5. Pinpoint join vs load vs version cause.

Starter pattern

-- detect fan-out
SELECT claim_id,COUNT(*) FROM fact GROUP BY 1 HAVING COUNT(*)>expected;
-- check dim uniqueness + version dedup
Common mistake: Fixing the symptom (halving the number) instead of the cause (duplicate load / two current dim rows / version double-count).
Principal follow-up: Walk through the three distinct causes of doubled paid and how you'd tell them apart in the data.
011
Negative paid amount spike: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumnegative paid amount spike

Model answer

Investigate the source of negatives — reversals/recoupments/voids are legitimate; a spike may be a correction batch or a sign error. Diagnose whether expected (adjustment cycle) or a defect. Resolve by confirming reversals are correctly applied and netted. Prevent with checks distinguishing legitimate reversals from anomalies and alerting on magnitude.

  • Investigate: source of negatives (reversals/voids vs defect).
  • Diagnose: expected adjustment batch vs sign error.
  • Resolve: confirm reversals netted correctly.
  • Prevent: reversal-vs-anomaly checks + magnitude alert.

Healthcare angle: Reversals and recoupments are normal; a negative spike needs confirming it's a real adjustment cycle, not a sign bug.

What strong answers cover

  1. Find the source of negatives — reversals/recoupments/voids are legitimate; a spike may be a correction batch or a sign error.
  2. Diagnose whether expected (adjustment cycle) or a defect.
  3. Resolve by confirming reversals are correctly applied and netted.
  4. Prevent with checks distinguishing legitimate reversals from anomalies and magnitude alerts.
  5. Don't 'fix' real reversals.

Starter pattern

classify negatives: reversal/void (legit) vs sign-flip bug
assert net(original+reversal) is sane; alert on magnitude
Common mistake: Filtering out all negatives as 'bad data' — you delete legitimate reversals and overstate paid.
Principal follow-up: How do you tell a legitimate reversal batch from a sign-error bug without suppressing real adjustments?
012
Schema drift in csv: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumschema drift in CSV

Model answer

Investigate the file vs expected schema (added/removed/reordered columns). Diagnose: additive (often safe) vs breaking (type/rename/reorder). Resolve by evolving for additive changes, quarantining for breaking ones, and capturing surprises (rescued-data). Prevent with schema/contract enforcement, a registry, and drift alerts.

  • Investigate: file vs expected schema.
  • Diagnose: additive vs breaking change.
  • Resolve: evolve additive; quarantine breaking; capture rescued data.
  • Prevent: contract enforcement + registry + drift alert.

Healthcare angle: A new claim column should evolve; a changed paid_amt type must be reviewed, not silently absorbed.

What strong answers cover

  1. Compare the file to the expected schema (added/removed/reordered columns).
  2. Diagnose: additive (often safe) vs breaking (type/rename/reorder).
  3. Resolve by evolving for additive changes, quarantining for breaking ones, capturing surprises (rescued data).
  4. Prevent with schema/contract enforcement, a registry, and drift alerts.
  5. Engage the producer for breaking changes.

Starter pattern

read with rescuedDataColumn; compare header to contract
additive -> evolve; breaking(type/reorder) -> quarantine + alert producer
Common mistake: Auto-merging any schema change — a silent column reorder or type change then corrupts values without any error.
Principal follow-up: Which schema changes can you safely auto-evolve, and which must hard-stop, and why?
013
Extra delimiter in source file: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumextra delimiter in source file

Model answer

Investigate the offending rows — an unescaped delimiter shifts columns, corrupting parsing. Diagnose blast radius (which rows/fields). Resolve by quarantining affected rows (don't fail the whole file), using proper quoting/escaping in the file format, and reprocessing fixed rows. Prevent with strict parsing (zip strict, column-count assertions) and supplier escaping fixes.

  • Investigate: rows where columns shifted.
  • Diagnose: affected rows/fields.
  • Resolve: quarantine + fix file format quoting; reprocess.
  • Prevent: column-count assertions + supplier escaping.

Healthcare angle: An unescaped delimiter in a claim file silently shifts fields; column-count validation catches it before corruption.

What strong answers cover

  1. Find the offending rows — an unescaped delimiter shifts columns and corrupts parsing.
  2. Diagnose the blast radius (which rows/fields).
  3. Resolve by quarantining affected rows (don't fail the whole file), fixing the file-format quoting, reprocessing.
  4. Prevent with strict parsing and column-count assertions; push escaping fixes upstream.
  5. Don't let shifted columns load silently.

Starter pattern

enforce quote char + column-count assertion per row
rows with wrong field count -> quarantine, not load
Common mistake: Loading rows where the delimiter shifted columns — member_id lands in paid_amt and every downstream number is wrong.
Principal follow-up: How does a per-row column-count assertion catch a shifted-delimiter file before it corrupts claims?
014
Missing trailer record: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediummissing trailer record

Model answer

Investigate whether the file's trailer (control count/total record) is absent or the file is truncated. Diagnose: incomplete/partial file. Resolve by rejecting the file (don't load a partial), requesting resend, and reconciling on the good file. Prevent by requiring trailer validation (count/total) before load.

  • Investigate: trailer present? file truncated?
  • Diagnose: partial/incomplete file.
  • Resolve: reject, request resend, reconcile good file.
  • Prevent: mandatory trailer validation pre-load.

Healthcare angle: A missing EDI trailer signals a truncated claim file; trailer-count validation prevents loading partial data.

What strong answers cover

  1. Check whether the file's trailer (control count/total) is absent or the file is truncated.
  2. Diagnose: incomplete/partial file.
  3. Resolve by rejecting the file (don't load a partial), requesting resend, reconciling the good file.
  4. Prevent by requiring trailer validation (count/total) before load.
  5. Treat a partial file as no file.

Starter pattern

if trailer missing OR trailer_count != row_count: REJECT file; request resend
-- never load a partial EDI file
Common mistake: Loading a truncated file because 'most of the rows are there' — you silently lose the tail and undercount claims.
Principal follow-up: Why is a missing trailer a hard-reject rather than a load-what-you-have situation?
015
Control total mismatch: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumcontrol total mismatch

Model answer

Investigate which stage broke the total by comparing per-stage counts/sums. Diagnose where rows/dollars were gained or lost (filter, join, dedup, late data). Resolve by fixing that stage and reprocessing. Prevent with stage-to-stage control totals asserted each run and tied to source trailers.

  • Investigate: per-stage counts/sums to localize.
  • Diagnose: where gained/lost (filter/join/dedup/late).
  • Resolve: fix stage + reprocess.
  • Prevent: stage-to-stage control totals every run.

Healthcare angle: Tying loaded totals to the file trailer and across stages localizes exactly where claims were lost.

What strong answers cover

  1. Compare per-stage counts/sums to localize which stage broke the total.
  2. Diagnose where rows/dollars were gained or lost (filter, join, dedup, late data).
  3. Resolve by fixing that stage and reprocessing.
  4. Prevent with stage-to-stage control totals asserted each run and tied to source trailers.
  5. Make the assertion fail the run loudly.

Starter pattern

assert source_trailer == raw == silver == gold (count & SUM(paid))
-- first failing boundary localizes the defect
Common mistake: Treating a control-total mismatch as a rounding nuisance and overriding it — a real loss/duplication is hiding behind it.
Principal follow-up: How do stage-to-stage control totals let you localize exactly where claims were lost or duplicated?
016
Cdc stopped for 3 days: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
MediumCDC stopped for 3 days

Model answer

Investigate why CDC halted (connector failure, stream stale beyond retention, permissions). Diagnose the change backlog and whether the stream lost its offset. Resolve by resuming from the last good offset or, if the stream staled past retention, re-snapshotting and reconciling. Prevent with CDC lag monitoring, stream-staleness alerts, and adequate retention.

  • Investigate: connector failure / stale stream / permissions.
  • Diagnose: backlog + offset loss risk.
  • Resolve: resume from offset or re-snapshot + reconcile.
  • Prevent: lag monitoring + staleness alert + retention.

Healthcare angle: If the membership CDC stream staled past retention, you must re-snapshot and reconcile to recover the dimension.

What strong answers cover

  1. Find why CDC halted (connector failure, stream stale past retention, permissions).
  2. Diagnose the change backlog and whether the stream lost its offset.
  3. Resolve by resuming from the last good offset, or re-snapshotting and reconciling if staled past retention.
  4. Prevent with CDC lag monitoring, stream-staleness alerts, and adequate retention.
  5. Verify current state after recovery.

Starter pattern

if stream staled past retention: full re-snapshot + reconcile to source
else resume from offset; apply backlog idempotently
Common mistake: Just 'restarting' CDC after a 3-day gap when the offset is lost — you silently skip 3 days of changes.
Principal follow-up: How do you know whether to resume from offset or re-snapshot after a multi-day CDC outage?
017
Stream checkpoint corrupted: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumstream checkpoint corrupted

Model answer

Investigate the failure — corruption vs incompatible code/schema change. Diagnose whether the checkpoint is recoverable. Resolve by starting a fresh checkpoint and replaying from a known-safe source offset into the idempotent sink. Prevent with checkpoint backups conceptually, controlled schema/code change process, and idempotent sinks.

  • Investigate: corruption vs incompatible change.
  • Diagnose: recoverability.
  • Resolve: new checkpoint + replay from safe offset into idempotent sink.
  • Prevent: controlled change process + idempotent sink.

Healthcare angle: Recovering a claims stream after checkpoint loss requires replaying source offsets into an idempotent sink to avoid duplicates.
