What strong answers cover

  1. Determine corruption vs an incompatible code/schema change.
  2. Diagnose whether the checkpoint is recoverable.
  3. Resolve by starting a fresh checkpoint and replaying from a known-safe source offset into the idempotent sink.
  4. Prevent with a controlled schema/code change process and idempotent sinks.
  5. Rely on sink idempotency to avoid duplicates.

Starter pattern

new checkpoint dir; replay from safe source offset
sink MERGE on business key -> replay is safe (no dupes)
Common mistake: Replaying into a non-idempotent (append) sink after resetting the checkpoint — you duplicate everything you reprocess.
Principal follow-up: Why is an idempotent sink the precondition for safely recovering from checkpoint loss?
018
Task dependency skipped: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumtask dependency skipped

Model answer

Investigate the DAG run — was a dependency mis-declared or a condition skipped a needed task? Diagnose downstream impact (stale/missing data). Resolve by running the skipped task and any dependents, then reconciling. Prevent by making dependencies explicit, avoiding fragile conditions, and alerting on skipped critical tasks.

  • Investigate: DAG run + dependency/condition logic.
  • Diagnose: stale/missing downstream data.
  • Resolve: run skipped task + dependents; reconcile.
  • Prevent: explicit deps + alert on skipped critical tasks.

Healthcare angle: If the reconciliation task was skipped, the claims mart may have published unverified — run it and re-validate.

What strong answers cover

  1. Inspect the DAG run — was a dependency mis-declared or a condition skipped a needed task?
  2. Diagnose the downstream impact (stale/missing data).
  3. Resolve by running the skipped task and any dependents, then reconciling.
  4. Prevent by making dependencies explicit, avoiding fragile conditions, alerting on skipped critical tasks.
  5. Re-validate the published output.

Starter pattern

make deps explicit (AFTER); alert if a critical task is skipped
re-run skipped task + dependents; reconcile
Common mistake: Assuming green = correct when the reconciliation task was conditionally skipped — the mart published unverified.
Principal follow-up: How do you ensure a skipped reconciliation/quality task blocks publication rather than passing silently?
019
Partial batch failure: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumpartial batch failure

Model answer

Investigate which records/partitions succeeded vs failed. Diagnose whether the target is in a partial state. Resolve idempotently — reprocess only the failed portion (partition overwrite/keyed MERGE), not the whole batch. Prevent with idempotent, resumable design and per-partition status tracking.

  • Investigate: succeeded vs failed records/partitions.
  • Diagnose: partial-state risk.
  • Resolve: idempotently reprocess only the failed portion.
  • Prevent: idempotent/resumable + per-partition status.

Healthcare angle: A half-loaded claims batch is recovered by reprocessing only the failed partitions, safely because writes are idempotent.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify which records/partitions succeeded vs failed.
  2. Diagnose whether the target is in a partial state.
  3. Resolve idempotently — reprocess only the failed portion (partition overwrite / keyed MERGE), not the whole batch.
  4. Prevent with idempotent, resumable design and per-partition status tracking.
  5. Avoid double-loading the succeeded portion.

Starter pattern

track per-partition status; reprocess only failed partitions
INSERT OVERWRITE partition / MERGE on key (idempotent)
Common mistake: Re-running the entire batch after a partial failure on a non-idempotent sink — the succeeded half loads twice.
Principal follow-up: How does per-partition status tracking plus idempotent writes make partial-failure recovery safe?
020
Bad deployment rollback: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumbad deployment rollback

Model answer

Investigate what the deployment changed and the symptom. Diagnose whether to roll back code, data, or both. Resolve by reverting to the prior version (code rollback + data restore/rebuild via Time Travel), then verify. Prevent with blue-green/canary releases, automated rollback, and post-deploy verification.

  • Investigate: what changed + symptom.
  • Diagnose: code vs data rollback needed.
  • Resolve: revert code + restore/rebuild data; verify.
  • Prevent: blue-green/canary + automated rollback + post-deploy check.

Healthcare angle: A bad claims-logic deploy is rolled back and the affected partition restored via Time Travel, then reconciled.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify what the deployment changed and the symptom.
  2. Diagnose whether to roll back code, data, or both.
  3. Resolve by reverting to the prior version (code rollback + data restore/rebuild via Time Travel), then verify.
  4. Prevent with blue-green/canary releases, automated rollback, and post-deploy verification.
  5. Reconcile after rollback.

Starter pattern

git revert + redeploy prior bundle
RESTORE / rebuild affected partition via Time Travel; reconcile
Common mistake: Rolling back code but leaving the corrupted data it wrote — the pipeline is fixed but the mart is still wrong.
Principal follow-up: When a bad deploy has already written data, how do you roll back both code and data safely?
021
Power bi dashboard wrong: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
MediumPower BI dashboard wrong

Model answer

Investigate whether it's the dashboard (definition/filter/refresh) or the underlying data. Diagnose by tracing the metric through lineage to source and reconciling. Resolve by fixing the actual defect (definition, stale refresh, or data). Prevent with a semantic layer (single metric definition), freshness indicators, and reconciliation.

  • Investigate: dashboard vs underlying data.
  • Diagnose: trace metric via lineage; reconcile.
  • Resolve: fix definition/refresh/data.
  • Prevent: semantic layer + freshness indicator + reconciliation.

Healthcare angle: A 'wrong' PMPM dashboard is usually a definition or stale-refresh issue traced via lineage to the source mart.

What strong answers cover

  1. Determine whether it's the dashboard (definition/filter/refresh) or the underlying data.
  2. Diagnose by tracing the metric through lineage to source and reconciling.
  3. Resolve by fixing the actual defect (definition, stale refresh, or data).
  4. Prevent with a semantic layer (single metric definition), freshness indicators, reconciliation.
  5. Don't patch the dashboard if the data is wrong.

Starter pattern

trace metric via lineage -> mart -> source; reconcile
check refresh time + measure definition vs semantic layer
Common mistake: 'Fixing' the dashboard by overriding a number in the report when the underlying mart is actually wrong.
Principal follow-up: How do you tell a dashboard-definition bug from an upstream data bug, and why does it matter?
022
Actuary disputes trend numbers: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumactuary disputes trend numbers

Model answer

Investigate the specific metric, period, and definition in question. Diagnose the gap: incompleteness (IBNR/late claims), definition difference, restatement, or a real data error. Resolve by reconciling to source, aligning definitions, and applying IBNR where recent periods are incomplete. Prevent with governed definitions, completeness indicators, and a semantic layer.

  • Investigate: which metric/period/definition.
  • Diagnose: incompleteness vs definition vs restatement vs error.
  • Resolve: reconcile, align definitions, apply IBNR.
  • Prevent: governed definitions + completeness flags + semantic layer.

Healthcare angle: Trend disputes usually trace to late-claim incompleteness or definition differences; restatement and clear definitions resolve them.

What strong answers cover

  1. Pin down the specific metric, period, and definition in question.
  2. Diagnose the gap: incompleteness (IBNR/late claims), definition difference, restatement, or a real data error.
  3. Resolve by reconciling to source, aligning definitions, applying IBNR where recent periods are incomplete.
  4. Prevent with governed definitions, completeness indicators, and a semantic layer.
  5. Approach it as partnership, not defense.

Starter pattern

reconcile disputed PMPM to source; check IBNR/completeness
align on one governed definition (semantic layer)
Common mistake: Getting defensive and 'proving the pipeline is right' when the real cause is recent-month incompleteness or a definition gap.
Principal follow-up: What are the top three reasons an actuary's trend differs from your dashboard, and how do you resolve each?
023
Downstream mart stale: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumdownstream mart stale

Model answer

Investigate the mart's last refresh and its upstream dependency status. Diagnose: failed/late upstream load or a broken refresh job. Resolve by completing the upstream and refreshing, then verify freshness. Prevent with freshness monitoring/alerts, dependency gating, and SLA tracking.

  • Investigate: mart refresh + upstream status.
  • Diagnose: failed/late upstream or broken refresh.
  • Resolve: complete upstream + refresh; verify.
  • Prevent: freshness alerts + dependency gating + SLA.

Healthcare angle: A stale claims mart usually means the upstream load failed/lagged; freshness alerts surface it before consumers notice.

What strong answers cover

  1. Check the mart's last refresh and its upstream dependency status.
  2. Diagnose: failed/late upstream load or a broken refresh job.
  3. Resolve by completing the upstream and refreshing, then verify freshness.
  4. Prevent with freshness monitoring/alerts, dependency gating, and SLA tracking.
  5. Surface staleness before consumers do.

Starter pattern

alert if mart.max_load_ts < now - sla
gate refresh on upstream success; show freshness on dashboard
Common mistake: No freshness monitoring — consumers discover the mart is days old before you do, eroding trust.
Principal follow-up: How would you surface mart staleness to both engineers and consumers before it's noticed downstream?
024
Quarantine table overflow: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumquarantine table overflow

Model answer

Investigate why quarantine volume spiked — an upstream schema/quality change is rejecting many records. Diagnose the dominant reason via the quarantine reason codes. Resolve by fixing the root cause and replaying held records. Prevent with quarantine-volume alerts (a leading quality signal) and upstream contract enforcement.

  • Investigate: spike cause via quarantine reasons.
  • Diagnose: upstream schema/quality change.
  • Resolve: fix root cause + replay held records.
  • Prevent: quarantine-volume alert + contract enforcement.

Healthcare angle: A quarantine spike on claims usually means an upstream feed changed; the reason codes point to the fix.

What strong answers cover

  1. Find why quarantine volume spiked — an upstream schema/quality change is rejecting many records.
  2. Diagnose the dominant reason via the quarantine reason codes.
  3. Resolve by fixing the root cause and replaying held records.
  4. Prevent with quarantine-volume alerts (a leading quality signal) and upstream contract enforcement.
  5. Treat the spike as a symptom, not the disease.

Starter pattern

GROUP BY quarantine_reason ORDER BY count DESC  -- dominant cause
fix upstream/mapping -> replay held records idempotently
Common mistake: Clearing the quarantine table to 'reduce the backlog' without fixing the upstream cause — you discard real records and it refills.
Principal follow-up: Why is quarantine volume a leading indicator, and how would you alert and triage on it?
025
File replay created duplicates: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Mediumfile replay created duplicates

Model answer

Investigate how the replay loaded — likely a non-idempotent append. Diagnose the duplicate scope by natural grain. Resolve by deduplicating to the latest version and reconciling totals. Prevent by replacing append with keyed MERGE/partition overwrite and tracking processed files so replays are no-ops.

  • Investigate: append vs idempotent load on replay.
  • Diagnose: duplicate scope by grain.
  • Resolve: dedup to latest + reconcile.
  • Prevent: keyed MERGE/overwrite + processed-file tracking.

Healthcare angle: Replaying a claim file with append duplicates rows; idempotent loads make replays safe.

What strong answers cover

  1. Determine how the replay loaded — likely a non-idempotent append.
  2. Diagnose the duplicate scope by natural grain.
  3. Resolve by deduplicating to the latest version and reconciling totals.
  4. Prevent by replacing append with keyed MERGE/partition overwrite and tracking processed files.
  5. Make replays no-ops by design.

Starter pattern

track processed_files(file_hash); skip if seen
load via MERGE on grain -> replay is idempotent
Common mistake: Using append for file loads so any replay duplicates rows — then cleaning up by hand each time instead of fixing the load.
Principal follow-up: How do processed-file tracking and keyed writes together make file replays safe?
026
Source system sent correction: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedsource system sent correction

Model answer

Investigate which records were corrected and the correction mechanism (replacement/adjustment/CDC update). Diagnose downstream impact. Resolve by applying corrections as versions (keep latest/net), reopening affected aggregates, and restating. Prevent with version-aware processing and restatement-capable aggregates.

  • Investigate: corrected records + mechanism.
  • Diagnose: downstream impact.
  • Resolve: apply as versions; reopen aggregates; restate.
  • Prevent: version-aware processing + restatable aggregates.

Healthcare angle: A source correction to old claims must replace the prior version and restate affected periods, not append.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify which records were corrected and the correction mechanism (replacement/adjustment/CDC update).
  2. Diagnose the downstream impact.
  3. Resolve by applying corrections as versions (keep latest/net), reopening affected aggregates, restating.
  4. Prevent with version-aware processing and restatement-capable aggregates.
  5. Communicate restatements to consumers.

Starter pattern

apply correction as new version (supersede prior)
reopen affected period aggregates; restate; notify consumers
Common mistake: Appending the correction as a new claim instead of superseding the old one — both versions now count.
Principal follow-up: How do you make aggregates restatement-capable so a correction to an old claim reopens only its period?
027
Watermark advanced too far: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedwatermark advanced too far

Model answer

Investigate why the watermark moved past unprocessed data (clock skew, processing-time vs event-time, a bad manual update). Diagnose the skipped window. Resolve by resetting the watermark and reprocessing the skipped range idempotently. Prevent by watermarking on event time, persisting it transactionally, and guarding manual changes.

  • Investigate: why watermark over-advanced.
  • Diagnose: skipped data window.
  • Resolve: reset watermark + reprocess skipped range.
  • Prevent: event-time watermark + transactional persistence.

Healthcare angle: An over-advanced watermark silently skips claims; reprocessing the skipped window (idempotently) recovers them.

What strong answers cover

  1. Find why the watermark moved past unprocessed data (clock skew, processing-time vs event-time, a bad manual update).
  2. Diagnose the skipped window.
  3. Resolve by resetting the watermark and reprocessing the skipped range idempotently.
  4. Prevent by watermarking on event time, persisting it transactionally, guarding manual changes.
  5. Verify nothing in the window is still missing.

Starter pattern

reset watermark to last-known-good event time
reprocess [skipped_from, skipped_to] idempotently
Common mistake: Watermarking on processing time (or wall clock) so a late/slow batch advances the watermark past unprocessed claims — silently skipped.
Principal follow-up: Why must watermarks track event time, not processing time, and how do you recover a skipped window?
028
Idempotency failed: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedidempotency failed

Model answer

Investigate where non-idempotent behavior crept in — a blind append, non-deterministic ordering, or untracked replay. Diagnose the duplicate/inconsistent output. Resolve by deduplicating and restating. Prevent by enforcing keyed MERGE/overwrite, deterministic tie-breakers, processed-input tracking, and a rerun test.

  • Investigate: source of non-idempotency.
  • Diagnose: duplicate/inconsistent output.
  • Resolve: dedup + restate.
  • Prevent: keyed writes + deterministic order + rerun test.

Healthcare angle: An idempotency failure on claims means a rerun duplicated rows; the fix is keyed writes plus a double-run test.

What strong answers cover

  1. Find where non-idempotent behavior crept in — a blind append, non-deterministic ordering, or an untracked replay.
  2. Diagnose the duplicate/inconsistent output.
  3. Resolve by deduplicating and restating.
  4. Prevent by enforcing keyed MERGE/overwrite, deterministic tie-breakers, processed-input tracking, and a rerun test.
  5. Add a double-run regression test.

Starter pattern

replace append -> MERGE on key / INSERT OVERWRITE
add deterministic tie-breaker; test: run twice == run once
Common mistake: Assuming a job is idempotent without testing it — the first rerun in an incident duplicates claims or payments.
Principal follow-up: What's your 'run it twice' test, and what makes a transform truly idempotent versus accidentally so?
029
Merge locked warehouse: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
AdvancedMERGE locked warehouse

Model answer

Investigate the long-running MERGE and what it blocked. Diagnose: an unconstrained MERGE rewriting too many files/holding locks, or contention. Resolve by scoping the MERGE (target pruning), shrinking the source, and isolating heavy writes from reads. Prevent with constrained MERGEs, clustering on the merge key, and workload isolation.

  • Investigate: long MERGE + what it blocked.
  • Diagnose: unconstrained rewrite / contention.
  • Resolve: scope MERGE + shrink source + isolate workloads.
  • Prevent: constrained MERGE + clustering + isolation.

Healthcare angle: An unbounded claims MERGE can lock/contend; constraining it to affected service_months and isolating reads fixes it.

What strong answers cover

  1. Inspect the long-running MERGE and what it blocked.
  2. Diagnose: an unconstrained MERGE rewriting too many files/holding locks, or contention.
  3. Resolve by scoping the MERGE (target pruning), shrinking the source, isolating heavy writes from reads.
  4. Prevent with constrained MERGEs, clustering on the merge key, and workload isolation.
  5. Confirm the join is selective.

Starter pattern

MERGE ... WHERE target.service_month IN (affected)  -- prune target
cluster on merge key; isolate write vs read workloads
Common mistake: Running an unbounded MERGE over the whole fact every cycle — it rewrites everything, locks/contends, and starves readers.
Principal follow-up: How do you constrain a large claims MERGE so it only touches affected partitions?
030
Small files explosion: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedsmall files explosion

Model answer

Investigate file counts/sizes on the affected table (often from streaming or over-partitioning). Diagnose the read/metadata overhead impact. Resolve with OPTIMIZE/compaction and right-sized writes. Prevent with optimizeWrite/autoCompact, fewer partition columns, and scheduled OPTIMIZE.

  • Investigate: file counts/sizes.
  • Diagnose: read/metadata overhead.
  • Resolve: OPTIMIZE/compact + right-size writes.
  • Prevent: optimizeWrite/autoCompact + scheduled OPTIMIZE.

Healthcare angle: Auto Loader micro-batches of 837 files create small files; scheduled OPTIMIZE keeps the claims table fast.

What strong answers cover

  1. Check file counts/sizes on the affected table (often from streaming or over-partitioning).
  2. Diagnose the read/metadata overhead impact.
  3. Resolve with OPTIMIZE/compaction and right-sized writes.
  4. Prevent with optimizeWrite/autoCompact, fewer partition columns, scheduled OPTIMIZE.
  5. Reduce partition granularity if needed.

Starter pattern

OPTIMIZE claims [ZORDER BY (member_id)];
spark.databricks.delta.optimizeWrite=true; autoCompact=true
Common mistake: Partitioning by a high-cardinality column (e.g. member_id) creating millions of tiny files and crushing query/metadata performance.
Principal follow-up: What causes small-file explosions in a streaming claims pipeline, and how do you prevent and remediate them?
031
Driver oom: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advanceddriver OOM

Model answer

Investigate what ran on the driver — usually collect()/toPandas(), an oversized broadcast, or excessive partitions. Diagnose the memory driver. Resolve by removing driver-side collection and bounding broadcast size. Prevent by banning collect on big data, writing results to storage, and right-sizing only when truly needed.

  • Investigate: collect/toPandas/broadcast/partition metadata.
  • Diagnose: what filled driver memory.
  • Resolve: remove collection; bound broadcast.
  • Prevent: no big-data collect; write to storage.

Healthcare angle: A 'quick' collect() of a month of claims OOMs the driver; sample or write out instead.

What strong answers cover

  1. Find what ran on the driver — usually collect()/toPandas(), an oversized broadcast, or excessive partitions.
  2. Diagnose the memory driver.
  3. Resolve by removing driver-side collection and bounding broadcast size.
  4. Prevent by banning collect on big data, writing results to storage, sizing only when truly needed.
  5. Distinguish driver OOM from executor OOM.

Starter pattern

remove .collect()/.toPandas() on big data -> write to storage
cap broadcast threshold; reduce shuffle partitions if huge
Common mistake: Calling collect()/toPandas() on a month of claims to 'peek' — it pulls everything to the driver and OOMs.
Principal follow-up: What are the usual driver-OOM causes, and why is more executor memory the wrong fix?
032
Executor oom: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedexecutor OOM

Model answer

Investigate task data sizes and skew in the Spark UI. Diagnose: skew, oversized partitions, over-caching, or large UDF objects. Resolve by increasing parallelism (more partitions), fixing skew (salt/AQE), and reducing cache footprint. Prevent with peak sizing, skew handling, and cache discipline.

  • Investigate: task sizes + skew.
  • Diagnose: skew / big partitions / over-cache.
  • Resolve: more partitions + skew fix + less cache.
  • Prevent: peak sizing + skew handling + cache discipline.

Healthcare angle: Month-end skew piling rows on one executor is a frequent claims OOM; salting or AQE skew-join resolves it.

What strong answers cover

  1. Inspect task data sizes and skew in the Spark UI.
  2. Diagnose: skew, oversized partitions, over-caching, or large UDF objects.
  3. Resolve by increasing parallelism (more partitions), fixing skew (salt/AQE), reducing cache footprint.
  4. Prevent with peak sizing, skew handling, cache discipline.
  5. Check spill before raising memory.

Starter pattern

increase shuffle partitions; AQE/salt for skew
unpersist unused caches; avoid huge per-task objects
Common mistake: Throwing memory at an executor OOM that's actually skew — the hot task still exceeds memory no matter the size.
Principal follow-up: How do you tell skew-driven executor OOM from genuinely undersized executors?
033
Remote spill spike: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedremote spill spike

Model answer

Investigate the spilling operator and its input size in the profile. Diagnose: too much data per task or skew exceeding memory. Resolve by reducing input (filter/pre-aggregate), raising parallelism, and fixing skew. Prevent by pre-aggregating heavy steps and sizing/partitioning for the data.

  • Investigate: spilling operator + input size.
  • Diagnose: data volume per task / skew.
  • Resolve: filter/pre-aggregate + more partitions + fix skew.
  • Prevent: pre-aggregate + right partitioning.

Healthcare angle: A full-year claims aggregation spills; pre-aggregating to member-month removes the pressure.

What strong answers cover

  1. Find the spilling operator and its input size in the profile.
  2. Diagnose: too much data per task or skew exceeding memory.
  3. Resolve by reducing input (filter/pre-aggregate), raising parallelism, fixing skew.
  4. Prevent by pre-aggregating heavy steps and sizing/partitioning for the data.
  5. Spill = pressure signal, not always fatal.

Starter pattern

pre-aggregate before the heavy join; raise parallelism
filter early; fix skew -> less data per task -> less spill
Common mistake: Ignoring heavy remote spill as 'just slow' — it's the warning sign of under-parallelism or skew that will eventually OOM.
Principal follow-up: What does heavy spill tell you, and what's your ordered checklist to reduce it?
034
Query plan changed: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedquery plan changed

Model answer

Investigate by comparing the new plan to the prior good one. Diagnose: stale stats, volume/skew change, a predicate/cast change defeating pruning, or a join-strategy flip. Resolve by refreshing stats and restoring sargable predicates/pruning. Prevent with stats maintenance, sargable queries, and a runtime regression alert.

  • Investigate: new vs prior plan.
  • Diagnose: stale stats / volume / lost pruning / join flip.
  • Resolve: refresh stats + restore pruning.
  • Prevent: stats maintenance + sargable queries + alert.

Healthcare angle: A claims query regressing from minutes to far longer usually lost service_month pruning or has stale stats.

What strong answers cover

  1. Compare the new plan to the prior good one.
  2. Diagnose: stale stats, volume/skew change, a predicate/cast change defeating pruning, or a join-strategy flip.
  3. Resolve by refreshing stats and restoring sargable predicates/pruning.
  4. Prevent with stats maintenance, sargable queries, and a runtime regression alert.
  5. Confirm pruning/join strategy in the plan.

Starter pattern

ANALYZE/refresh stats; check partitions scanned vs total
restore sargable filter; verify join strategy
Common mistake: Accepting a sudden 10x slowdown as 'more data' without checking the plan — it's usually lost pruning or stale stats.
Principal follow-up: A claims query went from 2 to 40 minutes overnight — how do you diagnose it from the plan?
035
Partition pruning lost: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedpartition pruning lost

Model answer

Investigate partitions scanned vs total. Diagnose: a function/cast on the partition column, a type mismatch, or a join-derived filter not pushing down. Resolve by filtering the raw partition column with literal ranges and enabling dynamic partition pruning. Prevent with query standards and a partitions-scanned check.

  • Investigate: partitions scanned vs total.
  • Diagnose: function/cast/type mismatch / no DPP.
  • Resolve: sargable partition filter + enable DPP.
  • Prevent: query standards + scan check.

Healthcare angle: Filtering claims on a derived period instead of service_month reads the whole fact; restore the direct filter.

What strong answers cover

  1. Look at partitions scanned vs total.
  2. Diagnose: a function/cast on the partition column, a type mismatch, or a join-derived filter not pushing down.
  3. Resolve by filtering the raw partition column with literal ranges and enabling dynamic partition pruning.
  4. Prevent with query standards and a partitions-scanned check.
  5. Keep predicates sargable.

Starter pattern

-- bad: WHERE to_char(service_month)='2025-01'
-- good: WHERE service_month BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-31'
Common mistake: Wrapping the partition column in a function/cast (or type mismatch) so the engine scans the whole fact instead of one month.
Principal follow-up: Why does a function on the partition column defeat pruning, and how do you write the filter so it prunes?
036
Snowflake stream stale: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
AdvancedSnowflake stream stale

Model answer

Investigate the stream's last consumed offset and the source retention. Diagnose whether it staled past retention (offset lost). Resolve by re-initializing the stream and reconciling/backfilling the missed changes. Prevent with reliable consuming tasks, staleness monitoring, and adequate retention.

  • Investigate: stream offset + source retention.
  • Diagnose: staled past retention?
  • Resolve: re-init stream + reconcile/backfill.
  • Prevent: reliable task + staleness alert + retention.

Healthcare angle: If the claims stream's consuming task paused too long, the stream stales and you must re-init and backfill.

What strong answers cover

  1. Check the stream's last consumed offset and the source retention.
  2. Diagnose whether it staled past retention (offset lost).
  3. Resolve by re-initializing the stream and reconciling/backfilling the missed changes.
  4. Prevent with reliable consuming tasks, staleness monitoring, and adequate retention.
  5. Don't assume the stream caught everything.

Starter pattern

SELECT SYSTEM$STREAM_HAS_DATA('s'); check staleness vs retention
if staled: recreate stream + backfill/reconcile
Common mistake: Re-enabling a stale stream and assuming it resumes cleanly — it lost its offset and silently missed changes.
Principal follow-up: How does a Snowflake stream go stale, and how do you recover missed changes when it does?
037
Dynamic table lag: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advanceddynamic table lag

Model answer

Investigate the dynamic table's refresh history and target lag vs actual. Diagnose: undersized warehouse, expensive query, or dependency lag. Resolve by tuning the query/warehouse or adjusting lag expectations. Prevent with refresh monitoring and realistic lag/cost balance.

  • Investigate: refresh history + lag vs target.
  • Diagnose: warehouse/query/dependency lag.
  • Resolve: tune query/warehouse or lag.
  • Prevent: refresh monitoring + realistic lag.

Healthcare angle: A lagging claims dynamic table may need a bigger warehouse or a cheaper incremental query to meet target lag.

What strong answers cover

  1. Check the dynamic table's refresh history and target lag vs actual.
  2. Diagnose: undersized warehouse, expensive query, or dependency lag.
  3. Resolve by tuning the query/warehouse or adjusting lag expectations.
  4. Prevent with refresh monitoring and a realistic lag/cost balance.
  5. Look at the whole dependency chain.

Starter pattern

check refresh_history: actual_lag vs target_lag
tune query / bigger warehouse / relax target_lag
Common mistake: Setting an aggressive target lag the warehouse can't sustain, then being surprised the dynamic table is always behind.
Principal follow-up: How do you balance a dynamic table's target lag against warehouse cost and query complexity?
038
Dlt expectation failed: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
AdvancedDLT expectation failed

Model answer

Investigate which expectation failed and on how many rows. Diagnose: an upstream data/quality change. Resolve per the rule's action (rows dropped to quarantine, or pipeline stopped) and fix the root cause. Prevent with calibrated expectations, quarantine review, and upstream contract enforcement.

  • Investigate: which expectation + row count.
  • Diagnose: upstream data/quality change.
  • Resolve: per rule action + fix root cause.
  • Prevent: calibrated expectations + contract enforcement.

Healthcare angle: A failed 'valid CARC' expectation flags an upstream code-set change; the reason points to the fix.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify which expectation failed and on how many rows.
  2. Diagnose: an upstream data/quality change.
  3. Resolve per the rule's action (rows dropped to quarantine, or pipeline stopped) and fix the root cause.
  4. Prevent with calibrated expectations, quarantine review, upstream contract enforcement.
  5. Tune expectations to be meaningful, not noisy.

Starter pattern

@dlt.expect_or_drop('valid_carc', 'carc in (...)')
-- review dropped rows; fix upstream; adjust threshold
Common mistake: Setting expectations so strict they drop legitimate rows (or so loose they catch nothing) — both corrupt or fail to protect.
Principal follow-up: How do you calibrate DLT expectations so they catch real issues without dropping valid claims?
039
Auto loader duplicate: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedauto loader duplicate

Model answer

Investigate whether files were reprocessed (schema-location/checkpoint reset) or the source re-delivered files. Diagnose duplicate scope. Resolve by deduplicating on business key and reconciling. Prevent with a stable checkpoint/schema location and idempotent downstream writes.

  • Investigate: checkpoint reset vs source re-delivery.
  • Diagnose: duplicate scope.
  • Resolve: dedup on key + reconcile.
  • Prevent: stable checkpoint + idempotent writes.

Healthcare angle: Auto Loader duplicates usually mean a checkpoint reset; idempotent downstream writes keep duplicates harmless.

What strong answers cover

  1. Determine whether files were reprocessed (schema-location/checkpoint reset) or the source re-delivered files.
  2. Diagnose the duplicate scope.
  3. Resolve by deduplicating on business key and reconciling.
  4. Prevent with a stable checkpoint/schema location and idempotent downstream writes.
  5. Make downstream tolerate at-least-once.

Starter pattern

keep stable checkpointLocation; downstream MERGE on key
-- Auto Loader is at-least-once -> idempotent sink
Common mistake: Resetting the Auto Loader checkpoint location (reprocessing all files) into an append sink — everything duplicates.
Principal follow-up: Why is Auto Loader at-least-once, and how do you keep duplicates harmless downstream?
040
Vacuum removed needed files: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedvacuum removed needed files

Model answer

Investigate the VACUUM retention setting and what broke (time travel, an active reader/stream). Diagnose: retention set below recovery/streaming needs. Resolve by recovering from another copy/backup if possible and restoring service. Prevent by setting retention to cover time-travel and streaming, and guarding VACUUM changes.

  • Investigate: retention setting + what broke.
  • Diagnose: retention below recovery/streaming needs.
  • Resolve: recover from backup/copy; restore service.
  • Prevent: retention covers recovery/streaming + guard changes.

Healthcare angle: Aggressive VACUUM can destroy time-travel recovery or break a stream; retention must cover real recovery windows.

What strong answers cover

  1. Check the VACUUM retention setting and what broke (time travel, an active reader/stream).
  2. Diagnose: retention set below recovery/streaming needs.
  3. Resolve by recovering from another copy/backup if possible and restoring service.
  4. Prevent by setting retention to cover time-travel and streaming, and guarding VACUUM changes.
  5. Never VACUUM below your recovery window.

Starter pattern

VACUUM retention >= max(time_travel_need, streaming_need)
-- guard: don't run VACUUM RETAIN 0 HOURS in prod
Common mistake: Running VACUUM with low/zero retention to save storage — it destroys time-travel recovery and can break active streams.
Principal follow-up: What retention must VACUUM respect to avoid breaking time travel and streaming reads?
041
Time travel recovery: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
AdvancedTime Travel recovery

Model answer

Investigate the last-good version/timestamp before the bad change. Diagnose the affected scope. Resolve by querying/restoring to that version (RESTORE/clone) and reconciling, all within retention. Prevent by setting retention to your recovery needs and preferring atomic publish to avoid needing recovery.

  • Investigate: last-good version/timestamp.
  • Diagnose: affected scope.
  • Resolve: RESTORE/clone to good version + reconcile.
  • Prevent: retention sized to recovery + atomic publish.

Healthcare angle: Recovering corrupted claims is a RESTORE to the last-good version — provided retention hasn't purged it.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify the last-good version/timestamp before the bad change.
  2. Diagnose the affected scope.
  3. Resolve by querying/restoring to that version (RESTORE/clone) and reconciling, within retention.
  4. Prevent by setting retention to recovery needs and preferring atomic publish.
  5. Reconcile after restore.

Starter pattern

RESTORE TABLE claims TO TIMESTAMP AS OF '<good_ts>';
-- or CLONE at version; then reconcile to source
Common mistake: Discovering corruption after retention has expired — you set retention too short to self-recover.
Principal follow-up: How do you size time-travel retention against real recovery scenarios for a critical claims table?
042
Zero-copy clone rollback: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedzero-copy clone rollback

Model answer

Investigate whether a clone-based deployment needs reverting. Diagnose the current vs desired state. Resolve by swapping back to the validated clone/prior table atomically. Prevent with blue-green clone deployments and a kept prior-version clone for instant rollback.

  • Investigate: clone-based deploy state.
  • Diagnose: current vs desired.
  • Resolve: swap back to validated clone.
  • Prevent: blue-green clones + kept prior version.

Healthcare angle: A bad claims-mart deploy rolls back instantly by swapping back to the prior cloned version.

What strong answers cover

  1. Determine whether a clone-based deployment needs reverting.
  2. Diagnose current vs desired state.
  3. Resolve by swapping back to the validated clone/prior table atomically.
  4. Prevent with blue-green clone deployments and a kept prior-version clone for instant rollback.
  5. Verify after swap-back.

Starter pattern

ALTER TABLE mart SWAP WITH mart_prev;  -- instant rollback
-- keep validated prior clone for fast revert
Common mistake: Deploying with no kept prior version, so rollback means a slow rebuild instead of an instant swap-back.
Principal follow-up: How do zero-copy clones plus SWAP give you instant, low-risk deploy and rollback?
043
Access issue for phi: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedaccess issue for PHI

Model answer

Investigate whether access is over-broad (exposure) or insufficient (blocking work) and treat over-exposure as a potential incident. Diagnose the policy/role gap. Resolve by correcting grants/masking/row policies to minimum-necessary and, if exposure occurred, follow breach/audit procedures. Prevent with access reviews, least-privilege design, and audit monitoring.

  • Investigate: over-broad (exposure) vs insufficient access.
  • Diagnose: policy/role gap.
  • Resolve: correct to minimum-necessary; follow breach procedure if exposed.
  • Prevent: access reviews + least privilege + audit monitoring.

Healthcare angle: Unintended PHI exposure is a reportable event; correct the policy and follow breach/audit procedures.

What strong answers cover

  1. Determine whether access is over-broad (exposure) or insufficient (blocking work); treat over-exposure as a potential incident.
  2. Diagnose the policy/role gap.
  3. Resolve by correcting grants/masking/row policies to minimum-necessary; if exposure occurred, follow breach/audit procedures.
  4. Prevent with access reviews, least-privilege design, audit monitoring.
  5. Contain exposure first.

Starter pattern

if over-broad PHI access: revoke immediately + audit who accessed
fix to minimum-necessary; follow breach procedure if exposed
Common mistake: Treating accidental PHI over-exposure as a routine permissions tweak instead of a potential reportable breach.
Principal follow-up: If a role had unintended access to member PHI, what are your first three actions?
044
Masking policy broke report: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedmasking policy broke report

Model answer

Investigate which report broke and which masking/role change caused it. Diagnose: a legitimate consumer lost needed access, or a policy was too broad. Resolve by adjusting the policy/role to grant minimum-necessary access for that use. Prevent by testing policy changes against consumer roles before deploy.

  • Investigate: report + masking/role change.
  • Diagnose: legitimate access lost vs over-broad policy.
  • Resolve: adjust policy/role to minimum-necessary.
  • Prevent: test policy changes vs consumer roles.

Healthcare angle: A masking change that hides a field a sanctioned report needs is fixed by granting that role minimum-necessary access.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify which report broke and which masking/role change caused it.
  2. Diagnose: a legitimate consumer lost needed access, or a policy was too broad.
  3. Resolve by adjusting the policy/role to grant minimum-necessary access for that use.
  4. Prevent by testing policy changes against consumer roles before deploy.
  5. Don't over-correct and re-expose PHI.

Starter pattern

test masking policy against each consumer role pre-deploy
grant the report role minimum-necessary unmask
Common mistake: Fixing a broken report by over-granting (disabling masking broadly) and re-exposing PHI to everyone.
Principal follow-up: How do you test a masking-policy change against all consumer roles before it breaks a report?
045
Row-level filter bug: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedrow-level filter bug

Model answer

Investigate whether the filter over-restricts (hides needed rows) or under-restricts (leaks). Treat under-restriction as a potential exposure incident. Diagnose the policy logic/mapping error. Resolve by correcting the filter and verifying with role-based tests. Prevent with tested, centralized row policies and audits.

  • Investigate: over- vs under-restriction.
  • Diagnose: policy logic/mapping error.
  • Resolve: correct filter + role-based tests.
  • Prevent: tested centralized policies + audits.

Healthcare angle: A row-filter bug that leaks another region's members is an exposure incident; fix and audit immediately.

What strong answers cover

  1. Determine whether the filter over-restricts (hides needed rows) or under-restricts (leaks); treat under-restriction as a potential exposure.
  2. Diagnose the policy logic/mapping error.
  3. Resolve by correcting the filter and verifying with role-based tests.
  4. Prevent with tested, centralized row policies and audits.
  5. Verify with each affected role.

Starter pattern

test row policy per role: region_role sees only its region
fix mapping; audit if cross-region leakage occurred
Common mistake: A row-policy bug that leaks another region's members treated as a minor filter fix rather than an exposure incident.
Principal follow-up: How would you test a row-access policy so a mapping bug can't leak another region's members?
046
Orchestration retry storm: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedorchestration retry storm

Model answer

Investigate cascading retries hammering a dependency or each other. Diagnose: aggressive retries without backoff/jitter or circuit-breaking. Resolve by halting the storm, adding backoff+jitter, and breaking the cascade. Prevent with bounded retries, jitter, circuit breakers, and idempotency.

  • Investigate: cascading retries.
  • Diagnose: no backoff/jitter/circuit-breaker.
  • Resolve: halt + add backoff/jitter + break cascade.
  • Prevent: bounded retries + jitter + circuit breaker.

Healthcare angle: Many ingestion tasks retrying a throttled API in lockstep create a storm; jittered backoff and circuit-breaking stop it.

What strong answers cover

  1. Spot cascading retries hammering a dependency or each other.
  2. Diagnose: aggressive retries without backoff/jitter or circuit-breaking.
  3. Resolve by halting the storm, adding backoff+jitter, breaking the cascade.
  4. Prevent with bounded retries, jitter, circuit breakers, and idempotency.
  5. Stop the storm before tuning.

Starter pattern

bounded retries + exponential backoff + jitter
circuit-breaker on the failing dependency
Common mistake: Tight, synchronized retries with no jitter so all tasks hammer a throttled API in lockstep, amplifying the outage.
Principal follow-up: How do jitter and circuit breakers prevent a retry storm from taking down a shared dependency?
047
Alert fatigue: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedalert fatigue

Model answer

Investigate which alerts fire most and whether they're actionable. Diagnose: noisy/duplicate/non-actionable alerts drowning real signals. Resolve by tuning thresholds, deduplicating, and removing non-actionable alerts. Prevent by alerting on symptoms tied to action, with clear ownership and severity.

  • Investigate: noisiest/least-actionable alerts.
  • Diagnose: noise drowning real signals.
  • Resolve: tune thresholds + dedup + prune.
  • Prevent: actionable symptom-based alerts + severity/ownership.

Healthcare angle: If claims alerts fire constantly, real incidents get missed; tune to actionable, owned, severity-tiered alerts.

What strong answers cover

  1. Find which alerts fire most and whether they're actionable.
  2. Diagnose: noisy/duplicate/non-actionable alerts drowning real signals.
  3. Resolve by tuning thresholds, deduplicating, removing non-actionable alerts.
  4. Prevent by alerting on symptoms tied to action, with clear ownership and severity.
  5. Fewer, better alerts.

Starter pattern

rank alerts by volume + action-taken rate
kill non-actionable; tune thresholds; add severity/ownership
Common mistake: Adding ever more alerts until on-call ignores them all — the real incident then gets missed in the noise.
Principal follow-up: How do you redesign a noisy alerting setup so every page is actionable and owned?
048
Silent corruption: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedsilent corruption

Model answer

Investigate when good data quietly went wrong without an error — often a logic change, bad join, or unhandled edge case. Diagnose via reconciliation/control totals and lineage. Resolve by restating from source for the affected window. Prevent with DQ assertions, control totals, and reconciliation that fail loudly.

  • Investigate: when/where data silently went wrong.
  • Diagnose: via reconciliation + control totals + lineage.
  • Resolve: restate from source for affected window.
  • Prevent: loud DQ assertions + control totals + reconciliation.

Healthcare angle: Silent claims corruption (e.g. a join fan-out) is caught only by reconciliation/DQ that fails loudly — add it.

What strong answers cover

  1. Find when good data quietly went wrong without an error — often a logic change, bad join, or unhandled edge case.
  2. Diagnose via reconciliation/control totals and lineage.
  3. Resolve by restating from source for the affected window.
  4. Prevent with DQ assertions, control totals, and reconciliation that fail loudly.
  5. Make correctness observable, not assumed.

Starter pattern

reconcile gold vs source by period to find the divergence point
restate affected window from source; add loud DQ assertion
Common mistake: Trusting 'the job is green' as proof of correctness — a join fan-out or logic bug corrupts data with no error raised.
Principal follow-up: How do you detect corruption that throws no error, and why is reconciliation your main defense?
049
Cost spike overnight: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedcost spike overnight

Model answer

Investigate the cost driver via query tags / per-warehouse usage / job attribution. Diagnose the offending job/query (runaway query, small-files scan, auto-suspend misconfig, accidental full reload). Resolve by fixing/killing it and restoring guardrails. Prevent with resource monitors, cost alerts, tagging, and auto-suspend.

  • Investigate: cost driver via tags/usage/attribution.
  • Diagnose: offending job/query.
  • Resolve: fix/kill + restore guardrails.
  • Prevent: resource monitors + cost alerts + tagging.

Healthcare angle: An overnight claims cost spike is traced via query tags to one runaway job; monitors prevent recurrence.

What strong answers cover

  1. Attribute the spike via query tags, per-warehouse usage, and job attribution.
  2. Diagnose the offending job/query (runaway query, small-files scan, auto-suspend misconfig, accidental full reload).
  3. Resolve by fixing/killing it and restoring guardrails.
  4. Prevent with resource monitors, cost alerts, tagging, and auto-suspend.
  5. Make cost attributable per product.

Starter pattern

query_history/ACCOUNT_USAGE by query_tag -> find driver
fix/kill; set resource_monitor + cost alert
Common mistake: Reacting to an overnight cost spike by globally downsizing compute instead of finding the one runaway job via tags.
Principal follow-up: How do query tags and resource monitors let you find and stop a cost spike fast?
050
Warehouse auto-suspend misconfigured: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedwarehouse auto-suspend misconfigured

Model answer

Investigate the auto-suspend setting and idle time billed. Diagnose: suspend disabled or set too high, paying for idle. Resolve by setting a sensible auto-suspend (e.g. 60s) balanced against cache warmth needs. Prevent with cluster/warehouse policies enforcing auto-suspend.

  • Investigate: auto-suspend setting + idle billing.
  • Diagnose: disabled/too-high suspend.
  • Resolve: sensible auto-suspend.
  • Prevent: policy-enforced auto-suspend.

Healthcare angle: A claims warehouse left running idle overnight wastes credits; a 60s auto-suspend (policy-enforced) fixes it.

What strong answers cover

  1. Check the auto-suspend setting and idle time billed.
  2. Diagnose: suspend disabled or set too high, paying for idle.
  3. Resolve by setting a sensible auto-suspend (e.g. 60s), balanced against cache-warmth needs.
  4. Prevent with cluster/warehouse policies enforcing auto-suspend.
  5. Trade idle cost vs cold-cache latency deliberately.

Starter pattern

ALTER WAREHOUSE wh SET AUTO_SUSPEND=60 AUTO_RESUME=TRUE;
-- policy-enforce across warehouses
Common mistake: Leaving a warehouse running 24/7 'so it's always warm' — paying for hours of idle to save seconds of cold start.
Principal follow-up: How do you balance auto-suspend aggressiveness against cache warmth for a latency-sensitive workload?
051
Runbook missing: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedrunbook missing

Model answer

Investigate the incident without a runbook by reasoning from architecture and recent changes. Diagnose and resolve as usual, but capture every step. Afterward, write the runbook from what you did. Prevent recurrence by making runbooks a production-readiness requirement and reviewing them after each incident.

  • Investigate from architecture + recent changes.
  • Resolve while documenting each step.
  • Write the runbook from the actual response.
  • Prevent: runbooks as a readiness gate + post-incident review.

Healthcare angle: A claims incident with no runbook is resolved and then becomes the runbook so the next on-call isn't starting blind.

What strong answers cover

  1. Reason from architecture and recent changes when there's no runbook.
  2. Diagnose and resolve as usual, capturing every step.
  3. Afterward, write the runbook from what you did.
  4. Prevent recurrence by making runbooks a production-readiness requirement and reviewing them after incidents.
  5. Turn the response into documentation.

Starter pattern

resolve from architecture+recent-changes; log each step
-> write runbook from the actual response; add to readiness gate
Common mistake: Resolving the incident and moving on without writing the runbook — the next on-call starts from scratch on the same issue.
Principal follow-up: How do you turn a runbook-less incident into a durable runbook and a readiness requirement?
052
Incident commander role: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedincident commander role

Model answer

The incident commander coordinates the response — not necessarily the deepest expert, but the person driving triage, comms, and decisions. Investigate by establishing scope/severity, assign roles (investigator, comms, scribe), drive to resolution, and own stakeholder updates. Prevent chaos with a defined IC process and rotation.

  • IC coordinates: triage, roles, decisions, comms.
  • Assign investigator/comms/scribe; set severity.
  • Drive resolution + own stakeholder updates.
  • Prevent: defined IC process + rotation.

Healthcare angle: In a major claims outage, the IC keeps the response organized and stakeholders informed while engineers fix the data.

What strong answers cover

  1. The IC coordinates the response — triage, roles, decisions, comms — not necessarily the deepest expert.
  2. Establish scope/severity; assign investigator/comms/scribe.
  3. Drive to resolution and own stakeholder updates.
  4. Prevent chaos with a defined IC process and rotation.
  5. Keep the response organized and communicated.

Starter pattern

IC: set severity -> assign roles -> drive triage -> comms cadence
separate 'fixing' from 'coordinating'
Common mistake: The most senior engineer both fixing and coordinating — comms drop and the response becomes chaotic.
Principal follow-up: Why separate the incident commander from the hands-on fixer in a major outage?
053
Rca write-up: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
AdvancedRCA write-up

Model answer

A good RCA states impact, timeline, root cause (not just symptom — use 5 whys), what went well/poorly, and concrete, owned, dated prevention actions. It's blameless and focused on systemic fixes. Investigate to true root cause, document honestly, and track actions to closure.

  • Impact + timeline + true root cause (5 whys).
  • What went well/poorly; blameless.
  • Concrete, owned, dated prevention actions.
  • Track actions to closure.

Healthcare angle: A duplicate-835 RCA names the missing idempotency control as root cause and assigns the dedup fix with a date.

What strong answers cover

  1. State impact, timeline, true root cause (5 whys, not the symptom), and what went well/poorly.
  2. Keep it blameless and focused on systemic fixes.
  3. Define concrete, owned, dated prevention actions.
  4. Track actions to closure.
  5. Aim to make the incident class extinct.

Starter pattern

RCA = impact + timeline + root_cause(5 whys) + actions(owner,date)
track actions to done
Common mistake: Stopping the RCA at the symptom ('bad file') instead of the missing control ('no idempotency') — so it recurs.
Principal follow-up: What separates a root cause from a symptom in an RCA, and why does it determine whether the incident recurs?
054
Prevention checklist: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advancedprevention checklist

Model answer

After resolving, add layered prevention: detection (DQ/reconciliation/alerts), robustness (idempotency, validation, contracts), and process (runbook, tests, readiness review). Map each to the failure mode so the same incident can't recur silently. Track items to completion.

  • Detection: DQ + reconciliation + alerts.
  • Robustness: idempotency + validation + contracts.
  • Process: runbook + tests + readiness review.
  • Map to failure mode; track to done.

Healthcare angle: Preventing a recurring claims incident means adding the missing dup-detection, DQ gate, and alert — not just fixing the data once.

What strong answers cover

  1. Add layered prevention: detection (DQ/reconciliation/alerts), robustness (idempotency/validation/contracts), process (runbook/tests/readiness).
  2. Map each item to the failure mode.
  3. Ensure the same incident can't recur silently.
  4. Track items to completion.
  5. Prefer systemic fixes over one-time cleanup.

Starter pattern

per incident: add detect + harden + process control mapped to the failure mode
track to done
Common mistake: Fixing only the data this once and skipping the detection/robustness controls — the same incident returns.
Principal follow-up: For a recurring duplicate-load incident, what detection and robustness controls would you add?
055
Data contract violation: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Advanceddata contract violation

Model answer

Investigate which contract term broke (schema, grain, semantics, freshness) and who owns the producer. Diagnose downstream impact. Resolve by quarantining violating data and engaging the producer to fix or formally change the contract. Prevent with enforced contracts, a schema registry, and producer accountability.

  • Investigate: which contract term + producer owner.
  • Diagnose: downstream impact.
  • Resolve: quarantine + engage producer.
  • Prevent: enforced contracts + registry + accountability.

Healthcare angle: A claims feed silently changing grain violates its contract; quarantine and engage the owner rather than absorbing the break.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify which contract term broke (schema, grain, semantics, freshness) and who owns the producer.
  2. Diagnose the downstream impact.
  3. Resolve by quarantining violating data and engaging the producer to fix or formally change the contract.
  4. Prevent with enforced contracts, a schema registry, producer accountability.
  5. Don't silently absorb the break.

Starter pattern

validate at boundary; quarantine violations; route to producer
schema registry + compatibility rules
Common mistake: Silently adapting your pipeline to absorb a producer's breaking change — masking the violation and accumulating fragility.
Principal follow-up: When a producer breaks the contract, when do you adapt versus quarantine and push back?
056
Source latency: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Expertsource latency

Model answer

Investigate whether the source is late vs failed and the downstream dependency. Diagnose SLA impact. Resolve by gating dependents (don't publish stale), processing on arrival, and escalating to the source owner. Prevent with arrival SLAs, alerts, and a degraded-mode/partial-publish plan.

  • Investigate: late vs failed + dependencies.
  • Diagnose: SLA impact.
  • Resolve: gate dependents; process on arrival; escalate.
  • Prevent: arrival SLA + alert + degraded-mode plan.

Healthcare angle: A late claims feed should gate the actuarial mart, not publish stale numbers; escalate to the source owner.

What strong answers cover

  1. Determine whether the source is late vs failed and the downstream dependency.
  2. Diagnose SLA impact.
  3. Resolve by gating dependents (don't publish stale), processing on arrival, escalating to the source owner.
  4. Prevent with arrival SLAs, alerts, and a degraded-mode/partial-publish plan.
  5. Communicate revised timing.

Starter pattern

alert on missed arrival SLA; gate dependents; escalate
have a degraded-mode plan
Common mistake: Letting downstream proceed on stale source data rather than gating, then everyone reports wrong numbers.
Principal follow-up: How do you design dependency gating so a late source can't silently produce stale marts?
057
Holiday volume spike: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Expertholiday volume spike

Model answer

Investigate expected vs actual volume around the holiday. Diagnose capacity/skew effects. Resolve by scaling compute and applying skew handling for the surge. Prevent with a volume forecast, peak capacity planning, autoscaling, and load-testing against holiday patterns.

  • Investigate: expected vs actual holiday volume.
  • Diagnose: capacity/skew effects.
  • Resolve: scale + skew handling.
  • Prevent: forecast + peak capacity + load test.

Healthcare angle: Post-holiday claim backlogs surge volume; capacity planning and skew handling keep SLAs intact.

What strong answers cover

  1. Compare expected vs actual volume around the holiday.
  2. Diagnose capacity/skew effects.
  3. Resolve by scaling compute and applying skew handling for the surge.
  4. Prevent with a volume forecast, peak capacity planning, autoscaling, and load-testing against holiday patterns.
  5. Plan for the predictable surge.

Starter pattern

forecast holiday backlog; scale + AQE skew handling
load-test against historical holiday volume
Common mistake: Sizing for average volume and getting overwhelmed by the predictable post-holiday claim backlog.
Principal follow-up: How would you plan capacity for a known seasonal claim surge without over-provisioning year-round?
058
Month-end volume spike: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Expertmonth-end volume spike

Model answer

Investigate the predictable month-end surge against capacity. Diagnose skew amplification and SLA risk. Resolve by scaling for peak and applying AQE skew/salting. Prevent by sizing for month-end (not average), pre-warming compute, and load-testing at peak volumes.

  • Investigate: month-end surge vs capacity.
  • Diagnose: skew amplification + SLA risk.
  • Resolve: peak scaling + skew handling.
  • Prevent: size for peak + pre-warm + peak load test.

Healthcare angle: Claims spike predictably at month-end; capacity and skew handling must be tuned for peak, not average.

What strong answers cover

  1. Recognize the predictable month-end surge against capacity.
  2. Diagnose skew amplification and SLA risk.
  3. Resolve by scaling for peak and applying AQE skew/salting.
  4. Prevent by sizing for month-end (not average), pre-warming compute, load-testing at peak volumes.
  5. Treat peak as the design point.

Starter pattern

size for month-end peak; pre-warm pools; enable AQE
load-test at peak volume + skew
Common mistake: Tuning the pipeline for average daily volume so it breaks every month-end when claims surge and skew amplifies.
Principal follow-up: Why size a claims pipeline for month-end peak rather than average, and how do you validate it?
059
New payer onboarded: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Expertnew payer onboarded

Model answer

Investigate the new feed's format, grain, codes, and volume. Diagnose integration gaps (mapping, schema, reconciliation baselines). Resolve by onboarding via the metadata-driven framework with DQ/quarantine and parallel-run reconciliation. Prevent issues with a standard onboarding checklist and contract.

  • Investigate: new feed format/grain/codes/volume.
  • Diagnose: mapping/schema/reconciliation gaps.
  • Resolve: metadata-driven onboarding + DQ + parallel-run.
  • Prevent: onboarding checklist + contract.

Healthcare angle: Onboarding a new payer/feed runs through the standard framework with reconciliation before it feeds production marts.

What strong answers cover

  1. Profile the new feed's format, grain, codes, and volume.
  2. Diagnose integration gaps (mapping, schema, reconciliation baselines).
  3. Resolve by onboarding via the metadata-driven framework with DQ/quarantine and parallel-run reconciliation.
  4. Prevent issues with a standard onboarding checklist and contract.
  5. Reconcile before it feeds production.

Starter pattern

metadata-driven onboard: config source/schema/DQ/mappings
parallel-run + reconcile before production cutover
Common mistake: Hand-building a bespoke pipeline for each new payer instead of using the framework — slow and inconsistent.
Principal follow-up: How does a metadata-driven framework let you onboard a new payer safely in weeks?
060
Historical backfill pressure: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Experthistorical backfill pressure

Model answer

Investigate the backfill scope and its contention with live processing. Diagnose cost/SLA impact. Resolve by isolating the backfill (separate compute/staging), bounding concurrency, and reconciling before swap-in. Prevent with off-peak scheduling, isolation, and incremental backfill design.

  • Investigate: backfill scope + live contention.
  • Diagnose: cost/SLA impact.
  • Resolve: isolate + bound + reconcile before swap.
  • Prevent: off-peak + isolation + incremental design.

Healthcare angle: A large claims backfill runs in isolated compute/staging so it doesn't starve the nightly SLA, then swaps in reconciled.

What strong answers cover

  1. Assess the backfill scope and its contention with live processing.
  2. Diagnose cost/SLA impact.
  3. Resolve by isolating the backfill (separate compute/staging), bounding concurrency, reconciling before swap-in.
  4. Prevent with off-peak scheduling, isolation, and incremental backfill design.
  5. Don't starve the nightly SLA.

Starter pattern

run backfill in isolated compute/staging; bound concurrency
reconcile then SWAP in
Common mistake: Running a big backfill on the same warehouse/cluster as the nightly job, starving it and breaking the SLA.
Principal follow-up: How do you run a large backfill without starving live processing or blowing the budget?
061
Six-month reprocess: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Expertsix-month reprocess

Model answer

Investigate why reprocessing is needed and the affected scope. Diagnose blast radius. Resolve by reprocessing per service_month idempotently, in bounded batches, reconciling each before publishing, resumably. Prevent the trigger (the bug) and communicate restatements to consumers.

  • Investigate: reason + scope.
  • Diagnose: blast radius.
  • Resolve: per-month idempotent reprocess + reconcile + resumable.
  • Prevent: fix trigger + communicate restatements.

Healthcare angle: A six-month claims restatement runs month-by-month with per-month reconciliation, not one risky rerun.

What strong answers cover

  1. Establish why reprocessing is needed and the affected scope.
  2. Diagnose the blast radius.
  3. Resolve by reprocessing per service_month idempotently, in bounded batches, reconciling each before publishing, resumably.
  4. Prevent the trigger (the bug) and communicate restatements.
  5. Never one giant rerun.

Starter pattern

for month in affected: idempotent overwrite + reconcile + publish
resumable; track completed months
Common mistake: Kicking off one giant six-month rerun that fails at month five with no resumability and no per-month validation.
Principal follow-up: Why reprocess month-by-month with per-unit reconciliation instead of one large rerun?
062
Dr test failure: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
ExpertDR test failure

Model answer

Investigate which DR step failed (replication, failover, access, data integrity). Diagnose the gap vs RPO/RTO. Resolve by fixing the step and re-testing. Prevent by treating DR tests as mandatory, scheduled, and tracked — an untested DR plan is the real failure.

  • Investigate: which DR step failed.
  • Diagnose: gap vs RPO/RTO.
  • Resolve: fix + re-test.
  • Prevent: scheduled, tracked DR tests.

Healthcare angle: A failed claims-platform DR test (e.g. catalog not replicated) is fixed and re-tested before it's needed for real.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify which DR step failed (replication, failover, access, data integrity).
  2. Diagnose the gap vs RPO/RTO.
  3. Resolve by fixing the step and re-testing.
  4. Prevent by treating DR tests as mandatory, scheduled, and tracked.
  5. An untested DR plan is the real failure.

Starter pattern

run DR drill: replicate -> failover -> validate access + data
fix failed step; re-test; schedule regularly
Common mistake: Assuming DR works because it's configured — the first real test (or real disaster) reveals the catalog wasn't replicated.
Principal follow-up: What does a credible DR test actually exercise, and why is an untested DR plan not DR?
063
Cross-region latency: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Expertcross-region latency

Model answer

Investigate latency between regions affecting replication/serving. Diagnose impact on freshness/RPO and cost (egress). Resolve by optimizing what crosses regions (share-in-place, minimize copies) and sizing replication. Prevent with a deliberate multi-region design balancing latency, cost, and residency.

  • Investigate: inter-region latency impact.
  • Diagnose: freshness/RPO + egress cost.
  • Resolve: minimize cross-region data + size replication.
  • Prevent: deliberate multi-region design.

Healthcare angle: Cross-region replication latency for a multi-region claims platform must respect RPO and PHI residency while controlling egress.

What strong answers cover

  1. Measure latency between regions affecting replication/serving.
  2. Diagnose impact on freshness/RPO and cost (egress).
  3. Resolve by optimizing what crosses regions (share-in-place, minimize copies) and sizing replication.
  4. Prevent with a deliberate multi-region design balancing latency, cost, residency.
  5. Mind PHI residency.

Starter pattern

minimize cross-region data movement; size replication to RPO
watch egress cost + PHI residency
Common mistake: Replicating everything cross-region without regard to egress cost or PHI residency rules.
Principal follow-up: How do you design cross-region replication to meet RPO while controlling egress and respecting PHI residency?
064
Api rate limit: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
ExpertAPI rate limit

Model answer

Investigate which API throttled and at what rate. Diagnose data-gap risk. Resolve with backoff+jitter, respecting Retry-After, and resuming from the cursor; reconcile retrieved vs expected. Prevent with rate-aware clients, request pacing, and parallelism within limits.

  • Investigate: which API + throttle rate.
  • Diagnose: data-gap risk.
  • Resolve: backoff/jitter + Retry-After + resume + reconcile.
  • Prevent: rate-aware client + pacing.

Healthcare angle: FHIR/eligibility API rate limits are handled with jittered backoff and pacing so national pulls stay complete.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify which API throttled and at what rate.
  2. Diagnose the data-gap risk.
  3. Resolve with backoff+jitter, respecting Retry-After, resuming from the cursor; reconcile retrieved vs expected.
  4. Prevent with rate-aware clients, request pacing, parallelism within limits.
  5. Pace, don't hammer.

Starter pattern

honor Retry-After; backoff+jitter; pace to rate limit
parallelize within quota; reconcile completeness
Common mistake: Hammering a rate-limited API with parallel retries, getting throttled harder and dropping data.
Principal follow-up: How do you maximize throughput against a rate-limited API without getting throttled or losing records?
065
Sftp outage: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
ExpertSFTP outage

Model answer

Investigate whether the SFTP source is down and which files are delayed. Diagnose downstream SLA impact. Resolve by retrying with backoff, gating dependents, and processing once available; escalate to the source. Prevent with connection retries, alternate transport/redundancy where critical, and arrival monitoring.

  • Investigate: SFTP availability + delayed files.
  • Diagnose: downstream SLA impact.
  • Resolve: retry + gate dependents + escalate.
  • Prevent: retries + arrival monitoring + redundancy.

Healthcare angle: An SFTP outage delaying claim files gates dependent marts and pages the source owner; don't publish stale.

What strong answers cover

  1. Determine whether the SFTP source is down and which files are delayed.
  2. Diagnose downstream SLA impact.
  3. Resolve by retrying with backoff, gating dependents, processing once available; escalate to the source.
  4. Prevent with connection retries, redundancy where critical, and arrival monitoring.
  5. Don't publish stale on a missing file.

Starter pattern

retry w/ backoff; gate dependents; escalate to source
monitor file arrival; redundancy for critical feeds
Common mistake: Letting the nightly job run on yesterday's file because the SFTP source is down, silently using stale claims.
Principal follow-up: When an SFTP feed is down, how do you decide between waiting, escalating, and degraded processing?
066
Network timeout: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Expertnetwork timeout

Model answer

Investigate which connection timed out and whether transient. Diagnose scope. Resolve with bounded retries + backoff on transient timeouts, ensuring idempotency so retried writes don't duplicate. Prevent with sensible timeouts, retries, and idempotent operations.

  • Investigate: which connection + transient?
  • Diagnose: scope.
  • Resolve: bounded retries on transient + idempotency.
  • Prevent: timeouts + retries + idempotent ops.

Healthcare angle: A transient network timeout mid-claims-load retries safely only because the write is idempotent.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify which connection timed out and whether transient.
  2. Diagnose the scope.
  3. Resolve with bounded retries + backoff on transient timeouts, ensuring idempotency so retried writes don't duplicate.
  4. Prevent with sensible timeouts, retries, and idempotent operations.
  5. Idempotency makes retries safe.

Starter pattern

bounded retries on transient timeout; idempotent write
-- retry only safe because load is keyed/MERGE
Common mistake: Retrying a non-idempotent write after a timeout — the original may have partially committed, so you duplicate.
Principal follow-up: Why is idempotency the precondition for safely retrying a load after a network timeout?
067
Bad secret rotation: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Expertbad secret rotation

Model answer

Investigate whether a rotated secret broke authentication for a pipeline. Diagnose which jobs failed. Resolve by updating the secret reference (from the secret manager) and rerunning idempotently. Prevent with coordinated rotation, secret-manager references (not hard-coded), and rotation testing.

  • Investigate: rotated secret broke auth?
  • Diagnose: failed jobs.
  • Resolve: update reference + idempotent rerun.
  • Prevent: coordinated rotation + manager references + testing.

Healthcare angle: A rotated warehouse/SFTP credential breaking the claims job is fixed by updating the secret-manager reference and rerunning.

What strong answers cover

  1. Determine whether a rotated secret broke authentication for a pipeline.
  2. Diagnose which jobs failed.
  3. Resolve by updating the secret reference (from the secret manager) and rerunning idempotently.
  4. Prevent with coordinated rotation, secret-manager references (not hard-coded), and rotation testing.
  5. Never hard-code secrets.

Starter pattern

update reference in secret manager; rerun idempotently
coordinate rotation; test before/after
Common mistake: Hard-coded credentials (not manager references) so a rotation silently breaks every job until manually hunted down.
Principal follow-up: How do secret-manager references and coordinated rotation prevent rotation-induced outages?
068
Expired certificate: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Expertexpired certificate

Model answer

Investigate the expired cert and affected connections (API/SFTP/TLS). Diagnose failure scope. Resolve by renewing/installing the cert and rerunning. Prevent with certificate expiry monitoring and automated renewal/alerts well before expiry.

  • Investigate: expired cert + affected connections.
  • Diagnose: failure scope.
  • Resolve: renew/install + rerun.
  • Prevent: expiry monitoring + auto-renewal/alerts.

Healthcare angle: An expired TLS cert breaking the FHIR pull is renewed and the pull rerun; expiry alerts prevent recurrence.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify the expired cert and affected connections (API/SFTP/TLS).
  2. Diagnose the failure scope.
  3. Resolve by renewing/installing the cert and rerunning.
  4. Prevent with certificate expiry monitoring and automated renewal/alerts well before expiry.
  5. Alert ahead of expiry.

Starter pattern

renew/install cert; rerun failed jobs
monitor expiry; auto-renew + alert N days before
Common mistake: No cert-expiry monitoring, so a known, scheduled expiry takes down ingestion as a surprise outage.
Principal follow-up: How would you ensure a certificate expiry never becomes a surprise outage?
069
Token failure: walk me through triage, root cause, recovery, stakeholder communication, and prevention.
Experttoken failure

Model answer

Investigate the auth token failure (expired/revoked/misconfigured). Diagnose affected jobs. Resolve by refreshing/reissuing the token and rerunning idempotently. Prevent with automatic token refresh, proper OAuth handling, and alerting on auth failures.

  • Investigate: expired/revoked/misconfigured token.
  • Diagnose: affected jobs.
  • Resolve: refresh/reissue + rerun.
  • Prevent: auto-refresh + OAuth handling + auth alerts.

Healthcare angle: An expired OAuth token failing the eligibility API is fixed with refresh handling so it self-recovers.

What strong answers cover

  1. Identify the auth token failure (expired/revoked/misconfigured).
  2. Diagnose affected jobs.
  3. Resolve by refreshing/reissuing the token and rerunning idempotently.
  4. Prevent with automatic token refresh, proper OAuth handling, and alerting on auth failures.
