Explain you've moved from building components to designing systems — owning grain/keys, history, idempotency, governance, SLAs, and cross-team contracts — and increasingly influence through standards and mentoring. Frame the principal/architect path as scaling impact from your code to the whole platform and team. Make it concrete with examples.

  • From building components to designing systems.
  • Own architecture: grain, history, idempotency, governance, SLA, contracts.
  • Influence via standards + mentoring.
  • Scale impact from your code to the platform/team.

Healthcare angle: Show the shift from writing pipelines to owning payer-platform architecture and raising the team's bar — that's the principal trajectory.

What strong answers cover

  1. Situation: tie the story to production impact, business stakeholder, data risk, or delivery pressure.
  2. Task: state your ownership clearly; do not hide behind the team.
  3. Action: show investigation, tradeoffs, communication, and concrete technical decisions.
  4. Result: quantify impact: SLA restored, cost reduced, defects avoided, cycle time improved, or trust rebuilt.
  5. Lesson: explain what process, test, alert, or design standard changed afterward.

Starter pattern

Investigate -> Diagnose -> Fix -> Prevent -> Monitor
Common mistake: Giving a vague motivational story with no metric, no conflict, no consequence, and no lesson.
Principal follow-up: What would your manager, business stakeholder, and junior engineer each say you did well?
050
Tell me about a time involving five-year plan. Answer at senior/architect level using STAR, not generic teamwork language.
Principalfive-year plan

Model answer

Show ambition aligned with the role. Explain you aim to grow as a principal/architect — deepening healthcare-data and platform expertise, driving enterprise standards, mentoring engineers, and owning increasingly strategic data architecture. Tie it to delivering more member/business impact at scale. Keep it genuine, role-aligned, and not rigidly scripted.

  • Grow as principal/architect.
  • Deepen healthcare-data + platform expertise.
  • Drive standards + mentor + own strategic architecture.
  • Aligned to bigger member/business impact.

Healthcare angle: Framing your five-year plan around owning payer-platform architecture and growing others aligns with what these roles want.

What strong answers cover

  1. Situation: tie the story to production impact, business stakeholder, data risk, or delivery pressure.
  2. Task: state your ownership clearly; do not hide behind the team.
  3. Action: show investigation, tradeoffs, communication, and concrete technical decisions.
  4. Result: quantify impact: SLA restored, cost reduced, defects avoided, cycle time improved, or trust rebuilt.
  5. Lesson: explain what process, test, alert, or design standard changed afterward.

Starter pattern

Investigate -> Diagnose -> Fix -> Prevent -> Monitor
Common mistake: Giving a vague motivational story with no metric, no conflict, no consequence, and no lesson.
Principal follow-up: What would your manager, business stakeholder, and junior engineer each say you did well?

Mock Interview Rounds

Company-style rounds across SQL, Python, Spark, Databricks, Snowflake, healthcare and behavioral.

100 questions
001
Solve Mock round 01: claim-line deduplication with deterministic ROW_NUMBER using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 01: claim-line deduplication with deterministic ROW_NUMBER

Model answer

Define the business grain first, then keep exactly one survivor per key with a fully deterministic ordering. The natural grain of a claim line is usually (claim_id, line_number) for the latest version, so PARTITION BY that key and ORDER BY a recency column plus a tie-breaker that can never be equal.

  • Always add a unique tie-breaker (e.g. ingest_ts DESC, file_seq DESC, source_row_id DESC) so reruns pick the same survivor every time.
  • ROW_NUMBER()=1 keeps one row; use QUALIFY in Snowflake/Databricks to avoid a wrapping subquery.
  • Distinguish true duplicates (same payload) from versions/corrections (same key, different paid_amt) — only collapse true dupes.
  • Validate with a count of keys having rn>1 and a paid_amt control total before vs after.

Healthcare angle: A resent 837 file or an 835 correction looks like a duplicate but is a legitimate replacement; keying on ingest recency keeps the corrected version, not the stale one.

SELECT *
FROM raw_claim_lines
QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
          PARTITION BY claim_id, line_number
          ORDER BY ingest_ts DESC, file_seq DESC, source_row_id DESC) = 1;

What strong answers cover

  1. State the grain (claim_id, line) and business key first.
  2. Use ROW_NUMBER with a deterministic ORDER BY plus a guaranteed-unique tie-breaker.
  3. Distinguish true duplicates from versions/corrections (same key, different paid).
  4. Validate: count keys with rn>1 and a paid control total before vs after.
  5. Use QUALIFY in Snowflake/Databricks to avoid a wrapping subquery.

Starter pattern

SELECT * FROM claim_lines
QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
  PARTITION BY claim_id, line_number
  ORDER BY ingest_ts DESC, file_seq DESC, source_row_id DESC)=1;
Common mistake: Omitting a unique tie-breaker so reruns pick a different survivor each time — non-deterministic, non-idempotent dedup.
Principal follow-up: How would you keep the latest version but still preserve the full history for audit?
002
Solve Mock round 02: enrollment gaps using LAG using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 02: enrollment gaps using LAG

Model answer

Order each member's coverage spans by start date, use LAG to pull the previous span's end date, and flag a gap whenever the current start is more than one day after the previous end. The gap length drives benefits, HEDIS continuous-enrollment, and risk-adjustment eligibility.

  • LAG(coverage_end) OVER (PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY coverage_start) gives the prior end per member.
  • Treat adjacent/contiguous spans (prev_end + 1 = curr_start) as no gap; only datediff>1 is a real gap.
  • Handle overlapping spans (curr_start <= prev_end) separately — that is an overlap, not a gap, and usually a data-quality defect.
  • NULL prior end on the first span means no preceding gap, not a gap of unknown size.

Healthcare angle: HEDIS allows up to a 45-day enrollment gap in the continuous-enrollment window; the gap-length logic feeds denominator eligibility directly.

WITH s AS (
  SELECT member_id, coverage_start, coverage_end,
         LAG(coverage_end) OVER (PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY coverage_start) AS prev_end
  FROM coverage)
SELECT member_id, prev_end, coverage_start,
       DATEDIFF('day', prev_end, coverage_start) - 1 AS gap_days
FROM s
WHERE prev_end IS NOT NULL AND coverage_start > prev_end + 1;

What strong answers cover

  1. Order coverage spans per member and compare each span's start to the prior span's end with LAG.
  2. A gap exists when start > prior_end + 1 (or beyond the allowed gap, e.g. 45 days for HEDIS).
  3. Handle the first span (NULL prior) and overlapping spans.
  4. Use it for continuous-enrollment logic.
  5. Validate against known member histories.

Starter pattern

SELECT member_id, valid_from,
  LAG(valid_to) OVER (PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY valid_from) AS prev_end
FROM coverage
QUALIFY DATEDIFF('day', prev_end, valid_from) > 45;
Common mistake: Ignoring overlapping spans, so an overlap reads as a negative gap and continuous-enrollment logic is wrong.
Principal follow-up: How would you adapt this to the HEDIS 45-day allowable gap and partial-month enrollment?
003
Solve Mock round 03: rolling 3-month paid amount with ROWS vs RANGE using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 03: rolling 3-month paid amount with ROWS vs RANGE

Model answer

ROWS counts a fixed number of physical rows; RANGE counts logical values within an ordered window. For a true rolling 3 calendar months you must aggregate to a month grain first, then use ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW — otherwise sparse months silently shrink the window.

  • ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW = exactly the prior 2 rows + current; correct only if every month is present.
  • RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '2' MONTH PRECEDING (where supported) is value-based and tolerates gaps, but support varies by engine.
  • Pre-aggregate to member_month and join to a calendar spine so months with zero claims still exist as rows.
  • RANGE with ties includes all peer rows at the same ORDER BY value — a common source of double-counting.

Healthcare angle: PMPM and trend reports need dense month grains; missing months from claim sparsity understate the rolling denominator if you rely on ROWS without a spine.

SELECT member_id, month,
       SUM(paid_amt) OVER (PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY month
            ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS paid_3mo
FROM member_month_paid;   -- member_month_paid is dense via a calendar spine

What strong answers cover

  1. Use a windowed SUM with an explicit frame; know ROWS (physical rows) vs RANGE (logical value range).
  2. For a true 3-calendar-month rolling sum, aggregate to month grain first, then frame over months.
  3. RANGE with date intervals handles gaps; ROWS assumes contiguous rows.
  4. Be explicit about the frame — defaults differ and surprise people.
  5. Validate boundaries (first months).

Starter pattern

SELECT member_id, month, SUM(paid) OVER (
  PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY month
  RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '2' MONTH PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS rolling_3m
FROM monthly_paid;
Common mistake: Using ROWS 2 PRECEDING on data with missing months — it sums the wrong window when a month has no rows.
Principal follow-up: Why does ROWS give a wrong 3-month total when a member has gap months, and how does RANGE fix it?
004
Solve Mock round 04: consecutive inpatient admissions using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 04: consecutive inpatient admissions

Model answer

This is a gaps-and-islands problem. Sort admissions per member, then form islands where each admission starts within the readmission window of the prior discharge; the difference of two row_numbers (or a running flag) groups consecutive stays into an episode.

  • Compute prev_discharge with LAG; mark a new island when admit_dt - prev_discharge > window (e.g. 30 days).
  • Cumulative SUM of the new-island flag gives an episode_id you can GROUP BY.
  • Define the window by the business question: 30-day readmission vs same-stay transfer logic differ.
  • Watch overlapping admits and same-day transfers — they should collapse into one episode, not two.

Healthcare angle: 30-day all-cause readmission (a CMS quality/penalty measure) is exactly this islands pattern keyed on discharge-to-admit spacing.

WITH x AS (
  SELECT member_id, admit_dt, discharge_dt,
         LAG(discharge_dt) OVER (PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY admit_dt) AS prev_dis
  FROM inpatient),
g AS (SELECT *, SUM(CASE WHEN prev_dis IS NULL OR admit_dt > prev_dis + 30 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
             OVER (PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY admit_dt) AS episode FROM x)
SELECT member_id, episode, MIN(admit_dt) ep_start, MAX(discharge_dt) ep_end, COUNT(*) stays
FROM g GROUP BY member_id, episode;

What strong answers cover

  1. This is gaps-and-islands: order admissions, detect readmissions within a window (e.g. 30 days of prior discharge).
  2. Use LEAD/LAG on admit/discharge dates; merge transfers into one stay.
  3. Exclude planned readmissions per the measure.
  4. Watch overlapping stays.
  5. Validate against transfer scenarios.

Starter pattern

SELECT *, CASE WHEN DATEDIFF('day',
   LAG(discharge_dt) OVER (PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY admit_dt), admit_dt) <= 30
  THEN 1 ELSE 0 END AS is_readmit
FROM admissions;
Common mistake: Counting hospital transfers as separate admissions, inflating admission and 30-day readmission rates.
Principal follow-up: How do you merge transfers into a single stay before computing 30-day readmissions?
005
Solve Mock round 05: top providers per specialty including ties using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 05: top providers per specialty including ties

Model answer

Use RANK() (not ROW_NUMBER) so tied providers share the top position, then filter rank<=N. ROW_NUMBER would arbitrarily drop a tied provider; DENSE_RANK changes how many rows survive when ties exist.

  • RANK() leaves gaps after ties (1,1,3); DENSE_RANK() is gapless (1,1,2) — pick based on whether 'top 3' means 3 positions or 3 distinct values.
  • Always state the tie-breaker explicitly even when you intend to keep ties, so results are reproducible.
  • PARTITION BY specialty; ORDER BY the metric DESC.
  • Decide whether ties at the boundary should all qualify (use rank<=N) or be cut (needs a deterministic tie-breaker).

Healthcare angle: 'Top providers by paid amount per specialty' is a standard network/payment-integrity ask; ties matter when contracts or audits depend on who made the list.

SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT provider_npi, specialty, total_paid,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY specialty ORDER BY total_paid DESC) rk
  FROM provider_specialty_paid)
WHERE rk <= 3;

What strong answers cover

  1. Use RANK (not ROW_NUMBER) when ties should share a rank and all be included.
  2. PARTITION BY specialty, ORDER BY the metric DESC.
  3. DENSE_RANK if you want no rank gaps.
  4. Decide explicitly whether ties are kept or broken.
  5. Validate with a known tie.

Starter pattern

SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT *, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY specialty ORDER BY paid DESC) rk
  FROM provider_metrics)
WHERE rk <= 10;  -- ties at the boundary all included
Common mistake: Using ROW_NUMBER for a 'top N including ties', which arbitrarily drops tied providers at the boundary.
Principal follow-up: When would you use RANK vs DENSE_RANK vs ROW_NUMBER here, and how does each treat ties?
006
Solve Mock round 06: first utilization after enrollment using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 06: first utilization after enrollment

Model answer

Find each member's first qualifying service on or after their coverage start. ROW_NUMBER over service date, filtered to rn=1, after joining claims to the coverage span — the join condition is the subtle part.

  • Join claim to the coverage span where service_dt BETWEEN coverage_start AND coverage_end so you attribute to the right enrollment period.
  • ORDER BY service_dt, then a tie-breaker (claim_id) for same-day services.
  • Decide what 'utilization' counts: any claim, or only specific service types (e.g. first PCP visit).
  • Members with no post-enrollment claim must still appear (LEFT JOIN) if you report 'days to first use'.

Healthcare angle: 'Time to first PCP visit after enrollment' is a care-engagement metric used in onboarding and gaps-in-care programs.

SELECT member_id, service_dt FROM (
  SELECT c.member_id, c.service_dt,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY c.member_id ORDER BY c.service_dt, c.claim_id) rn
  FROM claims c JOIN coverage v
    ON c.member_id=v.member_id AND c.service_dt BETWEEN v.coverage_start AND v.coverage_end)
WHERE rn=1;

What strong answers cover

  1. Find the earliest qualifying claim after each member's enrollment start.
  2. ROW_NUMBER ordered by service_dt = 1, filtered to service_dt >= enrollment_start.
  3. Handle members with no utilization (left join / not exists).
  4. Mind re-enrollment (multiple spans).
  5. Validate first-event correctness.

Starter pattern

SELECT * FROM claims c JOIN enrollment e USING(member_id)
WHERE c.service_dt >= e.start_dt
QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY service_dt)=1;
Common mistake: Forgetting members with no post-enrollment utilization (inner join drops them), biasing 'time to first use'.
Principal follow-up: How would you handle a member who re-enrolls, so 'first utilization' is per enrollment span?
007
Solve Mock round 07: provider monthly rank movement using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 07: provider monthly rank movement

Model answer

Rank providers within each month, then use LAG on the rank partitioned by provider to compute month-over-month movement. Positive movement = climbing; the trick is handling months where a provider has no activity.

  • RANK() within month by metric, then LAG(rank) OVER (PARTITION BY provider ORDER BY month).
  • Join to a provider x month spine so absent months show as 'unranked' rather than silently skipping the LAG.
  • Define how ties and new entrants are treated (NULL prior rank = new).
  • Cap or bucket extreme movement for readable dashboards.

Healthcare angle: Useful for network management and provider scorecards tracking volume or quality rank shifts over time.

WITH m AS (
  SELECT month, provider_npi,
         RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY month ORDER BY total_paid DESC) rk
  FROM provider_month)
SELECT *, LAG(rk) OVER (PARTITION BY provider_npi ORDER BY month) - rk AS moved_up
FROM m;

What strong answers cover

  1. Rank providers within each month, then compare to the prior month's rank with LAG.
  2. Movement = prev_rank - current_rank.
  3. Handle providers absent in a month (NULL prior).
  4. Use a calendar spine so months are contiguous.
  5. Validate sign convention (up vs down).

Starter pattern

WITH r AS (SELECT month, provider, RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY month ORDER BY paid DESC) rk FROM m)
SELECT *, LAG(rk) OVER (PARTITION BY provider ORDER BY month) - rk AS movement FROM r;
Common mistake: Comparing ranks across months without aligning on a contiguous month spine, so a missing month breaks the LAG.
Principal follow-up: How do you handle a provider with no activity in a month so rank movement stays meaningful?
008
Solve Mock round 08: running paid amount by member using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 08: running paid amount by member

Model answer

A cumulative SUM with an explicit frame: SUM(paid_amt) OVER (PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY service_dt ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING). State the frame — the default RANGE frame with ties can over-count same-day claims.

  • ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING gives a true row-by-row running total; default RANGE sums all rows sharing the ORDER BY value at once.
  • Add a tie-breaker in ORDER BY for deterministic intermediate totals.
  • For accumulator-to-date metrics (deductible), reset the partition at plan-year boundaries.
  • Negative adjustments and voids must be included so the running total reflects net paid.

Healthcare angle: Running paid total underlies deductible/out-of-pocket accumulators, which reset each plan year and must net out reversals.

SELECT member_id, service_dt, paid_amt,
       SUM(paid_amt) OVER (PARTITION BY member_id, plan_year ORDER BY service_dt, claim_id
            ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS running_paid
FROM claims;

What strong answers cover

  1. Cumulative SUM with an ordered window frame (UNBOUNDED PRECEDING to CURRENT ROW).
  2. PARTITION BY member; ORDER BY a deterministic key (service_dt, then a tie-breaker).
  3. Add a tie-breaker so equal dates produce a stable running total.
  4. Reset per member.
  5. Validate the final total equals the member's sum.

Starter pattern

SELECT *, SUM(paid) OVER (
  PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY service_dt, claim_id
  ROWS UNBOUNDED PRECEDING) AS running_paid
FROM claims;
Common mistake: No tie-breaker on equal service dates, so the running total is non-deterministic across runs.
Principal follow-up: How would you make the running total reset at each plan-year boundary?
009
Solve Mock round 09: same claim_id different paid amounts using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 09: same claim_id different paid amounts

Model answer

Same claim_id with differing paid_amt is almost always versioning (adjustment/replacement), not a true duplicate. Identify the latest version by adjustment sequence or ingest recency and keep it; never SUM across versions, which double-pays.

  • Order by claim_frequency_code / adjustment_seq / ingest_ts and keep the latest with QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER=1.
  • Confirm whether the source sends full replacement (use latest) or delta adjustments (must net).
  • A control total of paid_amt before/after collapsing detects accidental over-summing.
  • Log collapsed versions to an audit table for traceability.

Healthcare angle: 837 replacement claims (frequency code 7) and 835 reversals/corrections create this exact pattern; misreading it inflates paid trend.

SELECT *
FROM claim_versions
QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY claim_id
          ORDER BY adjustment_seq DESC, ingest_ts DESC) = 1;

What strong answers cover

  1. This signals versions/adjustments (or a data bug), not random duplicates.
  2. Identify claim_ids with >1 distinct paid; decide whether to keep the net latest version.
  3. Use version/adjustment sequence + recency to pick the survivor.
  4. Never sum across versions.
  5. Quarantine truly inexplicable cases.

Starter pattern

SELECT claim_id FROM claims GROUP BY claim_id
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT paid) > 1;  -- investigate: versions vs bug
-- then keep net latest via ROW_NUMBER on adj_seq DESC
Common mistake: Treating different paid amounts for one claim_id as duplicates to sum — that double-counts legitimate versions.
Principal follow-up: How do you distinguish legitimate adjustments from a genuine data defect here?
010
Solve Mock round 10: member utilization percentile with NTILE using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 10: member utilization percentile with NTILE

Model answer

NTILE(n) buckets ordered rows into n roughly equal groups; use it to stratify members by utilization (e.g. deciles of paid amount). Be explicit that NTILE distributes remainder rows to earlier buckets, so buckets aren't exactly equal.

  • NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY paid_amt) gives utilization deciles; tie values can fall in different buckets.
  • If you need value-based thresholds (top 5% spend), PERCENT_RANK/CUME_DIST is more honest than NTILE.
  • Stratify within a population (PARTITION BY line_of_business) for comparable cohorts.
  • Document the ordering and how nulls/zeros are placed.

Healthcare angle: Risk stratification and high-cost-claimant identification rely on percentile bucketing of member spend.

SELECT member_id, paid_amt,
       NTILE(10) OVER (ORDER BY paid_amt) AS spend_decile,
       CUME_DIST() OVER (ORDER BY paid_amt) AS pct
FROM member_annual_paid;

What strong answers cover

  1. NTILE(n) buckets members into n equal groups by a metric; or use PERCENT_RANK/CUME_DIST for true percentiles.
  2. Know NTILE distributes remainder rows, so buckets can differ by one.
  3. PARTITION appropriately (e.g. by plan).
  4. Choose NTILE vs percentile functions deliberately.
  5. Validate bucket sizes.

Starter pattern

SELECT member_id, paid,
  NTILE(100) OVER (ORDER BY paid) AS pctile_bucket,
  PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY paid) AS pct_rank
FROM member_paid;
Common mistake: Using NTILE(100) and calling it 'the 95th percentile value' — NTILE makes equal-count buckets, not value percentiles.
Principal follow-up: When do you use NTILE vs PERCENT_RANK vs a true percentile_cont, and how do they differ?
011
Solve Mock round 11: provider SCD2 MERGE using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 11: provider SCD2 MERGE

Model answer

SCD2 keeps history by closing the current row and inserting a new version when tracked attributes change. Detect change with a hash of tracked columns, then MERGE: update the open row's end_date/current_flag and insert the new version. Make it idempotent so a rerun does nothing.

  • Hash only the columns you track for history; ignore audit/load columns to avoid false versions.
  • Close prior version (set valid_to = effective_dt - 1, is_current=false) and insert new with valid_to = high date.
  • Two-step MERGE or a MERGE with INSERT-on-no-match using a staged 'changed rows' set.
  • Guard against late-arriving effective dates that fall before the current open row (bitemporal handling).

Healthcare angle: Provider attributes (specialty, address, network status, par/non-par) change over time and must be queried as-of the service date for correct pricing and attribution.

MERGE INTO provider_dim t
USING staged_changes s ON t.npi=s.npi AND t.is_current=TRUE
WHEN MATCHED AND t.row_hash<>s.row_hash THEN
  UPDATE SET t.valid_to=s.effective_dt-1, t.is_current=FALSE;
-- second pass: INSERT new current versions for changed/new npis

What strong answers cover

  1. SCD2 versions changes: close the old row (set valid_to, is_current=false) and insert a new current row.
  2. Detect change via a hash of tracked columns.
  3. Make the MERGE idempotent (no-op when nothing changed).
  4. Handle late/out-of-order updates.
  5. Validate one current row per key.

Starter pattern

MERGE INTO dim t USING staged s ON t.npi=s.npi AND t.is_current
WHEN MATCHED AND t.row_hash<>s.row_hash THEN UPDATE SET valid_to=s.eff, is_current=FALSE
-- then INSERT new current rows
Common mistake: A two-step close+insert that isn't atomic/idempotent, leaving two current rows or zero on a rerun.
Principal follow-up: How do you guarantee exactly one current row per provider even on reruns and out-of-order updates?
012
Solve Mock round 12: member address SCD2 using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 12: member address SCD2

Model answer

Same SCD2 mechanics as provider, applied to member demographic/address history. The nuance is high churn and the need to keep prior addresses for as-of correspondence, geographic attribution, and audit.

  • Track address fields with a hash; demographic noise (case, whitespace) should be normalized before hashing.
  • Effective-date the change from the source's reported change date, not the load date.
  • Support retroactive corrections that reopen/adjust historical spans.
  • is_current + valid_from/valid_to enables point-in-time lookups for any service date.

Healthcare angle: Geographic/region attribution for network adequacy and premium rating depends on the member's address as of the relevant date, not today's address.

-- normalize then hash, then SCD2 MERGE as with provider_dim
SELECT member_id, UPPER(TRIM(addr_line1)) addr1, zip5,
       MD5(CONCAT_WS('|',UPPER(TRIM(addr_line1)),zip5,state)) row_hash
FROM stg_member_address;

What strong answers cover

  1. Same SCD2 pattern for member address: version changes with effective dates and a current flag.
  2. Hash the tracked attributes (address fields) to detect real changes.
  3. Avoid churning versions on cosmetic differences (normalize first).
  4. Support as-of address lookups.
  5. Validate version continuity.

Starter pattern

row_hash = MD5(CONCAT_WS('|', street, city, state, zip))
-- new version only when hash changes; normalize before hashing
Common mistake: Not normalizing address before hashing, so casing/whitespace differences create spurious SCD2 versions.
Principal follow-up: How would you avoid version churn from trivial address formatting differences?
013
Solve Mock round 13: late-arriving provider dimension using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 13: late-arriving provider dimension

Model answer

When a claim references a provider not yet in the dimension, don't drop the fact. Insert an inferred/placeholder dimension row (or route to an 'unknown provider' surrogate), load the fact, and reconcile when the real provider record arrives later.

  • Use a stable 'inferred' surrogate keyed on the natural key so the real record can be matched and enriched later.
  • Track a status flag (inferred vs confirmed) and a reconciliation job that upgrades inferred rows.
  • Never assign the generic -1 unknown key if you can preserve the natural key for later repair.
  • Alert when inferred rows exceed a threshold — it signals a roster feed problem.

Healthcare angle: Provider roster feeds frequently lag claims; placeholder-then-enrich avoids losing claim facts while keeping attribution fixable.

-- on FK miss, create inferred dim row keyed by NPI, then enrich on roster arrival
INSERT INTO provider_dim (npi, name, status)
SELECT DISTINCT c.npi, 'INFERRED', 'inferred'
FROM claims c LEFT JOIN provider_dim d ON c.npi=d.npi
WHERE d.npi IS NULL;

What strong answers cover

  1. When a claim references a provider not yet in the dimension, insert an inferred placeholder keyed on NPI.
  2. Enrich the placeholder when the real record arrives.
  3. Track inferred vs confirmed status; alert if inferred volume is high.
  4. Keep the fact loadable meanwhile.
  5. Avoid dumping all to a generic 'unknown'.

Starter pattern

-- on claim load, if NPI not in dim: INSERT placeholder(npi, is_inferred=TRUE)
-- later MERGE real roster -> update placeholder, set is_inferred=FALSE
Common mistake: Routing all unknown providers to a single 'UNKNOWN' surrogate, losing the ability to fix attribution later.
Principal follow-up: How do you reconcile placeholder providers once the real roster arrives without breaking existing facts?
014
Solve Mock round 14: retroactive membership correction using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 14: retroactive membership correction

Model answer

Retro corrections (e.g. eligibility added/removed 45 days back) change history. Model membership bitemporally: keep effective (business) dates separate from system/load time so you can answer both 'what is true now' and 'what did we believe last month'. Reopen and re-version affected spans.

  • Bitemporal columns: valid_from/valid_to (business) + system_from/system_to (knowledge time).
  • Apply the correction by closing the stale system version and inserting a new one with corrected business dates.
  • Downstream marts built from prior snapshots must be reprocessed for the affected window.
  • Quantify blast radius: which claims/PMPM/HEDIS denominators changed.

Healthcare angle: Retroactive eligibility shifts member-months, capitation payments, and risk-adjustment eligibility; bitemporal modeling makes the restatement auditable.

-- corrected span supersedes prior; keep both system versions
UPDATE membership SET system_to=CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, is_current=FALSE
WHERE member_id=:m AND is_current=TRUE AND valid_to>=:retro_start;
-- then INSERT corrected current span(s)

What strong answers cover

  1. A retro change rewrites past coverage; apply it as a new effective/knowledge-time version (bitemporal).
  2. Recompute affected member-months and restate dependent aggregates.
  3. Bound the affected period; communicate restatement.
  4. Keep the prior view reproducible.
  5. Validate member-month deltas.

Starter pattern

-- bitemporal: insert corrected span with new system_from
-- recompute member_months for affected [from,to]; restate PMPM
Common mistake: Overwriting the old coverage in place, destroying the ability to reproduce what prior reports showed.
Principal follow-up: How does bitemporal modeling let you both correct history and reproduce the original report?
015
Solve Mock round 15: idempotent payment MERGE using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 15: idempotent payment MERGE

Model answer

An idempotent payment load produces the same end state no matter how many times it runs. Key the MERGE on the payment's natural identity (e.g. claim_id + remit_id + line) and make the match-update a no-op when the payload is unchanged, so a replayed 835 never double-credits.

  • MERGE on the full business key including remittance/check identifiers, not just claim_id.
  • Compare a payload hash on MATCH and skip if equal to avoid churn and double-application.
  • Use INSERT-only-on-not-matched; never blind INSERT into an append table for payments.
  • Track applied remit_ids in a control table to detect duplicate files before load.

Healthcare angle: Duplicate 835 files are common; idempotent MERGE keyed on remittance identity prevents inflated AR/payment totals.

MERGE INTO payments t
USING stg_remit s
  ON t.claim_id=s.claim_id AND t.remit_id=s.remit_id AND t.line=s.line
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (...);  -- matched = already applied, do nothing

What strong answers cover

  1. MERGE on a stable business key so reruns don't duplicate payments.
  2. Use remittance identity (payer + check/EFT + remit id) for 835 payments.
  3. WHEN MATCHED do nothing (or update to latest); WHEN NOT MATCHED insert.
  4. Test: running twice equals running once.
  5. Reconcile paid totals after.

Starter pattern

MERGE INTO payments t USING staged s ON t.remit_key=s.remit_key
WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ...;  -- second run is a no-op
Common mistake: Using INSERT (or a non-unique merge key), so a replay double-applies payments and corrupts AR.
Principal follow-up: What exactly makes the merge key unique for an 835 payment, and how do you test idempotency?
016
Solve Mock round 16: soft deletes in provider feed using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 16: soft deletes in provider feed

Model answer

A soft delete is a status flag (active=false / deleted_flag) rather than a physical row removal. In a provider feed, honor it by closing the SCD2 span or setting is_active=false, preserving history for claims that referenced the provider while active.

  • Never hard-delete a dimension row that facts point to — you'd orphan history.
  • Map source delete flags to an end-date + is_active=false in the dimension.
  • Full-snapshot feeds: providers absent from today's file may be implicit deletes — confirm the contract before deactivating.
  • Reactivation must reopen a new current span, not resurrect the old one.

Healthcare angle: A provider leaving the network is a soft delete; past claims must still join to the provider as it existed at service time.

UPDATE provider_dim SET is_active=FALSE, valid_to=:as_of_dt, is_current=FALSE
WHERE npi IN (SELECT npi FROM stg_provider WHERE delete_flag='Y') AND is_current=TRUE;

What strong answers cover

  1. A source delete should end-date/flag the row, not physically remove it (facts still reference it).
  2. Map deletes to is_active=false / valid_to=now in SCD2.
  3. Distinguish a true delete from a missing record in a partial feed.
  4. Preserve history.
  5. Validate that downstream as-of joins still work.

Starter pattern

MERGE ... WHEN MATCHED AND s.op='DELETE' THEN UPDATE SET is_active=FALSE, valid_to=CURRENT_DATE
-- never hard-delete a referenced provider
Common mistake: Hard-deleting a provider that historical claims join to, breaking attribution for past periods.
Principal follow-up: How do you tell a real delete from a record simply absent in a partial feed?
017
Solve Mock round 17: effective date overlap detection using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 17: effective date overlap detection

Model answer

Overlaps mean two 'current' or date-bounded rows cover the same key and time, which breaks as-of joins. Detect with a self-join or LAG: an overlap exists when a row's start is on/before the previous row's end for the same key.

  • LAG(valid_to) per key ordered by valid_from; overlap when valid_from <= prev_valid_to.
  • Distinguish overlaps (defect) from contiguous spans (fine) and gaps (different issue).
  • Resolve by truncating/merging spans per a documented rule, or quarantining for stewardship.
  • Add a constraint/test in the pipeline so overlaps fail loudly, not silently.

Healthcare angle: Overlapping eligibility or provider network spans cause as-of joins to fan out and double-count claims.

WITH x AS (
  SELECT *, LAG(valid_to) OVER (PARTITION BY key_id ORDER BY valid_from) prev_to
  FROM spans)
SELECT * FROM x WHERE prev_to IS NOT NULL AND valid_from <= prev_to;

What strong answers cover

  1. Overlaps in effective-dated spans break as-of joins (return >1 row).
  2. Detect with a self-join or LAG where a span's start < prior span's end.
  3. Resolve by trimming/merging spans per business rules.
  4. Run it as a DQ control.
  5. Validate spans are non-overlapping after fix.

Starter pattern

SELECT * FROM spans s
WHERE s.valid_from < LAG(s.valid_to) OVER (
  PARTITION BY member_id ORDER BY s.valid_from);  -- overlap flag
Common mistake: Letting overlapping spans through, so an as-of join multiplies the fact and corrupts measures.
Principal follow-up: How would you automatically repair overlapping spans without dropping legitimate coverage changes?
018
Solve Mock round 18: current record validation using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 18: current record validation

Model answer

Validate that each business key has exactly one current row, no overlaps, and contiguous-or-intended history. These are cheap assertions that catch SCD2 corruption early.

  • Assert COUNT(*) WHERE is_current=TRUE = 1 per key.
  • Assert no overlapping spans (see overlap detection).
  • Assert the current row's valid_to is the high/open date.
  • Run as a post-merge data-quality gate that fails the job, not a dashboard nobody reads.

Healthcare angle: A duplicate 'current' provider/member row silently double-counts every downstream join.

SELECT key_id, COUNT(*) FROM dim WHERE is_current=TRUE
GROUP BY key_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;   -- must return zero rows

What strong answers cover

  1. A core SCD2 control: exactly one is_current row per key, and current rows have an open valid_to.
  2. Assert COUNT of current rows per key = 1.
  3. Catch zero-current (lost) and multi-current (duplicate) defects.
  4. Run after every dimension load.
  5. Fail loudly on violations.

Starter pattern

SELECT key, COUNT(*) FROM dim WHERE is_current GROUP BY key HAVING COUNT(*)<>1;
-- must return zero rows
Common mistake: Not asserting one-current-row-per-key, so a botched SCD2 merge silently leaves duplicates that fan out joins.
Principal follow-up: What other invariants would you assert on an SCD2 dimension besides one-current-row-per-key?
019
Solve Mock round 19: hash-based change detection using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 19: hash-based change detection

Model answer

Hash the tracked columns into a single fingerprint; a changed hash means a real change worth versioning. It replaces brittle column-by-column comparisons and is the engine behind efficient SCD2 and CDC.

  • Hash only meaningful, normalized columns (trim, upcase, standardize nulls) to avoid false positives.
  • Use a stable concat with a delimiter and explicit null token (CONCAT_WS misses trailing nulls in some engines).
  • Store the hash on the row so the next run compares cheaply.
  • Pick a wide hash (MD5/SHA) to keep collision risk negligible at payer volumes.

Healthcare angle: Provider/member dimensions have many noisy columns; hashing the few governed ones prevents history churn on cosmetic source changes.

SELECT *, MD5(CONCAT_WS('|', COALESCE(a,'~'), COALESCE(b,'~'), COALESCE(c,'~'))) AS row_hash
FROM stg;

What strong answers cover

  1. Hash the tracked columns to detect real changes cheaply (compare incoming hash to stored).
  2. Only version/update when the hash differs — avoids churn and unnecessary writes.
  3. Normalize/canonicalize before hashing (casing, whitespace, nulls).
  4. Exclude audit columns from the hash.
  5. Validate hash stability.

Starter pattern

row_hash = MD5(CONCAT_WS('|', COALESCE(col1,''), COALESCE(col2,'')))
-- update only WHERE stored_hash <> incoming_hash
Common mistake: Hashing without normalizing/handling NULLs, so trivial differences (or NULL vs '') trigger spurious changes.
Principal follow-up: Why must you canonicalize and handle NULLs before hashing, and which columns do you exclude?
020
Solve Mock round 20: Type 1 vs Type 2 vs Type 6 tradeoff using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 20: Type 1 vs Type 2 vs Type 6 tradeoff

Model answer

Type 1 overwrites (no history), Type 2 adds versioned rows (full history), Type 6 combines both: Type 2 history plus a Type 1 'current value' column on every row for easy current-vs-historical reporting. Choose by whether history is needed and how consumers query it.

  • Type 1: simplest, smallest, but loses the past — fine for correcting typos.
  • Type 2: full as-of history; required for pricing/attribution by service date; larger and needs careful keys.
  • Type 6: Type 2 + current attribute columns so you can report current and historical without re-joining.
  • Decide per attribute, not per table — mix Type 1 and Type 2 columns in one dimension.

Healthcare angle: Member plan/network status needs Type 2 (as-of pricing); a corrected misspelled name needs only Type 1.

-- Type 6: historical row keeps version values + current_* columns updated everywhere
-- valid_from, valid_to, is_current, specialty (historical), current_specialty (T1 overwrite)

What strong answers cover

  1. Type 1 overwrites (no history); Type 2 versions (full history); Type 6 combines (current + historical attributes on each row).
  2. Choose by whether you need as-of history (Type 2) vs only current (Type 1).
  3. Type 6 eases 'current value' queries without a self-join.
  4. More history = more storage/complexity.
  5. Match the type to the query need.

Starter pattern

-- Type 1: UPDATE in place | Type 2: close+insert versions
-- Type 6: row carries both current_value and historical valid_from/to
Common mistake: Using Type 1 where as-of history is needed (e.g. provider network status), losing the ability to price historically.
Principal follow-up: When would you choose Type 6 over Type 2 for a member or provider dimension?
021
Solve Mock round 21: query regression from 2 minutes to 40 minutes using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 21: query regression from 2 minutes to 40 minutes

Model answer

Treat it as an investigation: capture the slow plan, diff it against the fast baseline, and isolate what changed — data volume/skew, stale stats, a plan flip (hash→nested loop, broadcast lost), partition pruning lost, or a new predicate. Fix the cause, then add a guard so it can't silently regress again.

  • Pull the actual execution plan and compare row estimates vs actuals to spot cardinality misestimates.
  • Check for stale statistics, a recently changed predicate/cast that defeats pruning, or data growth/skew.
  • Confirm whether a join strategy flipped or a broadcast/index stopped being used.
  • Prevent: refresh stats, add the missing filter/partition column, pin or hint only as a last resort, and add a runtime alert.

Healthcare angle: Month-end claim volume spikes routinely flip plans; the fix is often restoring pruning on service_month plus refreshed stats.

-- capture and compare
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT ...;   -- look for estimate vs actual gaps and lost pruning

What strong answers cover

  1. Compare the new plan to the prior good one first.
  2. Common causes: stale stats, lost partition pruning (function/cast on the column), a join-strategy flip, volume/skew change.
  3. Refresh stats; restore sargable predicates; verify pruning/join.
  4. Add a runtime regression alert.
  5. Confirm the fix in the plan.

Starter pattern

-- check partitions scanned vs total; refresh stats
-- restore: WHERE service_month BETWEEN ... (not a function on it)
Common mistake: Assuming 'more data' and adding compute, when it's actually stale stats or a lost pruning predicate.
Principal follow-up: Walk through diagnosing a 20x regression purely from the execution plan.
022
Solve Mock round 22: explain plan bottom-up analysis using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 22: explain plan bottom-up analysis

Model answer

Read execution plans from the most expensive leaf upward: find the operator consuming the most time/rows/bytes, confirm whether its row estimates match reality, and trace why (scan vs seek, join type, spill, pruning). The bottleneck is usually one operator, not the whole tree.

  • Sort plan operators by actual time/rows, not by position in the tree.
  • Big estimate-vs-actual gaps point to stale stats or bad cardinality assumptions.
  • Full scans where you expected pruning/index seeks reveal predicate or partitioning problems.
  • Spills, exchanges/shuffles, and repartitions flag memory and skew issues.

Healthcare angle: Claims fact scans that lose service_month pruning are the classic culprit visible at the plan's leaf.

EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) SELECT ...;  -- or Snowflake Query Profile / Spark UI SQL tab

What strong answers cover

  1. Read the plan to find the dominant operator, rows in/out, and whether pruning/joins behaved.
  2. Look for full scans where pruning was expected, row explosions between operators, and spill.
  3. Bottom-up: the deepest expensive operator usually drives runtime.
  4. Map operators back to query clauses.
  5. Use it to target one fix.

Starter pattern

EXPLAIN <query>; -- find: partitions scanned vs total, join type,
-- estimated vs actual rows (skew/stats), spill
Common mistake: Reading only the top of the plan and missing the deep operator (a full scan or exploding join) that drives cost.
Principal follow-up: What signals in a plan tell you pruning failed versus a join exploded versus stats were wrong?
023
Solve Mock round 23: stale statistics and cardinality using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 23: stale statistics and cardinality

Model answer

The optimizer chooses plans from statistics; when stats lag a big load, it misestimates row counts and picks bad join orders or strategies. The tell is a large gap between estimated and actual rows. Refresh stats (or let auto-stats run) and the plan corrects.

  • Symptom: estimated rows wildly off from actual, leading to nested loops on big inputs or wrong build side.
  • Fix: ANALYZE/refresh stats after large DML; ensure auto-stats thresholds suit your load size.
  • For skewed columns, histograms matter more than row counts.
  • Don't paper over with hints first — fix stats, then hint only if the optimizer still errs.

Healthcare angle: After a nightly multi-million-row claims load, stale stats are the most common cause of a sudden plan regression.

ANALYZE TABLE claims COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS service_month, provider_npi;

What strong answers cover

  1. Stale stats make the optimizer mis-estimate cardinality, choosing bad join orders/strategies.
  2. Symptom: estimated rows wildly differ from actual.
  3. Fix by refreshing stats; maintain them on volatile tables.
  4. Cardinality misestimates often cause the wrong join (broadcast vs shuffle).
  5. Validate estimated vs actual after refresh.

Starter pattern

ANALYZE TABLE claims COMPUTE STATISTICS;  -- refresh
-- compare estimated vs actual rows in the plan
Common mistake: Never refreshing stats after big loads, so the optimizer keeps picking plans tuned for stale row counts.
Principal follow-up: How do stale statistics lead to a broadcast-vs-shuffle join mistake, and how do you detect it?
024
Solve Mock round 24: join order regression using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 24: join order regression

Model answer

A join-order regression happens when the optimizer changes which tables join first/which is the build side, usually after stats or volume changes. Diagnose by comparing plans; fix by correcting stats, and only if necessary use a join order/leading hint as a controlled, documented override.

  • Compare old vs new plan: which table became the probe vs build side?
  • Root cause is usually cardinality misestimation from stale/missing stats.
  • Restoring stats often restores order without hints.
  • If hinting, encapsulate it and add a comment + test so it's revisited.

Healthcare angle: Joining a small provider dimension to a huge claims fact should keep the dimension as the build/broadcast side; a regression flips this and explodes runtime.

-- after fixing stats, last resort:
SELECT /*+ LEADING(d c) USE_HASH(c) */ ... FROM provider_dim d JOIN claims c ...;

What strong answers cover

  1. The optimizer's join order depends on stats/cardinality; a bad estimate flips it to a costly order.
  2. Symptom: a huge intermediate result from joining big tables first.
  3. Fix stats; sometimes hint/restructure to filter early.
  4. Compare to the prior good plan.
  5. Validate intermediate row counts.

Starter pattern

-- refresh stats so the optimizer joins the most selective first
-- or restructure to filter/aggregate before the big join
Common mistake: Forcing a join order with hints as a permanent fix instead of fixing the stats that caused the bad estimate.
Principal follow-up: When is a join hint justified versus fixing the underlying cardinality estimate?
025
Solve Mock round 25: predicate pushdown failure using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
MediumMock round 25: predicate pushdown failure

Model answer

Pushdown means filters execute at the scan/storage layer so less data is read. It fails when a predicate is non-sargable — wrapped in a function, implicitly cast, or applied after a non-deterministic step — forcing a full read then a late filter. Rewrite the predicate to be column-direct and typed.

  • Avoid functions on the filtered column (WHERE func(col)=x); compute the constant side instead.
  • Match data types to prevent implicit casts that block pushdown.
  • Filter on partition/clustering columns directly, before joins, where possible.
  • Confirm via the plan that the filter shows at the scan, not above it.

Healthcare angle: Filtering claims by CAST(service_dt AS DATE) or applying TO_CHAR on a partition column quietly disables pruning and reads the whole fact.

-- bad: WHERE TO_CHAR(service_dt,'YYYYMM') = '202406'
-- good: WHERE service_dt >= '2024-06-01' AND service_dt < '2024-07-01'

What strong answers cover

  1. Pushdown filters at the scan to read less; it fails when a function/cast wraps the column, a type mismatch, or the predicate sits above a non-pushable operator.
  2. Symptom: full scan despite a selective filter.
  3. Fix by making predicates sargable and matching types.
  4. Confirm in the plan that the filter pushed to the scan.
  5. Validate rows scanned dropped.

Starter pattern

-- bad: WHERE CAST(service_dt AS STRING) LIKE '2025%'
-- good: WHERE service_dt >= '2025-01-01' AND service_dt < '2026-01-01'
Common mistake: Wrapping the filter column in a function/cast so the predicate can't push down and the engine scans everything.
Principal follow-up: Why does a cast on the filter column block pushdown, and how do you rewrite to restore it?
026
Solve Mock round 26: partition pruning failure using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 26: partition pruning failure

Model answer

Pruning skips partitions/micro-partitions whose range can't match the filter. It fails when the filter doesn't reference the partition key directly, uses a function/cast on it, or the key isn't correlated with the data layout. Restore by filtering the raw partition column with sargable predicates.

  • Filter the actual partition column (service_month/service_dt) with literal ranges.
  • Functions/casts on the partition column defeat pruning.
  • Joins that derive the filter at runtime may need dynamic partition pruning to be enabled/effective.
  • Verify partitions scanned in the plan dropped as expected.

Healthcare angle: Claims facts partitioned by service_month must be filtered on service_month; filtering on a derived period column reads everything.

WHERE service_month = 202406        -- prunes
-- not: WHERE LEFT(CAST(service_dt AS STRING),7)='2024-06'  -- scans all

What strong answers cover

  1. Pruning skips partitions via min/max metadata; it fails with a function/cast on the partition column, type mismatch, or a join-derived filter not pushing down.
  2. Symptom: partitions scanned = total.
  3. Fix with literal sargable ranges on the raw partition column; enable dynamic partition pruning.
  4. Confirm partitions scanned dropped.
  5. Standardize sargable queries.

Starter pattern

-- good: WHERE service_month BETWEEN '2025-01-01' AND '2025-01-31'
SET spark.sql.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled=true;
Common mistake: Filtering on a derived/expression of the partition column, scanning the whole fact instead of one partition.
Principal follow-up: How does dynamic partition pruning help when the filter comes from a joined dimension?
027
Solve Mock round 27: correlated subquery rewrite using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 27: correlated subquery rewrite

Model answer

Correlated subqueries re-execute per outer row and can be quadratic. Rewrite as a JOIN, a window function, or an aggregated derived table so the work happens once set-based. Verify the rewrite preserves NULL and cardinality semantics.

  • EXISTS-style correlations → semi-join; per-row aggregates → window function or pre-aggregated join.
  • Watch NULL semantics when converting NOT IN/NOT EXISTS to anti-joins.
  • Confirm the rewrite doesn't change row multiplicity (fan-out).
  • Check the plan changed from per-row execution to a single hash/merge step.

Healthcare angle: 'Latest paid amount per claim via correlated subquery' rewrites cleanly to a window function and runs an order of magnitude faster on claims volumes.

-- correlated MAX per claim → window
SELECT claim_id, paid_amt FROM (
  SELECT claim_id, paid_amt, MAX(ingest_ts) OVER (PARTITION BY claim_id) mx, ingest_ts
  FROM payments) WHERE ingest_ts = mx;

What strong answers cover

  1. Correlated subqueries can execute per-row; rewrite as a JOIN or window for set-based performance.
  2. EXISTS/IN correlated checks often become semi/anti-joins.
  3. Per-group aggregates become a window or a grouped join.
  4. Validate result equivalence.
  5. Check the plan improved.

Starter pattern

-- slow correlated:
-- SELECT ... WHERE paid > (SELECT AVG(paid) FROM c c2 WHERE c2.member=c.member)
-- fast: window
SELECT * FROM (SELECT *, AVG(paid) OVER (PARTITION BY member) avg_p FROM c) WHERE paid>avg_p;
Common mistake: Leaving a correlated subquery that runs per row over a large table when a window would do it in one pass.
Principal follow-up: How do you rewrite a per-member correlated average filter as a window, and why is it faster?
028
Solve Mock round 28: NOT EXISTS vs NOT IN vs anti join using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 28: NOT EXISTS vs NOT IN vs anti join

Model answer

They look equivalent but differ on NULLs and performance. NOT IN returns no rows if the subquery yields any NULL (three-valued logic); NOT EXISTS and LEFT-anti-join are NULL-safe and usually optimize better. Prefer NOT EXISTS / anti-join for set difference.

  • NOT IN + a NULL in the list = empty result; a classic silent-bug.
  • NOT EXISTS is NULL-safe and typically plans as an anti-join.
  • LEFT JOIN ... WHERE right.key IS NULL is the explicit anti-join form.
  • Ensure the matching columns are non-null or handle nulls deliberately.

Healthcare angle: Finding claims with no matching remittance via NOT IN breaks the moment one remit key is NULL; NOT EXISTS keeps reconciliation correct.

SELECT c.* FROM claims c
WHERE NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM remit r WHERE r.claim_id=c.claim_id);

What strong answers cover

  1. NOT IN breaks on NULLs in the subquery (returns no rows); NOT EXISTS / anti-join are NULL-safe.
  2. Prefer NOT EXISTS or LEFT JOIN ... IS NULL (anti-join) for correctness and performance.
  3. Know the NULL semantics cold.
  4. Validate against a NULL-containing dataset.
  5. Check the plan uses an anti-join.

Starter pattern

-- NULL-safe anti-join
SELECT c.* FROM claims c
LEFT JOIN excluded e ON c.member=e.member
WHERE e.member IS NULL;
Common mistake: Using NOT IN with a subquery that can contain NULLs — a single NULL makes the whole predicate return nothing.
Principal follow-up: Why does one NULL break NOT IN, and how do NOT EXISTS and anti-joins avoid it?
029
Solve Mock round 29: spill and memory pressure using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 29: spill and memory pressure

Model answer

Spill happens when an operator (sort, hash join, aggregation) exceeds available memory and writes to disk, sharply slowing the query. Diagnose via plan/profile spill metrics; fix by reducing data per operator (earlier filters, better grain), fixing skew, or sizing compute up.

  • Identify the spilling operator and its input size in the profile.
  • Reduce input: push filters earlier, project fewer columns, pre-aggregate.
  • Skew concentrates rows on one node and causes local spill — address the skew, not just the size.
  • Only scale compute up after structural fixes; it's the costly lever.

Healthcare angle: A monster GROUP BY over a full claims year spills; pre-aggregating to member_month first removes the pressure.

-- Snowflake: 'Bytes spilled to local/remote storage' in Query Profile
-- Spark: spill metrics on the shuffle/aggregate stage in the SQL/Stages tab

What strong answers cover

  1. Spill happens when an operator (join/sort/aggregate) exceeds memory and writes to disk.
  2. Reduce input (filter/pre-aggregate), raise parallelism, fix skew.
  3. Spill is a pressure signal that can precede OOM.
  4. Find the spilling operator in the plan/profile.
  5. Validate spill dropped after the fix.

Starter pattern

-- pre-aggregate before the heavy join; increase parallelism
-- fix skew so no single task holds too much
Common mistake: Treating heavy spill as merely slow and ignoring it, until the same operator OOMs at higher volume.
Principal follow-up: What's your ordered checklist to reduce spill before reaching for more memory?
030
Solve Mock round 30: SQL skew diagnosis using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 30: SQL skew diagnosis

Model answer

Skew shows as one partition/reducer doing most of the work while others finish fast — a long-tail stage or a single slow node. Diagnose by profiling row counts per key/partition; fix with salting, isolating the heavy key, or a different join strategy.

  • Find the hot key: GROUP BY join key, COUNT(*), look for outliers (often UNKNOWN/0/default).
  • Salt the heavy key or broadcast the small side to spread work.
  • Filter/route default-key rows separately when they dominate.
  • Confirm even partition sizes after the fix.

Healthcare angle: A flood of claims with member_id='UNKNOWN' or provider_npi=0 creates one giant partition; isolating those keys fixes month-end skew.

SELECT join_key, COUNT(*) c FROM big GROUP BY join_key ORDER BY c DESC LIMIT 20;

What strong answers cover

  1. Skew = uneven data per group/partition (one straggler task); often a default/UNKNOWN key.
  2. Diagnose with per-key counts; the hot key dominates.
  3. Mitigate with salting, isolating the hot key, or engine skew handling (AQE).
  4. More compute doesn't fix a single hot key.
  5. Validate even task durations after.

Starter pattern

SELECT join_key, COUNT(*) c FROM fact GROUP BY join_key ORDER BY c DESC LIMIT 10;
-- isolate/salt the hot key; enable AQE skewJoin
Common mistake: Adding executors to fix skew — the single hot partition still bottlenecks regardless of cluster size.
Principal follow-up: Why doesn't adding compute fix skew, and how do salting and AQE actually resolve it?
031
Solve Mock round 31: FACETS vs 837 reconciliation using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 31: FACETS vs 837 reconciliation

Model answer

Reconcile the adjudication system (FACETS) against the inbound 837 source to prove no claims were lost or duplicated and amounts tie. Build a control table comparing counts and control totals by key/period, with tolerance thresholds and an exceptions list for breaks.

  • Match grain explicitly (header vs line) before comparing counts.
  • Compare both row counts and dollar control totals by service period.
  • Classify breaks: missing, extra, amount-mismatch; route each to an owner.
  • Store results in a reconciliation control table with run timestamps for trend.

Healthcare angle: FACETS/QNXT vs raw 837 reconciliation is a core payer integrity control; small dollar tolerances and clear exception buckets keep it actionable.

SELECT period, src.cnt src_cnt, fac.cnt fac_cnt, src.paid - fac.paid AS paid_break
FROM (SELECT period, COUNT(*) cnt, SUM(paid) paid FROM stg_837 GROUP BY period) src
FULL JOIN (SELECT period, COUNT(*) cnt, SUM(paid) paid FROM facets GROUP BY period) fac
  USING (period);

What strong answers cover

  1. Reconcile adjudicated (FACETS) claims against inbound 837 source by counts and dollars per period/key.
  2. Surface missing, extra, and amount-mismatched claims with an exceptions list.
  3. Tie to source file trailers.
  4. Run per period with tolerances.
  5. Persist results as a control.

Starter pattern

SELECT s.period, COUNT(*) src, COUNT(f.claim_id) loaded, SUM(s.amt)-SUM(f.amt) diff
FROM source_837 s LEFT JOIN facets f USING(claim_id) GROUP BY 1;
Common mistake: Reconciling only counts, not dollars (or vice versa), so an amount drift or an offsetting miss goes undetected.
Principal follow-up: Why reconcile both counts and dollars, and how do you localize where claims diverged?
032
Solve Mock round 32: row count mismatch using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 32: row count mismatch

Model answer

A count mismatch between source and target means rows were dropped, duplicated, or filtered. Reconcile at the same grain, by partition/period, and decompose the delta into missing vs extra rather than reporting a single net number.

  • Always compare at identical grain and after the same filters.
  • Break the delta down by period/source so you isolate where it happened.
  • List sample missing and extra keys — a net-zero delta can still hide offsetting errors.
  • Common causes: failed join (fan-out/loss), dedup over-collapse, late files, filter changes.

Healthcare angle: Claims dropped by a bad join or over-aggressive dedup show up here; per-period breakdown points to the offending load.

SELECT period, COUNT(*) FROM source GROUP BY period
EXCEPT
SELECT period, COUNT(*) FROM target GROUP BY period;

What strong answers cover

  1. Compare counts per stage/period to localize where rows were gained or lost.
  2. Causes: missing file, failed partition, bad join/filter, over-dedup.
  3. Reprocess the affected window from source.
  4. Assert counts as a gating control.
  5. Tie to source trailers.

Starter pattern

SELECT period, src_count, tgt_count, src_count - tgt_count AS delta
FROM recon WHERE src_count <> tgt_count;
Common mistake: Investigating a count mismatch by eyeballing totals instead of comparing per-period to pinpoint the stage.
Principal follow-up: How do per-stage, per-period counts localize exactly where rows were lost?
033
Solve Mock round 33: paid amount mismatch using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 33: paid amount mismatch

Model answer

Dollars must tie even when counts do. Compare SUM(paid) by period/key between source and target; a break usually comes from versioning (summing across adjustments), sign handling on reversals, rounding, or a join fan-out.

  • Check for double-counted claim versions and unhandled negative/reversal amounts.
  • Verify currency/rounding and that you SUM the net latest version only.
  • Decompose the dollar break by period and by claim type.
  • A small fixed tolerance handles legitimate rounding; anything above flags.

Healthcare angle: AR/paid trend breaks most often trace to a duplicated 835 or summing original+adjustment instead of the net latest.

SELECT period, SUM(paid) src - SUM(paid) tgt AS paid_break FROM ... GROUP BY period
HAVING ABS(SUM(paid) src - SUM(paid) tgt) > 0.01;

What strong answers cover

  1. Compare dollar control totals source-to-target by period/key.
  2. Causes: duplicate load, fan-out join, version double-count, dropped rows.
  3. Localize the offending stage; fix and restate.
  4. Pair with count reconciliation.
  5. Assert within tolerance.

Starter pattern

SELECT period, SUM(src_paid)-SUM(tgt_paid) AS diff
FROM recon GROUP BY period HAVING ABS(diff) > tolerance;
Common mistake: Chasing a paid mismatch without checking for a fan-out join or version double-count first.
Principal follow-up: What are the usual causes of a paid-total mismatch, and how do you tell them apart?
034
Solve Mock round 34: missing claims by service date using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 34: missing claims by service date

Model answer

Identify service-date windows where expected claims didn't land. Use an anti-join against the source or a volume-by-date baseline; a sudden zero/low day usually means a missing file or a partition that failed to load.

  • Anti-join source vs target keyed on claim_id for the window.
  • Compare daily volume to a trailing baseline to spot silent shortfalls.
  • Check for a missing/empty inbound file or a skipped partition.
  • Confirm timezone/service-date vs received-date alignment.

Healthcare angle: A skipped SFTP file for one service day shows as a volume cliff; baseline-by-date monitoring catches it before stakeholders do.

SELECT s.claim_id FROM source s
LEFT JOIN target t ON s.claim_id=t.claim_id
WHERE t.claim_id IS NULL AND s.service_dt BETWEEN :a AND :b;

What strong answers cover

  1. Detect gaps by comparing expected vs present claims per service date against a baseline/source.
  2. A date with far-below-baseline volume signals a missing file or failed partition.
  3. Use a calendar spine to make absent dates visible.
  4. Alert on volume cliffs.
  5. Reprocess the affected dates.

Starter pattern

SELECT d.dt, COUNT(c.claim_id) cnt
FROM calendar d LEFT JOIN claims c ON c.service_dt=d.dt
GROUP BY d.dt HAVING cnt < 0.7*baseline;
Common mistake: Not joining to a calendar spine, so a date with zero claims simply doesn't appear and the gap is invisible.
Principal follow-up: Why is a calendar spine essential to detect missing service dates?
035
Solve Mock round 35: duplicate claims by natural grain using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 35: duplicate claims by natural grain

Model answer

Define the natural grain (e.g. claim_id+line+version) and find keys with COUNT>1 that are true duplicates (identical payload), separating them from legitimate versions. Then dedup deterministically and add a uniqueness test.

  • GROUP BY the natural key HAVING COUNT(*)>1 to surface candidates.
  • Compare payload hash within the key to split true dupes from versions.
  • Collapse true dupes with QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER=1 and a tie-breaker.
  • Add a post-load uniqueness assertion so it fails loudly next time.

Healthcare angle: Resent files and replays create true duplicates; uniqueness-on-grain tests prevent inflated counts and payments.

SELECT claim_id, line, version, COUNT(*) FROM claims
GROUP BY 1,2,3 HAVING COUNT(*)>1;

What strong answers cover

  1. Define the natural grain (e.g. claim_id, line) and find groups with >1 row.
  2. Distinguish true duplicates from versions (frequency code 7/8).
  3. Dedup to the latest version; quarantine unexplained dupes.
  4. Validate paid totals before/after.
  5. Add a uniqueness assertion.

Starter pattern

SELECT claim_id, line_number, COUNT(*) FROM claims
GROUP BY 1,2 HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
Common mistake: Treating legitimate replacement versions as duplicates and deleting them, losing corrections.
Principal follow-up: How do you separate true duplicates from legitimate claim versions at the natural grain?
036
Solve Mock round 36: duplicate 835 remittance detection using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 36: duplicate 835 remittance detection

Model answer

Detect a reloaded 835 by tracking remittance identity (payer, check/EFT number, remit_id, production date) in a control table. If the identifier was already applied, reject or quarantine the file before it doubles payments.

  • Maintain an applied-remittance registry keyed on payer + check/EFT + remit date.
  • Check the registry pre-load; duplicates are rejected, not merged.
  • If already loaded, reconcile and back out the duplicate application.
  • Idempotent MERGE on remittance identity is the backstop.

Healthcare angle: Duplicate 835 loads corrupt AR aging and paid totals; identity-based detection is the standard payer control.

SELECT remit_id, check_no, COUNT(*) FROM applied_remits
GROUP BY 1,2 HAVING COUNT(*)>1;

What strong answers cover

  1. Detect re-loaded remittances by remittance identity (payer + check/EFT + remit id).
  2. A repeated identity means the file double-loaded — corrupts AR/paid.
  3. Track identities in a control table; loads MERGE on it.
  4. Recover by backing out the second application.
  5. Reconcile AR after.

Starter pattern

SELECT remit_key, COUNT(*) FROM payments
GROUP BY remit_key HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;  -- duplicate 835
Common mistake: Detecting duplicates on claim_id alone, missing that the whole remittance file re-applied payments.
Principal follow-up: What fields uniquely identify a remittance, and how do you prevent a re-load from double-paying?
037
Solve Mock round 37: cross-source reconciliation control table using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 37: cross-source reconciliation control table

Model answer

A reconciliation control table is a durable record of each run's comparison: source vs target counts and totals by period/key, the break, the tolerance, pass/fail, and timestamp. It turns reconciliation from an ad-hoc query into a monitored, trendable control.

  • Columns: run_ts, period, source, src_cnt, tgt_cnt, src_amt, tgt_amt, break, tolerance, status.
  • Persist every run so you can trend breaks and prove controls to audit.
  • Drive alerts off status='FAIL' rather than re-running queries by hand.
  • Pair with an exceptions table listing the specific breaking keys.

Healthcare angle: Auditors and actuaries want evidence that claims/payments tie across systems every cycle; the control table is that evidence.

INSERT INTO recon_control
SELECT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, period, 'src->tgt', s_cnt, t_cnt, s_amt, t_amt,
       s_amt-t_amt, 0.01, CASE WHEN ABS(s_amt-t_amt)<=0.01 THEN 'PASS' ELSE 'FAIL' END
FROM recon_stage;

What strong answers cover

  1. A control table persists per-run, per-source counts/dollars and reconciliation status with exceptions.
  2. It localizes loss/duplication and provides audit evidence.
  3. Assert source-to-target parity each run.
  4. Feed it into observability/alerts.
  5. Keep history of control totals.

Starter pattern

INSERT INTO recon_control(run_id, source, period, count, sum_paid, status)
SELECT ... ;  -- assert vs target; flag exceptions
Common mistake: Doing reconciliation ad-hoc per pipeline with no persisted control table, so there's no audit trail or trend.
Principal follow-up: What would you store in a reusable reconciliation control table, and how does it serve audits?
038
Solve Mock round 38: tolerance threshold design using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 38: tolerance threshold design

Model answer

Tolerances separate immaterial noise (rounding, timing) from real breaks. Set them per metric and per materiality: a tight absolute dollar tolerance for payments, a small relative tolerance for high-volume counts, and zero tolerance for things that must be exact (member identity).

  • Use absolute tolerance for money (e.g. $0.01–$1), relative for large counts.
  • Calibrate from historical break distributions, not guesses.
  • Document why each threshold exists; review periodically.
  • Anything beyond tolerance pages an owner with the breaking keys.

Healthcare angle: Penny rounding across millions of claim lines is expected; a $0.01 tolerance avoids alert fatigue while still catching material breaks.

What strong answers cover

  1. Define acceptable variance (absolute and/or percentage) so reconciliation flags meaningful drift, not rounding noise.
  2. Set thresholds per metric/source from historical variance.
  3. Too tight = noise; too loose = misses real loss.
  4. Escalate beyond tolerance.
  5. Review thresholds over time.

Starter pattern

flag WHERE ABS(src - tgt) > GREATEST(abs_tol, pct_tol * src)
-- tune from historical variance
Common mistake: Using a single fixed tolerance everywhere, causing either alert noise or missed real discrepancies.
Principal follow-up: How would you set reconciliation tolerances so they catch real loss without crying wolf?
039
Solve Mock round 39: incremental reconciliation using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 39: incremental reconciliation

Model answer

Reconcile only the changed window (today's load / late-arriving range) instead of the whole history each run. Track a high-water mark, reconcile the delta, and periodically run a full reconciliation to catch drift.

  • Bound reconciliation by load date / watermark for speed.
  • Account for late-arriving and corrected records that reopen prior periods.
  • Schedule periodic full reconciliations to catch silent drift incremental runs miss.
  • Store results per window in the control table.

Healthcare angle: Reconciling a 10-year claims history nightly is wasteful; incremental-by-load-window plus a monthly full pass balances cost and assurance.

What strong answers cover

  1. Reconcile only the changed window each run (incremental), with a periodic full reconciliation as a backstop.
  2. Cheaper than full each time; catches drift quickly.
  3. Track watermarks for what's reconciled.
  4. Combine incremental + periodic full.
  5. Validate the incremental matches a full run.

Starter pattern

-- reconcile only [last_watermark, now]; full recon weekly
assert src vs tgt for the incremental window
Common mistake: Relying only on incremental reconciliation, so a slow cumulative drift never gets caught without a periodic full check.
Principal follow-up: Why pair incremental reconciliation with a periodic full reconciliation?
040
Solve Mock round 40: daily control totals using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 40: daily control totals

Model answer

Capture per-day control totals (row counts and key dollar sums) at each pipeline stage and assert they match across stages and against the source trailer. Breaks point precisely to the stage where data was gained or lost.

  • Record counts/sums at ingest, silver, and gold for each day.
  • Compare to the inbound file trailer/control record when present.
  • Stage-to-stage equality (allowing intended filters) localizes the defect.
  • Persist daily so you can trend and prove lineage of numbers.

Healthcare angle: EDI files carry trailer control counts/amounts; tying loaded totals to the trailer is a basic but powerful claims integrity check.

What strong answers cover

  1. Capture daily counts and key dollar sums per source/stage and assert they tie.
  2. A daily cadence localizes issues to a day, not a quarter.
  3. Tie to source file trailers.
  4. Alert on breaks.
  5. Persist for trend/audit.

Starter pattern

SELECT load_date, COUNT(*), SUM(paid) FROM claims GROUP BY load_date;
-- assert vs source trailer; alert on break
Common mistake: Only reconciling at month-end, so a daily loss isn't found until totals are far off and hard to trace.
Principal follow-up: Why do daily control totals make incident triage far faster than monthly reconciliation?
041
Solve Mock round 41: billion-row fact table grain using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 41: billion-row fact table grain

Model answer

Pick the lowest grain consumers actually need and can be made unique. For claims that's usually the claim line (claim_id+line) for the latest version; declare it, key it, and enforce it. Grain mistakes are the most expensive modeling errors at billion-row scale.

  • State grain as a one-row-per sentence and back it with a uniqueness test.
  • Partition/cluster by the dominant filter (service_month) and a high-cardinality secondary key.
  • Keep facts additive; push slowly-changing context to dimensions.
  • Pre-aggregate common rollups (member-month) rather than scanning the base each time.

Healthcare angle: Claim line vs header grain determines whether SUM(paid) is correct; mixing them double-counts at the header or loses line detail.

What strong answers cover

  1. Declare the grain in one sentence and enforce it (uniqueness assertion).
  2. Partition/cluster by the common filter (e.g. service_month) for pruning.
  3. Keep the fact additive; push context to dimensions.
  4. Plan for incremental loads and skew at this scale.
  5. Validate grain after each load.

Starter pattern

-- grain: one row per claim line per version
-- partition by service_month; cluster/ZORDER by member_id
Common mistake: Leaving the grain ambiguous so header/line or versions mix, double-counting paid at billion-row scale.
Principal follow-up: How does the grain decision drive partitioning, clustering, and incremental strategy at a billion rows?
042
Solve Mock round 42: snapshot consistency using code or SQL and explain correctness, performance, and edge cases in a healthcare data pipeline.
AdvancedMock round 42: snapshot consistency

Model answer

A snapshot must reflect a single, well-defined point in time across all sources, even though they load at different times. Pin an as-of timestamp/effective date, build from versions valid at that point, and avoid mixing partially-loaded sources.

  • Define the as-of moment and select each table's version valid at it.
