Common mistake: Computing adherence on raw NDC or mishandling overlapping fills, corrupting a Stars measure.
Principal follow-up: How do you handle overlapping fills, switches, and reversals in a PDC adherence platform?
040
Design or evaluate a solution for payment integrity analytics. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedpayment integrity analytics

Model answer

Build payment-integrity analytics on accurate, deduplicated, reconciled claims: detect duplicates, overpayments, coding errors, and improper payments via rules and analytics, pre- and post-pay, with recovery tracking. It protects plan dollars, so data correctness (no duplicates, version-aware) is the foundation, and findings must be explainable for recovery.

  • Detect duplicates/overpayments/coding/improper payments.
  • Pre- and post-pay + recovery tracking.
  • Foundation: deduplicated, reconciled, version-aware claims.
  • Explainable findings for recovery.

Healthcare angle: Payment integrity protects plan dollars; it only works on deduplicated, version-aware claims data with explainable findings.

What strong answers cover

  1. Build on accurate, deduplicated, reconciled, version-aware claims: detect duplicates, overpayments, coding errors, improper payments.
  2. Pre- and post-pay, with recovery tracking; explainable findings.
  3. Data correctness is the foundation; protects plan dollars.
  4. False positives waste recovery effort.
  5. Reconcile before analyzing.

Starter pattern

flag duplicate/overpay/upcoding on deduplicated version-aware claims
explainable findings -> recovery queue
Common mistake: Running payment-integrity on non-deduplicated, version-unaware claims, generating false positives and missing real ones.
Principal follow-up: What data-quality foundations must exist before payment-integrity analytics are trustworthy?
041
Design or evaluate a solution for FWA detection lakehouse. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
AdvancedFWA detection lakehouse

Model answer

Build FWA detection on clean, complete claims with risk-adjusted peer baselines: anomaly detection, peer comparison, and rules (impossible volumes, upcoding patterns), feeding prioritized investigation queues while controlling false positives. Explainability and data quality are critical since outputs drive investigations with real consequences.

  • Anomaly detection + peer comparison + rules.
  • Risk-adjusted baselines; control false positives.
  • Prioritized investigation queues.
  • Explainability + data quality critical.

Healthcare angle: FWA analytics flag outlier providers (improbable volumes/upcoding) for investigation — needing clean data and explainable, low-false-positive outputs.

What strong answers cover

  1. Build FWA on clean, complete claims with risk-adjusted peer baselines: anomaly detection, peer comparison, rules.
  2. Prioritized investigation queues; control false positives; explainability.
  3. Outputs drive investigations with real consequences, so data quality and explainability are critical.
  4. Risk-adjust baselines for fairness.
  5. Tune to keep false positives low.

Starter pattern

flag where metric >> risk_adjusted_peer_baseline; explain the flag
prioritized investigation queue; control false positives
Common mistake: FWA flags without risk-adjusted baselines or explainability, generating false accusations and wasting investigators.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep FWA false positives low while catching real outliers, and why does explainability matter?
042
Design or evaluate a solution for feature store governance. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedfeature store governance

Model answer

Govern features as shared, reusable assets: point-in-time-correct retrieval (no leakage), online/offline consistency, versioning, lineage, drift monitoring, and PHI controls, served from certified gold data. Governance makes ML features trustworthy, reproducible, and compliant — and prevents training-serving skew across risk/quality models.

  • Point-in-time correct + online/offline consistent.
  • Versioning + lineage + drift monitoring.
  • PHI controls; served from certified gold.
  • Prevents leakage + training-serving skew.

Healthcare angle: Shared, governed member-risk features (point-in-time correct) keep models reproducible, compliant, and skew-free.

What strong answers cover

  1. Govern features as shared, reusable assets: point-in-time-correct retrieval (no leakage), online/offline consistency.
  2. Versioning, lineage, drift monitoring, PHI controls; served from certified gold data.
  3. Makes ML features trustworthy, reproducible, compliant; prevents training-serving skew.
  4. One definition, both paths.
  5. Trace prediction to source.

Starter pattern

feature store: point-in-time correct + online/offline consistent
versioned + lineage + drift monitoring + PHI controls
Common mistake: Ad-hoc features computed differently in training and serving, causing leakage and skew that can't be traced.
Principal follow-up: How does feature-store governance prevent leakage and training-serving skew for risk models?
043
Design or evaluate a solution for AI readiness for payer data. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
AdvancedAI readiness for payer data

Model answer

Make payer data AI-ready: high-quality, well-governed, documented, lineage-tracked data with PHI controls, a feature store, and clear definitions — because models are only as good as the data. Prioritize data foundations (quality, governance, reproducibility) over rushing models, and build fairness/monitoring in from the start given healthcare stakes.

  • AI-ready = quality + governance + lineage + PHI controls.
  • Feature store + clear definitions.
  • Foundations before models.
  • Fairness/monitoring built in.

Healthcare angle: Payer AI readiness is mostly data foundations (quality, governance, reproducibility, fairness) — not model novelty.

What strong answers cover

  1. Make payer data AI-ready: high-quality, well-governed, documented, lineage-tracked data with PHI controls, a feature store, clear definitions.
  2. Models are only as good as the data; prioritize foundations over rushing models.
  3. Build fairness/monitoring in from the start given healthcare stakes.
  4. Foundations first.
  5. Reproducibility and governance.

Starter pattern

AI-ready = quality + governance + lineage + PHI controls + feature store
foundations before models; fairness/monitoring built in
Common mistake: Rushing to build models on ungoverned, poor-quality data, then fighting unexplainable, non-reproducible, possibly-biased results.
Principal follow-up: What data foundations make a payer 'AI-ready', and why do they matter more than the model?
044
Design or evaluate a solution for vector search over policy docs. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedvector search over policy docs

Model answer

For semantic search/RAG over policy/clinical documents, build a governed pipeline: chunk and embed documents, store vectors, retrieve by similarity, and ground generation in retrieved text. Govern source quality, access (PHI-aware), freshness, and citation/traceability. The value is grounded, auditable retrieval — not ungrounded generation.

  • Chunk + embed + vector store + similarity retrieval.
  • Ground generation in retrieved text (RAG).
  • Govern source quality/access/freshness.
  • Citations/traceability; PHI-aware.

Healthcare angle: RAG over plan/clinical policy docs must be grounded and citable, with PHI-aware access — auditable retrieval over free generation.

What strong answers cover

  1. For RAG over policy/clinical docs: chunk and embed, store vectors, retrieve by similarity, ground generation in retrieved text.
  2. Govern source quality, PHI-aware access, freshness, citation/traceability.
  3. The value is grounded, auditable retrieval — not ungrounded generation.
  4. Cite sources; respect PHI access.
  5. Keep retrieval fresh.

Starter pattern

chunk + embed -> vector store -> similarity retrieve -> ground+cite
PHI-aware access; freshness; traceability
Common mistake: Letting the model generate ungrounded answers (no citations/retrieval), risking hallucination and PHI leakage.
Principal follow-up: How do you make RAG over plan/clinical policy docs grounded, citable, and PHI-aware?
045
Design or evaluate a solution for LLM over healthcare data risk. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
AdvancedLLM over healthcare data risk

Model answer

Treat LLMs over healthcare data with caution: risks include hallucination, PHI leakage, bias, and lack of auditability. Mitigate with grounding/RAG, strict PHI controls and access governance, human-in-the-loop for consequential outputs, output validation, and clear scope (assistive, not autonomous clinical/payment decisions). Govern prompts and monitor outputs.

  • Risks: hallucination, PHI leakage, bias, auditability.
  • Mitigate: grounding/RAG + PHI controls + human-in-the-loop.
  • Output validation + clear assistive scope.
  • Prompt governance + output monitoring.

Healthcare angle: LLMs on payer data need grounding, PHI controls, and human-in-the-loop — never autonomous payment/clinical decisions.

What strong answers cover

  1. Treat LLMs over healthcare data with caution: risks are hallucination, PHI leakage, bias, lack of auditability.
  2. Mitigate with grounding/RAG, strict PHI controls and access governance, human-in-the-loop for consequential outputs, output validation, clear assistive scope.
  3. Govern prompts and monitor outputs.
  4. Never autonomous clinical/payment decisions.
  5. Validate and log.

Starter pattern

ground (RAG) + PHI controls + human-in-the-loop + output validation
assistive scope only; prompt governance + monitoring
Common mistake: Deploying an LLM as an autonomous decision-maker over PHI with no grounding, validation, or human review.
Principal follow-up: What are the top risks of LLMs over payer data, and what guardrails make their use safe?
046
Design or evaluate a solution for synthetic healthcare data lab. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedsynthetic healthcare data lab

Model answer

Provide a synthetic/de-identified data lab so teams develop and test without PHI exposure: generate realistic synthetic claims/members or use governed de-identified data, refresh it, and keep it isolated from production identifiers. It accelerates development and lowers compliance risk, while ensuring synthetic data is realistic enough to be useful.

  • Synthetic or governed de-identified data for dev/test.
  • Realistic but PHI-free; refreshed; isolated.
  • Accelerates development, lowers compliance risk.
  • Ensure realism for usefulness.

Healthcare angle: A synthetic/de-identified claims lab lets engineers build and test safely without touching member PHI.

What strong answers cover

  1. Provide a synthetic/de-identified data lab so teams develop/test without PHI exposure.
  2. Generate realistic synthetic claims/members or use governed de-identified data; refresh; isolate from production identifiers.
  3. Accelerates development, lowers compliance risk; ensure synthetic data is realistic enough to be useful.
  4. Keep it isolated from prod PHI.
  5. Balance realism and safety.

Starter pattern

synthetic/de-identified claims+members for dev/test; refreshed; isolated
realistic enough to be useful; no prod PHI
Common mistake: Using real PHI in dev/test 'because synthetic isn't realistic enough', expanding exposure surface.
Principal follow-up: How would you build a dev/test data lab that's realistic yet PHI-safe?
047
Design or evaluate a solution for de-identification strategy. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedde-identification strategy

Model answer

Define a governed de-identification strategy: HIPAA Safe Harbor (remove 18 identifiers) or Expert Determination (statistical), producing documented de-identified datasets for broad analytics while restricting the identified source. Treat it as a controlled transformation with method documentation and re-identification-risk awareness, enabling analytics with minimized PHI.

  • Safe Harbor or Expert Determination.
  • Documented de-identified datasets for broad use.
  • Restrict identified source; manage re-id risk.
  • Controlled, documented transformation.

Healthcare angle: A Safe Harbor de-identified claims dataset enables analytics/ML with minimized PHI risk and documented method.

What strong answers cover

  1. Define a governed strategy: HIPAA Safe Harbor (remove 18 identifiers) or Expert Determination (statistical).
  2. Produce documented de-identified datasets for broad analytics while restricting the identified source.
  3. Controlled transformation with method documentation and re-identification-risk awareness.
  4. Restrict the identified source tightly.
  5. Manage quasi-identifier risk.

Starter pattern

Safe Harbor (drop 18 identifiers) or Expert Determination
document method; manage re-id risk; restrict identified source
Common mistake: Incomplete de-identification leaving quasi-identifiers (rare zip+DOB), allowing re-identification — a compliance failure.
Principal follow-up: When do you use Safe Harbor vs Expert Determination, and what re-identification risks remain?
048
Design or evaluate a solution for audit evidence automation. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedaudit evidence automation

Model answer

Automate audit evidence so it's a byproduct, not a scramble: persist access history, lineage, control-total reconciliation, DQ results, and change records continuously, and generate audit reports on demand. Audit-ready-by-design means a HIPAA/financial audit is answered from existing artifacts rather than a fire drill.

  • Persist access history/lineage/reconciliation/DQ/change records.
  • Generate audit reports on demand.
  • Audit-ready by design, not after the fact.
  • Answers audits from existing artifacts.

Healthcare angle: Automating access/lineage/reconciliation evidence means a HIPAA audit on claims is answered instantly, not via a scramble.

What strong answers cover

  1. Automate audit evidence so it's a byproduct: persist access history, lineage, control-total reconciliation, DQ results, change records continuously.
  2. Generate audit reports on demand.
  3. Audit-ready-by-design answers a HIPAA/financial audit from existing artifacts, not a fire drill.
  4. Persist, don't reconstruct.
  5. Cover access, integrity, lineage.

Starter pattern

persist access_history + lineage + reconciliation + DQ + change records
generate audit report on demand
Common mistake: Reconstructing audit evidence after the fact because controls weren't persisted — a slow, error-prone scramble.
Principal follow-up: How do you make a HIPAA audit a query against existing artifacts rather than a fire drill?
049
Design or evaluate a solution for regulatory reporting SLA. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedregulatory reporting SLA

Model answer

Treat regulatory reports (e.g. encounter submissions, transparency files) as compliance-grade SLAs: design backward from the regulatory deadline accounting for upstream latency, with reconciliation/accuracy controls, predictive breach alerting, and rollback-safe publishing. Late or wrong regulatory data carries penalties, so correctness and timeliness are both non-negotiable.

  • Design backward from the regulatory deadline.
  • Reconciliation/accuracy controls + predictive alerts.
  • Rollback-safe publishing.
  • Correctness + timeliness non-negotiable.

Healthcare angle: Encounter/transparency regulatory reporting needs deadline-driven design with accuracy controls — penalties make both correctness and timeliness mandatory.

What strong answers cover

  1. Treat regulatory reports (encounter submissions, transparency files) as compliance-grade SLAs.
  2. Design backward from the regulatory deadline accounting for upstream latency, with reconciliation/accuracy controls.
  3. Predictive breach alerting and rollback-safe publishing; late or wrong data carries penalties.
  4. Correctness and timeliness both non-negotiable.
  5. Reconcile before submission.

Starter pattern

design backward from regulatory deadline (account upstream latency)
reconciliation/accuracy controls + predictive alert + rollback-safe
Common mistake: Treating a regulatory submission like a best-effort report, risking penalties from lateness or inaccuracy.
Principal follow-up: How do you design an encounter/transparency regulatory submission so deadlines and accuracy are guaranteed?
050
Design or evaluate a solution for plan year rollover. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedplan year rollover

Model answer

Handle plan-year rollover as a known annual event: reset accumulators (deductible/OOP) at the boundary, apply new benefit/plan configurations effective-dated, ensure member-month and measurement-window logic respects plan-year boundaries, and test before January. A clean date dimension with plan-year attributes drives correct rollover behavior.

  • Reset accumulators at the plan-year boundary.
  • Effective-dated new benefit/plan config.
  • Plan-year-aware member-months/measurement windows.
  • Test before the rollover date.

Healthcare angle: Deductible/OOP accumulators must reset at plan-year start and benefits switch effective-dated — driven by a plan-year-aware date dimension.

What strong answers cover

  1. Handle plan-year rollover as a known annual event: reset accumulators (deductible/OOP) at the boundary.
  2. Apply new benefit/plan configurations effective-dated; member-month and measurement-window logic respects plan-year boundaries.
  3. A clean date dimension with plan-year attributes drives correct rollover; test before January.
  4. Don't carry balances across years.
  5. Validate the rollover ahead of time.

Starter pattern

reset accumulators at plan_year boundary; effective-date new benefits
date_dim plan_year attributes drive resets; test pre-rollover
Common mistake: Not resetting accumulators at the plan-year boundary, carrying deductible/OOP balances into the new year incorrectly.
Principal follow-up: What breaks at plan-year rollover if accumulators and benefits aren't handled, and how do you test it beforehand?
051
Design or evaluate a solution for calendar spine enterprise standard. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedcalendar spine enterprise standard

Model answer

Standardize one enterprise calendar/date dimension (dense, with plan-year/fiscal/holiday attributes) that all facts join to, so time logic, rolling windows, zero-fill, member-months, and measurement windows are consistent everywhere. A single conformed spine eliminates the subtle time-logic discrepancies that cause cross-report disputes.

  • One dense, conformed enterprise calendar.
  • Plan-year/fiscal/holiday attributes.
  • Consistent rolling windows/zero-fill/member-months.
  • Eliminates cross-report time discrepancies.

Healthcare angle: A single calendar spine ensures PMPM, HEDIS windows, and trend all use identical time logic across the enterprise.

What strong answers cover

  1. Standardize one enterprise calendar/date dimension (dense, with plan-year/fiscal/holiday attributes) that all facts join to.
  2. Consistent time logic, rolling windows, zero-fill, member-months, measurement windows everywhere.
  3. A single conformed spine eliminates subtle time-logic discrepancies that cause cross-report disputes.
  4. One spine, not many.
  5. Conform across all marts.

Starter pattern

one dense date_dim (plan_year, fiscal, holiday) joined by all facts
zero-fill metrics; consistent rolling windows
Common mistake: Each mart building its own date logic, so PMPM, HEDIS windows, and trend use subtly different time logic and disagree.
Principal follow-up: Why is a single conformed calendar spine worth standardizing across the enterprise?
052
Design or evaluate a solution for reference data master strategy. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedreference data master strategy

Model answer

Master reference/code data (CARC/RARC, CPT, ICD, NDC, taxonomy, plan codes) centrally: versioned, effective-dated, owned, with consistent mapping across pipelines and detection/quarantine of unmapped or stale codes. Centralized reference mastering prevents the silent misclassification that fragments analytics when each pipeline maps codes its own way.

  • Central, versioned, effective-dated code sets.
  • Consistent mapping across all pipelines.
  • Detect/quarantine unmapped + stale codes.
  • Owned reference data.

Healthcare angle: Mastering CARC/CPT/NDC/plan codes centrally stops pipelines from mapping the same code differently and corrupting analytics.

What strong answers cover

  1. Master reference/code data (CARC/RARC, CPT, ICD, NDC, taxonomy, plan codes) centrally: versioned, effective-dated, owned.
  2. Consistent mapping across pipelines; detection/quarantine of unmapped/stale codes.
  3. Prevents silent misclassification when each pipeline maps codes its own way.
  4. One governed reference, reused everywhere.
  5. Alert on unmapped/stale codes.

Starter pattern

central versioned effective-dated code sets; consistent mapping
quarantine + alert unmapped/stale codes
Common mistake: Each pipeline maintaining its own copy of code mappings, so the same code is classified differently across analytics.
Principal follow-up: How does central reference mastering prevent the same code being mapped differently across pipelines?
053
Design or evaluate a solution for CARC/RARC mapping ownership. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
AdvancedCARC/RARC mapping ownership

Model answer

Own denial-code mapping as governed reference: CARC/RARC-to-bucket mappings versioned, reviewed, validated against the standard code set, with unmapped-code quarantine/alerting. Clear ownership keeps denial analytics consistent and correct, and changes (new codes, re-bucketing) flow through governance rather than ad-hoc edits.

  • Versioned, reviewed CARC/RARC→bucket mappings.
  • Validate against standard code set.
  • Quarantine/alert unmapped codes.
  • Governed changes, clear owner.

Healthcare angle: Owning denial-bucket mappings keeps root-cause analytics consistent; unmapped codes quarantine rather than silently misclassify.

What strong answers cover

  1. Own denial-code mapping as governed reference: CARC/RARC-to-bucket mappings versioned, reviewed, validated against the standard set.
  2. Unmapped-code quarantine/alerting; clear ownership keeps denial analytics consistent.
  3. Changes (new codes, re-bucketing) flow through governance, not ad-hoc edits.
  4. Validate against the standard code set.
  5. Quarantine unmapped codes.

Starter pattern

versioned reviewed CARC/RARC->bucket map; validate vs standard
quarantine unmapped; govern changes
Common mistake: Ad-hoc edits to denial-bucket mappings, so denial root-cause analytics quietly shift and disagree over time.
Principal follow-up: How do you govern CARC/RARC mappings so denial analytics stay consistent as new codes appear?
054
Design or evaluate a solution for source system migration. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedsource system migration

Model answer

Migrate source systems with parallel-run rigor: map old-to-new schema/grain/keys, run both in parallel, reconcile counts/dollars until sustained parity, get consumer sign-off, keep rollback, then cut over and decommission. Sequence by domain to limit blast radius. The reconciliation-until-parity discipline is what makes it low-risk.

  • Map schema/grain/keys old→new.
  • Parallel-run + reconcile until sustained parity.
  • Consumer sign-off + rollback; cut over by domain.
  • Decommission after parity.

Healthcare angle: Migrating a claims source system safely means parallel-running with daily reconciliation until numbers match for several cycles.

What strong answers cover

  1. Migrate with parallel-run rigor: map old-to-new schema/grain/keys, run both in parallel, reconcile until sustained parity.
  2. Consumer sign-off, keep rollback, cut over, decommission; sequence by domain to limit blast radius.
  3. The reconciliation-until-parity discipline makes it low-risk.
  4. Don't cut over before parity.
  5. Sequence by domain.

Starter pattern

map old->new (schema/grain/keys); parallel-run; reconcile to parity
sign-off + rollback; cut over by domain; decommission
Common mistake: Cutting over before sustained parity, then discovering the new system's numbers don't match and trust is lost.
Principal follow-up: How do you de-risk a source-system migration so consumers trust the new numbers?
055
Design or evaluate a solution for legacy SSIS to Databricks migration. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Advancedlegacy SSIS to Databricks migration

Model answer

Migrate SSIS to Databricks by re-architecting, not transliterating: inventory packages, map ETL logic to Spark/lakehouse patterns (idempotent, scalable), modernize (medallion, Delta), parallel-run with reconciliation, and decommission after parity. Prioritize the highest-value/most-fragile packages first and add the controls (idempotency/DQ) the legacy lacked.

  • Inventory packages; map logic to Spark/lakehouse.
  • Re-architect (medallion/Delta/idempotent), don't transliterate.
  • Parallel-run + reconcile; decommission after parity.
  • Add missing controls (idempotency/DQ).

Healthcare angle: Moving SSIS claims ETL to Databricks is a re-architecture to scalable idempotent patterns, validated by parallel-run reconciliation.

What strong answers cover

  1. Re-architect, not transliterate: inventory packages, map ETL logic to Spark/lakehouse patterns (idempotent, scalable).
  2. Modernize (medallion, Delta); parallel-run with reconciliation; decommission after parity.
  3. Prioritize highest-value/most-fragile packages; add controls (idempotency/DQ) the legacy lacked.
  4. Don't port line-by-line.
  5. Parallel-run to validate.

Starter pattern

inventory SSIS -> map to Spark/lakehouse (medallion, idempotent)
parallel-run + reconcile; add DQ/idempotency; decommission
Common mistake: Transliterating SSIS row-by-row into Spark instead of re-architecting, carrying over the legacy's fragility and missing controls.
Principal follow-up: How do you migrate SSIS claims ETL to Databricks without carrying over its fragility?
056
Design or evaluate a solution for Teradata to Snowflake migration. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
ExpertTeradata to Snowflake migration

Model answer

Migrate Teradata to Snowflake by translating schema/SQL to Snowflake patterns (clustering instead of PI, no manual partitions), re-pointing ingestion, parallel-running with reconciliation, optimizing for Snowflake (pruning, warehouses, cost), and migrating consumers with sign-off. Decommission after sustained parity. Re-optimize rather than lift-and-shift naively.

  • Translate schema/SQL to Snowflake patterns.
  • Re-point ingestion; parallel-run + reconcile.
  • Optimize for Snowflake (clustering/pruning/cost).
  • Migrate consumers + sign-off; decommission after parity.

Healthcare angle: Teradata→Snowflake for claims is re-optimized (clustering/pruning/warehouses), validated by reconciliation, not a naive lift-and-shift.

What strong answers cover

  1. Translate schema/SQL to Snowflake patterns (clustering not PI, no manual partitions), re-point ingestion.
  2. Parallel-run with reconciliation, optimize for Snowflake (pruning, warehouses, cost), migrate consumers with sign-off.
  3. Decommission after sustained parity; re-optimize rather than lift-and-shift.
  4. Don't naively lift-and-shift.
  5. Reconcile to parity.

Starter pattern

translate schema/SQL to Snowflake (clustering, pruning, warehouses)
parallel-run + reconcile; optimize; migrate consumers
Common mistake: Lift-and-shifting Teradata structures naively (primary indexes, manual partitions) instead of re-optimizing for Snowflake.
Principal follow-up: What changes when moving claims from Teradata to Snowflake, and why isn't it a lift-and-shift?
057
Design or evaluate a solution for SQL Server to lakehouse CDC. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
ExpertSQL Server to lakehouse CDC

Model answer

Stream SQL Server changes into the lakehouse via log-based CDC (Debezium/native) landed in Bronze, applied with ordered idempotent MERGE (LSN ordering), deletes mapped to soft-deletes, SCD2 history maintained, and schema drift handled. Reconcile to source. This gives near-real-time, idempotent replication of operational data into the lakehouse.

  • Log-based CDC → Bronze with LSN ordering.
  • Idempotent keyed MERGE; deletes → soft-delete.
  • SCD2 history + schema-drift handling.
  • Reconcile to source.

Healthcare angle: Membership/provider source tables stream into the lakehouse via CDC with ordered idempotent MERGE, kept reconciled to source.

What strong answers cover

  1. Stream SQL Server changes via log-based CDC (Debezium/native) landed in Bronze, applied with ordered idempotent MERGE (LSN ordering).
  2. Deletes mapped to soft-deletes; SCD2 history; schema-drift handling; reconcile to source.
  3. Near-real-time, idempotent replication of operational data into the lakehouse.
  4. Order by LSN.
  5. Idempotent keyed MERGE.

Starter pattern

log-based CDC -> Bronze (LSN order) -> idempotent MERGE
deletes->soft-delete; SCD2; reconcile to source
Common mistake: Applying CDC out of order or with non-idempotent writes, so the current state is wrong after replays.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep the current state correct when streaming SQL Server CDC into the lakehouse?
058
Design or evaluate a solution for performance test strategy. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertperformance test strategy

Model answer

Test performance against realistic peak data (month-end volumes, skew, concurrency), not average, with representative workloads, before production. Establish baselines, catch regressions in CI, and validate SLA headroom. A performance-test strategy turns 'it worked in dev' into proven peak readiness for the claims platform.

  • Test at realistic peak (month-end volume/skew/concurrency).
  • Representative workloads; establish baselines.
  • Catch regressions in CI; validate SLA headroom.
  • Prove peak readiness, not average.

Healthcare angle: Performance-testing the claims pipeline at month-end peak (with skew) proves SLA headroom before real volume hits.

What strong answers cover

  1. Test against realistic peak data (month-end volumes, skew, concurrency), not average, with representative workloads, before production.
  2. Establish baselines, catch regressions in CI, validate SLA headroom.
  3. Turns 'it worked in dev' into proven peak readiness.
  4. Test at peak, not average.
  5. Baseline and regression-gate.

Starter pattern

test at realistic peak (month-end volume + skew + concurrency)
baselines + CI regression gate; validate SLA headroom
Common mistake: Performance-testing on small dev data so the pipeline 'passes' then breaks at month-end peak in production.
Principal follow-up: How would you build a performance-test strategy that proves peak readiness for a claims pipeline?
059
Design or evaluate a solution for capacity model. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertcapacity model

Model answer

Build a capacity model linking demand drivers (membership growth, claim volume, new feeds, peaks) to compute/storage needs and cost, so you size for peak with guardrails and forecast ahead of growth. It turns capacity from reactive firefighting into planned, cost-aware provisioning aligned to business growth.

  • Link demand drivers to compute/storage/cost.
  • Size for peak with guardrails; forecast ahead.
  • Planned, cost-aware provisioning.
  • Aligned to membership/volume growth.

Healthcare angle: A capacity model ties claims volume growth and month-end peaks to provisioning so the platform scales ahead of demand.

What strong answers cover

  1. Build a model linking demand drivers (membership growth, claim volume, new feeds, peaks) to compute/storage needs and cost.
  2. Size for peak with guardrails; forecast ahead of growth.
  3. Turns capacity from reactive firefighting into planned, cost-aware provisioning aligned to business growth.
  4. Plan ahead of demand.
  5. Tie capacity to demand drivers.

Starter pattern

model demand drivers -> compute/storage/cost; size for peak
forecast ahead; cost guardrails
Common mistake: Reacting to capacity incidents instead of modeling demand growth and peaks, so the platform is always behind.
Principal follow-up: How would you build a capacity model that provisions ahead of membership growth and month-end peaks?
060
Design or evaluate a solution for DR architecture. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
ExpertDR architecture

Model answer

Architect DR with defined RPO/RTO tiered by criticality: cross-region replication of critical data and metadata/catalog, automated failover of jobs and access, and regular tested runbooks. Reference data and gold marts may differ in tier. Untested DR isn't DR — the test cadence is part of the architecture.

  • Defined RPO/RTO tiered by criticality.
  • Cross-region replication of data + metadata/catalog.
  • Automated failover + regular tested runbooks.
  • Untested DR isn't DR.

Healthcare angle: Claims-platform DR replicates gold marts and catalog cross-region with a tested failover meeting the business RTO.

What strong answers cover

  1. Architect DR with defined RPO/RTO tiered by criticality: cross-region replication of critical data and metadata/catalog.
  2. Automated failover of jobs and access; regular tested runbooks.
  3. Reference data and gold marts may differ in tier; untested DR isn't DR — the test cadence is part of the architecture.
  4. Tier by criticality.
  5. Test regularly.

Starter pattern

RPO/RTO tiered; cross-region replicate data+catalog; auto-failover
scheduled, tested DR runbook
Common mistake: Configuring replication but never testing failover, so the first real disaster reveals the catalog wasn't replicated.
Principal follow-up: What does a credible claims-platform DR architecture include, and why is the test cadence part of it?
061
Design or evaluate a solution for zero-downtime deployment. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertzero-downtime deployment

Model answer

Achieve zero-downtime via blue-green/canary and atomic publish: build and validate the new version aside, swap it in atomically (table SWAP/pointer) with instant rollback, and release changes behind canaries on a slice first. Consumers never see partial state, and bad releases roll back instantly. This is the default for consumer-facing marts.

  • Blue-green/canary + atomic swap publish.
  • Validate aside; swap with instant rollback.
  • Canary on a slice first.
  • No partial-state exposure.

Healthcare angle: Refreshed claims marts deploy via build-aside-then-swap, so BI never sees in-progress data and bad releases revert instantly.

What strong answers cover

  1. Achieve zero-downtime via blue-green/canary and atomic publish: build and validate the new version aside.
  2. Swap it in atomically (table SWAP/pointer) with instant rollback; release behind canaries on a slice first.
  3. Consumers never see partial state; bad releases roll back instantly. Default for consumer-facing marts.
  4. Build aside, then swap.
  5. Canary first.

Starter pattern

build+validate new mart aside -> ALTER TABLE SWAP (atomic) -> instant rollback
canary on a slice first
Common mistake: Deploying by editing the live mart in place, so consumers see partial state and rollback means a slow rebuild.
Principal follow-up: How do blue-green and canary give you zero-downtime deploys and instant rollback for marts?
062
Design or evaluate a solution for platform roadmap. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertplatform roadmap

Model answer

Build a roadmap balancing foundational investment (governance, reconciliation, observability, cost), migration/modernization, and new capabilities (AI readiness, interoperability), sequenced by value and risk, tied to business outcomes, and communicated to stakeholders. A principal roadmap makes deliberate, defensible bets — not a feature list — and revisits them as conditions change.

  • Balance foundations + modernization + new capabilities.
  • Sequence by value/risk; tie to business outcomes.
  • Communicate to stakeholders.
  • Deliberate bets, revisited over time.

Healthcare angle: A payer-platform roadmap sequences foundations (reconciliation/observability), migrations, and AI readiness against business value.

What strong answers cover

  1. Balance foundational investment (governance, reconciliation, observability, cost), migration/modernization, and new capabilities (AI readiness, interoperability).
  2. Sequence by value and risk, tie to business outcomes, communicate to stakeholders.
  3. A principal roadmap makes deliberate, defensible bets — not a feature list — and revisits them as conditions change.
  4. Tie to business outcomes.
  5. Sequence by value/risk.

Starter pattern

balance foundations + modernization + new capabilities
sequence by value/risk; tie to outcomes; communicate; revisit
Common mistake: Presenting a roadmap as a feature list with no sequencing rationale or tie to business value.
Principal follow-up: How would you sequence a payer-platform roadmap across foundations, migration, and new capabilities?
063
Design or evaluate a solution for principal code review. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertprincipal code review

Model answer

At principal level, review for architecture and systemic risk, not just style: correctness, idempotency, grain/keys, PHI handling, scalability, failure modes, and adherence to standards — while teaching through the review and choosing which battles matter. The goal is raising the team's bar and catching the risks that cause incidents, kindly and concretely.

  • Review architecture/systemic risk, not just style.
  • Idempotency, grain, PHI, scalability, failure modes.
  • Teach through review; pick meaningful battles.
  • Raise the team's bar kindly + concretely.

Healthcare angle: A principal review catches a missing-idempotency claims load (a real duplicate-payment risk) and teaches why, raising the bar.

What strong answers cover

  1. Review for architecture and systemic risk, not just style: correctness, idempotency, grain/keys, PHI handling, scalability, failure modes, standards.
  2. Teach through the review; choose which battles matter.
  3. Goal: raise the team's bar and catch the risks that cause incidents, kindly and concretely.
  4. Focus on incident-causing risks.
  5. Teach, don't just gatekeep.

Starter pattern

review: idempotency/grain/PHI/scalability/failure modes (not just style)
teach through review; pick meaningful battles
Common mistake: Nitpicking style while missing a non-idempotent claims load that will duplicate payments in production.
Principal follow-up: At principal level, what do you prioritize in a code review, and how do you raise the team's bar through it?
064
Design or evaluate a solution for design review board. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertdesign review board

Model answer

Run a design review board as a consistent quality gate: a checklist (grain/keys, history, idempotency, reconciliation, DQ, PHI, SLA/observability, cost, lineage, DR, contracts) applied to significant designs before build, with constructive feedback and ADRs capturing decisions. It catches predictable failure modes early and spreads standards without becoming a bottleneck.

  • Consistent checklist-based review of significant designs.
  • Grain/history/idempotency/reconciliation/PHI/SLA/cost/DR/contracts.
  • Constructive feedback + ADRs.
  • Catch failures early; spread standards.

Healthcare angle: A design review board applies the same bar (grain, idempotency, reconciliation, PHI) to every claims design before build.

What strong answers cover

  1. Run a board as a consistent quality gate: a checklist (grain/keys, history, idempotency, reconciliation, DQ, PHI, SLA/observability, cost, lineage, DR, contracts).
  2. Applied to significant designs before build, with constructive feedback and ADRs.
  3. Catches predictable failure modes early and spreads standards without becoming a bottleneck.
  4. Same bar for every design.
  5. Constructive, not gatekeeping.

Starter pattern

checklist-based review of significant designs (grain/idempotency/reconciliation/PHI/SLA/DR/contracts)
constructive feedback + ADRs
Common mistake: Either no design review (predictable failures reach prod) or a heavy bottleneck board that slows everything.
Principal follow-up: What's on your design-review checklist, and how do you keep the board a quality gate without becoming a bottleneck?
065
Design or evaluate a solution for technical strategy memo. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Experttechnical strategy memo

Model answer

Write strategy memos that frame the problem, options, recommendation, tradeoffs, risks, and a clear ask — in business terms leaders act on, backed by data. A good memo aligns stakeholders around a deliberate technical direction (e.g. lakehouse/warehouse strategy) and creates a durable record of the reasoning. Clarity and decision-orientation matter more than length.

  • Frame problem/options/recommendation/tradeoffs/risks/ask.
  • Business terms + data-backed.
  • Align stakeholders on direction.
  • Decision-oriented, durable record.

Healthcare angle: A strategy memo on the payer-platform data architecture aligns leadership on a deliberate, data-backed direction.

What strong answers cover

  1. Write memos that frame the problem, options, recommendation, tradeoffs, risks, and a clear ask — in business terms, data-backed.
  2. Align stakeholders around a deliberate technical direction; create a durable record of the reasoning.
  3. Clarity and decision-orientation matter more than length.
  4. Lead with the recommendation and ask.
  5. Back it with data.

Starter pattern

memo: problem -> options -> recommendation -> tradeoffs -> risks -> ask
business terms, data-backed, decision-oriented
Common mistake: Writing a long technical memo with no clear recommendation or ask, so it informs but doesn't align or decide.
Principal follow-up: What makes a technical strategy memo actually drive an aligned decision?
066
Design or evaluate a solution for executive explanation of outage. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertexecutive explanation of outage

Model answer

Explain outages to executives in their terms: business impact (who/what affected, dollars/SLA), current status and ETA, what's being done, and prevention — concise, honest, and non-technical, without blame or jargon. Executives need impact and confidence in the response, not a root-cause lecture; follow up with the RCA separately.

  • Lead with business impact (who/what/dollars/SLA).
  • Status + ETA + actions + prevention.
  • Concise, honest, non-technical, no blame.
  • RCA follows separately.

Healthcare angle: Explaining a claims outage to execs means impact and ETA in business terms, with confidence in the fix — not a technical deep-dive.

What strong answers cover

  1. Explain to executives in their terms: business impact (who/what affected, dollars/SLA), current status and ETA, what's being done, prevention.
  2. Concise, honest, non-technical, no blame or jargon.
  3. Executives need impact and confidence in the response, not a root-cause lecture; the RCA follows separately.
  4. Lead with impact and ETA.
  5. Save the deep RCA for later.

Starter pattern

exec update: impact (who/$/SLA) + status + ETA + actions + prevention
concise, non-technical, no blame; RCA later
Common mistake: Giving executives a technical root-cause deep-dive when they need business impact, ETA, and confidence in the fix.
Principal follow-up: How do you communicate a major claims outage to executives versus to engineers?
067
Design or evaluate a solution for tradeoff under budget cuts. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Experttradeoff under budget cuts

Model answer

Under budget cuts, protect non-negotiables (PHI/compliance, data integrity, critical SLAs) and cut deliberately: prioritize by business value/risk, find efficiency (cost optimization, decommission low-value work), and make the tradeoffs explicit to stakeholders so they own the choices. Transparent, value-based prioritization beats across-the-board cuts that erode critical capabilities.

  • Protect non-negotiables (PHI/integrity/critical SLAs).
  • Prioritize by value/risk; find efficiency.
  • Make tradeoffs explicit; stakeholders own choices.
  • Value-based over across-the-board cuts.

Healthcare angle: Budget cuts on the claims platform protect compliance/integrity/critical SLAs first and make the remaining tradeoffs explicit to leadership.

What strong answers cover

  1. Under cuts, protect non-negotiables (PHI/compliance, data integrity, critical SLAs) and cut deliberately.
  2. Prioritize by business value/risk, find efficiency (cost optimization, decommission low-value work), make tradeoffs explicit so stakeholders own the choices.
  3. Transparent, value-based prioritization beats across-the-board cuts that erode critical capabilities.
  4. Protect compliance/integrity first.
  5. Make tradeoffs explicit.

Starter pattern

protect non-negotiables (PHI/integrity/critical SLA); prioritize by value/risk
find efficiency; make tradeoffs explicit to stakeholders
Common mistake: Applying across-the-board cuts that quietly erode compliance/integrity controls, creating future incidents.
Principal follow-up: Under a budget cut, how do you decide what to protect and what to cut on a claims platform?
068
Design or evaluate a solution for build reusable ingestion accelerator. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertbuild reusable ingestion accelerator

Model answer

Build a metadata-driven ingestion accelerator so new feeds are configuration (source, schema, DQ rules, mappings, target) executed by a shared engine with built-in idempotency, DQ/quarantine, reconciliation, lineage, and observability. It dramatically speeds onboarding and enforces standards — with an escape hatch for genuinely bespoke logic so the framework doesn't become a straitjacket.

  • Metadata-driven: new feed = config.
  • Shared engine with idempotency/DQ/reconciliation/lineage.
  • Speeds onboarding + enforces standards.
  • Escape hatch for bespoke logic.

Healthcare angle: A reusable accelerator lets payer feeds onboard via config with built-in reconciliation/DQ — until a feed needs bespoke handling.

What strong answers cover

  1. Build a metadata-driven accelerator so new feeds are configuration, executed by a shared engine with built-in idempotency, DQ/quarantine, reconciliation, lineage, observability.
  2. Dramatically speeds onboarding and enforces standards.
  3. Provide an escape hatch for genuinely bespoke logic so the framework doesn't become a straitjacket.
  4. Config over code for the common case.
  5. Escape hatch for the rest.

Starter pattern

metadata config -> shared engine (idempotency+DQ+reconciliation+lineage)
escape hatch for bespoke logic
Common mistake: Either bespoke pipelines per feed (slow/inconsistent) or an over-rigid framework with no escape hatch for real edge cases.
Principal follow-up: How would you build an ingestion accelerator that speeds onboarding without becoming a straitjacket?
069
Design or evaluate a solution for tenant isolation. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Experttenant isolation

Model answer

Isolate tenants (LOBs/clients) by separate catalogs/schemas or enforced row-level tenant filtering, with per-tenant access control, cost attribution, and SLA, balancing isolation (security/blast radius) against shared-infra efficiency. In healthcare, preventing cross-tenant PHI leakage is paramount, so isolation defaults strong and is tested.

  • Isolate via catalogs/schemas or enforced row-level filter.
  • Per-tenant access/cost/SLA.
  • Balance isolation vs shared efficiency.
  • Prevent cross-tenant PHI leakage (tested).

Healthcare angle: Serving multiple plans/clients requires strong, tested tenant isolation so no client ever sees another's member PHI.

What strong answers cover

  1. Isolate tenants (LOBs/clients) by separate catalogs/schemas or enforced row-level tenant filtering, with per-tenant access control, cost attribution, SLA.
  2. Balance isolation (security/blast radius) against shared-infra efficiency.
  3. In healthcare, preventing cross-tenant PHI leakage is paramount, so isolation defaults strong and is tested.
  4. Strong isolation by default.
  5. Test for leakage.

Starter pattern

isolate via catalogs/schemas or enforced row-level tenant filter
per-tenant access/cost/SLA; test cross-tenant leakage
Common mistake: Weak tenant isolation (a shared table with optional filters) that risks one client seeing another's member PHI.
Principal follow-up: How do you isolate multiple clients/LOBs so no tenant can ever see another's PHI?
070
Design or evaluate a solution for metadata-driven framework limits. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertmetadata-driven framework limits

Model answer

Know where metadata-driven frameworks stop helping: they excel at standardized ingestion/transformation but become contorted for genuinely complex/bespoke logic, where config grows more complex than code. Recognize the limit and provide an escape hatch (custom modules) rather than forcing everything through config. Over-abstraction is a real anti-pattern.

  • Great for standardized ingestion/transformation.
  • Contorted for complex/bespoke logic.
  • Escape hatch beats forcing config.
  • Over-abstraction is an anti-pattern.

Healthcare angle: Most payer feeds fit a metadata framework; a genuinely bespoke adjudication transform should use code, not tortured config.

What strong answers cover

  1. Know where metadata-driven frameworks stop helping: great for standardized ingestion/transformation, contorted for genuinely complex/bespoke logic.
  2. Recognize the limit and provide an escape hatch (custom modules) rather than forcing everything through config.
  3. Over-abstraction is a real anti-pattern.
  4. Config for the common, code for the bespoke.
  5. Don't force config past its limit.

Starter pattern

framework for standardized feeds; escape hatch (custom module) for bespoke
when config > code complexity, use code
Common mistake: Forcing a genuinely bespoke adjudication transform through tortured config because 'everything must go through the framework'.
Principal follow-up: Where does a metadata-driven framework stop helping, and how do you handle the cases it can't express?
071
Design or evaluate a solution for config-driven anti-patterns. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertconfig-driven anti-patterns

Model answer

Watch for config-driven anti-patterns: config so complex it's harder than code, logic hidden in opaque metadata, no testing/version control of config, and frameworks that can't express needed cases forcing ugly workarounds. Keep config simple and declarative, test and version it, and don't abstract things that vary more than they repeat.

  • Config more complex than code.
  • Logic hidden in opaque metadata.
  • Untested/unversioned config.
  • Keep config simple; test + version it.

Healthcare angle: If onboarding a claims feed needs pages of tangled config, that's an anti-pattern — simplify or use code.

What strong answers cover

  1. Watch for config-driven anti-patterns: config more complex than code, logic hidden in opaque metadata, untested/unversioned config, frameworks that force ugly workarounds.
  2. Keep config simple and declarative, test and version it, don't abstract things that vary more than they repeat.
  3. Treat config as code (tested, versioned).
  4. Simplicity over cleverness.
  5. Recognize when config has gone too far.

Starter pattern

keep config simple+declarative; test+version it (treat as code)
don't abstract high-variation/one-off logic
Common mistake: Pages of tangled, untested, unversioned config that's harder to reason about than the code it replaced.
Principal follow-up: What are the warning signs that a config-driven approach has become an anti-pattern?
072
Design or evaluate a solution for what not to abstract. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertwhat not to abstract

Model answer

Abstract what repeats and is stable; don't abstract what varies more than it repeats or is genuinely one-off — premature/over-abstraction creates brittle, hard-to-understand frameworks. Principal judgment is knowing when a pattern has earned an abstraction (rule of three) versus when explicit code is clearer and safer.

  • Abstract stable repetition; not high-variation/one-offs.
  • Premature abstraction = brittle frameworks.
  • Rule of three before abstracting.
  • Sometimes explicit code is clearer/safer.

Healthcare angle: Standard claims ingestion earns an abstraction; a unique payer's quirky logic is clearer as explicit code.

What strong answers cover

  1. Abstract what repeats and is stable; don't abstract what varies more than it repeats or is genuinely one-off.
  2. Premature/over-abstraction creates brittle, hard-to-understand frameworks.
  3. Principal judgment is knowing when a pattern has earned an abstraction (rule of three) versus when explicit code is clearer and safer.
  4. Rule of three before abstracting.
  5. Sometimes explicit code wins.

Starter pattern

abstract stable repetition (rule of three); not high-variation/one-offs
explicit code when clearer/safer
Common mistake: Abstracting prematurely (after one or two uses) and creating a brittle framework that's harder to change than duplication.
Principal follow-up: How do you decide when a pattern has earned an abstraction versus staying explicit code?
073
Design or evaluate a solution for data product SLAs. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertdata product SLAs

Model answer

Define an SLA per data product matched to consumer need (freshness, completeness, availability), monitored with predictive breach alerting and attainment tracking, and owned. SLAs make expectations explicit and enforceable, and designing backward from the consumer deadline (accounting for upstream latency) is how you actually meet them.

  • SLA per product matched to consumer need.
  • Freshness/completeness/availability monitored.
  • Predictive alerts + attainment tracking + ownership.
  • Design backward from consumer deadline.

Healthcare angle: Each payer data product (claims, eligibility, marts) gets an SLA designed backward from its consumer's deadline and monitored.

What strong answers cover

  1. Define an SLA per data product matched to consumer need (freshness, completeness, availability), monitored with predictive breach alerting and attainment tracking, owned.
  2. SLAs make expectations explicit and enforceable.
  3. Designing backward from the consumer deadline (accounting for upstream latency) is how you actually meet them.
  4. One SLA per product, to consumer need.
  5. Design backward from the deadline.

Starter pattern

per product: SLA = consumer need (freshness/completeness/availability)
predictive alert + attainment tracking; design backward from deadline
Common mistake: Setting a uniform SLA for all products regardless of consumer need, over-engineering some and under-serving others.
Principal follow-up: How do you set and actually meet a data-product SLA designed backward from the consumer's deadline?
074
Design or evaluate a solution for quality scorecards. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertquality scorecards

Model answer

Publish per-product quality scorecards (DQ pass rates, freshness attainment, reconciliation status, incident counts) so quality is visible, comparable, and owned. Scorecards turn quality from an afterthought into a tracked, improving metric and let consumers gauge trust. Tie them to ownership and improvement goals.

  • Per-product DQ/freshness/reconciliation/incident metrics.
  • Visible, comparable, owned.
  • Quality becomes tracked + improving.
  • Signal trust to consumers.

Healthcare angle: A claims-product quality scorecard (DQ, freshness, reconciliation) makes data trust visible and drives improvement.

What strong answers cover

  1. Publish per-product quality scorecards (DQ pass rates, freshness attainment, reconciliation status, incident counts) so quality is visible, comparable, owned.
  2. Scorecards turn quality from an afterthought into a tracked, improving metric and let consumers gauge trust.
  3. Tie them to ownership and improvement goals.
  4. Make quality visible and comparable.
  5. Tie to ownership.

Starter pattern

per-product scorecard: DQ pass rate + freshness attainment + reconciliation + incidents
visible, comparable, owned
Common mistake: Quality tracked informally (or not at all), so consumers can't gauge trust and no one is accountable for improving it.
Principal follow-up: How would a quality scorecard make data trust visible and drive improvement across products?
075
Design or evaluate a solution for enterprise lineage. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertenterprise lineage

Model answer

Provide enterprise lineage (table and column, source-to-consumer) via automated capture (Unity Catalog/ACCESS_HISTORY) plus business context, enabling impact analysis, dispute resolution, trust, and audit at scale. Lineage is foundational infrastructure: it's how a disputed number is traced and how change impact is assessed across the platform.

  • Automated table/column lineage source-to-consumer.
  • Impact analysis + dispute resolution + audit.
  • Plus business-context documentation.
  • Foundational platform infrastructure.

Healthcare angle: Enterprise lineage traces a disputed PMPM from the gold mart back to source and shows what a claims-schema change will break.

What strong answers cover

  1. Provide enterprise lineage (table and column, source-to-consumer) via automated capture plus business context.
  2. Enables impact analysis, dispute resolution (trace a number to source), trust, and audit at scale.
  3. Foundational infrastructure: how a disputed number is traced and how change impact is assessed.
  4. Automated + documented.
  5. Column-level, source-to-consumer.

Starter pattern

automated table+column lineage (source->consumer) + business context
impact analysis + dispute resolution + audit
Common mistake: No column-level lineage, so a disputed PMPM can't be traced to source and a schema change's blast radius is unknown.
Principal follow-up: How does enterprise lineage let you trace a disputed number to source and assess change impact?
076
Design or evaluate a solution for trusted layer certification. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Experttrusted layer certification

Model answer

Establish a certified/trusted data layer: datasets that meet defined quality, reconciliation, governance, and documentation standards are certified for broad consumption, so consumers know what to trust. Certification (with ongoing monitoring) separates production-grade data from raw/experimental, and is the backbone of safe self-service.

  • Certify datasets meeting quality/reconciliation/governance standards.
  • Consumers know what to trust.
  • Separate certified from raw/experimental.
  • Backbone of safe self-service.

Healthcare angle: A certified claims gold layer tells analysts which data is production-grade and safe to build on.

What strong answers cover

  1. Establish a certified/trusted data layer: datasets meeting defined quality, reconciliation, governance, documentation standards are certified for broad consumption.
  2. Consumers know what to trust; certification (with ongoing monitoring) separates production-grade from raw/experimental.
  3. The backbone of safe self-service.
  4. Certify, then monitor.
  5. Separate certified from raw.

Starter pattern

certify datasets meeting quality/reconciliation/governance standards
consumers consume certified layer; monitor ongoing
Common mistake: Letting consumers build on raw/experimental data because there's no certified layer signaling what's production-grade.
Principal follow-up: How does a certified/trusted layer make self-service safe, and what earns certification?
077
Design or evaluate a solution for claims ontology. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertclaims ontology

Model answer

Define a claims ontology/semantic model: the canonical entities, relationships, and definitions (claim, line, member, provider, adjustment, denial) that standardize meaning across the enterprise. It underpins the semantic layer, glossary, and conformed dimensions, ensuring everyone models and means the same things — reducing the definitional disputes that plague payer analytics.

  • Canonical entities/relationships/definitions.
  • Claim/line/member/provider/adjustment/denial.
  • Underpins semantic layer + glossary + conformed dims.
  • Shared meaning reduces disputes.

Healthcare angle: A shared claims ontology ensures claim, line, and adjustment mean the same thing across every payer mart and report.

What strong answers cover

  1. Define a claims ontology/semantic model: canonical entities, relationships, definitions (claim, line, member, provider, adjustment, denial).
  2. Standardize meaning across the enterprise; underpins the semantic layer, glossary, conformed dimensions.
  3. Ensures everyone models and means the same things, reducing definitional disputes.
  4. Canonical entities and relationships.
  5. Shared meaning reduces disputes.

Starter pattern

canonical entities/relationships/definitions (claim/line/member/provider/adjustment/denial)
underpins semantic layer + conformed dims
Common mistake: Every team defining claim/line/adjustment slightly differently, so marts and reports mean different things.
Principal follow-up: How does a shared claims ontology reduce the definitional disputes that plague payer analytics?
078
Design or evaluate a solution for business glossary adoption. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertbusiness glossary adoption

Model answer

Drive glossary adoption by making it useful and embedded: link terms to the data/lineage that implement them, integrate it into tools/workflows, assign stewards, and keep it current. A glossary nobody uses is shelfware; adoption comes from being the authoritative, accessible answer to 'what does this metric mean' at the point of need.

  • Link terms to implementing data/lineage.
  • Embed in tools/workflows; assign stewards.
  • Keep current; make it the authoritative answer.
  • Adoption from usefulness at point of need.

Healthcare angle: A glossary defining PMPM/denial-rate, linked to the marts and embedded in BI, actually gets used and stops definitional drift.

What strong answers cover

  1. Drive adoption by making the glossary useful and embedded: link terms to the data/lineage that implement them, integrate into tools/workflows, assign stewards, keep current.
  2. A glossary nobody uses is shelfware; adoption comes from being the authoritative, accessible answer at the point of need.
  3. Embed it where people work.
  4. Link terms to implementing data.
  5. Steward and maintain it.

Starter pattern

link terms to implementing data/lineage; embed in BI/tools; stewards keep current
authoritative answer at point of need
Common mistake: A glossary built once and never embedded or maintained, so it becomes shelfware and definitions still drift.
Principal follow-up: How do you drive real glossary adoption so definitions are used, not ignored?
079
Design or evaluate a solution for interoperability roadmap. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Expertinteroperability roadmap

Model answer

Sequence an interoperability roadmap around standards and mandates: FHIR API enablement (Patient Access, payer-to-payer), USCDI/US Core conformance, clinical data ingestion (HL7/C-CDA), and normalization into the warehouse — prioritized by regulatory deadlines and value. Treat it as building durable standards-based exchange, not one-off integrations.

  • FHIR APIs (Patient Access/payer-to-payer) + US Core/USCDI.
  • Ingest clinical (HL7/C-CDA); normalize to warehouse.
  • Prioritize by regulatory deadlines + value.
  • Durable standards-based exchange.

Healthcare angle: An interoperability roadmap sequences FHIR Patient Access, USCDI conformance, and clinical ingestion against CMS deadlines.

What strong answers cover

  1. Sequence around standards and mandates: FHIR API enablement (Patient Access, payer-to-payer), USCDI/US Core conformance, clinical ingestion (HL7/C-CDA), normalization.
  2. Prioritize by regulatory deadlines and value.
  3. Build durable standards-based exchange, not one-off integrations.
  4. Deadlines drive sequencing.
  5. Normalize to standards.

Starter pattern

sequence: FHIR APIs (Patient Access/payer-to-payer) + USCDI/US Core + clinical ingest
prioritize by regulatory deadline + value
Common mistake: Building one-off integrations per partner instead of standards-based exchange, creating brittle work that fails new mandates.
Principal follow-up: How would you sequence an interoperability roadmap against CMS deadlines and existing HL7 feeds?
080
Design or evaluate a solution for FHIR normalization. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
ExpertFHIR normalization

Model answer

Normalize FHIR by landing raw resources (VARIANT) in Bronze for fidelity/replay, then flattening/typing into Silver conformed to US Core, mapping resources to your dimensions (Patient→member, Coverage→eligibility, Claim/EOB→claims) with code normalization (LOINC/RxNorm). Materialize hot fields. The goal is interoperable, queryable clinical/claims data linked to internal keys.

  • Raw VARIANT Bronze → typed US Core Silver.
  • Map resources to dimensions (Patient/Coverage/EOB).
  • Normalize codes (LOINC/RxNorm); materialize hot fields.
  • Link to internal member/provider keys.

Healthcare angle: FHIR normalization maps Patient/Coverage/EOB to member/eligibility/claims with code normalization, linked to internal keys.

What strong answers cover

  1. Normalize FHIR by landing raw resources (VARIANT) in Bronze for fidelity/replay, then flattening/typing into Silver conformed to US Core.
  2. Map resources to dimensions (Patient->member, Coverage->eligibility, Claim/EOB->claims) with code normalization (LOINC/RxNorm); materialize hot fields.
  3. Goal: interoperable, queryable clinical/claims data linked to internal keys.
  4. Raw fidelity, then typed projection.
  5. Map resources to dimensions.

Starter pattern

raw VARIANT Bronze -> typed US Core Silver; map Patient/Coverage/EOB to dims
normalize codes; materialize hot fields
Common mistake: Flattening FHIR resources directly to typed tables with no raw landing, losing fidelity and replayability.
Principal follow-up: How do you normalize FHIR resources into your dimensional model while preserving fidelity and replay?
081
Design or evaluate a solution for HL7 and FHIR coexistence. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
PrincipalHL7 and FHIR coexistence

Model answer

Support HL7 v2 (real-time ADT/ORU messaging) and FHIR (resource/API-based) coexistence by ingesting each appropriately, normalizing both into a common internal model, and routing by use case (HL7 for real-time event feeds, FHIR for API exchange/Patient Access). Many systems will emit both for years, so the architecture bridges them rather than picking one.

  • HL7 v2 (real-time messaging) + FHIR (API/resources).
  • Normalize both to a common internal model.
  • Route by use case (HL7 events, FHIR exchange).
  • Bridge both; coexistence lasts years.

Healthcare angle: Real-time ADT (HL7) and Patient Access (FHIR) both feed the platform; normalize both into one model rather than choosing.

What strong answers cover

  1. Support HL7 v2 (real-time ADT/ORU messaging) and FHIR (resource/API-based) coexistence: ingest each appropriately, normalize both into a common internal model.
  2. Route by use case (HL7 for real-time event feeds, FHIR for API exchange/Patient Access).
  3. Many systems emit both for years, so the architecture bridges them rather than picking one.
  4. Normalize both to one model.
  5. Route by use case.

Starter pattern

HL7 v2 (real-time ADT/ORU) + FHIR (API/resources) -> common internal model
route by use case; coexistence lasts years
Common mistake: Assuming FHIR replaces HL7 immediately and not bridging both, so real-time HL7 event feeds are left unhandled.
Principal follow-up: Why will HL7 and FHIR coexist for years, and how do you normalize both into one model?
082
Design or evaluate a solution for EOB data product. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
PrincipalEOB data product

Model answer

Build the EOB as a governed member-facing data product: combine claim + adjudication detail (billed/allowed/paid/member-responsibility) accurately, serve it via FHIR ExplanationOfBenefit for Patient Access, and ensure correctness (member responsibility especially) since members see it. Treat accuracy, freshness, and PHI handling as product-grade requirements.

  • Combine claim + adjudication accurately.
  • Serve via FHIR EOB for Patient Access.
  • Correct member-responsibility (members see it).
  • Product-grade accuracy/freshness/PHI.

Healthcare angle: The EOB data product powers member-facing transparency, so member-responsibility accuracy and PHI handling are paramount.

What strong answers cover

  1. Build the EOB as a governed member-facing data product: combine claim + adjudication (billed/allowed/paid/member-responsibility) accurately.
  2. Serve via FHIR ExplanationOfBenefit for Patient Access; member-responsibility accuracy is paramount since members see it.
  3. Treat accuracy, freshness, and PHI handling as product-grade requirements.
  4. Member-facing accuracy is critical.
  5. Serve via FHIR EOB.

Starter pattern

EOB = claim + adjudication(billed/allowed/paid/member_resp); serve via FHIR EOB
product-grade accuracy/freshness/PHI
Common mistake: Treating the EOB as a back-office extract, so inaccurate member-responsibility reaches a member-facing API.
Principal follow-up: Why is the EOB member-responsibility number so sensitive, and how do you guarantee it ties to adjudication?
083
Design or evaluate a solution for actuarial data mart. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalactuarial data mart

Model answer

Serve actuaries a stable, governed mart: defined grain/metrics, full and reproducible history (as-of/bitemporal), incurred-vs-paid with IBNR support, trend decomposition, and restatement for late data — prioritizing correctness, auditability, and reproducibility over query speed. Actuaries must reproduce prior periods exactly, so snapshotting and stable definitions are central.

  • Defined grain/metrics; reproducible as-of history.
  • Incurred vs paid + IBNR; trend decomposition.
  • Restate for late data.
  • Correctness/reproducibility over speed.

Healthcare angle: The actuarial mart must reproduce a prior period exactly after retro corrections — bitemporal snapshots and stable definitions make it possible.

What strong answers cover

  1. Serve actuaries a stable, governed mart: defined grain/metrics, full and reproducible history (as-of/bitemporal), incurred-vs-paid with IBNR support, trend decomposition, restatement for late data.
  2. Prioritize correctness, auditability, reproducibility over query speed.
  3. Actuaries must reproduce prior periods exactly, so snapshotting and stable definitions are central.
  4. Reproducibility over speed.
  5. IBNR-aware incurred cost.

Starter pattern

stable grain/metrics; bitemporal as-of history; incurred=paid+IBNR; decompose trend
restate late data; reproducible
Common mistake: Serving actuaries a fast but non-reproducible mart, so they can't reproduce a prior period after restatements.
Principal follow-up: Why do actuaries need a reproducible, IBNR-aware mart, and what design makes that possible?
084
Design or evaluate a solution for cost trend forecasting platform. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalcost trend forecasting platform

Model answer

Build cost-trend forecasting on restated, IBNR-adjusted historical PMPM with trend decomposed into utilization/unit-cost/mix, feeding forecasting models with governed, reproducible features. Recent-period incompleteness must be handled (completion factors) so forecasts aren't biased by immature data. Treat it as a governed analytics/ML product with clear definitions and monitoring.

  • IBNR-adjusted historical PMPM + decomposed trend.
  • Governed, reproducible features for forecasting.
  • Handle recent-period incompleteness (completion factors).
  • Governed analytics/ML product.

Healthcare angle: Cost-trend forecasting must use IBNR-adjusted, decomposed trend so immature recent months don't bias the forecast.

What strong answers cover

  1. Build forecasting on restated, IBNR-adjusted historical PMPM with trend decomposed into utilization/unit-cost/mix, feeding models with governed, reproducible features.
  2. Handle recent-period incompleteness (completion factors) so forecasts aren't biased by immature data.
  3. Treat it as a governed analytics/ML product with clear definitions and monitoring.
  4. IBNR-adjust history.
  5. Don't let immature months bias forecasts.

Starter pattern

IBNR-adjusted decomposed PMPM history -> forecasting model (governed features)
completion factors for recent months; monitor
Common mistake: Forecasting on raw paid-to-date trend, so immature recent months bias the forecast and pricing is wrong.
Principal follow-up: How do you keep immature recent months from biasing a cost-trend forecast?
085
Design or evaluate a solution for member 360 architecture. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalmember 360 architecture

Model answer

Build Member 360 on a conformed member dimension (identity-resolved, bitemporal) integrating claims, pharmacy, clinical, eligibility, and engagement into a unified, governed view with minimum-necessary PHI. It serves care management, risk, and service use cases. Identity resolution and as-of correctness are the hard parts; govern access tightly given the PHI concentration.

  • Conformed, identity-resolved, bitemporal member dimension.
  • Integrate claims/pharmacy/clinical/eligibility/engagement.
  • Unified governed view; minimum-necessary PHI.
  • Identity resolution + as-of correctness are key.

Healthcare angle: Member 360 unifies a member's full history for care management — built on identity resolution, as-of correctness, and tight PHI governance.

What strong answers cover

  1. Build Member 360 on a conformed member dimension (identity-resolved, bitemporal) integrating claims, pharmacy, clinical, eligibility, engagement into a unified, governed view with minimum-necessary PHI.
  2. Serves care management, risk, service use cases.
  3. Identity resolution and as-of correctness are the hard parts; govern access tightly given the PHI concentration.
  4. Identity resolution is the hard part.
  5. Tight PHI governance.

Starter pattern

conformed identity-resolved bitemporal member dim + claims/pharmacy/clinical/eligibility
unified governed view; minimum PHI
Common mistake: Stitching Member 360 on weak identity resolution, fragmenting or wrongly merging members and concentrating PHI without tight governance.
Principal follow-up: What are the hard parts of Member 360, and how do you govern its concentrated PHI?
086
Design or evaluate a solution for provider 360 architecture. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalprovider 360 architecture

Model answer

Build Provider 360 on a conformed, identity-resolved provider dimension (NPI + crosswalks, effective-dated, group/network bridges) integrating roster, claims activity, quality, network, and contract data into a unified view. It serves network management, VBC, and quality. Effective-dating and many-to-many affiliations are the modeling challenges.

  • Conformed, identity-resolved provider dimension.
  • Integrate roster/claims/quality/network/contracts.
  • Effective-dated + group/network bridges.
  • Serves network mgmt/VBC/quality.

Healthcare angle: Provider 360 unifies roster, activity, quality, and network into one view — built on effective-dated identity and affiliation bridges.

What strong answers cover

  1. Build Provider 360 on a conformed, identity-resolved provider dimension (NPI + crosswalks, effective-dated, group/network bridges) integrating roster, claims activity, quality, network, contract data.
  2. Serves network management, VBC, quality.
  3. Effective-dating and many-to-many affiliations are the modeling challenges.
  4. Effective-dated identity + bridges.
  5. Unify roster/activity/quality/network.

Starter pattern

conformed identity-resolved provider dim (effective-dated, group/network bridges)
integrate roster/claims/quality/network/contracts
Common mistake: Building Provider 360 without effective-dating or affiliation bridges, so historical attribution and network views are wrong.
Principal follow-up: What makes Provider 360 hard, and how do effective-dating and bridges solve it?
087
Design or evaluate a solution for payer 360 architecture. Include scale, tradeoffs, failure handling, security, and operational ownership.
Principalpayer 360 architecture

Model answer

Payer/business 360 integrates member, provider, claims, financial, and quality domains into an enterprise view for executive and cross-domain analytics, built on conformed dimensions, a semantic layer, and certified data with one source of truth per domain. It's the capstone of conformed-dimension and governance work — its value depends entirely on the underlying consistency.

  • Integrate member/provider/claims/financial/quality.
